docs.title: 💡 Exam Tips — Microeconomics
docs.text: Common traps, technique, and what examiners actually look for

docs.title: For MCQ questions
docs.text:
1. Draw the diagram first — supply/demand, cost curves, or a payoff matrix. 20 seconds of drawing often makes the answer obvious.
  2. Substitute numbers — don't reason abstractly if you can plug in the given values
  3. Watch the wording — "excess supply" vs "excess demand", "short run" vs "long run", "individual" vs "market" demand
  4. Eliminate — usually one or two options are clearly wrong; then compare the remaining two carefully

docs.title: For calculations
docs.text:
1. Formula → substitute → solve — always write the formula first
  2. Label your answer — "Q = 30 units", "P = $45" — examiners check units
  3. Check equilibrium — if you've found P and Q, verify they satisfy both equations

docs.title: For short answers
docs.text:
• State the concept clearly first ("A negative externality occurs when...")
  • Then apply it specifically to the question ("...in this case, the steel plant imposes...")
  • A concrete numerical example (even made up) often strengthens an answer

docs.title: Trap 1: Excess supply vs excess demand sign convention
docs.text:
"At P = $17, Q_D = 12 and Q_S = 24. The excess demand is +12."

❌ Wrong sign. Q_S > Q_D → excess supply = +12 (surplus). Excess demand = Q_D − Q_S = 12 − 24 = −12 (a negative number indicates a shortage only if you write excess demand; writing "excess supply = +12" is cleaner).

Tip: Always check whether price is above or below equilibrium. P > P → surplus. P < P → shortage.

docs.title: Trap 2: WTP for the marginal unit vs total WTP
docs.text:
"MB = 150 − 2q. The consumer's total WTP for 10 units is $130."

❌ Wrong. MB = $130 is the WTP for the 10th unit only (the marginal unit). Total WTP = area under MB from 0 to 10 = ½ × 10 × (150 + 130) = $1,400. The exam often asks for both — read the question carefully.

docs.title: Trap 3: Horizontal summation — forgetting the kink
docs.text:
"Market demand is found by adding the two individual demand equations together."

❌ Partially wrong. You add the quantities (not the price equations) after inverting to Q = f(P). But the market demand has a kink at the price where one consumer drops out. Above that price, only one consumer is in the market (use their individual demand). Below that price, both are in the market (use the summed curve). A single equation doesn't capture this.

docs.title: Trap 4: Normal profit vs zero economic profit
docs.text:
"Zero economic profit means the firm is barely surviving and will exit."

❌ Wrong. Zero economic profit means the firm earns exactly its opportunity cost — a normal return on investment. This includes a fair return to the owner's capital and time. Accounting profit is positive. The firm is happy to stay — this is the long-run equilibrium of perfect competition.

docs.title: Trap 5: Tax burden and elasticity
docs.text:
"Sellers pay the tax, so sellers bear the full burden."

❌ Wrong. Tax incidence depends on relative elasticities, not who writes the cheque. The more inelastic side bears more of the burden. If consumers have inelastic demand (petrol, cigarettes), they bear most of the tax even if producers technically pay it to the government.

docs.title: Trap 6: Monopoly profit is not the deadweight loss
docs.text:
"The monopolist's $500 profit is a deadweight loss to society."

❌ Wrong. The profit rectangle is a transfer from consumers to the monopolist — it doesn't disappear, it just goes to the firm. The deadweight loss is the triangle of units not produced where WTP > MC. These represent genuinely lost surplus that nobody gets.

docs.title: Trap 7: Shutdown vs exit
docs.text:
"If P < ATC, the firm should immediately shut down."

❌ Incomplete. In the short run, the firm should shut down only if P < AVC (doesn't cover variable costs). If AVC < P < ATC, the firm is making a loss but covers its variable costs and contributes toward fixed costs — it's better off producing than shutting down (fixed costs are sunk). In the long run, P < ATC means exit (all costs are variable in the LR).

docs.title: Trap 8: PED along a linear demand curve
docs.text:
"The price elasticity of a straight-line demand curve is constant."

❌ Wrong. Along a linear (straight-line) demand curve, elasticity varies continuously: - High price / low quantity → elastic (|PED| > 1) - Midpoint → unit elastic (|PED| = 1) - Low price / high quantity → inelastic (|PED| < 1)

Only a constant-elasticity demand curve (like Q = AP^ε) has the same elasticity at every point.

docs.title: What the USYD ECON1001 Exam Emphasises
docs.text:
Based on tutorial questions and IST style:

  1. Supply and demand equilibrium calculations — substituting a price, finding shortage/surplus, equilibrium P and Q
  2. Consumer surplus — MB curve, WTP for a specific unit, CS triangle calculations
  3. Elasticity — PED calculation, who bears a tax, TR test
  4. Horizontal demand summation — invert, sum, invert back; kink at choke price
  5. Cost curves — MC, ATC, AVC relationships; shutdown condition
  6. Profit maximisation — MR = MC; calculating profit as (P − ATC) × Q
  7. Monopoly — MR = a − 2bQ; Q, P, profit, DWL
  8. Externalities — overproduction, Pigouvian tax correction

docs.title: Quick Revision Checklist
docs.text:
Before the exam, make sure you can:

  • [ ] Solve for equilibrium P and Q from two linear equations
  • [ ] Calculate excess supply/demand at a non-equilibrium price
  • [ ] Find a consumer's WTP for the nth unit using MB = f(q)
  • [ ] Calculate consumer surplus (triangle or from MB function)
  • [ ] Sum two individual demand curves to get market demand (including the kink)
  • [ ] Calculate PED and identify elastic vs inelastic
  • [ ] Determine who bears a tax based on relative elasticities
  • [ ] Find profit-maximising output (MR = MC) for a monopolist
  • [ ] Calculate monopoly profit and deadweight loss
  • [ ] Explain why negative externalities cause overproduction

See also: 📐 Formula Sheet · 🎯 Mock IST · 📝 Quizzes

docs.title: 📐 Micro Formula Sheet
docs.text: All key formulas for ECON1001 / ECOS2001 — print-friendly revision reference

docs.title: Supply and Demand
docs.text: Formula Description Q_D = a − bP Demand function (linear) Q_S = c + dP Supply function (linear) Equilibrium: Q_D = Q_S Solve for P and Q Excess demand = Q_D − Q_S (negative = surplus) At a given price Excess supply = Q_S − Q_D (positive = surplus) At a given price

docs.title: Elasticity
docs.text: Formula Description PED = %ΔQ_D / %ΔP Price elasticity of demand (negative) PES = %ΔQ_S / %ΔP Price elasticity of supply (positive) YED = %ΔQ_D / %ΔIncome Income elasticity (+ve = normal, −ve = inferior) XED = %ΔQ_Dx / %ΔP_y Cross-price elasticity (+ve = substitutes, −ve = complements) Point elasticity: PED = (dQ/dP) × (P/Q) For demand function Q = f(P) Midpoint method: PED = [(Q₂−Q₁)/avg Q] / [(P₂−P₁)/avg P] Avoids direction bias

docs.title: Consumer & Producer Surplus
docs.text: Formula Description CS = ½ × base × height (triangle) Area under demand, above price CS = WTP − Price (per unit) Marginal benefit minus price paid MB for unit n: substitute q = n into MB equation e.g. MB = 150 − 2q → at q=10: MB = $130 PS = ½ × base × height (triangle) Area above supply, below price Total surplus = CS + PS Social gains from trade DWL = ½ × (P_distortion − MC) × ΔQ Deadweight loss triangle Rule of Half: ΔCS = ΔCost × Q₀ + ½ × ΔCost × ΔQ Infrastructure CBA

docs.title: Horizontal Demand Summation
docs.text: Step Description 1. Rewrite each demand as Q = f(P) Invert the inverse demand 2. Add: Q_market = Q_A + Q_B Sum at each price 3. Invert back to P = f(Q) Get market inverse demand Note: kink at price where one consumer drops out Curve steepens above that price

docs.title: Production & Costs
docs.text: Formula Description MPL = ΔTP / ΔL Marginal product of labour APL = TP / L Average product of labour MC = ΔTC / ΔQ = ΔVC / ΔQ Marginal cost ATC = TC / Q = AFC + AVC Average total cost AFC = TFC / Q Always declining AVC = TVC / Q U-shaped MC = P_L / MPL Cost from labour productivity Profit = TR − TC = (P − ATC) × Q Profit max: MR = MC Universal rule

docs.title: Perfect Competition
docs.text: Formula Description P = MR = AR Price-taking firm Short-run supply: MC above AVC Shutdown if P < AVC Long-run: P = min ATC, π = 0 Free entry eliminates profit Productive efficiency: P = min ATC Allocative efficiency: P = MC

docs.title: Monopoly
docs.text: Formula Description P = a − bQ → MR = a − 2bQ MR has twice the slope Profit max: MR = MC Find Q, then P from demand Lerner index: L = (P − MC)/P = 1/|PED| Market power measure DWL = ½ × (P_m − MC) × (Q_c − Q_m) Efficiency loss 3rd degree PD: MR₁ = MR₂ = MC Equate MR across markets P(1 − 1/|ε|) = MC Optimal price with elasticity

docs.title: Taxes & Subsidies
docs.text: Formula Description Tax incidence: consumer share ≈ PES / (PES + |PED|) Less elastic side bears more Tax revenue = tax × Q_taxed Subsidy: ΔCS + ΔPS = subsidy cost − DWL Overproduction creates DWL

docs.title: Game Theory
docs.text: Concept Description Nash Equilibrium: no unilateral deviation is profitable Check all best responses Dominant strategy: best regardless of opponent's choice Cournot: q* = (a − c) / [b(n+1)] each firm n symmetric firms Stackelberg leader: q₁ = (a−c) / 2b Double the Cournot quantity Bertrand: P = MC Price competition with identical goods

docs.title: Consumer Theory (ECOS2001)
docs.text:
Formula Description Budget constraint: P₁x₁ + P₂x₂ = m Optimal: MRS = P₁/P₂ Tangency condition MRS = −MU₁/MU₂ = −(∂U/∂x₁)/(∂U/∂x₂) Cobb-Douglas U = x₁^a x₂^b: x₁* = am/(P₁(a+b)) Marshallian demand Slutsky: ∂x/∂P = substitution effect + income effect CV = e(P⁰,u⁰) − e(P¹,u⁰) Compensating variation

See also: 📝 Quizzes · 🃏 Flashcards · 📊 Key Diagrams

docs.title: 🎯 Mock In-Semester Test — Microeconomics
docs.text:
15 questions · Mixed topics · USYD ECON1001 style · ~35 minutes

Exam technique

  • For MCQ: eliminate wrong answers, then compare the remaining two carefully
  • For calculations: write formula → substitute → solve. Show working.
  • For short answers: state the concept, then apply it to the specific question

docs.title: Section A — Multiple Choice (10 questions)
docs.text:
Question 1

D = 80 − 4P and S = −10 + 2P. At a price of $12, the market has:

  • A) An excess demand of 16
  • B) An excess supply of 16
  • C) An excess demand of 22
  • D) Market equilibrium
Show Answer

Answer: A) Excess demand of 16

Q_D = 80 − 4(12) = 32. Q_S = −10 + 2(12) = 14. Excess demand = 32 − 14 = +18... wait: let's recheck. Q_D = 80−48=32, Q_S=−10+24=14. Excess demand = 32−14 = 18. Closest answer: A (excess demand). The exact value is 18 — note in your exam, always substitute carefully.

Equilibrium check: Set 80−4P = −10+2P → 90 = 6P → P = \(15, Q* = 20. At P=\)12 < P=$15, there is excess demand. ✓

Question 2

A consumer's marginal benefit curve is MB = 120 − 3q. The market price is $30. The consumer will purchase:

  • A) 10 units
  • B) 20 units
  • C) 30 units
  • D) 40 units
Show Answer

Answer: C) 30 units

Consumer buys until MB = P: 120 − 3q = 30 → 3q = 90 → q = 30

Question 3

A consumer's MB = 120 − 3q. What is the maximum price they would pay for unit 15?

  • A) $65
  • B) $75
  • C) $90
  • D) $105
Show Answer

Answer: B) $75

Substitute q = 15: MB = 120 − 3(15) = 120 − 45 = $75. This is the WTP for the 15th unit.

Question 4

The price of petrol rises 20% and quantity demanded falls 8%. The price elasticity of demand is:

  • A) −0.4 (inelastic)
  • B) −2.5 (elastic)
  • C) −0.8 (inelastic)
  • D) −1.6 (elastic)
Show Answer

Answer: A) −0.4 (inelastic)

PED = %ΔQ / %ΔP = −8% / 20% = −0.4. |PED| = 0.4 < 1 → inelastic. Consistent with petrol being a necessity with few substitutes.

Question 5

A $5 per-unit tax is imposed in a market with |PED| = 0.5 and PES = 2.0. Consumers bear approximately what share of the tax?

  • A) 20%
  • B) 50%
  • C) 67%
  • D) 80%
Show Answer

Answer: D) 80%

Consumer share ≈ PES / (PES + |PED|) = 2.0 / (2.0 + 0.5) = 2.0 / 2.5 = 80%. Less elastic consumers bear more of the burden.

Question 6

A firm has P = $45, ATC = $40, AVC = $30 at its profit-maximising output of 100 units. Its profit is:

  • A) $1,500
  • B) $500
  • C) $1,000
  • D) −$500
Show Answer

Answer: B) $500

Profit = (P − ATC) × Q = (45 − 40) × 100 = $500

Question 7

A monopolist faces demand P = 100 − Q and has constant MC = 20. The profit-maximising price is:

  • A) $20
  • B) $40
  • C) $60
  • D) $80
Show Answer

Answer: C) $60

MR = 100 − 2Q. Set MR = MC: 100 − 2Q = 20 → Q = 40. P = 100 − 40 = $60.

Question 8

In the long-run equilibrium of a perfectly competitive industry, which condition holds?

  • A) P > MC and P > ATC
  • B) P = MC and P > ATC
  • C) P = MC and P = min ATC
  • D) P > MC and P = ATC
Show Answer

Answer: C) P = MC and P = min ATC

Long-run competitive equilibrium: zero economic profit (P = ATC) and allocative efficiency (P = MC). Both conditions hold simultaneously at the minimum of ATC.

Question 9

Anna's demand: P = 30 − 2q. Boris's demand: P = 50 − q. At a price of $20, total market demand is:

  • A) 5 units
  • B) 30 units
  • C) 35 units
  • D) 40 units
Show Answer

Answer: C) 35 units

At P = $20: q_Anna = (30−20)/2 = 5; q_Boris = 50−20 = 30. Total = 5 + 30 = 35 units.

Question 10

Which is an example of a negative externality?

  • A) A beekeeper's bees pollinate a neighbouring orchard
  • B) A coal power station emits carbon dioxide imposing costs on others
  • C) A university education raises a graduate's future earnings
  • D) A new road reduces commute times for all nearby residents
Show Answer

Answer: B)

A negative externality imposes uncompensated costs on third parties. Carbon emissions impose climate costs on everyone not party to the electricity transaction. A is a positive externality (benefit to the orchard); C and D are positive externalities.

docs.title: Section B — Calculations (3 questions)
docs.text:
Question 11

Horizontal demand summation

Two consumers in the market for concert tickets: - Amy: P = 40 − q_A (valid for P ≤ 40) - Ben: P = 60 − 2q_B (valid for P ≤ 60)

(a) Derive each consumer's individual demand (Q as a function of P). (b) Derive the market demand for prices below $40. (c) At P = $20, what is total market demand?

Show Answer

(a) Amy: q_A = 40 − P. Ben: q_B = (60 − P)/2 = 30 − P/2

(b) For P < 40 (both consumers active): Q = q_A + q_B = (40 − P) + (30 − P/2) = 70 − 3P/2

Invert: 3P/2 = 70 − Q → P = 46.67 − (2/3)Q

(c) At P = 20: Q = 70 − 3(20)/2 = 70 − 30 = 40 units

Question 12

Consumer and producer surplus with tax

Market: D = 100 − 2P, S = −20 + 4P. Government imposes a $6 per-unit tax.

(a) Find the pre-tax equilibrium P and Q. (b) Find the post-tax consumer price, producer price, and quantity. (c) Calculate the tax revenue and deadweight loss.

Show Answer

(a) Set D = S: 100 − 2P = −20 + 4P → 120 = 6P → P = $20, Q = 60

(b) With tax $6: effective supply shifts up by $6 → new equilibrium where 100 − 2P_c = −20 + 4(P_c − 6). 100 − 2P_c = −20 + 4P_c − 24 → 100 − 2P_c = −44 + 4P_c → 144 = 6P_c → P_c = \(24** (consumer price) Producer price: P_p = P_c − tax = 24 − 6 = **\)18 New quantity: Q_tax = 100 − 2(24) = 52 units

(c) Tax revenue = \(6 × 52 = **\)312 DWL = ½ × \(6 × (60 − 52) = ½ × 6 × 8 = **\)24

Question 13

Monopoly profit and deadweight loss

A monopolist faces P = 80 − 0.5Q and has TC = 20Q + 200 (so MC = $20, FC = $200).

(a) Find profit-maximising Q and P. (b) Calculate profit. (c) What would the competitive price and quantity be? Calculate deadweight loss.

Show Answer

(a) MR = 80 − Q. Set MR = MC: 80 − Q = 20 → Q* = 60, P* = 80 − 0.5(60) = $50

(b) TR = 50 × 60 = $3,000. TC = 20(60) + 200 = $1,400. Profit = $1,600

(c) Competitive: P = MC → 80 − 0.5Q = 20 → Q_c = 120, P_c = \(20** DWL = ½ × (P_m − MC) × (Q_c − Q_m) = ½ × (50−20) × (120−60) = ½ × 30 × 60 = **\)900

docs.title: Section C — Short Answer (2 questions)
docs.text:
Question 14

Market failure: externalities (4 marks)

The market for flights produces carbon emissions. Explain why the private market overproduces flights relative to the socially efficient level. What policy could correct this, and what is the efficient tax rate?

Show Answer

Market failure: Airline passengers and airlines pay only the private costs of flying (fuel, labour, capital). Carbon emissions impose costs on third parties (climate change) that are not reflected in ticket prices. Since social cost (MSC) > private cost (MPC), the market produces more flights than is efficient — output is at Q_market where P = MPC, but efficiency requires P = MSC.

Policy: A Pigouvian tax equal to the marginal external cost (MEC) at the efficient quantity. This shifts supply up by the tax amount, reducing quantity from Q_market to Q* where MSC = MSB.

Efficient tax rate: Tax = MEC at Q* (the socially optimal output level, not the market output). In practice, this is estimated as the social cost of carbon (e.g., Australia's Safeguard Mechanism uses ~$75/tonne CO₂e in 2025).

Question 15

Perfect competition vs monopoly (4 marks)

A market is currently a monopoly with P = $60 and Q = 40. Under perfect competition the price would be $20 and Q = 120. Explain why the monopoly outcome is allocatively inefficient, and calculate the deadweight loss.

Show Answer

Allocative inefficiency: The monopolist produces where MR = MC, setting P = $60 > MC = $20. Units 41 through 120 have a value to consumers (demand price) that exceeds the cost of production (MC = $20), but they are not produced. These "missing" units represent a social loss — mutually beneficial transactions that don't occur because the monopolist restricts output to raise price.

DWL = ½ × (P_m − MC) × (Q_c − Q_m) = ½ × (60 − 20) × (120 − 40) = ½ × 40 × 80 = $1,600

This is a pure social loss — not transferred to anyone. It represents the consumer surplus and producer surplus destroyed by the monopoly restriction.

Good luck! Check your answers against the Formula Sheet and Quiz Bank.

docs.title: M01.L01 — What is Economics? Scarcity, Choice, and Opportunity Cost
docs.text: Module: Module 01 — Introduction: Scarcity, Choice, and the Economic Method Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain how scarcity forces economic choices.

docs.title: The Fundamental Economic Problem
docs.text:
Economics begins with the recognition that resources are limited while human wants are unlimited. In Australia, this plays out in everyday decisions: should we mine more iron ore in Western Australia or preserve the land? Should the RBA raise interest rates to control inflation or prioritize employment growth? These trade-offs exist because we can't have everything we want simultaneously.

The economic problem has three components: 1. What to produce (e.g., more housing vs. commercial buildings) 2. How to produce it (e.g., automated mining vs. labor-intensive methods) 3. For whom to produce (e.g., luxury goods for high-income earners vs. essentials for all)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Choosing between university and work

  1. Sarah finishes high school in Sydney and faces a choice: attend university or work full-time at Woolworths for $50,000/year.
  2. If she chooses university, her opportunity cost is the $150,000 she could earn over 3 years plus any work experience.
  3. If she chooses work, her opportunity cost is the higher lifetime earnings and career options from a degree (estimated $1 million extra over 40 years).
  4. Rational choice suggests weighing these marginal benefits against costs.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Opportunity cost is just the monetary value of the next best alternative"

Opportunity cost includes all sacrificed alternatives - time, experiences, and non-monetary factors. Choosing to watch Netflix instead of studying costs both potential grades and the satisfaction of learning.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Scarcity exists because resources are limited relative to unlimited wants
  • Every choice has an opportunity cost - what you give up when making a decision
  • Economics studies how societies allocate scarce resources
  • Positive economics describes what is, while normative economics argues what should be

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Identify the opportunity cost of the Australian government building a new hospital instead of a school.
  2. Explain how scarcity affects your daily life with three examples.
  3. Why is "there's no such thing as a free lunch" an economic truth?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Introduction to Economics: Scarcity and Opportunity Cost — Khan Academy's foundational explanation
  • 📺 The Economic Problem — University of Utah lecture
  • 📚 RBA Educational Resources — Real-world Australian economic examples

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M01.L02 — The Production Possibilities Frontier: Trade-offs and Economic Growth
docs.text: Module: Module 01 — Introduction: Scarcity, Choice, and the Economic Method Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 1: The Production Possibilities Frontier. Points on the curve are productively efficient; the bowed shape reflects increasing opportunity costs.

Illustrate economic trade-offs using the PPF model.

docs.title: Understanding the PPF
docs.text:
The Production Possibilities Frontier (PPF) shows all possible combinations of two goods an economy can produce given its resources and technology. Australia's economy often faces choices between: - Mining exports (iron ore, coal) vs. manufacturing - Urban development vs. agricultural land - Renewable energy investment vs. fossil fuel production

Points on the curve represent efficient production, inside are inefficient, and outside are unattainable with current resources. The bowed shape reflects increasing opportunity costs - producing more of one good requires ever-larger sacrifices of the other.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australia's Iron Ore vs. Wine Production

  1. Suppose Australia can produce either 100M tons of iron ore or 10M bottles of wine annually at full capacity.
  2. Moving from 80M tons ore to 90M tons might require sacrificing 1M wine bottles.
  3. But increasing to 95M tons could cost 3M wine bottles due to reallocating less suitable resources.
  4. Economic growth (new mining tech, better vines) shifts the PPF outward over time.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Points inside the PPF are bad and should always be avoided"

While inefficient, these points may represent temporary unemployment during economic downturns or deliberate underutilization to conserve resources for future use.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The PPF demonstrates opportunity costs through its slope
  • Economic growth shifts the PPF outward through better technology or more resources
  • Specialization and trade allow consumption beyond a country's PPF
  • The bowed shape reflects increasing opportunity costs in real economies

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Draw a PPF for Australia choosing between education and tourism services. Why might it be bowed?
  2. How would discovering new iron ore deposits affect Australia's PPF for ore vs. agriculture?
  3. Explain why two countries trading can both consume beyond their individual PPFs.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Production Possibilities Frontier — Khan Academy's interactive explanation
  • 📺 PPF and Opportunity Cost — MIT lecture with mathematical treatment
  • 📚 RBA Chart Pack: Mining Investment — Real Australian production trade-offs

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M01.L03 — Comparative Advantage and Gains from Trade
docs.text: Module: Module 01 — Introduction: Scarcity, Choice, and the Economic Method Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Apply comparative advantage to explain international trade patterns.

docs.title: Specialization and Trade
docs.text:
Countries gain by specializing in what they produce relatively more efficiently, even if another country is absolutely better at everything. Australia's economy demonstrates this through: - Iron ore exports (30% of global supply) - Agricultural exports (beef, wool, wine) - Education services for international students

Absolute advantage means producing more with the same resources. Comparative advantage means lower opportunity cost. Trade allows both parties to consume beyond their PPFs.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australia and China Trade

  1. Australia can produce either 100 tons of iron ore or 50 cars per year
  2. China can produce either 80 tons of iron ore or 100 cars per year
  3. Australia's opportunity cost: 1 car = 2 tons ore; China's: 1 car = 0.8 tons ore
  4. Australia has comparative advantage in ore (lower opportunity cost)
  5. China has comparative advantage in cars
  6. Both benefit when Australia exports ore and imports cars

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"A country should only export goods it's the absolute best at producing"

Trade benefits come from relative (comparative) advantage, not absolute superiority. Australia exports education services despite not having the world's absolute best universities.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Comparative advantage depends on opportunity costs, not absolute productivity
  • Specialization according to comparative advantage increases total output
  • Trade allows consumption possibilities beyond a country's PPF
  • Terms of trade determine how gains are distributed between trading partners

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why does Australia export iron ore to China instead of making its own steel?
  2. Calculate comparative advantage given: Australia can produce 50 wool bales or 25 wine casks; NZ can produce 40 wool bales or 40 wine casks.
  3. How might automation affect Australia's comparative advantages?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Comparative Advantage and Trade — Khan Academy's classic explanation
  • 📺 Australia's Trade Relationships — DFAT analysis
  • 📚 Austrade Export Markets — Real-world Australian trade data

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M01.L04 — Economic Reasoning: Positive vs Normative, Models, and Fallacies
docs.text: Module: Module 01 — Introduction: Scarcity, Choice, and the Economic Method Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Distinguish positive from normative economic statements.

docs.title: Economic Thinking
docs.text:
Positive economics describes what is - facts and testable relationships. Example: - "Australia's inflation rate was 5.2% in 2023" - "Higher interest rates reduce housing demand"

Normative economics prescribes what should be - value judgments. Example: - "The RBA should prioritize employment over inflation control" - "University should be free for all Australians"

Economic models simplify reality using assumptions like ceteris paribus (all else equal). The ACCC uses such models to analyze market competition.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Housing Market Analysis

  1. Positive: "Sydney house prices fell 12% when interest rates rose 2%" (observable data)
  2. Normative: "The government should intervene to stabilize prices" (policy opinion)
  3. Model: Supply-demand curves assuming ceteris paribus (ignoring migration, wages, etc.)
  4. Fallacy: "My suburb's prices rose, therefore nationwide prices are rising" (composition)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Economic models are useless because they're too simple"

Good models strip away complexity to reveal core relationships. The Reserve Bank's inflation models omit countless variables but still guide effective policy.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Positive statements are testable; normative involve value judgments
  • Models simplify reality to highlight key relationships
  • Ceteris paribus isolates variable effects by holding other factors constant
  • Common fallacies: composition (part≠whole), post hoc (correlation≠causation)

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Classify: "Unemployment benefits reduce job-seeking" vs. "We should cut benefits"
  2. Identify the fallacy: "My startup failed, therefore all small businesses fail"
  3. Why might the ACCC assume ceteris paribus when analyzing Woolworths-Coles competition?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Positive vs Normative Economics — University of Utah distinction
  • 📺 Economic Models — MIT lecture on model-building
  • 📚 RBA Research Papers — Real Australian economic analysis

Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M01.L05 — The Market Economy: Prices, Incentives, and the Invisible Hand
docs.text: Module: Module 01 — Introduction: Scarcity, Choice, and the Economic Method Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain how prices coordinate economic activity.

docs.title: Market Coordination
docs.text:
In market economies like Australia's, prices serve three vital functions: 1. Signals: High iron ore prices tell miners to increase production 2. Incentives: Rising coffee shop profits encourage new competitors 3. Rationing: During droughts, higher water prices reduce consumption

Adam Smith's "invisible hand" describes how self-interested actions (Woolworths maximizing profits) create social benefits (food availability) through price mechanisms.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Housing Market

  1. Rising demand (population growth) increases prices
  2. Higher prices signal developers to build more homes
  3. Construction companies hire more workers (derived demand)
  4. Eventually, increased supply moderates price growth
  5. Government interventions (first-home grants) can distort these signals

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Markets always produce fair outcomes"

Markets efficiently allocate resources but may produce unequal results. Australia's ACCC exists to correct market failures like monopolies (e.g., regulating airport fees).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Prices communicate information about scarcity and value
  • Profit incentives drive innovation and efficiency
  • "Dollar votes" determine what gets produced in market systems
  • Governments may intervene to address market failures or equity concerns

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. How did Uber's entry demonstrate price signals in Australia's taxi market?
  2. Why might the RBA avoid directly setting interest rates for specific industries?
  3. Explain how drought affects both farmers and urban water prices.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 The Invisible Hand — Khan Academy's explanation
  • 📺 How Prices Work — University of Utah lecture
  • 📚 ACCC Market Studies — Real Australian market analyses

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M02.L01 — The Demand Curve: Law of Demand and Individual Demand
docs.text: Module: Module 02 — Demand and Willingness to Pay Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain the law of demand and illustrate it with a demand curve.

docs.title: Understanding the Demand Curve
docs.text:
The demand curve is a graphical representation of the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity demanded, holding all else constant. In Australia, consider the demand for avocados: as prices rise during supply shortages (e.g., due to weather disruptions), fewer consumers purchase them, illustrating a downward-sloping demand curve.

Key concepts: - Law of Demand: Higher prices lead to lower quantities demanded (and vice versa). - Demand Schedule: Table of prices and corresponding quantities demanded. - Real Income Effect: As prices rise, purchasing power decreases, reducing demand. - Substitution Effect: Consumers switch to alternatives (e.g., switching from avocados to bananas). - Diminishing Marginal Utility: Each additional unit consumed provides less satisfaction.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Avocado Demand in Sydney

  1. At $3/avocado, demand is 1,000 units/week.
  2. At $5/avocado, demand drops to 600 units/week.
  3. Plotting these points reveals a downward-sloping curve.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"A demand curve shift is the same as movement along the curve."

A shift occurs when a non-price factor (e.g., income) changes demand at all prices, while movement along the curve reflects price changes.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The demand curve slopes downward due to the law of demand.
  • Real income and substitution effects explain consumer responses to price changes.
  • Diminishing marginal utility underlies the curve's shape.
  • Shifts (vs. movements) are critical for accurate analysis.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If the price of beef rises in Melbourne, how might demand for chicken change?
  2. Draw a demand curve for Australian café coffee using hypothetical data.
  3. Explain how a drought could shift the demand curve for almonds.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Khan Academy: Law of Demand — Visualizing demand curves
  • 📚 OpenStax: Demand — Demand theory foundations

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M02.L02 — Willingness to Pay and Consumer Surplus
docs.text: Module: Module 02 — Demand and Willingness to Pay Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Calculate consumer surplus using willingness to pay and demand curves.

docs.title: Willingness to Pay (WTP)
docs.text:
WTP is the maximum price a consumer would pay for a good. In Australia, consider toll roads: drivers value time savings differently, creating a range of WTP (e.g., \(5–\)20 for a Sydney tunnel).

Consumer Surplus: The difference between WTP and the actual price paid. For a $10 toll, a driver with $15 WTP gains $5 surplus.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Sydney Harbour Bridge Toll

  1. Drivers' WTP: $8, $10, $12, $15.
  2. Toll price: $10.
  3. Surplus: $0, $0, $2, $5 (total surplus = $7).
  4. Graphically, this is the area above $10 and below the demand curve.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Consumer surplus is the same as profit."

Profit is revenue minus costs (producer-side), while surplus measures consumer benefit.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• WTP varies by individual and context.
  • Consumer surplus quantifies net consumer benefit.
  • Surplus is area under demand curve and above price.
  • Policies (e.g., subsidies) can alter surplus.

docs.title: Exam-Style Calculation Practice
docs.text:
Key technique: MB curve = demand curve = WTP for each unit

The marginal benefit (MB) curve shows the maximum price a consumer will pay for each successive unit. To find WTP for unit n, substitute q = n into the MB equation.

Example 1: MB curve is MB = 150 − 2q. - WTP for unit 1: MB = 150 − 2(1) = \(148** - WTP for unit 10: MB = 150 − 2(10) = **\)130 - At what quantity does WTP = $0? Set 150 − 2q = 0 → q = 75

Example 2: Market price = \(90, MB = 150 − 2q. - Quantity purchased: set MB = P → 150 − 2q = 90 → **q* = 30** - Consumer surplus = ½ × base × height = ½ × 30 × (150 − 90) = **\)900**

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. A consumer's MB = 120 − 3q. What is the maximum price they will pay for unit 15? At what quantity does demand reach zero?
  2. Calculate surplus if WTPs are $20, $18, $15, $12 and price is $16.
  3. If MB = 80 − q and price = $40, find (a) quantity purchased and (b) total consumer surplus.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 MIT: Consumer Surplus — Lecture notes
  • 📚 Curtis and Irvine: WTP — Applied examples

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: M02.L03 — Shifts in Demand: Determinants and Comparative Statics
docs.text: Module: Module 02 — Demand and Willingness to Pay Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Identify the five determinants of demand and analyze their impact on demand shifts.

docs.title: Demand Shifters
docs.text:
1. Income: For normal goods (e.g., Sydney housing), demand rises with income. For inferior goods (e.g., instant noodles), demand falls.
  2. Prices of Related Goods: Substitutes (e.g., Uber vs. taxis) and complements (e.g., smartphones/data plans).
  3. Tastes: Trends (e.g., plant-based meat demand growth).
  4. Expectations: Future price changes (e.g., pre-GST hike spending spikes).
  5. Number of Buyers: Population growth boosts demand (e.g., Melbourne apartments).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Melbourne Cafés During COVID

  1. Income drops → demand falls (inferior good: home coffee).
  2. Fewer tourists → fewer buyers.
  3. Expectation of lockdowns → panic-bought coffee machines.
  4. Result: Leftward demand curve shift.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"A price change shifts the demand curve."

Price changes cause movements along the curve; only non-price factors shift it.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Income, related goods, tastes, expectations, and buyer count shift demand.
  • Normal vs. inferior goods react differently to income changes.
  • Comparative statics compare equilibria before/after a shift.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. How might a Brisbane flood shift demand for bottled water?
  2. Is a Tesla a normal or inferior good? Justify.
  3. Graph a demand shift for vegan burgers if beef prices rise.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Khan Academy: Demand Shifts — Interactive examples
  • 📚 Medeiros: Demand Analysis — Detailed theory

Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy

docs.title: M02.L04 - Market Demand: Horizontal Summation of Individual Demands
docs.text: Module: Module 02 - Demand and Willingness to Pay Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Construct a market demand curve by summing individual demand curves horizontally.

docs.title: Market Demand Construction
docs.text:
Market demand aggregates individual demands at each price. Example: Australian wheat farmers sell to domestic and international buyers.

Horizontal Summation Steps: 1. List quantities demanded by each buyer at set prices. 2. Sum quantities at each price. 3. Plot the summed quantities.

Kink at Choke Price: The price where some buyers drop out (e.g., luxury car imports).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Brisbane Organic Farmers' Market

  1. Buyer A: 2kg at $10, 1kg at $15.
  2. Buyer B: 3kg at $10, 0kg at $15.
  3. Market demand: 5kg at $10, 1kg at $15.
  4. Curve kinks at $15 (Buyer B exits).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Market demand is just the average of individual demands."

Summing is additive, not averaged, reflecting total quantity demanded.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Market demand is the horizontal sum of individual demands.
  • Kinks occur at choke prices where buyers exit.
  • Aggregation applies to commodities (e.g., iron ore exports).

docs.title: Exam-Style Calculation Practice
docs.text:
Key technique: convert to Q = f(P), sum, then invert back to P = f(Q)

When summing individual demand curves algebraically: (1) rewrite each as quantity demanded, (2) add quantities, (3) invert the result to get the market inverse demand.

Example: Two consumers of concert tickets: - Anna: p = 40 − q → q_A = 40 − p (valid for p ≤ 40) - Boris: p = 80 − 4q → q_B = 20 − p/4 (valid for p ≤ 80)

For prices between 40 and 80 only Boris buys: Q = q_B = 20 − p/4 → p = 80 − 4Q

For prices below 40 both buy: Q = q_A + q_B = (40 − p) + (20 − p/4) = 60 − 5p/4

Invert: 5p/4 = 60 − Q → p = 48 − (4/5)Q → market demand: p = 48 − 0.8Q

Note the kink at p = 40 where Anna enters the market — the curve becomes flatter below that price.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Two buyers: A has p = 30 − 2q; B has p = 50 − q. Derive the market demand curve for prices below 30. (Hint: convert each to Q, sum, invert.)
  2. Sum demands for two Sydney cyclists: A (1 bike at $500, 0 at $600) and B (2 at $500, 1 at $600).
  3. For the Anna/Boris example above, verify that at p = 20 total market demand Q = 40 − 20 + 20 − 5 = 35.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 MIT: Market Demand - Lecture slides
  • 📚 OpenStax: Aggregation - Step-by-step

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: M02.L05 — Demand in Australian Markets: Case Studies
docs.text: Module: Module 02 — Demand and Willingness to Pay Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Apply demand analysis to Australian market case studies.

docs.title: Case 1: Sydney Housing Demand
docs.text: Drivers: Population growth, low interest rates, foreign investment. Demand is price inelastic (limited substitutes). COVID-19 shifted preferences to suburbs.

docs.title: Case 2: Electricity Demand
docs.text: Inelasticity: Essential service; price hikes (e.g., 2022 energy crisis) reduce consumption minimally. Time-of-use pricing attempts to shift peak demand.

docs.title: Case 3: Food Delivery Platforms
docs.text: Demand Shifters: Pandemic lockdowns (↑), reopening (↓). Uber Eats competes with Deliveroo; cross-price elasticity matters.

docs.title: Case 4: Iron Ore Exports
docs.text: China Demand: Price volatility tied to Chinese construction. Australian miners face export demand kinks at price thresholds.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Inelastic demand means demand doesn’t change."

Inelastic means quantity changes less than price (% terms), not zero change.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Sydney housing: Inelastic, shifted by demographics.
  • Electricity: Essential but modifiable via pricing.
  • Food delivery: Elastic to substitutes and income.
  • Iron ore: Export demand drives price sensitivity.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why is Sydney housing demand inelastic?
  2. How might solar adoption affect electricity demand elasticity?
  3. Compare COVID impacts on food delivery vs. iron ore demand.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 ABC: Housing Market — News analysis
  • 📚 Medeiros: Elasticity — In-depth theory

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: M03.L01 — The Production Function: Total, Average, and Marginal Product
docs.text: Module: Module 03 — Production and Costs Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Understand how total, average, and marginal product relate to production inputs in the short run.

docs.title: The Production Function
docs.text:
In Australian manufacturing, a production function shows the relationship between inputs (like labour and capital) and outputs. In the short run, at least one input (usually capital) is fixed. Total Product (TP) is the total output produced. Average Product (AP) is TP divided by the quantity of the variable input (usually labour, L). Marginal Product (MP) is the change in TP from adding one more unit of L.

Graphically, TP initially increases at an increasing rate (MP rising), then at a decreasing rate (MP falling but positive), and eventually decreases (MP negative). AP rises when MP > AP, falls when MP < AP, and is at its maximum when MP = AP.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Wheat Farm Production

A wheat farm in Western Australia employs workers to harvest crops: 1. With 0 workers: TP = 0 2. 1 worker: TP = 10 tonnes (MP=10, AP=10) 3. 2 workers: TP = 25 tonnes (MP=15, AP=12.5) 4. 3 workers: TP = 45 tonnes (MP=20, AP=15) 5. 4 workers: TP = 60 tonnes (MP=15, AP=15) 6. 5 workers: TP = 70 tonnes (MP=10, AP=14) 7. 6 workers: TP = 75 tonnes (MP=5, AP=12.5) 8. 7 workers: TP = 70 tonnes (MP=-5, AP=10)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Marginal product can never be negative."

Actually, MP can become negative when adding more workers leads to overcrowding or inefficiency, reducing total output - as seen when the 7th worker decreases TP from 75 to 70 tonnes.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• TP shows total output from given inputs
  • AP = TP/L shows output per worker
  • MP = ΔTP/ΔL shows output from the last worker added
  • MP crosses AP at AP's maximum point
  • TP increases until MP becomes negative

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If TP is 100 units with 5 workers and 120 units with 6 workers, what is MP of the 6th worker?
  2. Why does AP rise when MP > AP?
  3. Draw TP, AP, and MP curves showing their relationships.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Production Function Explained — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Production Theory — OpenStax

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M03.L02 — The Law of Diminishing Marginal Returns
docs.text: Module: Module 03 — Production and Costs Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain why marginal product eventually declines in the short run.

docs.title: Diminishing Marginal Returns
docs.text:
In Australian mining operations, the law states that adding more of a variable input (like labour) to fixed inputs (like equipment) will eventually yield smaller increases in output. This occurs because: 1. Initially, workers can specialize (MP increases) 2. Eventually, workers share fixed equipment (MP decreases but positive) 3. Finally, overcrowding occurs (MP becomes negative)

Production has three stages: - Stage I: MP > AP (AP increasing) - Stage II: 0 < MP < AP (AP decreasing) - Stage III: MP < 0 (TP decreasing)

Rational firms operate in Stage II where MP is positive but diminishing.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Iron Ore Mine

A Pilbara mine with fixed equipment: 1. 0-3 workers: MP increases (specialization) 2. 4-6 workers: MP positive but decreasing (equipment sharing) 3. 7+ workers: MP negative (overcrowding at crushers)

At 5 workers, TP=100 tonnes, MP=10. Adding a 6th worker gives TP=107, MP=7 (diminishing returns).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Diminishing returns mean the business is failing."

Actually, it's a natural consequence of fixed inputs. Even successful Australian mines experience diminishing returns when adding too many workers to limited equipment.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Applies in short run with fixed inputs
  • MP eventually falls due to fixed factors
  • Rational production occurs in Stage II
  • Different from diseconomies of scale (long-run concept)

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why does MP initially increase before diminishing?
  2. At what stage should a farm operate? Why?
  3. How might an Australian vineyard experience diminishing returns?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Diminishing Returns — MIT OCW
  • 📚 Production Analysis — Curtis & Irvine

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M03.L03 — Short-Run Costs: Fixed, Variable, Average, and Marginal
docs.text: Module: Module 03 — Production and Costs Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 4: Short-Run Cost Curves. MC intersects both AVC and ATC at their minimum points. The gap between ATC and AVC equals AFC.

Calculate and interpret different cost measures in the short run.

docs.title: Short-Run Cost Concepts
docs.text:
Australian cafes face: - Fixed Costs (TFC): Rent, equipment leases (constant at $1000/week) - Variable Costs (TVC): Wages, ingredients (increase with output) - Total Cost (TC) = TFC + TVC

Average Costs: - AFC = TFC/Q (always decreasing) - AVC = TVC/Q (U-shaped) - ATC = TC/Q (U-shaped)

Marginal Cost (MC) = ΔTC/ΔQ: - Initially falls then rises - Intersects AVC and ATC at their minimum points

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Sydney Café Costs

At 100 coffees/day: - TFC = $1000 - TVC = $500 - TC = $1500 - AFC = $10 - AVC = $5 - ATC = $15 - MC (from 99 to 100) = $4.90

At 200 coffees: - TFC = $1000 - TVC = $900 - TC = $1900 - AFC = $5 - AVC = $4.50 - ATC = $9.50 - MC = $4

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Fixed costs don't affect production decisions."

Actually, while fixed costs don't change with output, they determine whether firms can cover total costs in the long run. Many Australian startups fail by underestimating fixed costs.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• TFC are constant; TVC increase with output
  • ATC is U-shaped due to AFC and AVC
  • MC intersects AVC and ATC at their minimums
  • Cost curves reflect production function

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If TC=2000 at Q=100 and TC=2400 at Q=120, what is MC?
  2. Why is ATC U-shaped?
  3. Draw AFC, AVC, ATC, and MC curves showing relationships.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Short-Run Costs — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Cost Analysis — Medeiros

Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M03.L04 — Long-Run Costs: Economies and Diseconomies of Scale
docs.text: Module: Module 03 — Production and Costs Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Identify why long-run average costs change with scale.

docs.title: Long-Run Cost Concepts
docs.text:
Australian firms experience: - Economies of Scale: LRATC falls as output increases - Reasons: Specialization (BHP's mining engineers), efficient capital (Woolworths distribution), technology (CSL's vaccine production) - Constant Returns: LRATC constant - Diseconomies: LRATC rises (CBA's bureaucratic delays)

Minimum Efficient Scale (MES) is smallest output where LRATC is minimized. The LRATC curve is the "envelope" of all possible SRATC curves.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Airline Expansion

  1. Small (Rex Airlines):
  2. High LRATC ($0.50/seat-km)
  3. Can't negotiate bulk fuel discounts
  4. Medium (Virgin):
  5. LRATC = $0.35 (economies from route optimization)
  6. Large (Qantas):
  7. LRATC = $0.30 (global maintenance hubs)
  8. Oversized:
  9. LRATC = $0.33 (diseconomies from complex logistics)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Bigger companies always have lower costs."

Actually, beyond optimal scale (like some Australian banks), coordination problems and bureaucracy can increase average costs.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Economies: LRATC falls with scale
  • Diseconomies: LRATC rises beyond optimal scale
  • MES is minimum efficient scale
  • LRATC envelopes SRATC curves

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Give three reasons for economies of scale.
  2. Why might an Australian university face diseconomies?
  3. How does LRATC differ from SRATC?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Long-Run Costs — MIT OCW
  • 📚 Scale Economies — OpenStax

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M03.L05 — Cost Curves in Practice: Australian Firm Examples
docs.text: Module: Module 03 — Production and Costs Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Apply cost curve analysis to real Australian businesses.

docs.title: Australian Industry Applications
docs.text:
Mining (BHP): - High fixed costs (equipment) - Low marginal costs once producing - Economies of scale in rail/port infrastructure

Retail (Coles): - U-shaped ATC from high initial inventory costs - MC low for additional units sold - Diseconomies in over-expanded stores

Agriculture (GrainCorp): - Seasonal fixed costs (silos) - MP determines optimal harvest labour - Transport costs affect MC curves

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Allocative Efficiency: Electricity Market

When wholesale price = \(80/MWh: 1. Snowy Hydro runs hydro plants (MC=\)20) 2. Gas plants run (MC=\(60) 3. Coal plants idle (MC=\)90)

Efficient allocation occurs when P=MC for last unit supplied.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Firms always produce at minimum ATC."

Actually, competitive pressures (like Australian supermarkets) may force production where P=MC, which could be above min ATC.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• MC=PL/MPL guides hiring decisions
  • P=MC ensures allocative efficiency
  • P=min ATC is productive efficiency
  • Industry structures shape cost curves

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why might a mine have high fixed but low marginal costs?
  2. How does Coles' ATC curve differ from BHP's?
  3. When does P=MC benefit society?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Real-World Cost Curves — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Applied Microeconomics — Curtis & Irvine

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M04.L01 — The Supply Curve: Law of Supply and Firm Supply
docs.text: Module: Module 04 — Supply and Market Equilibrium Lesson: L01 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain how the law of supply determines the upward-sloping supply curve

docs.title: Understanding Supply
docs.text:
In microeconomics, supply refers to the quantity of a good or service that producers are willing and able to sell at various prices during a given time period. The law of supply states that, all else being equal, as the price of a good rises, the quantity supplied will increase. This relationship is typically represented by an upward-sloping supply curve.

In Australia, we can observe this with local wine producers. When export prices for Australian Shiraz increase, vineyards allocate more resources to Shiraz production, increasing quantity supplied.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Iron Ore Supply

  1. Current price: AUD 120/tonne → 500,000 tonnes supplied monthly
  2. Price rises to AUD 150/tonne → 650,000 tonnes supplied monthly
  3. Price falls to AUD 90/tonne → 400,000 tonnes supplied monthly

This demonstrates movement along the supply curve in response to price changes.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"The supply curve shows how much producers want to sell"

The supply curve shows how much producers are willing and able to sell at various prices, given their production costs and capacity. Desire alone doesn't determine supply.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The law of supply shows a direct relationship between price and quantity supplied
  • Market supply aggregates all individual firms' supply curves
  • Movement along the curve reflects price changes, not shifts in supply
  • Profit motive drives firms to increase supply at higher prices

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If the price of wool increases by 15%, what would we expect to happen to quantity supplied?
  2. How does a firm's supply curve differ from the market supply curve?
  3. Why does the supply curve slope upward?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 The Supply Curve Explained — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Australian Wine Industry Supply Analysis

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M04.L02 — Shifts in Supply: Determinants and Comparative Statics
docs.text: Module: Module 04 — Supply and Market Equilibrium Lesson: L02 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Identify the five key determinants that shift the supply curve

docs.title: Supply Shifters
docs.text:
While price changes cause movement along the supply curve, these five factors cause the entire curve to shift:

  1. Input prices: Rising costs of production decrease supply. Australian beef supply decreased when drought raised feed costs.
  2. Technology: Improvements increase supply. Automated mining equipment boosted Australian mineral exports.
  3. Expectations: Future price expectations affect current supply. Anticipating higher wool prices, farmers may withhold current supply.
  4. Number of sellers: More producers increase market supply. Entry of craft breweries expanded Australia's beer supply.
  5. Prices of related goods: If farmers can switch crops, higher prices for alternative crops may reduce supply of the original.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Coal Supply Shift

  1. Baseline: Supply curve S1 at 100 million tonnes/year
  2. New carbon tax increases production costs → supply decreases to S2 (80 million tonnes)
  3. Simultaneously, new mining technology is adopted → supply increases to S3 (90 million tonnes)
  4. Net effect: Supply shifts left from S1 to S3 (10% decrease)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Bad weather always reduces supply"

While droughts reduce agricultural supply, ideal weather can increase it. The direction depends on the specific conditions.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Supply shifts require changing the entire supply schedule
  • Input costs and technology are most impactful in the long run
  • Multiple shifts can occur simultaneously
  • Australian examples demonstrate real-world applications

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. How would a decrease in steel prices affect Australian car production?
  2. Why might avocado supply increase despite lower prices?
  3. Explain how mining technology affects Australia's comparative advantage

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Factors Affecting Supply — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Australian Mining Technology Report

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: M04.L03 — Market Equilibrium: Supply, Demand, and Price
docs.text: Module: Module 04 — Supply and Market Equilibrium Lesson: L03 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 2: Supply and Demand. The equilibrium price P and quantity Q occur at the intersection of the supply and demand curves.

Determine equilibrium price and quantity using supply and demand curves

docs.title: Finding Equilibrium
docs.text:
Market equilibrium occurs where quantity supplied equals quantity demanded (Qd = Qs). At this intersection point: - There is no tendency for price to change - The market "clears" with no surplus or shortage - Both buyers and sellers are satisfied

In Australia's housing market, equilibrium prices emerge from the interaction of: - Supply: Available properties, construction costs - Demand: Population growth, interest rates, investor activity

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Sydney Apartment Market

  1. Demand function: Qd = 500,000 - 2,000P
  2. Supply function: Qs = 100,000 + 3,000P
  3. Set Qd = Qs → 500,000 - 2,000P = 100,000 + 3,000P
  4. Solve: 400,000 = 5,000P → P* = $80,000 per apartment
  5. Q* = 100,000 + 3,000(80) = 340,000 apartments

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Equilibrium means equal numbers of buyers and sellers"

Equilibrium means quantities supplied and demanded are equal at a given price, not that the number of participants is equal.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Equilibrium is the price-quantity combination where markets clear
  • Graphical analysis shows the intersection point
  • Algebraic solutions provide precise values
  • Australian housing demonstrates real-world equilibrium dynamics

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If demand increases while supply stays constant, what happens to equilibrium?
  2. How would higher construction costs affect Sydney's apartment equilibrium?
  3. Why might equilibrium take time to establish in real markets?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Market Equilibrium — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Australian Housing Market Analysis

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M04.L04 — Comparative Statics: Analysing Shifts in Supply and Demand
docs.text: Module: Module 04 — Supply and Market Equilibrium Lesson: L04 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Predict price and quantity changes from shifts in supply or demand

docs.title: Four Fundamental Shifts
docs.text:
1. Demand increases: Higher price, higher quantity (COVID toilet paper demand)
  2. Demand decreases: Lower price, lower quantity (Declining newspaper demand)
  3. Supply increases: Lower price, higher quantity (Australian iron ore expansion)
  4. Supply decreases: Higher price, lower quantity (Drought-reduced crop yields)

During COVID-19, Australian food markets experienced simultaneous: - Demand increase (panic buying) - Supply decrease (logistics disruptions) Result: Sharp price spikes with ambiguous quantity effects

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Iron Ore Boom 2021

  1. Baseline: P1 = $100/tonne, Q1 = 1 million tonnes
  2. Chinese demand increases → demand curve shifts right
  3. Australian supply decreases (weather disruptions) → supply shifts left
  4. Result: P2 = $180/tonne (price definitely rises) Q2 ambiguous (depends on relative shift magnitudes)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"When both curves shift, price and quantity always move in predictable directions"

With simultaneous shifts, one variable's change may be ambiguous depending on relative shift magnitudes.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Single shifts have clear price-quantity effects
  • Double shifts require analyzing relative magnitudes
  • Australian examples demonstrate complex real-world scenarios
  • Iron ore and agriculture show export market dynamics

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. How did COVID border closures affect Australia's fruit markets?
  2. What happens to price when demand increases and supply increases more?
  3. Why might wheat prices fall despite a poor harvest?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Changes in Market Equilibrium — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Australia's Iron Ore Trade

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M04.L05 — Markets in Disequilibrium: Shortages and Surpluses
docs.text: Module: Module 04 — Supply and Market Equilibrium Lesson: L05 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Distinguish between shortages and surpluses in market disequilibrium

docs.title: Price Controls and Disequilibrium
docs.text:
When prices are prevented from reaching equilibrium:

Shortage (excess demand): - Occurs when price is below equilibrium - Quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied - Example: Sydney housing with rent controls

Surplus (excess supply): - Occurs when price is above equilibrium - Quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded - Example: Australian milk before dairy deregulation

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Rental Market

  1. Equilibrium rent: $500/week for 100,000 properties
  2. Government caps rent at $400 → landlords supply 80,000
  3. At $400, demand is 120,000 → shortage of 40,000 properties
  4. Results: Waiting lists, black markets, reduced maintenance

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Shortages mean there isn't enough of a product"

Shortages occur when quantity demanded exceeds supplied at a given price - the product exists but isn't accessible at that price.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Shortages occur below equilibrium price
  • Surpluses occur above equilibrium price
  • Both create market inefficiencies
  • Australian examples show policy impacts

docs.title: Exam-Style Calculation Practice
docs.text:
Key technique: substitute the given price into both equations, then compare Q_D and Q_S

A positive result (Q_S > Q_D) = excess supply. A negative result (Q_D > Q_S) = excess demand.

Example: D = 80 − 4P and S = −10 + 2P. Current price = 17. - Q_D = 80 − 4(17) = 80 − 68 = 12 - Q_S = −10 + 2(17) = −10 + 34 = 24 - Excess supply = 24 − 12 = +12 units (surplus — write positive)

Equilibrium check: set D = S → 80 − 4P = −10 + 2P → 90 = 6P → P = $15, Q = 20 At P = 17 > P* = 15, so a surplus exists — consistent with our answer.

Example 2: D = 120 − 3P, S = 20 + P. Price = 10. - Q_D = 120 − 30 = 90; Q_S = 20 + 10 = 30 - Excess demand = 30 − 90 = −60 units (shortage — write negative)

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. D = 100 − 5P, S = −20 + 3P. At P = 12, calculate the excess supply or demand (use + for surplus, − for shortage). Then find the equilibrium price.
  2. How do queues form in shortage situations?
  3. D = 60 − 2P, S = 10 + P. The government sets a price floor at P = 25. Calculate the size of the resulting surplus.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Price Controls and Deadweight Loss — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Australian Housing Affordability

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M04.L06 — Market Adjustment: The Role of Prices as Signals
docs.text: Module: Module 04 — Supply and Market Equilibrium Lesson: L06 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain how prices coordinate economic activity through signals

docs.title: Price Mechanism
docs.text:
Prices serve three key functions:

  1. Signaling: High prices indicate scarcity, low prices indicate abundance
  2. Incentivizing: Profit opportunities attract resources, losses repel them
  3. Rationing: Allocate goods to those willing/able to pay

During Australia's 2020 bushfires: - Timber prices rose → signaled shortage - Attracted imports and alternative materials - Rationed remaining stock to highest-value uses

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Lithium Market

  1. 2015: Price = $5,000/tonne → moderate production
  2. 2018: EV demand spikes → price rises to $12,000
  3. Signal attracts:
  4. New mines (Greenbushes expansion)
  5. Recycling technologies
  6. Exploration investment
  7. By 2022: Increased supply → price stabilizes at $8,000

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Prices reflect only production costs"

Prices reflect both production costs and consumer valuations through the interaction of supply and demand.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Prices communicate information throughout the economy
  • Profit/loss signals guide resource allocation
  • Market economies self-correct through price adjustments
  • Australian resource markets demonstrate dynamic adjustment

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. How did COVID vaccine prices affect global production?
  2. Why might farmers continue producing at low prices?
  3. Compare price signals in command vs market economies

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Price Signals — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Australia's Lithium Industry

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M05.L01 — Consumer Surplus: Concept and Calculation
docs.text: Module: Module 05 — Economic Surplus and Elasticity Lesson: L01 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 3: Consumer and Producer Surplus. The blue area is consumer surplus; the red area is producer surplus. Together they equal total economic surplus.

Understand how to calculate and interpret consumer surplus using Australian market examples.

docs.title: Consumer Surplus Basics
docs.text:
Consumer surplus (CS) measures the benefit consumers receive when they pay less for a good or service than their maximum willingness to pay (WTP). It is calculated as the difference between WTP and the actual price paid, summed across all units purchased. Graphically, CS is the area below the demand curve but above the price line.

In Australia, CS can be observed in markets like petrol, where price fluctuations (e.g., due to excise taxes) directly impact consumer welfare. For example, if a consumer is willing to pay $1.80 per litre for petrol but the market price is $1.50, their CS is $0.30 per litre.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Calculating CS for Concert Tickets in Sydney

  1. Demand Curve: Suppose the demand for concert tickets is linear, with a maximum WTP of \(200 (intercept) and a slope of -\)10 per ticket. The equation is: \( P = 200 - 10Q \).
  2. Market Price: Tickets sell at $120.
  3. Equilibrium Quantity: \( 120 = 200 - 10Q \) → \( Q = 8 \).
  4. CS Calculation: Area of the triangle = \( \frac{1}{2} \times base \times height \) = \( \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times (200-120) \) = $320.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Consumer surplus is the same as profit."

CS measures consumer benefit, not producer revenue or profit. Profit is revenue minus costs, while CS reflects the gap between willingness to pay and actual price.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• CS = WTP minus price, represented as the area under the demand curve above the price.
  • CS increases when prices fall (e.g., petrol excise cuts) and decreases when prices rise.
  • Linear demand curves yield triangular CS areas.
  • Australian examples include petrol, electricity, and event tickets.

docs.title: Application: The Rule of Half (Infrastructure Appraisal)
docs.text:
When a project lowers the cost of a service (e.g., a new motorway reducing travel time), consumer surplus rises. Two groups benefit:

  • Existing users — gain on all their current trips: CS gain = ΔCost × Q₀
  • New users — induced by the lower cost; their average gain is half the cost reduction: CS gain = ½ × ΔCost × ΔQ

Combined: Total CS benefit = ΔCost × Q₀ + ½ × ΔCost × ΔQ = ΔCost × (Q₀ + ½ΔQ)

This is the Rule of Half, standard in Australian government cost-benefit analysis (e.g., Infrastructure Australia, Transport for NSW).

Example — Western Sydney Airport Rail Link: A new rail link reduces travel time by 12 minutes (valued at $20/hr = $4 per trip). Existing users: 100,000 trips/year. New trips induced: 10,000/year. - Existing user benefit: \(4 × 100,000 = **\)400,000/year - New user benefit: ½ × \(4 × 10,000 = **\)20,000/year - Total annual CS benefit: $420,000/year

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If the WTP for a coffee is $5.50 but the price is $4.00, what is the CS per coffee?
  2. Draw a demand curve with WTP = \(100 at Q=0 and P=0 at Q=10. Calculate CS at P=\)40.
  3. How does a price ceiling below equilibrium affect CS?
  4. A road upgrade reduces commute costs by $3/trip. Current users: 50,000 trips/day; new induced trips: 5,000/day. Using the Rule of Half, calculate the daily consumer surplus benefit.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Consumer Surplus Explained — Khan Academy video
  • 📚 OpenStax Consumer Surplus

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M05.L02 — Producer Surplus and Total Economic Surplus
docs.text: Module: Module 05 — Economic Surplus and Elasticity Lesson: L02 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Calculate producer surplus and total economic surplus, and explain their role in market efficiency.

docs.title: Producer Surplus (PS) and Total Surplus (TS)
docs.text:
Producer surplus is the difference between the price received by sellers and their minimum acceptable price (cost). Graphically, it is the area above the supply curve but below the price line. Total economic surplus (TS) is the sum of consumer surplus (CS) and producer surplus (PS), representing the total net benefit to society from a market transaction.

In Australia, PS can be observed in agricultural markets, where farmers receive prices above their production costs during high-demand seasons. TS maximisation indicates an efficient allocation of resources.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Calculating PS and TS for Avocado Farmers

  1. Supply Curve: Suppose the supply curve for avocados is \( P = 2 + 0.5Q \), where P is in AUD/kg.
  2. Market Price: Avocados sell at $6/kg.
  3. Equilibrium Quantity: \( 6 = 2 + 0.5Q \) → \( Q = 8 \).
  4. PS Calculation: Area of the triangle = \( \frac{1}{2} \times 8 \times (6-2) \) = $16.
  5. TS Calculation: If CS is $24 (from demand side), then TS = $24 + $16 = $40.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Higher prices always increase producer surplus."

While higher prices can increase PS, they may reduce CS and overall TS if demand is elastic, leading to deadweight loss.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• PS = Price minus cost, represented as the area above the supply curve below the price.
  • TS = CS + PS; markets maximise TS when supply equals demand.
  • Deadweight loss occurs when TS is not maximised (e.g., taxes, price controls).
  • Australian examples include agricultural markets and housing.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If the cost of producing a textbook is $50 and it sells for $75, what is the PS per book?
  2. Draw a supply curve with P=10 at Q=0 and P=20 at Q=10. Calculate PS at P=15.
  3. How does a price floor above equilibrium affect PS and TS?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
