docs.title: M05.L03 — Price Elasticity of Demand: Definition and Calculation
docs.text: Module: Module 05 — Economic Surplus and Elasticity Lesson: L03 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Calculate and interpret price elasticity of demand (PED) using the midpoint method.

docs.title: Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)
docs.text:
PED measures how quantity demanded responds to price changes: \( PED = \frac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P} \). Its absolute value determines elasticity: - Elastic (>1): Quantity changes more than price (e.g., luxury goods). - Inelastic (<1): Quantity changes less than price (e.g., necessities). - Unit elastic (=1): Proportional changes.

Australian examples include fuel (inelastic) and airline tickets (elastic).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Calculating PED for Cigarettes in Australia

  1. Initial Price (P1): $30/pack, Quantity (Q1): 1,000 packs.
  2. New Price (P2): $33/pack, Quantity (Q2): 900 packs.
  3. Midpoint Method:
  4. \( \%\Delta Q = \frac{900-1000}{(900+1000)/2} \times 100 = -10.53\% \)
  5. \( \%\Delta P = \frac{33-30}{(33+30)/2} \times 100 = 9.52\% \)
  6. \( PED = \frac{-10.53}{9.52} \approx -1.11 \) (elastic).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Elasticity is the same as slope."

Slope measures absolute changes, while elasticity measures percentage changes, which vary along a linear demand curve.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• PED = %ΔQd / %ΔP (midpoint method avoids direction bias).
  • Elasticity depends on substitutes, necessity, income proportion, and time.
  • Australian tobacco taxes exploit inelastic demand.
  • Perfectly elastic/inelastic curves are theoretical extremes.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If PED = -0.8 and price rises 5%, what happens to quantity demanded?
  2. Calculate PED when price falls from $10 to $8 and Qd rises from 50 to 70.
  3. Why is PED for petrol inelastic in Australia?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Elasticity Explained — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Curtis and Irvine PED

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M05.L04 — Elasticity Along the Demand Curve and Revenue Effects
docs.text: Module: Module 05 — Economic Surplus and Elasticity Lesson: L04 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain how elasticity varies along a linear demand curve and its impact on total revenue.

docs.title: Elasticity Along the Demand Curve
docs.text:
For a linear demand curve: - Top: Elastic (PED >1), where small price changes cause large quantity changes. - Midpoint: Unit elastic (PED =1), where total revenue (TR = P × Q) is maximised. - Bottom: Inelastic (PED <1), where price changes have minimal effect on quantity.

Australian example: Electricity demand is inelastic at low prices but becomes elastic as prices rise.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Revenue Effects for Sydney Coffee Shops

  1. Demand Curve: \( P = 10 - Q \).
  2. Midpoint: At P=$5, Q=5 (unit elastic).
  3. TR Test:
  4. Price ↑ $6 → Q=4: TR = $24 (was \(25 at P=\)5).
  5. Price ↓ $4 → Q=6: TR = $24 (again, less than $25).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Lowering prices always increases revenue."

Only true in elastic regions; in inelastic regions, price cuts reduce revenue.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Elasticity varies along linear demand curves.
  • TR is maximised at unit elasticity.
  • The "total revenue test" predicts TR changes from price moves.
  • Australian firms use this for pricing (e.g., airlines, utilities).

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If PED = 1.5 and price rises 10%, what happens to TR?
  2. Draw a demand curve with P=20 at Q=0 and P=0 at Q=20. Identify elastic, unit, and inelastic regions.
  3. Why do Australian cinemas charge differently for peak vs. off-peak tickets?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Total Revenue Test — Medeiros
  • 📚 OpenStax Elasticity

Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e

docs.title: M05.L05 — Price Elasticity of Supply; Income and Cross-Price Elasticity
docs.text: Module: Module 05 — Economic Surplus and Elasticity Lesson: L05 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Calculate price elasticity of supply (PES), income elasticity (YED), and cross-price elasticity (XED) for Australian markets.

docs.title: Price Elasticity of Supply (PES)
docs.text: PES measures how quantity supplied responds to price changes: \( PES = \frac{\%\Delta Q_s}{\%\Delta P} \). - Time horizons: Short-run PES is low (fixed capacity); long-run PES is higher (adjustable inputs).

docs.title: Income Elasticity (YED)
docs.text: YED measures how demand changes with income: \( YED = \frac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta Y} \). - Normal goods: YED >0 (e.g., cars). - Inferior goods: YED <0 (e.g., instant noodles).

docs.title: Cross-Price Elasticity (XED)
docs.text:
XED measures how demand for Good A responds to price changes in Good B: \( XED = \frac{\%\Delta Q_{dA}}{\%\Delta P_B} \). - Substitutes: XED >0 (e.g., butter vs. margarine). - Complements: XED <0 (e.g., petrol vs. cars).

Australian example: Housing supply elasticity varies across cities (Sydney vs. Adelaide).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Calculating PES for Australian Wheat

  1. Initial Price (P1): $200/tonne, Quantity (Q1): 1,000 tonnes.
  2. New Price (P2): $220/tonne, Quantity (Q2): 1,100 tonnes.
  3. PES: \( \frac{(1100-1000)/1050}{(220-200)/210} \times 100 = 1.0 \) (unit elastic).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"All goods have positive income elasticity."

Inferior goods (e.g., used cars) have negative YED as demand falls with rising income.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• PES depends on time horizon and input flexibility.
  • YED classifies goods as normal or inferior.
  • XED identifies substitutes and complements.
  • Australian housing markets show low short-run PES.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If PES = 0.5 and price rises 10%, what happens to Qs?
  2. Calculate XED if the price of tea rises 5% and coffee demand rises 3%.
  3. Why is Sydney housing supply inelastic?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Elasticity Types — MIT OCW
  • 📚 Curtis and Irvine Elasticity

Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M05.L06 — Elasticity Applications: Taxes, Housing, and Australian Markets
docs.text: Module: Module 05 — Economic Surplus and Elasticity Lesson: L06 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyse tax incidence and deadweight loss using elasticity in Australian markets.

docs.title: Tax Incidence and Elasticity
docs.text:
Tax burden falls more heavily on the inelastic side of the market (buyers or sellers). Deadweight loss (DWL) occurs when taxes reduce TS below its maximum.

Australian examples: - Tobacco tax: Inelastic demand → consumers bear most burden. - Fuel excise: Inelastic demand → minimal quantity change, high revenue. - Housing: Inelastic supply in Sydney → taxes raise prices more than quantity.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Tax Incidence in the Sydney Housing Market

  1. Pre-tax: Equilibrium price = $1M, quantity = 1,000 homes.
  2. Tax: $50k levy on sellers (supply shifts left).
  3. Post-tax: Buyers pay $1.04M, sellers receive $0.99M, quantity = 950 homes.
  4. Burden: Buyers pay 80% of tax (\(40k), sellers 20% (\)10k) due to inelastic supply.
  5. DWL: Triangle area = \( \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times (1.04M - 0.99M) \) = $1.25M.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Taxes always hurt sellers more than buyers."

Tax burden depends on relative elasticities, not who physically pays the tax.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Tax incidence is split based on relative elasticities.
  • DWL is larger when elasticity is high.
  • Sydney housing taxes show high buyer burden due to inelastic supply.
  • Fuel and tobacco taxes exploit inelastic demand.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If demand is perfectly elastic, who bears the tax burden?
  2. Draw a market with elastic demand and inelastic supply. Show tax burden.
  3. Why does Australia tax tobacco heavily despite DWL?

docs.text:

  • 📚 OpenStax Tax Policy

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M06.L01 — Market Structures Overview: The Four Models
docs.text: Module: Module 06 — Market Equilibrium and Perfect Competition Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Identify the four basic market structures and their key characteristics.

docs.title: Market Structures in Economics
docs.text:
Market structures describe the competitive environment in which firms operate. Economists classify markets into four main types based on: 1. Number of firms 2. Type of product (identical vs differentiated) 3. Degree of price control 4. Barriers to entry

In Australia, we can observe all four structures: - Perfect competition: Grain markets (wheat, barley) - Monopolistic competition: Cafés in Sydney - Oligopoly: Major banks (CBA, Westpac, NAB, ANZ) - Monopoly: Local utilities (Sydney Water)

docs.title: The Four Market Models
docs.text:
1. Perfect Competition
  2. Many small firms
  3. Identical products (wheat, shares on ASX)
  4. No price control (price takers)

  5. No barriers to entry/exit

  6. Monopolistic Competition

  7. Many firms
  8. Differentiated products (restaurants, clothing)
  9. Some price control

  10. Low barriers (common in retail)

  11. Oligopoly

  12. Few large firms (Australian supermarkets: Woolworths, Coles)
  13. Similar or differentiated products
  14. Significant price control (interdependent pricing)

  15. High barriers (capital requirements)

  16. Monopoly

  17. Single firm (Australia Post for standard mail)
  18. Unique product
  19. Complete price control (price makers)
  20. Very high barriers (legal or natural)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Comparing Australian Banking and Agriculture

  1. Banking sector (oligopoly):
  2. 4 major banks control ~75% market share
  3. Similar products with branding differences

  4. Follow each other's interest rate changes

  5. Wheat farming (perfect competition):

  6. Thousands of farmers
  7. Standardized product (ASX-traded wheat)
  8. Take global market prices

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Monopolies always charge the highest possible prices"

While monopolies have price-setting power, they still face demand constraints. Australian monopolies like utilities have price caps set by regulators (ACCC) to protect consumers.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Market structures range from perfect competition to monopoly
  • The number of firms and product differentiation determine competition
  • Australian examples show real-world applications
  • Barriers to entry affect long-term profitability

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Which market structure best describes the Australian supermarket industry?
  2. Why can't wheat farmers set their own prices?
  3. What prevents new firms from entering the banking sector easily?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Market Structures Explained — Overview with Australian examples
  • 📚 ACCC Market Studies — Real-world market analysis

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: M06.L02 — Perfect Competition: Characteristics and the Price-Taking Firm
docs.text: Module: Module 06 — Market Equilibrium and Perfect Competition Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 5: Perfect Competition. The market (left) determines price; the individual firm (right) takes that price as given and produces where MC = P.

Explain the five characteristics that define perfect competition.

docs.title: The Perfectly Competitive Market
docs.text:
Perfect competition represents an ideal market structure with maximum efficiency. While rare in pure form, some Australian markets approximate these conditions:

  1. Many small firms: No single producer can influence market price (e.g., wheat farmers)
  2. Identical products: Commodities where branding doesn't matter (ASX-listed shares)
  3. Free entry/exit: No significant barriers (agricultural markets)
  4. Perfect information: All participants know prices and quality (financial markets)
  5. Price takers: Firms accept the market price (grain prices set globally)

docs.title: The Firm's Demand Curve
docs.text:
Key distinction: - Market demand: Downward sloping (all consumers) - Firm demand: Perfectly elastic (horizontal) at market price

Why? In perfect competition: - P = AR = MR - If a firm raises price → loses all customers - If lowers price → unnecessary revenue loss

Australian example: Individual dairy farmers take the wholesale milk price set by the market.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
ASX Share Trading

  1. Market characteristics:
  2. Thousands of buyers/sellers
  3. Identical shares of the same company
  4. Real-time price information

  5. No barriers to trading

  6. Individual trader perspective:

  7. Must accept current market price
  8. No quantity restrictions
  9. Can enter/exit positions instantly

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Perfect competition means all firms earn zero profit"

In the long run, economic profit tends to zero due to entry/exit. However, firms can earn short-run profits or losses depending on cost structures and market price fluctuations (e.g., seasonal crop prices).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Perfect competition requires five specific conditions
  • Firms are price takers facing horizontal demand
  • Real-world markets may approximate some features
  • Efficiency comes from inability to influence prices

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why can't a wheat farmer charge more than the market price?
  2. How does free entry/exit maintain perfect competition?
  3. What happens to a firm's demand curve if the market price rises?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Perfect Competition Explained — Khan Academy tutorial
  • 📚 ASX Education — Real-world price-taking examples

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M06.L03 — Profit Maximisation: The MR = MC Rule
docs.text: Module: Module 06 — Market Equilibrium and Perfect Competition Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Apply the MR = MC rule to determine profit-maximizing output.

docs.title: The Golden Rule of Profit Maximisation
docs.text:
All firms, regardless of market structure, maximize profit where:

Marginal Revenue (MR) = Marginal Cost (MC)

Why this works: - If MR > MC: Producing more adds to profit - If MR < MC: Producing less reduces losses - At MR = MC: No further profit gains possible

Australian context: This rule applies equally to: - Small farms (perfect competition) - Local cafes (monopolistic competition) - Major banks (oligopoly)

docs.title: Four-Step Profit Analysis
docs.text:
1. Find Q* where MR = MC
  2. Find P from demand curve at Q*
  3. Find ATC from cost curve at Q*
  4. Calculate profit: (P - ATC) × Q*

Example: Australian wheat farmer - Market price: $300/tonne (P = MR) - MC = $280 at Q* = 1000 tonnes - ATC = \(290 at Q* - Profit = (\)300 - $290) × 1000 = $10,000

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Café Economics

  1. Given:
  2. P = $5 per coffee (price taker)
  3. MC = $4 at Q* = 200 coffees/day

  4. ATC = $4.50 at Q*

  5. Profit calculation:

  6. MR = P = $5
  7. MR = MC at 200 units

  8. Profit = ($5 - $4.50) × 200 = $100/day

  9. Decision:

  10. If demand falls to $4.25, should they operate?
  11. Yes, if P > AVC (covers variable costs)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Firms should produce where MC is lowest"

Actually, firms produce where MR = MC, which may be at higher MC levels. Minimizing MC alone would lead to underproduction and lost profit opportunities.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Profit is maximized when MR = MC
  • The rule applies universally across market structures
  • Requires comparing revenue and cost at the margin
  • Per-unit profit equals P - ATC

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If a firm's MR = $10 and MC = $8, should it produce more?
  2. How does perfect competition simplify profit analysis?
  3. Why can't we use average revenue instead of marginal?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Profit Maximization Rule — MIT lecture clip
  • 📚 ABARES Reports — Australian farm profit analyses

Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e

docs.title: M06.L04 — Short-Run Supply and the Shutdown Decision
docs.text: Module: Module 06 — Market Equilibrium and Perfect Competition Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Determine when a firm should continue operating or shut down in the short run.

docs.title: The Shutdown Rule
docs.text:
In the short run, firms face this critical decision:

Operate if: P ≥ AVC (covers variable costs) Shutdown if: P < AVC (can't cover basics)

Key points: - Fixed costs are irrelevant (sunk in short run) - Loss minimization ≠ shutdown decision - Applies to all market structures

Australian example: Seasonal tourism businesses - Operate in peak season (P > AVC) - Shutdown in off-season (P < AVC)

docs.title: The Firm's Short-Run Supply Curve
docs.text:
For a price-taking firm: - Supply = MC curve above AVC - No supply below AVC (shutdown region)

Why? Because: - At P1 > ATC: Earns profit - At P2 (ATC > P > AVC): Loss but operates - At P3 < AVC: Shuts down

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Airline During COVID

  1. Pre-pandemic:
  2. P = $200/ticket
  3. AVC = $150
  4. ATC = $180

  5. Clearly operates (P > ATC)

  6. During travel bans:

  7. P drops to $100
  8. AVC = $120 (reduced flights)

  9. Decision: Shutdown (P < AVC)

  10. Recovery phase:

  11. P = $130
  12. AVC = $110
  13. Operates at loss (P > AVC)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Firms should shutdown whenever they're losing money"

Actually, firms should operate in the short run if they can cover variable costs, even if making an overall loss. This minimizes losses while waiting for better conditions.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Shutdown depends on variable costs, not fixed
  • Supply exists only where P ≥ AVC
  • Short-run ≠ long-run decisions
  • Real-world examples show dynamic applications

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why are fixed costs irrelevant to shutdown?
  2. How would a drought affect a farm's shutdown point?
  3. What happens to industry supply if many firms shutdown?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Shutdown Point Explained — Economics tutor
  • 📚 ACCC COVID Reports — Industry case studies

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M06.L05 — Long-Run Equilibrium in Perfect Competition: Entry, Exit, and Normal Profit
docs.text: Module: Module 06 — Market Equilibrium and Perfect Competition Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain how entry and exit drive long-run equilibrium in perfect competition.

docs.title: The Adjustment Process
docs.text:
Perfect competition reaches long-run equilibrium through:

  1. Economic Profit → Entry
  2. New firms enter
  3. Market supply increases

  4. Price falls until profit = 0

  5. Economic Loss → Exit

  6. Firms exit
  7. Market supply decreases
  8. Price rises until loss = 0

Australian example: Solar panel installation market - Early 2010s: High profits → surge of new installers - By 2020: Prices stabilized → normal profits

docs.title: Long-Run Conditions
docs.text:
At equilibrium: - P = MC = min ATC - Firms earn normal profit (zero economic profit) - Productive efficiency (min ATC) - Allocative efficiency (P = MC)

Key distinction: - Economic profit = 0 - Accounting profit > 0 (includes opportunity costs)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Taxi Industry Post-Uber

  1. Pre-Uber:
  2. Limited licenses → high P

  3. Economic profits

  4. Ride-sharing entry:

  5. Supply increases
  6. Prices fall

  7. Traditional taxis exit

  8. Long-run:

  9. Normal returns for remaining operators
  10. Lower prices for consumers

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Zero economic profit means firms aren't making money"

Actually, normal profit means firms earn exactly their opportunity cost - enough to keep them in the industry. Owners still get fair compensation for their work and capital.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Entry/exit eliminate economic profits/losses
  • Long-run equilibrium achieves dual efficiency
  • Normal profit sustains business activity
  • Real-world markets constantly adjust

docs.title: Extension: Monopolistic Competition — a Realistic Middle Ground
docs.text:
Perfect competition assumes identical products. Most real markets have product differentiation — restaurants, cafes, clothing brands, petrol stations. These are examples of monopolistic competition:

Feature Perfect competition Monopolistic competition Number of firms Many Many Products Identical Differentiated Entry/exit Free Free Price Taker (P = MR) Maker (downward-sloping demand) Long-run profit Zero Zero

Short run: Each firm faces a downward-sloping demand curve (like a mini-monopolist) and may earn positive profit.

Long run: Free entry attracts new firms → each firm's demand shrinks and becomes more elastic → entry continues until P = ATC (zero economic profit). Unlike perfect competition, P > MC still holds, so there is some residual inefficiency.

Welfare trade-off — business stealing vs product variety: - Business stealing effect: A new entrant takes customers from existing firms — a negative externality the entrant ignores, suggesting too many firms may enter. - Product variety effect: More firms mean more choice for consumers — a positive externality the entrant ignores, suggesting too few firms may enter. - Net result: ambiguous. Monopolistic competition may have too many or too few firms; the model is honest about this uncertainty.

Australian examples: Independent cafes in Melbourne, petrol stations on a suburban strip, gyms, hairdressers.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why don't firms exit when earning normal profit?
  2. In monopolistic competition, what happens to a firm's demand curve as new rivals enter? Why does this eliminate economic profit in the long run?
  3. What happens to industry supply if input prices rise?
  4. Explain the "business stealing vs product variety" trade-off in monopolistic competition. Why is the welfare effect ambiguous?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Long-Run Equilibrium — MIT lecture
  • 📚 Productivity Commission Reports — Australian industry studies

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M07.L01 — Monopoly: Sources of Market Power and Barriers to Entry
docs.text: Module: Module 07 — Monopoly Lesson: L01 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Define pure monopoly and identify the major barriers to entry that create monopolies.

docs.title: Understanding Monopoly Power
docs.text:
A pure monopoly exists when a single firm is the sole producer of a product with no close substitutes, giving it significant market power to set prices. Unlike competitive markets where firms are price takers, monopolists are price makers. In Australia, examples include the National Broadband Network (NBN) for fixed-line broadband infrastructure and state-owned water utilities.

Key barriers to entry that create and maintain monopolies include:

  1. Natural monopoly: When economies of scale are so large that one firm can supply the entire market at lower cost than multiple firms (e.g., electricity grids, NBN)
  2. Government regulation/licenses: Legal barriers like patents (20 years for pharmaceuticals) or operating licenses (Australia Post's reserved postal service)
  3. Control of key resources: Exclusive access to essential inputs (e.g., mining rights)
  4. Network effects: When a product's value increases with more users (e.g., social media platforms)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australia Post's Reserved Postal Service

  1. Identify the market: Standard letter delivery in Australia
  2. Recognize the legal barrier: The Competition and Consumer Act 2010 grants Australia Post exclusive rights to deliver letters under 250g
  3. Understand the economic rationale: Maintaining universal service obligations
  4. Consider alternatives: Courier services can operate for parcels but not basic letters

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Monopolies always earn huge profits"

While monopolies have pricing power, they may not always earn excessive profits. Natural monopolies like water utilities often have regulated prices, and some monopolies face high fixed costs (e.g., NBN infrastructure). Profit depends on the relationship between price, costs, and quantity sold.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• A pure monopoly has a single seller with no close substitutes
  • Barriers to entry prevent competition and sustain monopoly power
  • Australian examples include regulated utilities and government-granted monopolies
  • Not all monopolies earn excessive profits due to regulation or high costs

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. What characteristic makes NBN Co a natural monopoly?
  2. How does a patent create a temporary monopoly?
  3. Why might network effects create monopoly power in digital platforms?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Monopoly and Barriers to Entry — Khan Academy overview
  • 📚 ACCC on Natural Monopolies

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M07.L02 — The Monopolist's Demand and Marginal Revenue
docs.text: Module: Module 07 — Monopoly Lesson: L02 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain why marginal revenue is less than price for a monopolist.

docs.title: The Monopolist's Demand Curve
docs.text:
Unlike perfect competitors who face perfectly elastic demand, a monopolist faces the downward-sloping market demand curve. This means:

  • To sell more units, the monopolist must lower price on all units
  • Price (P) > Marginal Revenue (MR) for all quantities
  • The MR curve lies below the demand curve

For a linear demand curve P = a - bQ: - MR = a - 2bQ (twice the slope) - MR becomes negative when demand is inelastic (|PED| < 1)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Calculating MR from a Demand Schedule

Given Australian electricity prices: | Quantity (GWh) | Price ($/MWh) | Total Revenue | Marginal Revenue | |----------------|---------------|---------------|------------------| | 100 | 80 | 8,000 | - | | 200 | 70 | 14,000 | 60 | | 300 | 60 | 18,000 | 40 |

  1. Observe price must fall from $80 to $70 to sell 200 vs 100 units
  2. Calculate TR = P × Q at each quantity
  3. MR = ΔTR/ΔQ = (14,000-8,000)/(200-100) = $60
  4. Note MR (\(60) < P (\)70) at Q=200

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Monopolists can charge any price they want"

While monopolists have pricing power, they are constrained by market demand. Setting price too high results in fewer sales. Profit maximization occurs where MR = MC, not at the highest possible price.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Monopolists face downward-sloping demand
  • MR < P because price cuts apply to all units
  • MR curve has twice the slope of linear demand
  • MR is negative in the inelastic portion of demand
  • Monopolists never produce where demand is inelastic

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why does MR fall faster than price?
  2. At Q=300 in our example, what would MR be if price fell to $50?
  3. Why wouldn't a monopolist operate where MR is negative?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Monopoly: Marginal Revenue — Khan Academy
  • 📚 MIT Lecture on Monopoly Pricing

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M07.L03 — Profit Maximisation for a Monopolist
docs.text: Module: Module 07 — Monopoly Lesson: L03 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

![Figure 6: Monopoly. The monopolist produces where MR = MC (Q=40, P=\(60). The red triangle shows deadweight loss — units not produced despite creating positive surplus.](../../assets/diagrams/06-monopoly.png) *Figure 6: Monopoly. The monopolist produces where MR = MC (Q*=40, P*=\)60). The red triangle shows deadweight loss — units not produced despite creating positive surplus.*

Determine the profit-maximizing quantity and price for a monopolist.

docs.title: The MR = MC Rule
docs.text:
A monopolist maximizes profit by producing where Marginal Revenue (MR) equals Marginal Cost (MC):

  1. Find Q* where MR = MC
  2. Determine P from the demand curve at Q
  3. Calculate profit as (P - ATC) × Q

Key implications: - Monopolists produce less than the socially optimal quantity (where P = MC) - They charge a price above MC, creating deadweight loss - Profit depends on the ATC at Q* - even with market power, high costs can eliminate profits

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Pharmaceutical Monopoly

Given: - Demand: P = 100 - Q - MC = $20 constant - ATC = $40 at Q*

Steps: 1. MR = 100 - 2Q (twice demand slope) 2. Set MR = MC: 100 - 2Q = 20 → Q = 40 3. Find P = 100 - 40 = $60 4. Profit = (60 - 40) × 40 = $800

This shows how patent-protected drugs can maintain high prices until generics enter.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Monopolists always charge the highest possible price"

Monopolists maximize profit, not price. Charging the absolute highest price would minimize quantity sold. The optimal price balances per-unit profit against total sales volume.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Profit max occurs where MR = MC
  • Price is determined from demand at Q*
  • Graphical method: 1) Find MR=MC, 2) Up to demand for P, 3) Compare P to ATC
  • Monopolists produce in the elastic region of demand (where MR > 0)
  • Barriers to entry allow long-run economic profits

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If MC rises to $30 in our example, what happens to Q and P?
  2. Why can't a monopolist use the P = MC rule like competitive firms?
  3. How would a per-unit tax affect the monopolist's output decision?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Monopoly Profit Maximization — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Pharmaceutical Patents in Australia

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M07.L04 — Monopoly vs Perfect Competition: Deadweight Loss
docs.text: Module: Module 07 — Monopoly Lesson: L04 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Compare monopoly and perfect competition outcomes and identify deadweight loss.

docs.title: Comparing Market Structures
docs.text:
Perfect Competition (PC) vs Monopoly:

  • Output: Monopoly produces less (Qm < Qpc)
  • Price: Monopoly charges more (Pm > Ppc)
  • Efficiency: PC is allocatively efficient (P=MC); monopoly is not (P>MC)
  • Profit: PC firms earn zero economic profit long-run; monopolies can earn persistent profits

The deadweight loss triangle represents the net loss to society from monopoly power - the value of the transactions that would occur under competition but don't under monopoly.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Domestic Airline Market

  1. Competitive scenario (pre-1990s):
  2. Multiple carriers on major routes
  3. Prices close to marginal cost ($100 SYD-MEL)

  4. Frequent price wars

  5. Monopoly scenario (Qantas dominance post-Ansett collapse):

  6. Single carrier on many routes
  7. Prices rise to $180
  8. Output falls by 30%
  9. Deadweight loss = area between Qpc and Qm, above MC, below demand

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Natural monopolies should always be broken up"

While monopolies create deadweight loss, breaking up natural monopolies (like electricity grids) could raise costs through duplication. The tradeoff is between allocative efficiency (competition) and productive efficiency (single producer).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Monopolies produce less and charge more than competitive markets
  • Deadweight loss represents lost gains from trade
  • Allocative inefficiency occurs because P > MC
  • Natural monopolies present a policy dilemma
  • Australian examples include airports and utilities

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Draw the DWL triangle for our airline example
  2. Why doesn't breaking up a natural monopoly necessarily improve welfare?
  3. How might privatizing a government monopoly affect DWL?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Deadweight Loss of Monopoly — Khan Academy
  • 📚 ACCC on Airport Pricing

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M07.L05 — Price Discrimination: First, Second, and Third Degree
docs.text: Module: Module 07 — Monopoly Lesson: L05 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Classify different types of price discrimination and identify Australian examples.

docs.title: Conditions for Price Discrimination
docs.text: Requires: 1. Market power (ability to set prices) 2. Ability to segment markets (prevent arbitrage) 3. Different price elasticities of demand across groups

docs.title: Types of Price Discrimination
docs.text:
First-Degree (Perfect): - Charge each consumer their maximum willingness to pay - Captures all consumer surplus (e.g., auctions, personalized pricing)

Second-Degree: - Prices vary by quantity or version - Examples: Bulk discounts (Costco), software tiers (Basic/Pro)

Third-Degree: - Group pricing based on elasticity - Australian examples: - Student discounts (movies, software) - Seniors cards (public transport) - Regional pricing (textbooks)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
University Textbook Pricing

  1. Identify groups: Australian vs international students
  2. Assess elasticity: International students less price-sensitive (visa requirements)
  3. Implement pricing: AU\(100 domestic edition vs AU\)200 global edition
  4. Prevent arbitrage: Different ISBNs, "Not for sale in Australia" labels

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Price discrimination is always bad for consumers"

While it transfers surplus to producers, some consumers (more price-sensitive groups) may gain access to goods they couldn't afford at uniform pricing (e.g., student discounts).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Price discrimination requires market power and separable markets
  • First-degree captures all CS; second uses quantity tiers; third targets groups
  • Common in education, software, and travel industries
  • Can increase total output compared to uniform pricing
  • ACCC monitors for anti-competitive cases

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why can't perfect competition firms price discriminate?
  2. How do airlines prevent arbitrage between business and economy fares?
  3. Is senior pricing on public transport first, second, or third-degree discrimination?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Price Discrimination Explained — Khan Academy
  • 📚 ACCC on Price Discrimination

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: M07.L06 — Monopoly Regulation in Australia: ACCC and Competition Policy
docs.text: Module: Module 07 — Monopoly Lesson: L06 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: ACCC Official Website | Competition and Consumer Act 2010

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Describe the ACCC's role in regulating monopolies and promoting competition.

docs.title: The ACCC's Powers and Tools
docs.text:
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) regulates monopolies through:

  1. Price Surveillance: Monitoring utilities like electricity and water
  2. Access Declarations: Forcing infrastructure sharing (e.g., NBN, gas pipelines)
  3. Anti-competitive Conduct Laws: Preventing abuse of market power (Section 46)
  4. Merger Control: Blocking acquisitions that substantially lessen competition

Key legislation: Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (CCA)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Regulating Natural Monopolies: Electricity Networks

  1. Identify natural monopoly characteristics: High fixed costs, economies of scale
  2. Apply regulatory tools:
  3. Price caps (CPI-X framework)
  4. Performance standards (reliability metrics)
  5. Ring-fencing (separating competitive/generation from regulated/transmission)
  6. Recent cases: 2023 default market offer decisions limiting price rises

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"The ACCC sets prices for all monopolies"

The ACCC doesn't directly set most prices - it establishes frameworks (like price caps) and intervenes in specific cases. Many prices are still determined by markets within regulated constraints.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• ACCC balances efficiency and fairness in monopoly regulation
  • Natural monopolies are price-controlled rather than broken up
  • Access regimes promote competition in dependent markets
  • Recent focus areas: digital platforms, supermarkets, airlines
  • Penalties for anti-competitive conduct can reach 10% of turnover

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why is the NBN subject to different regulation than a tech startup?
  2. How does the CPI-X price cap formula work?
  3. What recent cases has the ACCC pursued against digital platforms?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 ACCC Explained — ACCC's own overview
  • 📚 Digital Platform Services Inquiry

Provenance: ACCC Official Website | Competition and Consumer Act 2010

docs.title: M08.L01 — Strategic Interaction and Game Theory Basics
docs.text: Module: Module 08 — Game Theory and Oligopoly Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Understand how strategic interaction shapes decision-making in competitive markets.

docs.title: Introduction to Strategic Interaction
docs.text: Strategic interaction occurs when the payoff of one player's decision depends on the choices of others. In business, this is common in markets with few competitors, such as Australia's supermarket duopoly (Coles and Woolworths). Firms must anticipate rivals' reactions to price changes or marketing strategies.

docs.title: Game Theory Basics
docs.text:
A normal form game consists of: - Players (e.g., firms, individuals) - Strategies (possible actions each player can take) - Payoffs (outcomes based on combined strategies)

Example: A simplified payoff matrix for Coles and Woolworths deciding whether to "Discount" or "Maintain Prices":

Woolworths: Discount Woolworths: Maintain Coles: Discount -5, -5 10, -10 Coles: Maintain -10, 10 0, 0

docs.title: Worked Example: Supermarket Price War
docs.text:
Scenario: Coles and Woolworths must decide whether to discount prices for Christmas sales.

  1. Identify strategies: Discount or Maintain.
  2. Analyze payoffs:
  3. If both discount, profits drop (mutual loss).
  4. If one discounts and the other doesn’t, the discounter gains market share.
  5. Dominant strategy: Discount (higher payoff regardless of rival's choice).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Game theory only applies to zero-sum games."

While zero-sum games are a subset, most real-world scenarios (like pricing) involve mixed incentives where cooperation can benefit all parties.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Payoffs depend on others' choices in strategic interactions.
  • A dominant strategy yields the best outcome regardless of rivals' actions.
  • Oligopolies (e.g., Coles/Woolworths) frequently engage in strategic pricing.
  • Game theory models help predict competitive behavior.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If Coles believes Woolworths will discount, what should Coles do?
  2. Define "best response" in your own words.
  3. Why might a dominant strategy lead to suboptimal group outcomes?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Game Theory: The Science of Decision-Making — Brief overview
  • 📚 Stanford Game Theory Course Notes

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M08.L02 — The Prisoners' Dilemma and Nash Equilibrium
docs.text: Module: Module 08 — Game Theory and Oligopoly Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain why rational individuals might not cooperate, even when it benefits everyone.

docs.title: The Prisoners' Dilemma Setup
docs.text:
Two suspects (A and B) are arrested. Prosecutors lack evidence for a major charge but can convict on a minor charge (1 year). Each prisoner is offered: - Betray (Defect): Go free if the other stays silent; serve 3 years if both betray. - Stay Silent (Cooperate): Serve 1 year if the other betrays; serve 2 years if both stay silent.

Payoff matrix (years in prison, lower is better):

B: Betray B: Silent A: Betray -3, -3 0, -3 A: Silent -3, 0 -2, -2

docs.title: Nash Equilibrium
docs.text: A Nash Equilibrium occurs when no player can improve their outcome by unilaterally changing strategy. In the Prisoners' Dilemma, the Nash Equilibrium is (Betray, Betray) even though (Silent, Silent) is better collectively.

docs.title: Worked Example: ACCC and Cartels
docs.text: Scenario: Two rival firms secretly agree to fix prices (cartel). 1. Payoffs: - If both comply, high profits. - If one defects (lower prices), they gain market share. - If both defect, profits plummet. 2. Incentive: Defect to undercut the rival (dominant strategy). 3. Outcome: Cartels collapse without enforcement (ACCC penalties).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Nash Equilibrium always leads to the best group outcome."

The Prisoners' Dilemma shows that Nash Equilibrium can be suboptimal due to individual incentives.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The Prisoners' Dilemma models conflict between individual and group rationality.
  • Nash Equilibrium describes stable (but not always optimal) outcomes.
  • Cartels are unstable because firms have incentives to cheat.
  • The ACCC enforces anti-collusion laws to protect consumers.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why does the Nash Equilibrium in the Prisoners' Dilemma involve both betraying?
  2. How might repeated interactions change firms' incentives?
  3. Explain why the ACCC offers leniency to whistleblowers.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Prisoners' Dilemma Explained — Quick intro
  • 📚 ACCC Cartel Leniency Policy

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M08.L03 — Oligopoly: Characteristics and the Interdependence Problem
docs.text: Module: Module 08 — Game Theory and Oligopoly Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Describe how oligopolies balance competition and interdependence.

docs.title: Oligopoly Definition
docs.text: An oligopoly is a market dominated by a few large firms (e.g., Australia's "Big 4" banks: CBA, Westpac, NAB, ANZ). Key traits: - High barriers to entry (e.g., regulations, economies of scale). - Strategic interdependence (firms react to rivals' actions). - Product differentiation (e.g., brand loyalty in airlines like Qantas vs. Virgin).

docs.title: The Kinked Demand Curve Model
docs.text: Explains price rigidity: - If Firm A raises prices, rivals don’t follow → demand drops sharply. - If Firm A lowers prices, rivals match → demand rises slightly. Result: Prices "stick" at the kink to avoid profit losses.

docs.title: Worked Example: Australian Supermarkets
docs.text: Scenario: Coles considers a 10% price cut on milk. 1. If Woolworths ignores: Coles gains market share. 2. If Woolworths matches: Minimal gain for Coles, profits drop for both. 3. Likely outcome: Woolworths matches → prices remain stable (kinked demand).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Oligopolies always collude to exploit consumers."

While collusion is illegal, tacit coordination (e.g., price leadership) can arise without explicit agreements.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Oligopolies feature mutual dependence among few firms.
  • The kinked demand curve explains price stability.
  • Collusion is illegal but tacit coordination occurs.
  • Australian examples include banking, airlines, and supermarkets.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why might an oligopoly avoid price wars?
  2. How do barriers to entry sustain oligopolies?
  3. Compare Qantas and Virgin Australia’s pricing strategies.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Oligopoly Explained — Short video
  • 📚 ACCC on Oligopolies

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M08.L04 — Collusion, Cartels, and the ACCC
docs.text: Module: Module 08 — Game Theory and Oligopoly Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | ACCC Cartel Guidelines

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyze why cartels form and why they often fail.

docs.title: Types of Collusion
docs.text:
• Explicit collusion: Formal agreements (e.g., cartels) to fix prices or output. Illegal in Australia under the Competition and Consumer Act.
  • Tacit collusion: Unspoken coordination (e.g., price leadership by a dominant firm).

docs.title: Cartel Instability
docs.text: Cartels collapse because: 1. Incentive to cheat: Members can profit by secretly undercutting the agreed price. 2. Detection lag: Cheating isn’t immediately noticed. 3. Punishment: If caught, firms face ACCC fines (e.g., Visy/Amcor cardboard cartel: $36M penalty).

docs.title: Worked Example: Visy/Amcor Cartel
docs.text: Scenario: Rival packaging firms conspired to fix prices (2000s). 1. Collusion: Agreed to share markets and raise prices. 2. Defection: One firm secretly offered discounts to gain clients. 3. Outcome: ACCC investigation → massive fines and reputational damage.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Cartels are stable because members trust each other."

Cartels fail because trust is undermined by profit motives—the Prisoners' Dilemma in action.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Cartels are illegal and unstable due to cheating incentives.
  • The ACCC detects and penalizes cartels to protect competition.
  • Leniency policies reward whistleblowers (first to confess avoids fines).
  • Tacit collusion is harder to regulate but still harmful.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why might a firm join a cartel despite the risks?
  2. How does the ACCC’s leniency policy destabilize cartels?
  3. Compare explicit and tacit collusion.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 How Cartels Operate — Documentary clip
  • 📚 ACCC Cartel Case Studies

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | ACCC Cartel Guidelines

docs.title: M08.L05 — First-Mover Advantage and Sequential Games
docs.text: Module: Module 08 — Game Theory and Oligopoly Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Evaluate how timing affects strategic decisions in sequential games.

docs.title: Sequential vs. Simultaneous Games
docs.text:
• Simultaneous games: Players act without knowing rivals' choices (e.g., Prisoners' Dilemma).
  • Sequential games: Players move in sequence, observing prior actions (e.g., Stackelberg model).

docs.title: First-Mover Advantage
docs.text: The first player ("leader") can shape the game to their benefit: - Entry deterrence: Incumbents invest heavily to discourage competitors (e.g., Australian mining firms securing port access). - Commitment devices: Irreversible actions (e.g., Qantas pre-booking airport slots) force rivals to adapt.

docs.title: Worked Example: Airport Slot Allocation
docs.text: Scenario: Two airlines (Qantas, Virgin) compete for Sydney Airport slots. 1. Qantas moves first: Secures prime departure times. 2. Virgin responds: Chooses less optimal slots → lower profitability. 3. Outcome: Qantas gains long-term advantage.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"First movers always win."

First-mover advantage depends on the ability to sustain commitments (e.g., R&D costs). Late movers can sometimes leapfrog with innovation.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Sequential games are modeled using game trees (extensive form).
  • First-mover advantage arises from credible commitments.
  • Backward induction predicts rational play by working backward from outcomes.
  • Australian examples include mining, aviation, and telecom.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why might a mining firm bid early for exploration rights?
  2. How does backward induction simplify decision-making?
  3. Name a disadvantage of being a first mover.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Sequential Games Tutorial — Step-by-step
  • 📚 Stackelberg Model Explained

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: M09.L01 — Price Ceilings: Rent Control and Shortages
docs.text: Module: Module 09 — Price Regulation, Taxes, and Subsidies Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Understand how price ceilings create persistent shortages and unintended consequences in markets.

docs.title: Price Ceilings in the Housing Market
docs.text: In Sydney and Melbourne, rent control policies (a type of price ceiling) set maximum prices below equilibrium levels, leading to persistent housing shortages. Rent control aims to make housing affordable but often reduces the quantity and quality of available housing. Landlords may under-maintain properties, and black markets can emerge where tenants pay additional fees to secure housing.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Rent Control in Sydney

  1. Equilibrium without control: Market rent = $500/week, Quantity = 1,000 units.
  2. Price ceiling imposed: Maximum rent = $400/week.
  3. Result: Quantity demanded rises (more people seek cheap rent), but quantity supplied falls (landlords exit the market). Shortage = 300 units.
  4. Long-term effects: Landlords reduce maintenance, leading to poorer housing quality.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Rent control helps all low-income renters."

Rent control benefits current tenants but harms future renters by reducing supply. It also creates inefficiencies like black markets and quality deterioration.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Price ceilings set below equilibrium cause shortages.
  • Rent control can reduce housing quality and supply.
  • Black markets may emerge to circumvent price controls.
  • Long-run effects depend on elasticity of supply and demand.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why does a price ceiling below equilibrium create a shortage?
  2. How might landlords respond to rent control in the long run?
  3. Who benefits and who loses from rent control policies?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 The Economics of Rent Control — Visual explanation of rent control effects
  • 📚 Rent Control in Australian Cities — Research on local impacts

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: M09.L02 — Price Floors: Minimum Wages and Surpluses
docs.text: Module: Module 09 — Price Regulation, Taxes, and Subsidies Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyze how price floors create surpluses and affect labor markets.

docs.title: Minimum Wage in Australia
docs.text: The Fair Work Commission sets a national minimum wage ($23.23/hour as of 2023). This price floor ensures workers earn a living wage but can reduce employment opportunities, especially for low-skilled workers. The impact depends on labor demand elasticity: in competitive markets, higher wages may reduce jobs, but in monopsony markets, they can increase both wages and employment.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Minimum Wage and Employment

  1. Equilibrium wage: $20/hour, Quantity of labor = 1,000 workers.
  2. Minimum wage imposed: $23.23/hour.
  3. Result: Quantity demanded falls (fewer jobs), but quantity supplied rises (more people seek work). Surplus = 200 workers unemployed.
  4. Monopsony exception: If the employer has market power, a minimum wage can increase employment.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Minimum wages always reduce employment."

In monopsony markets (single employer), a minimum wage can increase both wages and employment by countering employer power.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Price floors set above equilibrium create surpluses.
  • Minimum wages can reduce employment in competitive markets but may increase it in monopsonies.
  • Agricultural price floors (e.g., wheat boards) similarly create surplus produce.
  • Elasticity of demand determines the employment impact.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. How does a price floor affect the quantity supplied and demanded?
  2. Why might a minimum wage increase employment in a monopsony?
  3. What are the trade-offs of agricultural price supports?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Minimum Wage: Pros and Cons — Debate on minimum wage effects
  • 📚 Fair Work Commission Reports — Official wage decisions

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M09.L03 — Taxes: Incidence, Deadweight Loss, and the Role of Elasticity
docs.text: Module: Module 09 — Price Regulation, Taxes, and Subsidies Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Khan Academy Microeconomics | OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain how taxes affect market outcomes and why elasticity determines who bears the burden.

docs.title: Tax Incidence in Australia
docs.text: A per-unit tax (e.g., fuel excise at ~49c/L) shifts the supply curve upward by the tax amount. Consumers pay a higher price, producers receive a lower net price, and the tax burden falls more heavily on the side with inelastic demand or supply. Deadweight loss occurs because the tax reduces mutually beneficial trade.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Fuel Excise Tax

  1. Pre-tax equilibrium: Price = $1.50/L, Quantity = 10M liters.
  2. Tax imposed: $0.49/L.
  3. New equilibrium: Consumer price = $1.80/L, Producer price = $1.31/L, Quantity = 8M liters.
  4. Incidence: Consumers pay $0.30 more, producers receive $0.19 less. Deadweight loss = loss of 2M liters traded.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Consumers always bear the full burden of taxes."

Tax incidence depends on elasticity: if demand is elastic, producers bear more of the burden; if supply is elastic, consumers do.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Taxes create a wedge between consumer and producer prices.
  • The more inelastic side bears more of the tax burden.
  • Deadweight loss represents lost gains from trade.
  • Australian examples include GST (broad-based) and tobacco taxes (targeted).

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. How does a tax affect the equilibrium price and quantity?
  2. Why does deadweight loss occur with taxes?
  3. How does elasticity determine tax incidence?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Tax Incidence and Deadweight Loss — Visual explanation
  • 📚 ATO Tax Guides — Australian tax policies

Provenance: Khan Academy Microeconomics | OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e

docs.title: M09.L04 — Subsidies: Effects on Price, Quantity, and Welfare
docs.text: Module: Module 09 — Price Regulation, Taxes, and Subsidies Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Evaluate how subsidies alter market outcomes and create efficiency costs.

docs.title: Subsidies in Australia
docs.text: Subsidies (e.g., childcare subsidies, renewable energy incentives) shift the supply curve downward, lowering consumer prices and increasing quantity. However, they can lead to overproduction and deadweight loss. The JobKeeper wage subsidy during COVID-19 aimed to preserve employment but also distorted labor markets.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Childcare Subsidy

  1. Pre-subsidy equilibrium: Price = $100/day, Quantity = 500 spots.
  2. Subsidy introduced: $30/day.
  3. New equilibrium: Consumer price = $80/day, Producer price = $110/day, Quantity = 600 spots.
  4. Welfare cost: Deadweight loss from overproduction (some spots go unused).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Subsidies always benefit society."

While subsidies can make goods more affordable, they often lead to inefficient overproduction and fiscal costs.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Subsidies lower consumer prices and raise producer prices.
  • They increase quantity but may cause overproduction.
  • Deadweight loss occurs when the subsidy cost exceeds the benefit.
  • Australian examples include renewable energy and childcare subsidies.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. How does a subsidy affect the equilibrium price and quantity?
  2. What is the efficiency cost of a subsidy?
  3. Why might a wage subsidy (e.g., JobKeeper) distort labor markets?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Subsidies and Market Efficiency — Explanation of subsidy effects
  • 📚 Australian Childcare Subsidy — Policy details

Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M09.L05 — Australian Policy Applications: Carbon Price, Fuel Excise, Rent Assistance
docs.text: Module: Module 09 — Price Regulation, Taxes, and Subsidies Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Compare the effectiveness of different policy instruments in addressing market failures.

docs.title: Carbon Pricing in Australia
docs.text: Australia’s carbon tax (2012-2014) and current Safeguard Mechanism aim to reduce emissions by internalizing the social cost of carbon. The tax faced political opposition but was economically efficient. Fuel excise (~49c/L, indexed to CPI) similarly corrects for road-use externalities. Rent Assistance (a direct payment) avoids the pitfalls of rent control by subsidizing tenants without distorting the housing market.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Carbon Tax vs. Cap-and-Trade

  1. Carbon tax: Sets a fixed price per ton of emissions, creating price certainty but uncertain quantity reductions.
  2. Cap-and-trade: Sets a fixed emissions cap, creating quantity certainty but volatile prices.
  3. Australian experience: The carbon tax reduced emissions but was repealed due to political opposition.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"All price regulations are equally effective."

Policies vary in efficiency: taxes/subsidies can be precise but unpopular, while direct payments (e.g., Rent Assistance) avoid market distortions.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Carbon pricing internalizes environmental costs but faces political challenges.
  • Fuel excise corrects for road-use externalities.
  • Rent Assistance avoids the inefficiencies of rent control.
  • Policy design balances efficiency and equity.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why was Australia’s carbon tax repealed despite its economic efficiency?
  2. How does Rent Assistance differ from rent control?
  3. What are the trade-offs between carbon taxes and cap-and-trade?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Carbon Pricing Explained — Comparison of carbon policies
  • 📚 Safeguard Mechanism — Current Australian policy

Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M10.L01 — Externalities: Negative and Positive
docs.text: Module: Module 10 — Externalities and Market Failure Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 7: Externalities. A negative externality (left) causes overproduction; a positive externality (right) causes underproduction. Government intervention can restore efficiency.

Define and differentiate between negative and positive externalities using Australian examples.

docs.title: Understanding Externalities
docs.text: An externality occurs when a third party is affected by an economic activity, but this impact is not reflected in the market price. Externalities can be negative (costs imposed) or positive (benefits conferred). In Australia, coal mining is a classic example of a negative externality due to pollution, while vaccination programs demonstrate positive externalities by reducing disease spread.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Calculating Social Cost of Coal Mining in Queensland

  1. Private Cost: Mining company spends $50/tonne on extraction.
  2. External Cost: Pollution impacts local health and agriculture, valued at $30/tonne.
  3. Social Cost: $50 + $30 = $80/tonne.
  4. Market Outcome: Without regulation, the market produces at private cost ($50), leading to overproduction compared to the socially optimal level.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Externalities only affect the immediate parties in a transaction."

Externalities extend beyond buyers and sellers to third parties. For example, a factory's pollution affects the entire community, not just its customers or workers.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Negative externalities lead to overproduction; positive externalities lead to underproduction.
  • Social cost includes private cost plus external cost.
  • Market prices often fail to account for externalities.
  • Government intervention (e.g., taxes, subsidies) can correct externality-driven inefficiencies.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why does a market without regulation overproduce goods with negative externalities?
  2. How does vaccination create a positive externality?
  3. Draw a graph showing the difference between private and social cost curves.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Negative Externalities (Khan Academy) — Visual explanation of negative externalities
  • 📚 Australian Government Pollution Policies — Overview of environmental regulations

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: M10.L02 — The Coase Theorem and Private Solutions
docs.text: Module: Module 10 — Externalities and Market Failure Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain the Coase theorem and its implications for resolving externalities through private bargaining.

docs.title: The Coase Theorem
docs.text:
The Coase theorem states that if property rights are clearly defined and transaction costs are low, private bargaining between parties can achieve an efficient outcome regardless of how rights are initially assigned. For example, water rights trading in the Murray-Darling Basin allows farmers to negotiate allocations efficiently.

However, the theorem fails when transaction costs are high (e.g., negotiating with thousands of polluters) or property rights are unclear.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Resolving Noise Pollution Between Neighbours in Sydney

  1. Scenario: A musician practices loudly, disturbing their neighbor.
  2. Property Rights: If the musician has the right to play, the neighbor can pay them to stop.
  3. Outcome: The efficient solution (e.g., soundproofing) emerges if negotiation costs are low.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"The Coase theorem guarantees efficient outcomes in all cases."

The theorem only applies when transaction costs are negligible and property rights are enforceable. In reality, many externalities (e.g., climate change) involve high transaction costs.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Private solutions work best with low transaction costs and clear property rights.
  • Social norms and charities can address externalities when markets fail.
  • Water trading in Australia demonstrates successful Coasian bargaining.
  • Government intervention is needed when private solutions are impractical.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why does the Coase theorem fail for global externalities like climate change?
  2. How does water rights trading in Australia illustrate the Coase theorem?
  3. What are some "private solutions" to externalities besides bargaining?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Coase Theorem Explained (Khan Academy) — Simplified explanation
  • 📚 Murray-Darling Water Trading — Case study on Australian water markets

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M10.L03 — Government Solutions: Pigouvian Taxes and Subsidies
docs.text: Module: Module 10 — Externalities and Market Failure Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Design Pigouvian taxes and subsidies to correct market failures caused by externalities.

docs.title: Pigouvian Taxes and Subsidies
docs.text:
A Pigouvian tax is set equal to the marginal external cost of a negative externality (e.g., carbon tax on pollution). A Pigouvian subsidy equals the marginal external benefit of a positive externality (e.g., subsidies for renewable energy). These tools "internalize" externalities by aligning private and social costs/benefits.

Tradeable permits (like Australia's Safeguard Mechanism) are an alternative that caps total emissions while allowing market trading.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Calculating a Pigouvian Tax for Plastic Bag Use

  1. External Cost: Each plastic bag creates $0.10 in environmental harm.
  2. Tax: $0.10 per bag discourages overuse without banning it outright.
  3. Outcome: Consumers reduce usage to the socially optimal level.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Pigouvian taxes are just another way for governments to raise revenue."

The primary goal is to correct market failures, not generate revenue. Ideally, the tax revenue offsets external costs (e.g., funding clean-up programs).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Pigouvian taxes reduce negative externalities; subsidies encourage positive ones.
  • Taxes should equal marginal external cost at the efficient quantity.
  • Tradeable permits offer flexibility but require careful cap setting.
  • Command-and-control regulation (e.g., bans) is often less efficient than market-based solutions.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why is a carbon tax more efficient than a flat emissions limit?
  2. How would you set a subsidy for solar panel installation?
  3. Compare tradeable permits to Pigouvian taxes.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Pigouvian Taxes (Khan Academy) — Video tutorial
  • 📚 Australia’s Safeguard Mechanism — Policy details

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M10.L04 — Environmental Policy in Australia: Carbon, Emissions, and Efficiency
docs.text: Module: Module 10 — Externalities and Market Failure Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Evaluate Australia’s carbon pricing policies using microeconomic principles.

docs.title: Australia’s Carbon Policy Landscape
docs.text:
Australia’s carbon pricing has evolved from the 2012-14 carbon tax ($23/tonne) to the current Safeguard Mechanism (2023), which sets emissions baselines for large polluters. Fuel efficiency standards and renewable energy targets complement these market-based tools.

Political economy factors often shape policy design—e.g., the carbon tax’s repeal in 2014 due to industry lobbying.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Comparing Carbon Tax vs. Safeguard Mechanism

  1. Carbon Tax (2012-14): Fixed price per tonne, simple but politically contentious.
  2. Safeguard Mechanism (2023): Baseline-and-credit system, flexible but complex to administer.
  3. Outcome: Both aim to reduce emissions, but the mechanism allows firms more adaptability.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Market-based policies like carbon taxes hurt the economy."

Well-designed policies can reduce emissions while minimizing economic disruption. For example, revenue from carbon taxes can offset other taxes or fund transitions.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Australia has experimented with both carbon taxes and cap-and-trade systems.
  • Policy stability is often disrupted by political factors.
  • Fuel efficiency standards address transportation emissions.
  • Economic efficiency depends on policy design and implementation.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why was the 2012 carbon tax repealed?
  2. How does the Safeguard Mechanism differ from a traditional cap-and-trade system?
  3. What are the pros and cons of fuel efficiency standards?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Carbon Pricing Explained (ABC News) — Australian context
  • 📚 Clean Energy Regulator — Safeguard Mechanism details

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M10.L05 — Network Externalities and Technology Markets
docs.text: Module: Module 10 — Externalities and Market Failure Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyze how network effects shape technology markets and competition.

docs.title: Network Externalities
docs.text:
Network externalities occur when a product’s value increases with the number of users, leading to winner-take-all markets. Examples include EFTPOS (Australia’s payment network) and Afterpay (BNPL), where widespread adoption creates lock-in effects and high switching costs.

Tipping points can occur—once a platform reaches critical mass, competitors struggle to survive.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Afterpay’s Dominance in Australia

  1. Early Adoption: First-mover advantage attracted merchants and consumers.
  2. Network Effect: More users made Afterpay more valuable for new participants.
  3. Lock-in: Switching to competitors became costly due to established merchant partnerships.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Better technology always wins in competitive markets."

Network effects can lead to inferior technologies dominating (e.g., QWERTY keyboards). Market power often hinges on adoption speed, not just quality.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Network effects create winner-take-all dynamics.
  • Positive feedback loops accelerate market dominance.
  • Lock-in and switching costs protect incumbents.
  • Competition policy must balance innovation with market power concerns.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why did EFTPOS become Australia’s dominant payment network?
  2. How do social media platforms exhibit network effects?
  3. What policy tools can address excessive market power from network effects?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Network Effects Explained (Khan Academy) — Video tutorial
  • 📚 ACCC Digital Platforms Inquiry — Australian regulatory approach

Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M11.L01 — Rivalry and Excludability: The Four Types of Goods
docs.text: Module: Module 11 — Public Goods and Common Resources Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Distinguish between goods based on rivalry and excludability characteristics.

docs.title: Understanding Rivalry and Excludability
docs.text:
Goods can be classified based on two key characteristics: rivalry and excludability. Rivalry means consumption by one person reduces availability for others (e.g., eating a sandwich). Excludability means it's possible to prevent non-payers from consuming the good (e.g., movie tickets).

In Australia, we encounter all four types: 1. Private goods: Rival and excludable (e.g., groceries from Woolworths) 2. Public goods: Non-rival and non-excludable (e.g., national defense) 3. Club goods: Non-rival but excludable (e.g., Foxtel pay TV) 4. Common resources: Rival but non-excludable (e.g., public fishing grounds)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text: Classifying Sydney's Transport Options 1. Private car: Private good (rival - only one driver; excludable - need to own/rent) 2. Public bus: Common resource during peak (rival - crowded; non-excludable - can't easily refuse riders) 3. Toll roads: Club good (non-rival - space for more cars; excludable - e-Tag required) 4. Harbor views: Public good (non-rival - many can enjoy; non-excludable - can't prevent viewing)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Public goods are just things the government provides"

Public goods are defined by their economic characteristics, not who provides them. While governments often provide public goods (like lighthouses), they also provide private goods (like Australia Post services).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Rivalry means one person's use reduces others' availability
  • Excludability means non-payers can be prevented from using
  • Four categories exist based on these traits
  • Australian examples demonstrate all types in daily life

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Is a Netflix subscription rival or non-rival? Excludable or non-excludable?
  2. Why are public parks often considered common resources rather than public goods?
  3. Give an Australian example of a good that changed categories (e.g., from common resource to club good)

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 ABC Education: Public vs Private Goods — Short explainer with local examples
  • 📚 Productivity Commission on Public Goods — Government perspective

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M11.L02 — Public Goods and the Free-Rider Problem
docs.text: Module: Module 11 — Public Goods and Common Resources Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain why markets underprovide public goods due to the free-rider problem.

docs.title: The Free-Rider Problem
docs.text:
Public goods face underprovision because people can benefit without paying (free-riding). Since providers can't exclude non-payers, private firms lack profit incentive.

Australian examples: - ABC broadcasting: Listeners benefit even if they don't pay license fees - Street lighting: All residents benefit regardless of tax contributions - National defense: Protection extends to all citizens

Demand for public goods sums vertically (willingness-to-pay stacked) rather than horizontally as with private goods.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text: Calculating Optimal Public Good Provision 1. Three residents value additional streetlights differently: - Resident A: $150 - Resident B: $100 - Resident C: $50 2. New light costs \(250 to install 3. Sum of valuations (\)300) > Cost → efficient to provide 4. Market failure occurs if no mechanism exists to collect payments

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Free-riders are just being selfish"

Free-riding is rational individual behavior - why pay if you'll benefit anyway? The problem is structural, not moral. Solutions require collective action mechanisms.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Free-riders enjoy benefits without contributing
  • Markets underprovide non-excludable goods
  • Optimal quantity occurs where ∑MWP = MC
  • Government provision often necessary

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why don't private companies build lighthouses?
  2. How does Medicare differ from a pure public good?
  3. Calculate if a neighborhood park should be expanded when:
  4. 5 families value expansion at $200 each
  5. Expansion costs $900

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Khan Academy: Free Rider Problem — Animated explanation
  • 📚 Grattan Institute on Public Funding — Australian policy perspective

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M11.L03 — Common Resources and the Tragedy of the Commons
docs.text: Module: Module 11 — Public Goods and Common Resources Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)
