docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyze how unrestricted access leads to overuse of common resources.

docs.title: Tragedy of the Commons
docs.text:
Garrett Hardin's (1968) concept explains why shared resources get depleted: - Each user gains individually from increased use - Costs of overuse are shared by all - Rational individuals overconsume

Australian examples: - Murray-Darling Basin: Water allocations exceed sustainable levels - Great Barrier Reef: Overfishing threatens ecosystem - Urban roads: Congestion from unlimited access

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text: Fisheries Management 1. Unregulated: Each fisher maximizes catch → stock depletion 2. Solutions: - Quotas (individual transferable quotas in Australian fisheries) - Licenses (limited commercial fishing permits) - Community management (Indigenous ranger programs)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Privatization always solves commons problems"

While privatization can help (e.g., grazing rights), some resources can't be easily divided (e.g., air quality). Elinor Ostrom showed community governance often works better.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Unregulated common resources face overuse
  • Individual incentives conflict with group welfare
  • Solutions include privatization, regulation, and community management
  • Australian examples show real-world applications

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why does adding lanes often fail to reduce traffic congestion?
  2. How do Sydney parking meters address commons problems?
  3. Compare water markets in the Murray-Darling to traditional quotas

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 ABC: Murray-Darling Basin Crisis — Documentary clip
  • 📚 Ostrom's Governing the Commons — Nobel-winning analysis

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros)

docs.title: M11.L04 — Government Provision of Public Goods: Cost-Benefit Analysis
docs.text: Module: Module 11 — Public Goods and Common Resources Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Evaluate public projects using cost-benefit analysis principles.

docs.title: Cost-Benefit Analysis
docs.text:
Governments use CBA to determine efficient provision: 1. Identify all costs and benefits 2. Quantify in monetary terms where possible 3. Discount future values to present terms 4. Compare aggregate benefits to costs

Australian practice: - Infrastructure Australia: Requires CBA for major projects - Sydney Metro: Justified through transport time savings - Bruce Highway Upgrade: Safety benefits quantified

Challenges include valuing non-market goods (e.g., environmental impacts).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text: Evaluating a New Hospital 1. Costs: - Construction: $500 million - Annual operations: $50 million 2. Benefits: - Health outcomes valued at $60 million/year - Reduced travel time: $10 million/year 3. Over 30 years (discounted): - Present cost: $1.1 billion - Present benefit: $1.3 billion 4. Net benefit → Project proceeds

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"All government spending is wasteful"

CBA provides systematic evaluation. While imperfect, it prevents arbitrary decisions. Australian projects like the Snowy Hydro scheme show successful applications.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• CBA compares societal costs and benefits
  • Discounting adjusts for time preferences
  • Non-market valuation remains challenging
  • Australian institutions employ rigorous methods

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why discount future benefits in CBA?
  2. How might you value cleaner air near a school?
  3. Critique this CBA element: "Increased property values near new rail line"

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Infrastructure Australia: How We Assess Projects — Official guide
  • 📚 CBA in Public Policy — Productivity Commission review

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M11.L05 — Australian Examples: National Parks, Roads, and Broadband
docs.text: Module: Module 11 — Public Goods and Common Resources Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Apply goods classification to Australian infrastructure debates.

docs.title: Case Studies
docs.text:
National Parks - Club good aspects: Entry fees fund maintenance (excludable) - Public good arguments: Conservation benefits all (non-excludable)

Road Pricing - Toll roads (WestConnex): Excludable via e-Tags - Congestion charging: Proposed for Sydney CBD to manage demand

NBN Broadband - Near-public good: Universal service obligation - Private provision: Retail competition over public infrastructure

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text: Assessing Sydney Harbour Bridge 1. Without toll: Common resource (rivalrous in peak, non-excludable) 2. With toll: Club good (excludable, but still rivalrous at capacity) 3. Dynamic pricing: Higher tolls in peak periods manage rivalry

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"User-pays systems are always more efficient"

While user-pays can improve allocation (e.g., toll roads), equity concerns arise for essential services. Australia's mixed approach balances efficiency and access.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Many goods have mixed characteristics
  • Pricing mechanisms can alter good classification
  • Australian examples show policy tradeoffs
  • No one-size-fits-all solution

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Should national parks charge higher fees for foreign tourists?
  2. Why does Melbourne have fewer toll roads than Sydney?
  3. Debate: "NBN should have been fully privatized"

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Four Corners: NBN Debate — Investigative report
  • 📚 Road Pricing Reforms — Treasury analysis

Provenance: Microeconomics 1e (Medeiros) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M12.L01 — Comparative Advantage Revisited: The Case for Free Trade
docs.text: Module: Module 12 — International Trade and Synthesis Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain how comparative advantage drives gains from international trade.

docs.title: The Foundation of Trade
docs.text:
Comparative advantage, first articulated by David Ricardo in 1817, remains the bedrock justification for free trade. It states that countries should specialize in producing goods where they have the lowest opportunity cost, then trade for other goods. Even if one nation is absolutely more efficient at producing everything (absolute advantage), trade still benefits both parties when based on comparative advantage.

Australia exemplifies this principle through its exports of iron ore, LNG, and agricultural products. While Australia could produce manufactured goods, its opportunity cost is lower in resource extraction due to abundant natural endowments. Specializing in these sectors and trading for manufactured goods expands Australia's consumption possibilities beyond what its domestic production alone could achieve.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australia's Iron Ore Trade with China

  1. Suppose Australia produces either 100 tons of iron ore or 50 cars per year
  2. China produces either 80 tons of iron ore or 100 cars per year
  3. Australia's opportunity cost: 1 car = 2 tons iron ore
  4. China's opportunity cost: 1 car = 0.8 tons iron ore
  5. Terms of trade must be between these ratios (e.g., 1 car = 1.5 tons)
  6. Both nations gain by specializing and trading at intermediate terms

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Countries should only export goods they're best at producing"

This confuses absolute advantage (being best) with comparative advantage (lowest opportunity cost). Trade benefits occur when nations specialize based on relative, not absolute, efficiencies. Australia exports education services not because it's the "best" but because its opportunity cost is favorable compared to alternatives.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Trade creates gains when based on comparative (not absolute) advantage
  • Specialization expands total production possibilities
  • Terms of trade must lie between nations' domestic opportunity costs
  • Australia's comparative advantages include resource extraction and education

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. If Australia can produce 1 ton of wheat with 2 units of labor or 1 barrel of wine with 4 units, while France produces 1 ton wheat with 3 units or 1 barrel wine with 3 units, which good should Australia specialize in?
  2. How does the production possibility frontier shift with trade?
  3. Why might some Australian industries oppose free trade despite aggregate gains?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Comparative Advantage Explained — Khan Academy
  • 📚 Australia's Trade Composition — DFAT

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M12.L02 — Winners and Losers from Trade: Gains and Distributional Effects
docs.text: Module: Module 12 — International Trade and Synthesis Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Identify the distributional effects of international trade on consumers and producers.

docs.title: Gains from Trade: The Aggregate Picture
docs.text:
When Australia opens to international trade in a good, the world price determines the domestic price. If the world price is below the pre-trade domestic price, Australia imports — domestic consumers gain from lower prices while domestic producers face increased competition.

Using supply-and-demand analysis: - Consumer surplus rises (area under demand, above the lower world price) - Producer surplus falls (area above supply, below the lower world price) - Net gain: the gain to consumers exceeds the loss to producers — total economic surplus increases

This net gain is the fundamental economic case for free trade. The gains are real and measurable; Australia's access to cheaper manufactured goods, electronics, and clothing has raised household living standards significantly since tariff reductions in the 1980s–2000s.

docs.title: Winners and Losers
docs.text:
Despite aggregate gains, trade creates clear distributional effects:

Winners from imports: - Consumers (lower prices, greater variety) - Downstream industries using imported inputs (e.g. manufacturers using imported steel) - Export industries (partner countries earn income to buy Australian exports)

Losers from imports: - Import-competing domestic producers (e.g. Australian textile manufacturers, car makers) - Workers in those industries (job losses, wage pressure)

Australia's automotive industry is a stark example: Holden, Ford, and Toyota all ceased Australian manufacturing by 2017, partly due to import competition. Workers displaced faced real adjustment costs.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Steel and Manufacturing

Suppose the pre-trade domestic price of steel is $900/tonne and the world price is $600/tonne.

  • At $600, Australian consumers (manufacturers) buy more steel — consumer surplus rises
  • Australian steel producers lose market share — producer surplus falls
  • Net welfare calculation: if consumer gain = $2.1bn and producer loss = $1.4bn, net gain = $700m

The $700m net gain is real, but the $1.4bn loss is concentrated in steel workers and shareholders while the $2.1bn gain is spread across all steel-using industries.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Free trade always makes everyone better off."

Free trade increases total economic surplus but creates winners and losers. Import-competing workers and firms can experience genuine hardship. The correct statement is that the gains are large enough to potentially compensate the losers — but compensation rarely happens fully in practice.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Trade raises total economic surplus but redistributes income from import-competing industries to consumers
  • Consumer gains from imports exceed producer losses in aggregate
  • Adjustment costs for displaced workers are real and politically significant
  • The compensation principle says gainers could compensate losers, but this requires active policy

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Australia opens to imported clothing at a world price below the domestic price. Using a supply-demand diagram, show the change in consumer surplus, producer surplus, and total surplus.
  2. Why might a worker in Australia's former car manufacturing industry oppose free trade even if aggregate gains are positive?
  3. If the world price of a good is above Australia's domestic price, who gains and who loses when Australia opens to trade?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Winners and Losers from Trade (Khan Academy) — consumer/producer surplus analysis of trade
  • 📺 University of Utah — International Trade — principles playlist covers trade gains
  • 📚 OpenStax Microeconomics — International Trade

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M12.L03 — Trade Barriers: Tariffs, Quotas, and Their Effects
docs.text: Module: Module 12 — International Trade and Synthesis Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 8: Tariff Effects. Areas A (blue) = producer surplus gain; C (orange) = government revenue; B and D = deadweight loss borne by society.

Calculate the welfare effects of a tariff on consumers, producers, government, and total surplus.

docs.title: Tariffs: A Tax on Imports
docs.text:
A tariff is a tax levied on imported goods. When a government imposes a tariff of $T per unit:

  1. The domestic price rises from the world price P_W to P_W + T
  2. Domestic quantity supplied rises (domestic producers expand)
  3. Domestic quantity demanded falls (consumers face higher prices)
  4. Imports = Q_D − Q_S fall

Welfare effects: - Consumer surplus falls (area A + B + C + D) - Producer surplus rises (area A) - Government revenue rises (area C = tariff × imports) - Deadweight loss = areas B + D (triangles representing efficiency loss)

Net welfare effect: consumers lose more than producers + government gain → net loss = DWL (areas B + D).

docs.title: Quotas
docs.text:
A quota limits the quantity of imports directly. Effects on domestic price, output, and consumption are similar to a tariff — but the revenue rectangle (area C) goes to quota licence holders (often importers), not the government. This makes quotas generally less efficient than equivalent tariffs from a national welfare perspective.

Voluntary Export Restraints (VERs): An agreement where a foreign country limits its own exports. The revenue equivalent goes to foreign exporters — even worse for the importing country's welfare.

docs.title: Australia's Tariff History
docs.text: Australia historically protected manufacturing through high tariffs (25–35% in the 1960s–70s). Progressive tariff reductions from the 1980s under the Hawke–Keating governments exposed Australian manufacturers to international competition. The car industry's tariffs fell from 57.5% (1988) to 5% (2010). Most Australian tariffs are now 0–5%, with the standard rate at 5%.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Tariff on imported steel in Australia

World price P_W = $600/tonne. Tariff = $150/tonne → domestic price = $750.

At $600: Q_S = 2m tonnes, Q_D = 8m tonnes → imports = 6m tonnes At $750: Q_S = 3.5m tonnes, Q_D = 6.5m tonnes → imports = 3m tonnes

  • Consumer surplus loss: large area (consumers pay $150 more on all 6.5m tonnes purchased)
  • Producer surplus gain: domestic producers gain on 3.5m tonnes
  • Government revenue: $150 × 3m = $450m
  • Deadweight loss: two triangles from reduced imports and distorted production

Net effect: Australians as a whole lose; steel producers and government gain at consumers' expense.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Tariffs protect jobs and are good for the economy."

Tariffs protect jobs in the protected industry but destroy jobs elsewhere — consumers spend less on other goods (due to higher import prices), and export industries lose competitiveness as trading partners retaliate. The net employment effect is typically zero or negative; the real effect is a redistribution from consumers to protected producers.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Tariffs raise domestic prices, reduce imports, generate government revenue, and create deadweight loss
  • Quotas have similar price/quantity effects but transfer revenue to quota holders, not government
  • Australia dramatically reduced tariffs from the 1980s onwards; most goods now face 0–5%
  • Net welfare effect of tariffs is negative — losses to consumers exceed gains to producers and government

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Draw a supply-demand diagram for an imported good. Mark the world price, the tariff-inclusive price, and label areas A, B, C, D. Identify what each area represents.
  2. If Australia imposes a $20/tonne tariff on imported sugar: domestic production rises from 3m to 4m tonnes, consumption falls from 9m to 7m tonnes. Calculate government tariff revenue.
  3. Why is a quota generally considered less efficient than a tariff that achieves the same import reduction?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Tariffs and Quotas (Khan Academy) — welfare analysis of trade barriers
  • 📺 University of Utah — Trade Policy — principles of trade policy
  • 📚 OpenStax Microeconomics — Trade Policy

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M12.L04 — Australia and International Trade: Agreements and Policy
docs.text: Module: Module 12 — International Trade and Synthesis Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Describe Australia's major trading relationships and the role of free trade agreements.

docs.title: Australia's Trade Structure
docs.text:
Australia is a highly trade-exposed economy — trade (exports + imports) represents roughly 45% of GDP. The structure is distinctive:

Top exports (goods): - Iron ore (~$130bn/yr) — overwhelmingly to China - LNG (liquefied natural gas) — Japan, China, South Korea - Coal (metallurgical and thermal) - Gold, beef, wheat, wool

Top service exports: - Education (~$35bn pre-COVID) — largest service export - Tourism/travel

Top trading partners: China (by far the largest, ~30% of exports), Japan, South Korea, USA, India, UK

Australia runs a trade surplus in goods (driven by resources) but a deficit in services (tourism, education imports exceed exports in some periods).

docs.title: Free Trade Agreements
docs.text:
Australia has pursued bilateral and regional FTAs extensively:

Agreement Partners In force AUSFTA USA 2005 SAFTA Singapore 2003 TAFTA Thailand 2005 AANZFTA ASEAN + NZ 2010 ChAFTA China 2015 KAFTA Korea 2014 JAEPA Japan 2015 CPTPP 11 countries incl. Japan, Canada, Mexico 2018 AIFTA India 2022 AUKESFTA UK 2023

FTAs reduce tariffs and sometimes non-tariff barriers (like quarantine standards), increase market access, and lock in trade liberalisation reforms.

docs.title: Trade Diversification Challenge
docs.text:
Australia's heavy dependence on China creates vulnerability. In 2020–21, China imposed trade restrictions on Australian barley (80.5% tariff), wine, beef, coal, cotton, and lobster — largely as political retaliation over Australian calls for a COVID-19 inquiry. Australian exporters lost ~$20bn in market access.

Australia responded by successfully diversifying some exports (coal to Japan/Korea/India; barley to Saudi Arabia) while pursuing WTO dispute resolution. By 2023, most restrictions were lifted. The episode highlighted the risk of trade concentration.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
ChAFTA and Australian Beef

Before ChAFTA, Chinese tariffs on Australian beef were 12–25%. ChAFTA phased these to zero by 2024. Australian beef exports to China grew from ~$600m (2014) to over $3bn (2019). Consumer prices in China fell; Australian cattle farmers expanded production.

This illustrates comparative advantage in action: Australia's vast land, efficient ranching, and food safety reputation give it genuine comparative advantage in beef production.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Australia should be self-sufficient and reduce dependence on imports."

Pursuing self-sufficiency means producing goods at high opportunity cost domestically rather than buying them cheaply overseas. This reduces living standards. The goal of trade policy is not self-sufficiency but diversification of trading partners to manage the risk of any single partner disrupting trade.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Australia is resource-rich and export-dependent; China is the dominant trading partner (~30% of exports)
  • FTAs have expanded market access and locked in tariff reductions across Asia-Pacific
  • Trade concentration creates vulnerability — the 2020 China trade restrictions demonstrated this clearly
  • Australia's policy response was to diversify export markets while pursuing WTO dispute resolution

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Australia exports iron ore worth $130bn per year, mostly to China. What does this reveal about Australia's comparative advantage? What are the risks of this concentration?
  2. ChAFTA reduced Chinese tariffs on Australian beef to zero. Using supply-demand analysis, show who benefits and who loses in Australia and China.
  3. Why might Australia join the CPTPP even if it already has bilateral FTAs with several CPTPP members?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Australia's Trade Relationships (ABC News) — background on Australia-China trade tensions
  • 📺 International Trade Explained (Khan Academy) — comparative advantage and trade
  • 📚 DFAT — Australia's Trade in Goods and Services

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M12.L05 — Synthesis: What Have We Learnt? Key Micro Debates
docs.text: Module: Module 12 — International Trade and Synthesis Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Introductory Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Synthesize microeconomic principles through contemporary policy debates.

docs.title: The Microeconomic Framework Revisited
docs.text:
Our journey through microeconomics has built a systematic way to analyze choices under scarcity:

  1. Scarcity → PPF: Tradeoffs define production possibilities
  2. Markets: Supply/demand allocate resources efficiently
  3. Market Failure: When markets don't achieve efficiency
  4. Market power (monopolies)
  5. Externalities (pollution)
  6. Public goods (national defense)
  7. Common resources (fisheries)
  8. Information asymmetry (used cars)
  9. Government Role: Correcting failures while managing equity-efficiency tradeoffs

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Housing Affordability Debate

  1. Market Perspective:
  2. High prices signal scarcity (supply < demand)
  3. Zoning laws restrict supply (market failure)

  4. Negative externalities from homelessness

  5. Policy Responses:

  6. Upzoning to increase supply (efficiency)
  7. Rent control (equity but efficiency costs)

  8. First-home buyer grants (demand-side, may raise prices)

  9. Australian Context:

  10. Sydney/Melbourne prices rose 150% 2009-2022
  11. Debate over local vs. state planning controls

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Government intervention always reduces economic efficiency"

Well-designed policies can correct market failures and increase total surplus. Australian emissions policy (Safeguard Mechanism) aims to internalize carbon externalities without abandoning market mechanisms.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Microeconomics provides tools to analyze policy tradeoffs
  • Market failures justify targeted interventions
  • Australian debates (housing, climate) reflect core micro concepts
  • Economic analysis informs but doesn't dictate policy choices

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. How might digital platforms exhibit market power differently than traditional monopolies?
  2. Why is climate change considered a "tragedy of the commons"?
  3. How could information asymmetry affect Australia's education exports?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Market Failures — MIT OCW
  • 📚 Australia's Safeguard Mechanism — DCCEEW

Provenance: OpenStax Principles of Microeconomics 3e | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: M13.L01 — Budget Constraints: The Consumer's Choice Set
docs.text: Module: Module 13 — Budget Constraints, Preferences, and Utility Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 9: Consumer Optimum. The optimal bundle occurs where the budget line is tangent to the highest attainable indifference curve — the MRS equals the price ratio.

Derive and interpret the budget constraint equation for consumer choice problems.

docs.title: The Budget Constraint
docs.text:
A consumer with income m faces prices P₁ and P₂ for goods x₁ and x₂ respectively. The budget constraint is:

P₁x₁ + P₂x₂ ≤ m

The budget line (all bundles where the consumer spends exactly their income) is:

P₁x₁ + P₂x₂ = m

Rewriting to show intercepts:

x₂ = (m/P₂) - (P₁/P₂)x₁

  • Vertical intercept (x₁=0): m/P₂
  • Horizontal intercept (x₂=0): m/P₁
  • Slope: -P₁/P₂ (opportunity cost of x₁ in terms of x₂)

Australian Example: Consider a student with \(100 weekly budget for textbooks (P₁=\)50) and coffee (P₂=$5). Maximum textbooks = 2, maximum coffees = 20.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Budget Adjustment

  1. Original budget: 50x₁ + 5x₂ = 100
  2. If textbook price rises to $60:
  3. New intercepts: x₁ = 100/60 ≈ 1.67, x₂ = 20
  4. New slope: -60/5 = -12 (steeper)
  5. If income increases to $120:
  6. New intercepts: x₁ = 120/50 = 2.4, x₂ = 24
  7. Parallel shift outward

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"A price increase always reduces the maximum quantity of both goods"

Correction: Only the good whose price increased sees reduced maximum quantity. The other good's maximum remains unchanged (though affordable combinations change).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The budget line shows all combinations of goods that exhaust income
  • Slope represents the market's tradeoff rate between goods
  • Income changes cause parallel shifts
  • Price changes rotate the budget line
  • The numeraire good simplifies analysis by setting one price to 1

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive the budget line equation when P₁=10, P₂=4, m=200
  2. How does the budget set change if P₂ increases to 5?
  3. Calculate the new intercepts if income rises by 20% with original prices

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Budget Constraints Explained — University of Sydney
  • 📚 Varian Ch.2: Budget Constraint

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M13.L02 — Preferences and Indifference Curves
docs.text: Module: Module 13 — Budget Constraints, Preferences, and Utility Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Construct indifference curves from preference relations and calculate marginal rates of substitution.

docs.title: Preference Relations
docs.text:
For any two bundles A and B:

  • A ≿ B: "A is weakly preferred to B"
  • A ≻ B: "A is strictly preferred to B"
  • A ∼ B: "Indifferent between A and B"

An indifference curve shows all bundles yielding equal satisfaction.

Properties: 1. Downward sloping (more is better) 2. Cannot cross (transitivity) 3. Higher curves = more preferred 4. Diminishing MRS (convex to origin)

Marginal Rate of Substitution (MRS): MRS = -dx₂/dx₁ along IC

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Housing Preferences

Consider preferences between housing size (x₁) and commute time (x₂):

  1. Given U = x₁^(0.6)x₂^(0.4)
  2. MRS = -MU₁/MU₂ = -(0.6x₂)/(0.4x₁)
  3. At bundle (100m², 30min): MRS = -(0.630)/(0.4100) = -0.45 → Willing to sacrifice 0.45min commute per extra m²

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Indifference curves show actual consumption choices"

Correction: They represent potential tradeoffs - actual choices depend on budget constraints.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Indifference curves map equally preferred bundles
  • MRS measures willingness to substitute between goods
  • Well-behaved preferences are complete, transitive, monotonic, and convex
  • Diminishing MRS reflects decreasing willingness to substitute

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Sketch indifference curves for perfect substitutes
  2. Calculate MRS for U=x₁ + 2x₂ at (4,3)
  3. Explain why crossing indifference curves violate transitivity

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Indifference Curves and MRS — UNSW Economics
  • 📚 Varian Ch.3: Preferences

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M13.L03 — Properties of Preferences: Convexity, Monotonicity, and More
docs.text: Module: Module 13 — Budget Constraints, Preferences, and Utility Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyze how preference properties shape indifference curves and consumer behavior.

docs.title: Fundamental Properties
docs.text:
1. Completeness: Can compare any two bundles (A≿B or B≿A)
  2. Transitivity: If A≿B and B≿C, then A≿C
  3. Monotonicity: More is better (if A has ≥ quantities than B, then A≿B)
  4. Convexity: Averages preferred to extremes (if A∼B, then (A+B)/2 ≿ A)

Special Cases: - Strict convexity: Averages strictly preferred - Satiation: Bliss point exists

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Food Preferences

Consider a consumer choosing between meat pies (x₁) and vegemite toast (x₂):

  1. Show convexity holds for U=min(x₁, 2x₂)
  2. Take A=(2,1) and B=(4,2) → U=2 for both
  3. Average bundle C=(3,1.5) → U=min(3,3)=3

  4. 3 > 2 → convexity satisfied

  5. Show MRS is undefined at kinks (perfect complements)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Convex preferences mean the utility function is convex"

Correction: The utility function is quasiconcave - convex preferences correspond to convex lower contour sets.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Well-behaved preferences satisfy completeness, transitivity, monotonicity, and convexity
  • Convexity implies diminishing MRS
  • Strict convexity guarantees unique optimal bundles
  • Non-monotonic preferences have bliss points

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Prove that U=x₁x₂ represents convex preferences
  2. Sketch indifference curves for a satiated preference
  3. Explain why transitivity prevents preference cycles

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Properties of Preferences — ANU Economics
  • 📚 Varian Ch.3: Preferences

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M13.L04 — Utility Functions: Ordinal Representation of Preferences
docs.text: Module: Module 13 — Budget Constraints, Preferences, and Utility Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Transform utility functions while preserving preference orderings and calculate marginal rates of substitution.

docs.title: Utility Functions
docs.text:
A utility function U(x₁,x₂) assigns numbers to bundles such that: U(A) > U(B) ⇔ A ≻ B

Key Properties: 1. Ordinal (only ranking matters) 2. Preserved under monotonic transformations 3. MRS = -MU₁/MU₂

Common Forms: 1. Cobb-Douglas: U = x₁ᵃx₂ᵇ 2. Perfect substitutes: U = ax₁ + bx₂ 3. Perfect complements: U = min(ax₁, bx₂)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Energy Preferences

Consider U = √(electricity)×√(gas):

  1. Show V = ln(U) represents same preferences: V = 0.5ln(x₁) + 0.5ln(x₂) → monotonic transformation

  2. Calculate MRS: MU₁ = 0.5/√x₁, MU₂ = 0.5/√x₂ MRS = -MU₁/MU₂ = -√x₂/√x₁

  3. At (4,9): MRS = -3/2

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Higher utility numbers mean greater happiness"

Correction: Utility is ordinal - only rankings matter, not absolute values.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Utility functions numerically represent preference orderings
  • Monotonic transformations preserve preference rankings
  • MRS depends only on preference structure, not utility scale
  • Marginal utilities measure local tradeoff rates

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Show U=x₁x₂ and V=lnx₁+lnx₂ yield same MRS
  2. Find MRS for U=x₁²x₂ at (2,3)
  3. Explain why U=100+x₁ and V=eˣ¹ represent identical preferences

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Utility Functions and MRS — University of Melbourne
  • 📚 Varian Ch.4: Utility

Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M13.L05 — Special Preferences: Perfect Substitutes, Complements, and Cobb-Douglas
docs.text: Module: Module 13 — Budget Constraints, Preferences, and Utility Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Distinguish between major classes of utility functions and their implications for consumer choice.

docs.title: Special Preference Classes
docs.text:
1. Perfect Substitutes:
  2. U = ax₁ + bx₂
  3. Linear ICs, constant MRS = -a/b

  4. Example: Different brands of rice

  5. Perfect Complements:

  6. U = min(ax₁, bx₂)
  7. L-shaped ICs, MRS undefined at kink

  8. Example: Left/right shoes

  9. Cobb-Douglas:

  10. U = x₁ᵃx₂ᵇ
  11. Smooth convex ICs, MRS = -a/b × x₂/x₁

  12. Example: General consumer goods

  13. Quasilinear:

  14. U = v(x₁) + x₂
  15. Parallel ICs, MRS depends only on x₁
  16. Example: Goods with no income effect

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Beverage Choices

  1. Perfect substitutes: Tea (x₁) and coffee (x₂) for caffeine: U = 2x₁ + x₂ → MRS = -2 always

  2. Perfect complements: Coffee (x₁) and sugar (x₂) in fixed ratio: U = min(x₁, 5x₂) → 1 coffee : 5 sugars

  3. Cobb-Douglas: U = √x₁√x₂ → MRS = -x₂/x₁

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Cobb-Douglas preferences imply equal spending on both goods"

Correction: Spending shares equal exponent ratios (a:b), which needn't be 1:1.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Perfect substitutes have linear ICs and constant MRS
  • Perfect complements have L-shaped ICs with optimal bundles at fixed ratios
  • Cobb-Douglas yields smooth convex ICs with variable MRS
  • Quasilinear preferences eliminate income effects for one good

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Sketch ICs for U=2x₁ + 3x₂
  2. Derive MRS for U=x₁^(0.3)x₂^(0.7)
  3. Explain why perfect complements lead to corner solutions off the fixed ratio

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Types of Utility Functions — UNSW Economics
  • 📚 Varian Ch.4: Utility

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M14.L01 — Optimal Consumer Choice: Utility Maximisation
docs.text: Module: Module 14 — Consumer Choice and Demand Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Solve the consumer's utility maximisation problem to find optimal consumption bundles.

docs.title: Utility Maximisation Problem
docs.text:
In Australian supermarkets, consumers face budget constraints when choosing between goods like Vegemite (good 1) and Tim Tams (good 2). The utility maximisation problem is:

max U(x₁, x₂) subject to p₁x₁ + p₂x₂ ≤ m

At an interior solution, the optimal bundle satisfies the tangency condition:

MRS = p₁/p₂

Where MRS is the marginal rate of substitution (slope of indifference curve). For Cobb-Douglas utility U(x₁,x₂) = x₁ᵃx₂ᵇ, this becomes:

(a/x₁)/(b/x₂) = p₁/p₂

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Optimising Aussie Snack Consumption

Suppose a student has $20 to spend on snacks (Vegemite: $5, Tim Tams: $4) with utility U = x₁⁰·⁵x₂⁰·⁵:

  1. Set up Lagrangian: ℒ = x₁⁰·⁵x₂⁰·⁵ + λ(20 - 5x₁ - 4x₂)
  2. FOCs: ∂ℒ/∂x₁ = 0.5x₁⁻⁰·⁵x₂⁰·⁵ - 5λ = 0 ∂ℒ/∂x₂ = 0.5x₁⁰·⁵x₂⁻⁰·⁵ - 4λ = 0
  3. Solve for λ and set equal: (x₂/x₁) = 5/4
  4. Substitute into budget: 5x₁ + 4(1.25x₁) = 20 → x₁ = 2, x₂ = 2.5

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Corner solutions are rare in real-world consumer choice"

Many Australian consumers regularly choose corner solutions - like buying only petrol (no public transport) in rural areas or only streaming services (no DVDs) in cities.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The optimal bundle occurs where the budget line is tangent to the highest indifference curve
  • MRS = price ratio at interior solutions
  • Lagrangian method systematically solves constrained optimization
  • Corner solutions occur when MRS ≠ price ratio at zero consumption
  • Geometrically, we seek the highest IC touching the budget line

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Solve for optimal (x₁,x₂) when U = x₁x₂², p₁=2, p₂=3, m=60
  2. Graphically show why MRS ≠ p₁/p₂ leads to corner solutions
  3. Find conditions when a consumer would buy only good 1

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Utility Maximisation (Uni of Adelaide) — Australian lecturer explains with local examples
  • 📚 Varian Ch.5: Choice — Textbook reference

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M14.L02 — Interior and Corner Solutions
docs.text: Module: Module 14 — Consumer Choice and Demand Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Identify when consumer optimization yields interior versus corner solutions.

docs.title: Types of Solutions
docs.text:
Interior Solution: Occurs when consuming positive quantities of all goods (MRS = p₁/p₂). Common with normal goods like Australian households buying both electricity and gas.

Corner Solution: Occurs when consuming zero of one good. Three main cases: 1. Perfect substitutes (e.g., Coles vs Woolworths brand) 2. Strong preferences (vegetarians buying no meat) 3. Extreme price ratios (no private school at high tuition)

Mathematically, corner solutions violate the tangency condition:

MRS > p₁/p₂ ⇒ consume only good 1 MRS < p₁/p₂ ⇒ consume only good 2

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian Energy Choice

Consider a consumer choosing between electricity (x₁, $0.30/kWh) and gas (x₂, $0.10/MJ) with utility U = 2x₁ + x₂ (perfect substitutes):

  1. MRS = 2 (constant)
  2. Price ratio = 0.30/0.10 = 3
  3. Since 2 < 3, consumer chooses only gas (x₂ = m/0.10, x₁ = 0)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Corner solutions mean the consumer is irrational"

Corner solutions often reflect rational specialization - like Australian miners using only diesel vehicles (no electric) in remote sites.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Interior solutions satisfy MRS = price ratio
  • Corner solutions occur when preferences or technology prevent substitution
  • Perfect substitutes always yield corner solutions
  • Real-world examples include: medication choices, fuel selection, education modes
  • Kinked budget constraints (like Medicare levy) can create corner solutions

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Find the optimal bundle for U = min{x₁, 2x₂} with p₁=4, p₂=2, m=40
  2. Show graphically why U = x₁ + x₂ always gives corner solutions
  3. Explain why Australian retirees might choose corner solutions in leisure/work choices

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Corner Solutions (Khan Academy)
  • 📚 Varian Ch.5.4: Corner Solutions

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M14.L03 — Deriving the Demand Curve from Utility Maximisation
docs.text: Module: Module 14 — Consumer Choice and Demand Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Derive individual demand functions from utility maximisation problems.

docs.title: Demand Function Derivation
docs.text:
From the utility maximisation solution, we obtain demand functions x₁*(p₁,p₂,m). For Cobb-Douglas utility U = x₁ᵃx₂ᵇ:

x₁* = (a/(a+b))·(m/p₁)

Key properties: 1. Homogeneous of degree 0: x₁(tp₁,tp₂,tm) = x₁(p₁,p₂,m) 2. Downward sloping in own-price (law of demand) 3. Engel curve shows x₁* vs m

Australian example: The demand for avocado toast (x₁) follows x₁* = 0.6m/p₁ in Sydney cafes.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Melbourne Coffee Demand

Suppose utility over coffee (x₁) and tea (x₂) is U = x₁⁰·⁷⁵x₂⁰·²⁵ with m = $50:

  1. Demand functions: x₁ = (0.75/1)·(50/p₁) = 37.5/p₁ x₂ = (0.25/1)·(50/p₂) = 12.5/p₂
  2. At p₁ = $4.50 (typical Melbourne flat white): x₁* ≈ 8.33 coffees/week
  3. Demand curve: x₁* = 37.5/p₁ (rectangular hyperbola)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Demand curves are linear in practice"

Most Australian demand curves (housing, education, healthcare) show non-linear price sensitivity, especially at income thresholds.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Demand functions emerge from utility maximisation solutions
  • Cobb-Douglas yields constant budget shares
  • Homogeneity means demand responds to real (not nominal) values
  • Price-consumption curve traces optimal bundles as price changes
  • Australian examples include: petrol demand, NBN internet plans

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive demand functions for U = x₁ + ln(x₂)
  2. Show that x₁* = 10/p₁ is homogeneous of degree 0
  3. Draw the price-consumption curve for perfect complements

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Deriving Demand Curves (Uni of Sydney) — Australian lecturer demo
  • 📚 Varian Ch.6: Demand

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M14.L04 — Income and Substitution Effects: Normal and Inferior Goods
docs.text: Module: Module 14 — Consumer Choice and Demand Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Decompose price effects into income and substitution components.

docs.title: Effect Decomposition
docs.text:
When p₁ falls: 1. Substitution Effect (SE): Movement along IC to new MRS (always negative) 2. Income Effect (IE): Parallel shift of budget line

For normal goods: SE & IE reinforce (↓p₁ ⇒ ↑x₁) For inferior goods: SE & IE oppose (Giffen if IE dominates)

Australian example: Rice is inferior (↑income ⇒ ↓consumption), while quinoa is normal.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Brisbane Transport Choices

Initial: p₁=\(5 (bus), p₂=\)20 (Uber), m=$200, U=x₁x₂ Optimal: x₁=20, x₂=5

After bus fare cut to p₁'=$4: 1. New optimal: x₁'=25, x₂'=5 2. SE: Find bundle at new prices with original utility (U=100) Solve x₁x₂=100 s.t. 4x₁+20x₂=200 → x₁ˢ≈22.36, x₂ˢ≈4.47 3. SE = 22.36 - 20 = +2.36 4. IE = 25 - 22.36 = +2.64

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Giffen goods violate the law of demand"

Giffen goods (like cheap staples during Australian gold rushes) are rare but don't violate theory - they're inferior goods where IE > SE.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Substitution effect always opposes price change
  • Income effect direction depends on good type
  • Slutsky equation: Total Effect = SE + IE
  • Hicksian decomposition holds utility constant
  • Australian Giffen candidates: cheap bread, milk in recessions

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Calculate SE/IE for U=x₁x₂ when p₁ falls from $6 to \(4 (p₂=\)12, m=$120)
  2. Draw SE/IE for inferior non-Giffen good
  3. Explain why Sydney housing might show positive IE despite being normal

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Income/Substitution Effects (UNSW) — Australian analysis
  • 📚 Varian Ch.8: Slutsky Equation

Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M14.L05 — Revealed Preference and the Weak Axiom
docs.text: Module: Module 14 — Consumer Choice and Demand Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Test consumer consistency using revealed preference theory.

docs.title: Revealed Preference Concepts
docs.text:
Directly Revealed Preferred (DRP): Bundle A is DRP to B if A chosen when B was affordable

Weak Axiom of Revealed Preference (WARP): If A DRP to B, then B cannot be DRP to A under different prices/budget

Australian application: Test whether household energy choices (2019 vs 2023) satisfy WARP despite price shocks.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Adelaide Grocery Choices

Observation 1 (Jan): p₁=\(4 (milk), p₂=\)5 (juice), chosen bundle A=(2,3) Observation 2 (Jul): p₁'=\(5, p₂'=\)4, chosen bundle B=(3,2)

Check WARP: 1. Cost of A at Jan prices: 4×2 + 5×3 = $23 Cost of B at Jan prices: 4×3 + 5×2 = $22 → A was feasible when B chosen 2. Cost of B at Jul prices: 5×3 + 4×2 = $23 Cost of A at Jul prices: 5×2 + 4×3 = $22 → B was feasible when A chosen 3. Violation: Both A DRP to B and B DRP to A

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"WARP violations mean irrationality"

Australian consumers may rationally violate WARP due to: - Changing preferences (seasonal produce) - Quality adjustments (smaller "shrinkflation" packages) - New information (health studies)

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Choices reveal preferences without utility functions
  • WARP tests consistency across budget scenarios
  • Index numbers (Laspeyres/Paasche) measure welfare changes
  • Australian CPI uses revealed preference logic
  • Policy applications: NBN adoption, Medicare utilization

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Check WARP for bundles A=(1,4) at prices (\(10,\)5) and B=(4,1) at (\(5,\)10)
  2. Calculate Laspeyres index for above if base is Observation 1
  3. Explain why fuel choice data might show WARP violations

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Revealed Preference (ANU) — Australian perspective
  • 📚 Varian Ch.7: Revealed Preference

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M15.L01 — The Slutsky Equation: Decomposing Price Effects
docs.text: Module: Module 15 — Comparative Statics and Demand Theory Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 10: Slutsky Decomposition. A price rise moves the consumer from A to B. The substitution effect (A→C, along the compensated budget line) and income effect (C→B) sum to the total effect.

Derive and interpret the Slutsky equation to decompose price effects into substitution and income effects.

docs.title: Price Effect Decomposition
docs.text:
The Slutsky equation (∂x₁/∂p₁ = ∂x₁ʰ/∂p₁ - x₁ × ∂x₁/∂m) shows how a price change affects demand through two channels: 1. Substitution effect (∂x₁ʰ/∂p₁): Always negative for own-price changes, showing the pure effect of relative price changes holding utility constant. 2. Income effect (-x₁ × ∂x₁/∂m): Sign depends on whether the good is normal (negative) or inferior (positive).

In Australia, consider fuel prices: a 10% price increase reduces consumption through both effects - consumers substitute toward public transport (substitution) and have less real income (income effect).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Calculating Slutsky Decomposition for Australian Coffee Demand

Given: - Initial consumption: x₁ = 5 cups/week at p₁ = $4 - Price increase to $5 - Hicksian demand slope: ∂x₁ʰ/∂p₁ = -2 - Income effect: ∂x₁/∂m = 0.1

Substitution effect = -2 cups/$ Income effect = -5 × 0.1 = -0.5 cups/$ Total effect = -2 + (-0.5) = -2.5 cups/$

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"The income effect is always negative"

This only holds for normal goods. For inferior goods (e.g., instant noodles during economic downturns), the income effect is positive - as real income decreases, demand increases.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The Slutsky equation mathematically separates price effects
  • Substitution effect always opposes price changes
  • Income effect sign reveals good's normality
  • Matrix form shows demand theory consistency conditions

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive the Slutsky equation for x₂ when p₁ changes
  2. Calculate effects when ∂x₁ʰ/∂p₁ = -1.5, x₁ = 3, ∂x₁/∂m = 0.2
  3. Graphically show how a Giffen good violates standard assumptions

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Slutsky Equation Visualized — MIT OpenCourseWare
  • 📚 Demand Theory

Provenance: Barreto | MIT OCW

docs.title: M15.L02 — Hicksian (Compensated) vs Marshallian (Ordinary) Demand
docs.text: Module: Module 15 — Comparative Statics and Demand Theory Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Distinguish Hicksian compensated demand from Marshallian ordinary demand and derive the expenditure function.

docs.title: Two Concepts of Demand
docs.text:
Marshallian (ordinary) demand x(p, m) holds nominal income constant. When price changes, the consumer's real purchasing power also changes — the demand response reflects both substitution and income effects.

Hicksian (compensated) demand h(p, u) holds utility constant at some reference level u. As price changes, income is hypothetically adjusted to keep the consumer on the same indifference curve. Only the substitution effect is captured.

The key difference: Marshallian demand is what we observe in markets (income fixed). Hicksian demand is a theoretical construct useful for welfare analysis.

docs.title: The Expenditure Function
docs.text:
The expenditure function e(p, u) gives the minimum expenditure needed to achieve utility level u at prices p:

e(p, u) = min{p₁x₁ + p₂x₂ : U(x₁,x₂) ≥ u}

Shephard's Lemma: The partial derivative of the expenditure function with respect to price equals the Hicksian demand:

∂e(p,u)/∂pᵢ = hᵢ(p, u)

This is one of the most useful results in demand theory — it lets us recover Hicksian demands from the expenditure function without solving the full minimisation problem.

docs.title: The Slutsky Relationship
docs.text:
The Slutsky equation connects Marshallian and Hicksian demands:

∂xᵢ/∂pⱼ = ∂hᵢ/∂pⱼ - xⱼ × ∂xᵢ/∂m

Substituting the expenditure function: hᵢ(p, u) = xᵢ(p, e(p, u))

For own-price effects (i = j):

∂x₁/∂p₁ = ∂h₁/∂p₁ - x₁ × ∂x₁/∂m = (substitution effect) + (income effect)

The substitution effect ∂h₁/∂p₁ ≤ 0 always (Law of Compensated Demand). The income effect depends on whether the good is normal or inferior.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Electricity tariff reform in Australia

A household has Cobb-Douglas utility U = x₁^0.5 × x₂^0.5 where x₁ = electricity (kWh), x₂ = other goods.

Expenditure function: e(p₁, p₂, u) = 2u(p₁p₂)^0.5

Hicksian demand: h₁ = ∂e/∂p₁ = u(p₂/p₁)^0.5

Marshallian demand: x₁ = m/(2p₁)

Electricity price rises from p₁ = 0.30 to p₁ = 0.40 ($/kWh). Income m = $200.

Marshallian response: x₁ falls from 200/(2×0.30) = 333 to 200/(2×0.40) = 250 kWh.

Hicksian response (holding u constant at original level): - Original utility: U = (333)^0.5 × (333)^0.5 ≈ 100 (assuming p₂=1, x₂=100) - h₁ at new price: 100 × (1/0.40)^0.5 ≈ 250 kWh

Here income and substitution effects nearly coincide because electricity is a small budget share — a common real-world pattern.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Hicksian demand is just demand with a lower income."

Hicksian demand is not simply demand at a reduced income level. It adjusts income precisely to keep utility constant — the amount of compensation depends on the shape of the indifference curves and can vary nonlinearly with price changes. For large price changes, Marshallian and Hicksian demands can diverge substantially.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Marshallian demand holds income constant; Hicksian demand holds utility constant
  • The expenditure function e(p,u) gives minimum cost to achieve utility u at prices p
  • Shephard's Lemma: ∂e/∂pᵢ = Hicksian demand hᵢ(p,u)
  • Hicksian demand satisfies the law of compensated demand (negative own-price derivative always)

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. For U = min(x₁, x₂) (perfect complements), derive the expenditure function e(p₁, p₂, u) and verify Shephard's Lemma.
  2. For Cobb-Douglas U = x₁^a × x₂^(1-a), derive the Hicksian demand h₁(p,u) using the expenditure function e = u × p₁^a × p₂^(1-a) / (a^a(1-a)^(1-a)).
  3. A government carbon tax raises electricity prices. Using Marshallian vs Hicksian demand, explain why the Marshallian estimate of the welfare cost exceeds the Hicksian (compensating variation) estimate.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Expenditure Function and Hicksian Demand (University of Utah) — intermediate micro series
  • 📺 Compensated vs Uncompensated Demand (Khan Academy) — conceptual overview
  • 📚 Barreto — Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M15.L03 — Income Consumption Curves and Engel Curves
docs.text: Module: Module 15 — Comparative Statics and Demand Theory Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Derive the income consumption curve and Engel curve from utility maximisation.

docs.title: The Income Consumption Curve
docs.text:
The income consumption curve (ICC) traces the locus of optimal consumption bundles as income m increases, holding prices constant. For each income level, we solve the utility maximisation problem and plot the optimal bundle. Connecting these points traces the ICC.

For a normal good: as income rises, the optimal quantity of x₁ rises. The ICC slopes upward and to the right through the origin (for homothetic preferences).

For an inferior good: as income rises beyond some threshold, the optimal quantity actually falls. The ICC bends backward toward the x₂ axis.

For homothetic preferences (like Cobb-Douglas), the ICC is a ray through the origin — income expansion proportionally increases all consumption.

docs.title: Engel Curves
docs.text:
An Engel curve plots quantity demanded of one good against income, holding prices fixed. It is derived directly from the ICC by projecting the income-quantity relationship.

For Cobb-Douglas U = x₁^a × x₂^(1-a):

x₁* = am/p₁ → Engel curve is linear through origin with slope a/p₁

Normal good: Engel curve slopes upward (dx₁/dm > 0) Inferior good: Engel curve slopes backward (dx₁/dm < 0) above some income level Luxury good: expenditure share rises with income (income elasticity > 1) Necessity: expenditure share falls with income (0 < income elasticity < 1)

docs.title: Engel's Law
docs.text:
Ernst Engel (1857) observed that as household income rises, the share of income spent on food decreases. This Engel's Law holds remarkably consistently across countries and time periods.

Australian ABS Household Expenditure Survey (2015-16): food and non-alcoholic beverages accounted for 17% of low-income household budgets but only 11% of high-income households — consistent with Engel's Law. Housing, recreation, and transport are the spending categories that grow with income.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Deriving Engel curves for an Australian household

Utility: U = x₁^0.3 × x₂^0.7 (x₁ = food, x₂ = other goods) Prices: p₁ = $10, p₂ = $1

Marshallian demands: x₁ = 0.3m/p₁ = 0.3m/10 = 0.03m x₂ = 0.7m/p₂ = 0.7m

Engel curve for food: x₁ = 0.03m — linear, normal good Income elasticity of food: ε = dx₁/dm × m/x₁ = 0.03 × m/(0.03m) = 1 (unit elastic)

Expenditure on food: p₁x₁* = 10 × 0.03m = 0.3m = 30% of income (constant share — homothetic)

For a luxury like international travel (income elasticity > 1), the Engel curve would be convex — spending share rises with income.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"All goods have upward-sloping Engel curves."

Inferior goods have backward-bending Engel curves above some income threshold — demand falls as income rises. Classic examples include budget cuts of meat (replaced by premium cuts), instant noodles (replaced by restaurant meals), and bus travel (replaced by car ownership). In Australia, demand for government housing is an inferior good — higher-income households exit the waiting list.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The ICC traces optimal bundles as income varies (prices fixed); the Engel curve maps one good's quantity against income
  • Normal goods have upward-sloping Engel curves; inferior goods have backward-bending curves
  • Engel's Law: food budget share falls as income rises — consistently observed in ABS data
  • For homothetic preferences (Cobb-Douglas), all goods have linear Engel curves through the origin

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. For U = x₁^0.4 × x₂^0.6 with p₁=\(5, p₂=\)2: derive the Engel curves and calculate income elasticity for each good.
  2. A good has income elasticity 1.8. Is it a normal good, inferior good, luxury, or necessity? What does its Engel curve look like?
  3. Using ABS HES data, food spending by quintile is: Q1=\(220/wk, Q2=\)280, Q3=\(320, Q4=\)380, Q5=\(480. Income by quintile is: Q1=\)600, Q2=\(900, Q3=\)1,200, Q4=\(1,700, Q5=\)3,500. Calculate income elasticity between Q1 and Q5 and verify Engel's Law.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Income Consumption Curve and Engel Curves (University of Utah) — intermediate series
  • 📺 Normal and Inferior Goods (Khan Academy) — conceptual overview
  • 📚 ABS Household Expenditure Survey

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M15.L04 — Price Consumption Curves and the Demand Function
docs.text: Module: Module 15 — Comparative Statics and Demand Theory Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Derive the price consumption curve and explain its relationship to the demand function.

docs.title: The Price Consumption Curve
docs.text:
The price consumption curve (PCC) traces the locus of optimal consumption bundles as the price of one good changes, holding income and the other price constant.

As p₁ falls (budget line rotates outward along x₁ axis), the consumer's optimal bundle moves. Connecting all optimal bundles as p₁ varies traces the PCC in (x₁, x₂) space.

Deriving the demand curve from the PCC: For each optimal bundle on the PCC, read off x₁ and the corresponding p₁. Plotting (p₁, x₁) gives the Marshallian demand curve for good 1.

docs.title: PCC Slope and Gross Substitutes/Complements
docs.text:
The slope of the PCC reveals whether good 2 is a gross substitute or gross complement for good 1:

  • If as p₁ falls, x₂ also falls (PCC slopes downward): goods are gross complements — demand for good 2 moves with demand for good 1. Example: coffee (good 1) and coffee cups (good 2).

  • If as p₁ falls, x₂ rises (PCC slopes upward): goods are gross substitutes — when good 1 becomes cheaper, consumers buy more good 1 and less good 2. Example: Coles (good 1) and Woolworths (good 2).

For Cobb-Douglas U = x₁^a × x₂^(1-a): x₂* = (1-a)m/p₂ — independent of p₁! The PCC is horizontal, meaning goods are neither gross substitutes nor complements in the Cobb-Douglas case.

docs.title: Homogeneity and the Demand Function
docs.text:
An important property: Marshallian demand functions are homogeneous of degree zero in (p₁, p₂, m). Doubling all prices and income leaves demand unchanged — only real purchasing power and relative prices matter, not the price level.

Formally: xᵢ(λp₁, λp₂, λm) = xᵢ(p₁, p₂, m) for all λ > 0.

This has policy implications: if the RBA targets 2-3% inflation and wages rise proportionally, real consumption baskets are unchanged.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Petrol price fall and public transport demand in Australian cities

Let x₁ = kilometres driven (petrol), x₂ = public transport trips, p₁ = fuel cost per km, m = transport budget.

When petrol prices fell 30% in 2020 (COVID demand collapse), if PCC slopes upward (substitutes), public transport demand should fall. PTV and Translink data showed exactly this: car use rebounded faster than public transport after prices fell.

For U = x₁^0.6 × x₂^0.4, p₁ = $0.15/km, p₂ = $3/trip, m = $300/month: x₁ = 0.6×300/0.15 = 1,200 km; x₂ = 0.4×300/3 = 40 trips

If p₁ falls to $0.10: x₁ = 0.6×300/0.10 = 1,800 km; x₂ = 0.4×300/3 = 40 trips (unchanged — Cobb-Douglas)

The Cobb-Douglas PCC is horizontal — the goods are income-independent (neither substitutes nor complements in this model). Real demand data suggests they are substitutes, requiring a different utility specification.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"The demand curve and the PCC are the same thing."

The PCC lives in quantity-quantity space (x₁, x₂), tracing bundles as price varies. The demand curve lives in price-quantity space (p₁, x₁). The demand curve is derived FROM the PCC by reading off (p₁, x₁) coordinates for each bundle on the PCC.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The PCC traces optimal bundles as one price varies; the demand curve plots price against optimal quantity
  • PCC sloping downward → gross complements; PCC sloping upward → gross substitutes; PCC horizontal → neither (Cobb-Douglas)
  • Marshallian demand is homogeneous of degree zero in prices and income
  • Australia case: petrol and public transport are empirical gross substitutes — PCC slopes upward

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. For U = min(x₁, 2x₂) (perfect complements), derive x₁ and x₂ as functions of p₁, holding p₂=\(5 and m=\)100. What is the shape of the PCC?
  2. U = x₁ + 2x₂ (perfect substitutes). Derive demand and the PCC. What happens at p₁/p₂ = 1/2?
  3. Australian mobile data prices fell 40% between 2017-2022. Using the PCC framework, predict what happens to demand for fixed broadband (complement or substitute?).

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Price Consumption Curve (University of Utah) — intermediate series
  • 📺 Deriving Demand from Indifference Curves (Khan Academy)
  • 📚 Barreto — Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M15.L05 — Compensating and Equivalent Variation: Welfare Measurement
docs.text: Module: Module 15 — Comparative Statics and Demand Theory Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Calculate compensating and equivalent variation to measure the welfare effect of a price change.

docs.title: Two Welfare Measures
docs.text:
When price changes, we want to measure the welfare gain or loss in dollar terms. Two theoretically rigorous measures exist:

Compensating Variation (CV): How much income must be taken from (given to) the consumer after the price change to return them to their original utility level?

  • Price falls: CV > 0 (take away income — consumer gained)
  • Price rises: CV < 0 (give income — consumer lost)
  • Uses original utility u⁰ as reference, new prices p¹

CV = e(p⁰, u⁰) - e(p¹, u⁰) = m - e(p¹, u⁰)

Equivalent Variation (EV): How much income would give the consumer the same welfare change as the price change, evaluated at original prices?

  • Price falls: EV > 0 (consumer equivalent to gaining this much income)
  • Uses new utility u¹ as reference, original prices p⁰

EV = e(p⁰, u¹) - e(p¹, u¹) = e(p⁰, u¹) - m

docs.title: CV vs EV vs Consumer Surplus Change
docs.text:
For a price fall: - EV ≥ ΔCS ≥ CV (for normal goods) - EV ≤ ΔCS ≤ CV (for inferior goods) - When income effect is zero: EV = ΔCS = CV (quasilinear preferences)

The Marshallian consumer surplus change (area under demand curve) is a convenient approximation lying between CV and EV. For small price changes or when the income effect is small, all three are close.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Carbon tax welfare cost for an Australian household

Cobb-Douglas utility: U = x₁^0.3 × x₂^0.7 x₁ = electricity (kWh), x₂ = other goods Income m = $2,000/month, p₂ = $1 Original electricity price p₁⁰ = $0.25/kWh Carbon tax raises price to p₁¹ = $0.35/kWh

Expenditure function: e(p₁, p₂, u) = u × (p₁/0.3)^0.3 × (p₂/0.7)^0.7

Original utility: u⁰ found from x₁ = 0.3m/p₁ = 0.3×2000/0.25 = 2,400 kWh x₂ = 0.7×2000/1 = 1,400 u⁰ = (2400)^0.3 × (1400)^0.7 ≈ 1,704

CV (how much can be taken away after tax to restore u⁰): e(p¹, u⁰) = minimum expenditure at new prices to reach u⁰ = u⁰ × (0.35/0.3)^0.3 × (1/0.7)^0.7 CV = m - e(p¹, u⁰) ≈ $2,000 - \(2,082 = -\)82/month

The household is $82/month worse off — the CV welfare cost of the carbon tax.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"CV and EV always give the same answer."

CV and EV coincide only when there is no income effect (quasilinear preferences). For normal goods they diverge: CV uses the old utility level (more conservative welfare loss) while EV uses the new utility level. For large price changes, the gap between them can be substantial.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• CV: income adjustment needed at NEW prices to restore ORIGINAL utility — practical for policy analysis
  • EV: income change at ORIGINAL prices equivalent to the price change — preferred for comparing multiple policies
  • For normal goods: EV ≥ ΔCS ≥ CV for a price fall; the gap grows with the size of income effects
  • Treasury and Infrastructure Australia use variants of CV/EV in cost-benefit analysis

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. For U = x₁^0.5 × x₂^0.5, m = $1,000, p₂ = $1: Price of x₁ rises from $2 to $4. Calculate CV and EV using the expenditure function e(p₁,p₂,u) = 2u√(p₁p₂).
  2. For quasilinear U = ln(x₁) + x₂, show that CV = EV = ΔCS for a price change (no income effect on x₁).
  3. A petrol excise of $0.10/L is proposed. Use CV to estimate the welfare cost per Australian household (assume average consumption 1,200 L/year and income elasticity of petrol ≈ 0.3).

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Compensating and Equivalent Variation (University of Utah) — intermediate series
  • 📺 Consumer Surplus and Welfare (MIT OCW)
  • 📚 Barreto — Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M16.L01 — Intertemporal Choice: Saving and Borrowing
docs.text: Module: Module 16 — Applications of Consumer Theory Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyze consumption decisions across time periods using the two-period budget constraint.

docs.title: Intertemporal Budget Constraint
docs.text:
The two-period budget constraint shows the tradeoff between present (c₁) and future consumption (c₂):

c₁ + c₂/(1+r) = m₁ + m₂/(1+r)

Where: - r = interest rate - m₁, m₂ = income in periods 1 and 2

In Australia (RBA cash rate target 4.35% as of March 2024), this means $1 saved today becomes $1.0435 next year. The slope of the budget line is -(1+r), showing the opportunity cost of current consumption.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian context: Suppose an Australian worker earns $50,000 this year (m₁) and $55,000 next year (m₂) with r=5%.

  1. Maximum c₁ if they borrow against all future income: c₁ = m₁ + m₂/(1+r) = 50,000 + 55,000/1.05 ≈ $102,381

  2. Maximum c₂ if they save all current income: c₂ = m₁(1+r) + m₂ = 50,000*1.05 + 55,000 = $107,500

  3. Optimal choice where MRS = (1+r)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Higher interest rates always make people save more"

Actually, for borrowers, higher rates may reduce saving as the cost of current consumption rises. In Australia, household saving ratio fell to 3.2% in Q3 2023 (ABS 5206.0) despite rate rises.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The endowment point (m₁,m₂) shows income without borrowing/saving
  • Interest rates represent the price of current vs future consumption
  • Borrowers and savers are affected differently by rate changes
  • Revealed preference can show time consistency in choices

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive the slope of the budget line from c₁ + c₂/(1+r) = m₁ + m₂/(1+r)
  2. Calculate maximum c₁ and c₂ for m₁=\(40k, m₂=\)45k, r=4%
  3. Graph how a rate rise affects a borrower vs saver

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 RBA: How Interest Rates Affect Spending
  • 📚 ABS Household Savings Ratio

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M16.L02 — Labour-Leisure Choice and Labour Supply
docs.text: Module: Module 16 — Applications of Consumer Theory Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Derive an individual's labour supply curve from utility maximization under time constraints.

docs.title: The Labour-Leisure Tradeoff
docs.text:
Assume a worker has T total hours (e.g., 168/week) to allocate between: - Leisure (R) - Labour (L = T - R)

The budget constraint is: wL + V = wT + V Where: - w = hourly wage (e.g., Australia's $23.23 minimum wage) - V = non-labour income

The slope -w shows the opportunity cost of leisure in forgone wages.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian context: Consider a worker with: - T = 100 hours/fortnight - V = $200/fortnight (Centrelink payment) - w = $25/hour

  1. If they work 60 hours: Income = 25*60 + 200 = $1700 Leisure = 40 hours

  2. Optimal choice where MRS = w: Maximize U(R, C) s.t. C = w(T-R) + V

At current Australian tax rates (19% above $18,200), the after-tax wage becomes critical.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Higher wages always increase labour supply"

Actually, when income effects dominate (at high wages), labour supply may decrease as people "buy" more leisure. Australia's workforce participation rate was 66.6% in Feb 2024 (ABS 6202.0).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The wage rate is the price of leisure
  • Labour supply curves can bend backward
  • Taxes create a wedge between gross and net wages
  • Australian Fair Work Act sets minimum conditions

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive the labour supply curve for U(R,C) = R^0.4*C^0.6
  2. Calculate optimal L for w=\(30, V=\)300, T=80
  3. Graph how a tax rate increase affects labour supply

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 ABS Labour Force Data
  • 📚 Fair Work Ombudsman

Provenance: University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: M16.L03 — Taxes and Subsidies in Consumer Theory
docs.text: Module: Module 16 — Applications of Consumer Theory Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Compare the efficiency of different tax types using consumer theory.

docs.title: Tax Effects on Budget Constraints
docs.text:
1. Quantity Tax (e.g., GST): Rotates budget line inward
  2. Example: 10% GST on good x makes px → 1.1px

  3. Slope changes from -py/px to -py/(1.1px)

  4. Lump-Sum Tax: Parallel shift inward

  5. Reduces income but preserves relative prices

  6. More efficient (less utility loss per dollar raised)

  7. Income Tax: Similar to lump-sum but percentage-based

  8. Australian marginal rates: 19%-45%

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian context: Compare two ways to raise $1000 revenue from a consumer with: - Income: $50,000 - Utility: U(x,y) = x^0.5 y^0.5 - Prices: px = $10, py = $20

  1. 10% GST on x: New px = $11 → Solve compensated demand DWL ≈ $120

  2. Lump-sum $1000: M = $49,000 → Less distortion DWL ≈ $0

Australia's GST raises ~\(75b/year vs income tax's ~\)250b (ATO 2022-23).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"All taxes equally distort consumer choices"

Actually, lump-sum taxes (though politically unpopular) are theoretically most efficient as they don't alter relative prices.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Quantity taxes distort relative prices
  • Lump-sum taxes minimize deadweight loss
  • GST vs income tax involves equity-efficiency tradeoffs
  • Australian tax mix relies heavily on income taxes

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive the DWL formula for a quantity tax
  2. Calculate equivalent variation for 10% GST on x
  3. Graph Australian GST vs UK VAT (20%)

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 ATO Tax Statistics
  • 📚 GST Policy Design

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M16.L04 — Uncertainty and Expected Utility
docs.text: Module: Module 16 — Applications of Consumer Theory Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Evaluate choices under uncertainty using the expected utility framework.

docs.title: Expected Utility Theory
docs.text:
Von Neumann-Morgenstern utility function: EU = π₁U(c₁) + π₂U(c₂) Where: - π = probabilities of states - U(c) = utility from consumption

Key properties: 1. Risk aversion: U''(c) < 0 (concave) 2. Risk neutrality: U''(c) = 0 (linear) 3. Risk loving: U''(c) > 0 (convex)

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian context: Consider flood insurance for a $500k home: - 5% chance of total loss - 95% chance no loss - Premium: $30k

  1. Expected value: EV = 0.05(-500k) + 0.95(0) = -$25k

  2. Risk premium: For U(c)=√c, EU(no insurance) = 0.050 + 0.95√500k ≈ 672.67 Certainty equivalent ≈ $452k Risk premium = $500k - $452k - $25k = $23k

Australians pay ~$12b annually in general insurance premiums (APRA 2023).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"People always choose the option with highest expected value"

Actually, risk-averse individuals may accept lower EV to reduce uncertainty (e.g., buying insurance).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Expected utility ≠ expected monetary value
  • Risk preferences determine insurance demand
  • State-contingent commodities allow hedging
  • Australian insurance markets reflect risk aversion

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Calculate EU for U(c)=ln(c) with 50% chance of \(100/\)200
  2. Find the risk premium for U(c)=c^0.5 and a 10% chance of $1m loss
  3. Graph risk aversion levels for different utility functions

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 APRA Insurance Statistics
  • 📚 RBA: Household Risk Management

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: M16.L05 — Risk Aversion and Insurance
docs.text: Module: Module 16 — Applications of Consumer Theory Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Measure risk aversion and analyze insurance market equilibrium.

docs.title: Arrow-Pratt Measure
docs.text:
Absolute risk aversion: ARA(c) = -U''(c)/U'(c)

Examples: 1. Constant ARA: U(c) = -e^{-αc} 2. Decreasing ARA: U(c) = ln(c) 3. Zero ARA: U(c) = c (risk neutral)

Australian health insurance: - 55% have private hospital cover (APRA 2023) - Medicare provides universal baseline

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
Australian context: Consider a farmer with: - Initial wealth: $200k - 20% chance of $80k drought loss - Utility: U(c) = √c

  1. Risk premium calculation: EU = 0.2√120k + 0.8√200k ≈ 440.91 CE ≈ $194,400 Risk premium = $200k - $194.4k - $16k = $9.6k

  2. Actuarially fair premium = 0.2*$80k = $16k

  3. Maximum willingness to pay ≈ $25.6k

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Insurance companies always profit from risk aversion"

Actually, adverse selection (e.g., sicker people buying more health insurance) can create market failures. Australia addresses this with: - Lifetime Health Cover loading - Private Health Insurance Rebate

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Concave utility ⇒ risk aversion
  • Insurance demand increases with risk aversion
  • Actuarially fair insurance provides full coverage
  • Australian dual system balances public/private

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Calculate ARA for U(c) = c^0.8
  2. Find the optimal insurance coverage for a $50k loss with 10% probability
  3. Graph how risk aversion affects insurance demand

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 APRA Private Health Insurance
  • 📚 Medicare Explained

Provenance: University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics | Khan Academy Microeconomics

docs.title: M17.L01 — The Edgeworth Box and Endowments
docs.text: Module: Module 17 — Exchange Economy and General Equilibrium Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 11: The Edgeworth Box. Consumer A reads from the bottom-left; Consumer B reads from the top-right. The green dashed line is the contract curve of Pareto-efficient allocations.

Construct and interpret the Edgeworth box diagram for a two-consumer, two-good exchange economy.

docs.title: The Edgeworth Box Framework
docs.text:
