The Edgeworth box is a graphical representation of an exchange economy with two consumers (A and B) and two goods (X and Y). The box's dimensions represent the total quantities of goods X (width) and Y (height) in the economy. Any point in the box represents an allocation where:

  • Consumer A gets (x_A, y_A)
  • Consumer B gets (x_B, y_B) = (X_total - x_A, Y_total - y_A)

The endowment point (ω₁, ω₂) shows the initial allocation before any trade occurs. For example, in an Australian context, consider two households:

  • Household A starts with 80% of food but only 20% of clothing
  • Household B has the remaining 20% of food and 80% of clothing

Mathematically, if total endowments are X̄ and Ȳ, then:

x_A + x_B = X̄ y_A + y_B = Ȳ

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Consider two Australian farmers trading water rights (X) and livestock (Y), with total endowments X̄ = 1000 ML, Ȳ = 500 head. Farmer A starts with ω_A = (800 ML, 100 head), Farmer B with ω_B = (200 ML, 400 head).

  1. Draw the Edgeworth box (1000 units wide, 500 tall)
  2. Plot ω at (800,100)
  3. Show indifference curves (Cobb-Douglas utility: U_A = x_A^0.6 y_A^0.4)
  4. The lens-shaped area between the indifference curves represents mutually beneficial trades

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"The endowment point must be at the center of the box"

Correction: The endowment can be anywhere in the box; its position reflects initial inequality in resource distribution, like Australia's uneven water allocations.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• The Edgeworth box visually represents all possible allocations in a two-good, two-consumer economy
  • The endowment point shows initial resource distribution before trade
  • The lens-shaped area between indifference curves shows potential Pareto improvements
  • Australian examples include water rights trading and emissions permit markets

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Given U_A = x_A y_A and U_B = x_B y_B with ω_A = (4,1), ω_B = (1,4), find the contract curve equation
  2. Draw the Edgeworth box for two Australian states trading renewable energy credits (X) and fossil fuel permits (Y)
  3. Calculate MRS for both consumers at the endowment point in Q1

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Edgeworth Box Construction
  • 📚 Water Markets in Australia

Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 | University of Utah

docs.title: M17.L02 — Pareto Efficiency and the Contract Curve
docs.text: Module: Module 17 — Exchange Economy and General Equilibrium Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Identify Pareto efficient allocations using the contract curve in an Edgeworth box.

docs.title: Pareto Efficiency Defined
docs.text:
An allocation is Pareto efficient if no reallocation can make one consumer better off without making the other worse off. In the Edgeworth box:

  1. Pareto improvements move northeast/southwest within the lens-shaped area
  2. Efficiency occurs where indifference curves are tangent (MRS_A = MRS_B)
  3. The contract curve connects all such efficient points

Australian example: The National Electricity Market achieves Pareto efficiency when state energy trades balance marginal benefits.

Mathematically, for utility functions U_A(x_A,y_A) and U_B(x_B,y_B):

∂U_A/∂x_A / ∂U_A/∂y_A = ∂U_B/∂x_B / ∂U_B/∂y_B

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Two Australian states trade electricity (X) and gas (Y) with: - U_A = ln(x_A) + 2ln(y_A) - U_B = 2ln(x_B) + ln(y_B) - Total endowments: X̄ = 3000 GWh, Ȳ = 2000 TJ

  1. Calculate MRS for both states: MRS_A = y_A/(2x_A) MRS_B = (2y_B)/x_B
  2. Set MRS_A = MRS_B and use x_B = 3000 - x_A, y_B = 2000 - y_A
  3. Solve to find contract curve: y_A = (4000x_A)/(3000 + x_A)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Any point on the contract curve is equally fair"

Correction: While all points are efficient, they represent different distributions of welfare. Australia's energy market rules explicitly consider equity alongside efficiency.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Pareto efficiency requires MRS_A = MRS_B
  • The contract curve contains all efficient allocations
  • Efficiency ≠ fairness - Australia's carbon policy debates highlight this distinction
  • Core allocations are those where no coalition can do better on their own

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Find the contract curve for U_A = x_A + y_A and U_B = min(x_B, y_B)
  2. Draw the contract curve for two Australian farmers with Cobb-Douglas utilities
  3. Explain why the core shrinks as the number of traders increases

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Pareto Optimality Visualized
  • 📚 Australia's National Electricity Market

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | MIT OCW 14.01

docs.title: M17.L03 — Competitive Equilibrium in an Exchange Economy
docs.text: Module: Module 17 — Exchange Economy and General Equilibrium Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Determine competitive equilibrium prices and allocations in a pure exchange economy.

docs.title: Walrasian Equilibrium
docs.text:
In a competitive exchange economy:

  1. Consumers take prices (p_x, p_y) as given
  2. Markets clear when supply = demand for both goods
  3. Walras' Law: If n-1 markets clear, the nth must also clear
  4. Equilibrium allocation is Pareto efficient (First Welfare Theorem)

Australian context: The Emissions Reduction Fund creates a market where p_x = price per ton CO₂, with equilibrium emerging from bid/ask spreads.

Mathematically, solve:

  1. Consumer A's problem: max U_A(x_A,y_A) s.t. p_x x_A + p_y y_A = p_x ω_xA + p_y ω_yA
  2. Consumer B's problem: similarly
  3. Market clearing: x_A + x_B = X̄, y_A + y_B = Ȳ

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Two regions trade renewable energy (X) and coal (Y): - U_A = x_A^0.7 y_A^0.3, ω_A = (100,50) - U_B = x_B^0.3 y_B^0.7, ω_B = (50,100) - Total endowments: X̄ = 150, Ȳ = 150

  1. Set p_y = 1 (numeraire), find p_x
  2. Solve demand functions: x_A = 0.7(100p_x + 50)/p_x y_A = 0.3(100p_x + 50)
  3. Market clearing: x_A + x_B = 150 → p_x = 1
  4. Equilibrium allocation: x_A = 105, y_A = 45, x_B = 45, y_B = 105

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Equilibrium prices are always positive"

Correction: Free goods can have zero price (e.g., Australia's solar surplus periods when p_x ≈ 0).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Competitive equilibrium requires market clearing and utility maximization
  • Walras' Law simplifies finding equilibrium prices
  • Numeraire normalization reduces degrees of freedom
  • Australian energy markets show real-world price discovery

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Find equilibrium prices for U_A = min(x_A, y_A), U_B = x_B + y_B with ω_A = (2,0), ω_B = (0,2)
  2. Derive excess demand functions for the worked example
  3. Explain how Australia's Renewable Energy Target affects equilibrium prices

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Walrasian Equilibrium Explained
  • 📚 Australia's Emissions Reduction Fund

Provenance: University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M17.L04 — The First and Second Welfare Theorems
docs.text: Module: Module 17 — Exchange Economy and General Equilibrium Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain the conditions under which competitive markets achieve Pareto efficiency.

docs.title: The Fundamental Welfare Theorems
docs.text:
1. First Welfare Theorem: Any competitive equilibrium is Pareto efficient
  2. Assumes: no externalities, perfect information, complete markets

  3. Australian example: National Electricity Market achieves efficiency under normal conditions

  4. Second Welfare Theorem: Any efficient allocation can be decentralized via competitive markets after appropriate lump-sum transfers

  5. Requires: convex preferences, no wealth effects
  6. Australian application: Carbon tax revenue redistribution

Mathematically, the theorems rely on: - Separation of efficiency and equity - Convexity of preference sets - Price-taking behavior

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Consider two welfare states: 1. Initial unequal endowments lead to equilibrium at point E1 2. Social planner prefers more equitable allocation E2 on contract curve 3. Implement lump-sum transfer from rich to poor (e.g., Australia's GST redistribution) 4. New equilibrium E2 is both efficient and equitable

Calculus: For U_A = x_A^α y_A^(1-α), U_B = x_B^β y_B^(1-β), the transfer must satisfy:

(p_x Δx + p_y Δy)_A = -(p_x Δx + p_y Δy)_B

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"The Second Theorem means we can always redistribute without efficiency costs"

Correction: Real-world taxes distort behavior (e.g., Australia's debate over dividend imputation).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• First Theorem: markets → efficiency under ideal conditions
  • Second Theorem: redistribution + markets → any desired efficient outcome
  • Australian policy debates often center on these theorems' limitations
  • Practical applications require careful attention to assumptions

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Show why the First Theorem fails with externalities
  2. Design lump-sum transfers to achieve a specific efficient allocation
  3. Analyze how Australia's Medicare levy affects the theorems' applicability

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Welfare Theorems Visualized
  • 📚 Australia's GST Distribution

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M17.L05 — Market Failure and the Limits of the First Welfare Theorem
docs.text: Module: Module 17 — Exchange Economy and General Equilibrium Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Identify situations where competitive markets fail to achieve Pareto efficiency.

docs.title: When Markets Fail
docs.text:
The First Welfare Theorem's assumptions often fail in reality:

  1. Externalities: Unpriced side effects (e.g., Australia's coal seam gas water contamination)
  2. Social cost > private cost → overproduction

  3. Solution: Pigovian taxes (Australia's carbon pricing)

  4. Public Goods: Non-excludable, non-rival (e.g., national defense, clean air)

  5. Free rider problem → underprovision

  6. Australian example: Public broadcasting (ABC)

  7. Market Power: Monopolies distort prices (e.g., Australia's banking oligopoly)

  8. MR ≠ P → deadweight loss
  9. Remedy: ACCC regulation

Mathematically, externalities create divergence between private and social MRS.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Coal power externality: 1. Private MC = $30/MWh 2. Social MC = $50/MWh (includes health costs) 3. Competitive equilibrium: Q_market where P = $30 4. Efficient quantity: Q where P = $50 5. Deadweight loss = ∫_{Q}^{Q_market} (50 - 30) dQ

Policy options: - Carbon tax of $20/MWh - Cap-and-trade (Australia's Safeguard Mechanism)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Government intervention always improves efficiency"

Correction: Government failure can worsen outcomes (e.g., Australia's home insulation program).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Market failures are pervasive in real economies
  • Australian policy provides concrete examples of each failure type
  • The Second Theorem suggests redistribution + markets can address equity
  • Policy design must account for potential government failures

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Calculate deadweight loss from a monopoly with given demand and cost curves
  2. Design a Pigovian tax for Australian agricultural runoff
  3. Compare Australia's NBN rollout with public goods theory predictions

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Market Failure Explained
  • 📚 ACCC Market Studies

Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto)

docs.title: M18.L01 — Technology and Production Sets
docs.text: Module: Module 18 — Technology, Production, and Profit Maximisation Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Understand the concept of a production set and how it defines feasible input-output combinations.

docs.title: Production Sets and Functions
docs.text: A production set represents all feasible combinations of inputs (e.g., labor, capital) and outputs (e.g., goods). For two inputs \(x_1\) and \(x_2\), the production function \(y = f(x_1, x_2)\) maps inputs to output. Isoquants are curves showing all input combinations yielding the same output level.

docs.title: Key Properties:
docs.text:
• Marginal Rate of Technical Substitution (MRTS): The rate at which one input can be substituted for another while maintaining output: \(MRTS = -\frac{dx_2}{dx_1} = \frac{MP_1}{MP_2}\), where \(MP_i\) is the marginal product of input \(i\).
  • Returns to Scale: Describes how output scales with inputs:
  • Increasing (IRS): \(f(tx_1, tx_2) > tf(x_1, x_2)\)
  • Constant (CRS): \(f(tx_1, tx_2) = tf(x_1, x_2)\)
  • Decreasing (DRS): \(f(tx_1, tx_2) < tf(x_1, x_2)\)
  • Cobb-Douglas: A common functional form: \(f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^a x_2^b\).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Suppose an Australian wheat farm uses labor (\(x_1\)) and machinery (\(x_2\)) with the production function \(y = 2x_1^{0.5} x_2^{0.5}\) (Cobb-Douglas). 1. Calculate MRTS: [ MP_1 = \frac{\partial y}{\partial x_1} = x_1^{-0.5} x_2^{0.5}, \quad MP_2 = \frac{\partial y}{\partial x_2} = x_1^{0.5} x_2^{-0.5} ] [ MRTS = \frac{MP_1}{MP_2} = \frac{x_2}{x_1} ] 2. Returns to Scale: [ f(tx_1, tx_2) = 2(tx_1)^{0.5} (tx_2)^{0.5} = t \cdot 2x_1^{0.5} x_2^{0.5} = t f(x_1, x_2) \quad \text{(CRS)} ]

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Isoquants show output levels; higher isoquants mean higher output."

Isoquants represent equal output levels. Higher output is represented by moving to a new isoquant.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Production functions map inputs to outputs, and isoquants represent equal output levels.
  • MRTS measures the substitutability of inputs while holding output constant (slope of isoquant).
  • Cobb-Douglas functions exhibit CRS if exponents sum to 1.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. For \(y = x_1^{0.3} x_2^{0.7}\), find MRTS and returns to scale.
  2. Sketch the isoquant for \(y = 10\) with \(x_1\) on the x-axis and \(x_2\) on the y-axis.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 MIT: Production Functions
  • 📚 Cobb-Douglas in Australian Agriculture

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M18.L02 — Isoquants, Returns to Scale, and Technical Efficiency
docs.text: Module: Module 18 — Technology, Production, and Profit Maximisation Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyze isoquant shapes and returns to scale properties for different production technologies.

docs.title: Isoquant Shapes and Technologies
docs.text: Isoquants vary by production technology: 1. Perfect Substitutes: Linear isoquants (constant MRTS). Example: \(y = ax_1 + bx_2\). 2. Perfect Complements (Leontief): L-shaped isoquants. Example: \(y = \min(ax_1, bx_2)\). 3. Cobb-Douglas: Convex isoquants. Example: \(y = x_1^a x_2^b\).

docs.title: Returns to Scale:
docs.text:
• CRS: \(f(tx_1, tx_2) = tf(x_1, x_2)\) (e.g., Cobb-Douglas with \(a + b = 1\)).
  • IRS: \(f(tx_1, tx_2) > tf(x_1, x_2)\) (e.g., \(a + b > 1\)).
  • DRS: \(f(tx_1, tx_2) < tf(x_1, x_2)\) (e.g., \(a + b < 1\)).

Euler’s Theorem: For homogeneous functions, \(x_1 \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} + x_2 \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = k f(x_1, x_2)\), where \(k\) is the degree of homogeneity.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

An Australian solar panel manufacturer uses \(y = x_1^{0.6} x_2^{0.6}\): 1. Returns to Scale: [ f(tx_1, tx_2) = (tx_1)^{0.6} (tx_2)^{0.6} = t^{1.2} x_1^{0.6} x_2^{0.6} = t^{1.2} f(x_1, x_2) \quad \text{(IRS)} ] 2. Euler’s Theorem: [ x_1 (0.6 x_1^{-0.4} x_2^{0.6}) + x_2 (0.6 x_1^{0.6} x_2^{-0.4}) = 1.2 f(x_1, x_2) ]

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"All Cobb-Douglas functions exhibit CRS."

Only if exponents sum to 1. Here, \(0.6 + 0.6 = 1.2\) implies IRS.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Isoquant shape reveals input substitutability (linear → perfect substitutes).
  • Returns to scale depend on the sum of exponents in Cobb-Douglas.
  • Euler’s theorem links marginal products to total output for homogeneous functions.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Classify returns to scale for \(y = 2x_1 + 3x_2\).
  2. Sketch isoquants for \(y = \min(x_1, 2x_2)\).

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 University of Utah: Isoquants
  • 📚 Returns to Scale in Australian Manufacturing

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M18.L03 — Cost Minimisation: The Isocost-Isoquant Approach
docs.text: Module: Module 18 — Technology, Production, and Profit Maximisation Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 12: Cost Minimisation. The firm minimises cost at the tangency of the isocost line and isoquant — where MRTS = w/r.

Derive cost-minimizing input combinations using isocost-isoquant tangency.

docs.title: Cost Minimisation
docs.text: Given input prices \(w_1\) and \(w_2\), the firm minimizes cost \(C = w_1x_1 + w_2x_2\) subject to producing output \(y\).

docs.title: Key Concepts:
docs.text:
1. Isocost Line: \(w_1x_1 + w_2x_2 = C\). Slope = \(-w_1/w_2\).
  2. Tangency Condition: MRTS = input price ratio: \(\frac{MP_1}{MP_2} = \frac{w_1}{w_2}\).
  3. Conditional Factor Demands: \(x_1^*(w_1, w_2, y)\) and \(x_2^*(w_1, w_2, y)\) solve the cost-minimization problem.
  4. Cost Function: \(C(w_1, w_2, y)\) is the minimized cost.
  5. Shephard’s Lemma: \(\frac{\partial C}{\partial w_i} = x_i^*\).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

An Australian brewery uses labor (\(x_1\)) and barley (\(x_2\)) with \(y = x_1^{0.5}x_2^{0.5}\). Input prices: \(w_1 = 20\) AUD/hour, \(w_2 = 5\) AUD/kg. 1. Tangency Condition: [ \frac{MP_1}{MP_2} = \frac{x_2}{x_1} = \frac{w_1}{w_2} = 4 \implies x_2 = 4x_1 ] 2. Substitute into Production Function: [ y = x_1^{0.5}(4x_1)^{0.5} = 2x_1 \implies x_1^ = \frac{y}{2}, \quad x_2^ = 2y ] 3. Cost Function: [ C = 20 \left(\frac{y}{2}\right) + 5 (2y) = 20y ]

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Cost minimization always occurs where MRTS = price ratio."

Only true for interior solutions. Corner solutions (e.g., perfect substitutes) may not satisfy tangency.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Cost minimization requires equating MRTS to input price ratio.
  • Shephard’s Lemma links cost function derivatives to conditional factor demands.
  • Cobb-Douglas production leads to linear conditional demands in output.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. For \(y = x_1 + x_2\) and \(w_1 = 2\), \(w_2 = 3\), find cost-minimizing inputs.
  2. Derive Shephard’s Lemma for \(C = 2w_1^{0.5}w_2^{0.5}y\).

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 MIT: Cost Minimization
  • 📚 Brewery Cost Analysis in Australia

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M18.L04 — Profit Maximisation and the Competitive Firm
docs.text: Module: Module 18 — Technology, Production, and Profit Maximisation Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Derive profit-maximizing conditions for a competitive firm using calculus.

docs.title: Profit Maximisation
docs.text: The firm maximizes profit \(\pi = py - w_1x_1 - w_2x_2\), where \(p\) is output price.

docs.title: Key Conditions:
docs.text:
1. Input Side: \(p \times MP_i = w_i\) for each input \(i\).
  2. Output Side: \(p = MC\) (marginal cost).
  3. Supply Function: \(y^*(p, w_1, w_2)\) solves the profit-maximization problem.
  4. Hotelling’s Lemma: \(\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial p} = y^*\).
  5. Profit Function Properties:
  6. Non-decreasing in \(p\).
  7. Non-increasing in input prices.
  8. Convex in prices.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

An Australian winery has \(y = x_1^{0.4}x_2^{0.4}\), with \(w_1 = 10\) AUD, \(w_2 = 5\) AUD, and \(p = 20\) AUD/bottle. 1. Profit Maximization: [ p \times MP_1 = 20 (0.4 x_1^{-0.6} x_2^{0.4}) = 10 \implies x_2 = 2x_1 ] [ p \times MP_2 = 20 (0.4 x_1^{0.4} x_2^{-0.6}) = 5 \implies x_1 = 2x_2 ] Contradiction implies corner solution or DRS adjustment. 2. Supply Function (Simplified): If \(y = x_1^{0.5}x_2^{0.5}\) (CRS), supply is infinitely elastic at \(p = MC\).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Profit maximization always occurs where \(p = MC\)."

Only true if MC is increasing. For CRS, profits are zero or infinite.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Profit maximization equates the value of marginal product to input prices.
  • Hotelling’s Lemma links profit sensitivity to output supply.
  • CRS technologies lead to zero profits in competitive equilibrium.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. For \(y = x_1^{0.3}x_2^{0.3}\) and \(w_1 = w_2 = 1\), find \(y^*(p)\).
  2. Verify Hotelling’s Lemma for \(\pi = p^2/4w\).

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 University of Utah: Profit Maximization
  • 📚 Wine Industry Profit Analysis

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M18.L05 — Short-Run and Long-Run Firm Behaviour
docs.text: Module: Module 18 — Technology, Production, and Profit Maximisation Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Compare short-run and long-run cost curves and firm supply decisions.

docs.title: Short-Run vs. Long-Run
docs.text:
1. Short-Run: At least one input is fixed (e.g., capital).
  2. Cost Minimization: Constrained by fixed input.

  3. Supply Decision: Produce if \(p \geq AVC\); shutdown otherwise.

  4. Long-Run: All inputs are variable.

  5. Cost Curves: LRAC is the envelope of SRAC curves.
  6. Supply: More elastic than short-run (Le Chatelier principle).

docs.title: Key Relationships:
docs.text:
• Shutdown vs. Exit:
  • Short-run: Shutdown if \(p < AVC\).
  • Long-run: Exit if \(p < AC\).
  • Le Chatelier Principle: LR supply is more price-responsive than SR supply.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

An Australian dairy farm has: - Short-Run: \(C_{SR}(y) = 100 + 2y^2\) (fixed cost = 100 AUD). - Long-Run: \(C_{LR}(y) = 3y^2\).

  1. Short-Run Supply: [ MC_{SR} = 4y = p \implies y_{SR}^*(p) = \frac{p}{4} ] (For \(p \geq AVC_{min} = 0\)).

  2. Long-Run Supply: [ MC_{LR} = 6y = p \implies y_{LR}^*(p) = \frac{p}{6} ] More elastic than SR supply.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Firms always produce at minimum ATC in the short-run."

Only true in long-run equilibrium. Short-run production may deviate due to fixed inputs.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Short-run decisions are constrained by fixed inputs; long-run allows full adjustment.
  • LRAC envelopes SRAC curves, reflecting flexibility.
  • Le Chatelier principle: LR responses are more elastic than SR.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. For \(C_{SR}(y) = 50 + y^2\), find the shutdown price.
  2. Sketch SRAC and LRAC for a technology with economies of scale.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 MIT: Short-Run vs. Long-Run
  • 📚 Dairy Farm Cost Structures in Australia

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M19.L01 — Cost Functions: Short-Run and Long-Run
docs.text: Module: Module 19 — Costs, Competitive Supply, and Industry Equilibrium Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Understand the derivation and properties of short-run and long-run cost functions.

docs.title: Cost Functions: Short-Run and Long-Run
docs.text:
In microeconomics, firms face different cost structures depending on the time horizon. In the short-run, at least one input (typically capital, \(k\)) is fixed, leading to the short-run cost function \(c(w, y, \overline{k})\), where \(w\) is the vector of input prices, \(y\) is output, and \(\overline{k}\) is fixed capital. In the long-run, all inputs are variable, so the cost function is \(c(w, y)\).

The long-run cost function serves as an "envelope" of the short-run cost functions, representing the minimal cost for each output level when all inputs are adjustable. Key properties include: - Non-decreasing in input prices (\(w\)): Higher input prices cannot lower costs. - Homogeneous degree 1 in \(w\): Doubling all input prices doubles total cost. - Concave in \(w\): Cost functions exhibit diminishing marginal costs of input price changes.

Shephard's Lemma states that the derivative of the cost function with respect to an input price gives the conditional demand for that input: \(\frac{\partial c}{\partial w_i} = x_i^*(w, y)\).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Consider a Sydney-based firm producing coffee with a Cobb-Douglas production function \(y = k^{0.5} l^{0.5}\). In the short-run, capital is fixed at \(\overline{k} = 100\). The wage rate is \(w_l = 20\) AUD/hour, and the rental rate is \(w_k = 10\) AUD/hour.

  1. Short-run cost function: Solve for labor demand \(l\) from the production function: \(l = \frac{y^2}{100}\). Then, \(c(w, y, \overline{k}) = w_l l + w_k \overline{k} = 20 \cdot \frac{y^2}{100} + 10 \cdot 100 = 0.2 y^2 + 1000\).

  2. Long-run cost function: Minimize \(c = w_l l + w_k k\) subject to \(y = k^{0.5} l^{0.5}\). The solution is \(k = y \sqrt{\frac{w_l}{w_k}}\) and \(l = y \sqrt{\frac{w_k}{w_l}}\), leading to \(c(w, y) = 2y \sqrt{w_l w_k} = 2y \sqrt{200} \approx 28.28 y\).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
**"Long-run costs are always lower than short-run costs because firms can adjust all inputs."

Correction: Long-run costs are never higher than short-run costs (due to flexibility), but they can be equal if the fixed input in the short-run is already at its optimal long-run level.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Short-run cost functions include fixed inputs; long-run functions do not.
  • The long-run cost function is the lower envelope of short-run cost functions.
  • Shephard's Lemma links cost functions to conditional input demands.
  • Cost functions are concave and homogeneous in input prices.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive the short-run cost function for a firm with \(y = 2k^{0.3} l^{0.7}\), \(\overline{k} = 50\), \(w_l = 15\), and \(w_k = 10\).
  2. Explain why the long-run cost function is concave in input prices.
  3. Calculate the conditional labor demand using Shephard's Lemma for \(c(w, y) = 5y w_l^{0.4} w_k^{0.6}\).

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Short-Run vs. Long-Run Costs
  • 📚 Cost Minimization in Microeconomics

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M19.L02 — The Competitive Firm's Supply Decision
docs.text: Module: Module 19 — Costs, Competitive Supply, and Industry Equilibrium Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Derive a competitive firm's supply function from profit maximization.

docs.title: The Competitive Firm's Supply Decision
docs.text:
In a perfectly competitive market, firms are price takers and maximize profit by choosing output \(y\) such that price \(p\) equals marginal cost \(MC\): \(p = MC(y, w)\). The supply function \(y^*(p, w)\) is derived from this condition.

Key properties of the supply function: - Non-decreasing in \(p\): Higher prices incentivize greater output. - Homogeneous degree 0 in \(p, w\): Doubling all prices leaves output unchanged.

The profit function \(\pi(p, w)\) represents maximal profits and is convex in \(p\). Hotelling's Lemma states that \(\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial p} = y^*(p, w)\), linking profit to supply.

Comparative statics: How does \(y^*\) change with \(p\) and \(w\)? - \(\frac{\partial y^*}{\partial p} \geq 0\) (upward-sloping supply). - \(\frac{\partial y^*}{\partial w} \leq 0\) (higher input prices reduce supply).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

A Melbourne bakery produces bread with cost function \(c(y) = 2y^2 + 10\). The market price is \(p = 24\) AUD/loaf.

  1. Supply decision: Set \(p = MC\), where \(MC = \frac{dc}{dy} = 4y\). Thus, \(24 = 4y\) ⇒ \(y^* = 6\).
  2. Profit: \(\pi = py - c(y) = 24 \cdot 6 - (2 \cdot 6^2 + 10) = 144 - 82 = 62\) AUD.
  3. Hotelling's Lemma: Verify \(\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial p} = y^* = 6\).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
**"Firms always profit in competitive markets."

Correction: Firms earn zero economic profit in long-run equilibrium due to free entry/exit. Short-run profits can be positive, negative, or zero.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Competitive firms supply where \(p = MC\).
  • Supply functions are non-decreasing in price and homogeneous degree 0.
  • Hotelling's Lemma links profit and supply functions.
  • Higher input prices reduce supply.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Find the supply function for \(c(y) = y^3 + 5y + 10\).
  2. Show that \(y^*(p, w)\) is homogeneous degree 0.
  3. Calculate \(\frac{\partial y^*}{\partial w}\) for \(c(y) = w y^2\).

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Profit Maximization in Competitive Markets
  • 📚 MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M19.L03 — Industry Supply and Partial Equilibrium
docs.text: Module: Module 19 — Costs, Competitive Supply, and Industry Equilibrium Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyze industry supply and partial equilibrium under different cost conditions.

docs.title: Industry Supply and Partial Equilibrium
docs.text:
Short-run industry supply is the horizontal sum of individual firms' marginal cost curves (above AVC). In the long-run, supply depends on entry/exit: - Constant-cost industry: Horizontal long-run supply (LRS) curve (e.g., identical firms with unlimited input availability). - Increasing-cost industry: Upward-sloping LRS (e.g., specialized inputs become scarce as industry expands). - Decreasing-cost industry: Downward-sloping LRS (e.g., external economies of scale like infrastructure improvements).

Partial equilibrium analysis studies market equilibrium (supply = demand) for one good, ignoring other markets.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Consider the Australian wine industry. In the short-run, 100 identical wineries each have \(MC = 2y\). Market demand is \(D(p) = 1000 - 10p\).

  1. Short-run supply: Each firm supplies \(y = p/2\), so industry supply is \(S(p) = 100 \cdot p/2 = 50p\).
  2. Equilibrium: Set \(S(p) = D(p)\): \(50p = 1000 - 10p\) ⇒ \(p = 16.67\), \(y = 833.33\).
  3. Long-run: If wine production increases labor costs (increasing-cost industry), LRS slopes upward.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
**"Industry supply curves are always upward-sloping."

Correction: Long-run supply can be horizontal (constant-cost) or downward-sloping (decreasing-cost).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Short-run industry supply sums firms' MC curves.
  • Long-run supply depends on entry/exit and cost conditions.
  • Partial equilibrium analyzes one market in isolation.
  • External economies can cause decreasing-cost industries.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive the short-run industry supply for 50 firms with \(MC = 0.5y + 2\).
  2. Sketch LRS curves for constant-, increasing-, and decreasing-cost industries.
  3. Explain why the Australian mining industry might be increasing-cost.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Industry Supply in Competitive Markets
  • 📚 Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

docs.title: M19.L04 — Producer Surplus and Profit in Competitive Markets
docs.text: Module: Module 19 — Costs, Competitive Supply, and Industry Equilibrium Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Measure producer surplus and its relationship to profit in competitive markets.

docs.title: Producer Surplus and Profit
docs.text:
Producer surplus (PS) is the area above the supply curve and below the price: \(PS = TR - \text{Variable Costs}\). It equals total revenue minus the minimum amount firms would accept to produce \(y\).

In competitive markets: - \(PS = \pi + \text{Fixed Costs}\) (since \(\pi = TR - \text{Total Costs}\)). - Welfare analysis: Total surplus (consumer + producer) measures gains from trade. Competitive markets maximize total surplus (efficient).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

A Brisbane firm has cost function \(c(y) = y^2 + 10\) and faces price \(p = 12\).

  1. Supply: \(MC = 2y = p\) ⇒ \(y^* = 6\).
  2. PS: Area above \(MC\) and below \(p\) is \(\int_0^6 (12 - 2y) dy = 12y - y^2 \big|_0^6 = 36\).
  3. Profit: \(\pi = 12 \cdot 6 - (6^2 + 10) = 26\), so \(PS = 26 + 10 = 36\) (matches integral).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
**"Producer surplus equals profit."

Correction: Only true if fixed costs are zero; otherwise, \(PS = \pi + \text{Fixed Costs}\).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Producer surplus is the area above supply and below price.
  • PS equals profit plus fixed costs.
  • Competitive markets maximize total surplus.
  • Efficiency requires \(P = MC\), which happens in competitive equilibrium.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Compute PS and \(\pi\) for \(c(y) = 0.5y^2 + 5\) at \(p = 8\).
  2. Why does producer surplus include fixed costs?
  3. Show that total surplus is maximized when \(P = MC\).

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Producer and Consumer Surplus
  • 📚 MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

Provenance: MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M19.L05 — Long-Run Industry Equilibrium and Zero Profit
docs.text: Module: Module 19 — Costs, Competitive Supply, and Industry Equilibrium Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Explain zero-profit conditions in long-run competitive equilibrium.

docs.title: Long-Run Industry Equilibrium and Zero Profit
docs.text:
In the long-run, free entry/exit drives economic profit to zero: - Firms produce at minimum long-run average total cost (\(P = \min LRATC\)). - The efficient scale is the output where \(LRATC\) is minimized. - Industry size adjusts until \(P = LRATC\) (zero economic profit).

Rent and quasi-rent: - Economic rent: Payments to inputs above opportunity cost. - Quasi-rent: Short-run returns to fixed inputs (e.g., machinery).

Zero economic profit ≠ zero accounting profit: Accountants exclude opportunity costs (e.g., owner's time).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Australian cafes face \(LRATC = y^2 - 10y + 30\). Market demand is \(D(p) = 200 - 2p\).

  1. Efficient scale: Minimize \(LRATC\): \(\frac{d}{dy} LRATC = 2y - 10 = 0\) ⇒ \(y = 5\), \(\min LRATC = 5^2 - 10 \cdot 5 + 30 = 5\).
  2. Equilibrium: \(P = 5\), \(D(5) = 200 - 10 = 190\), so \(\# \text{firms} = \frac{190}{5} = 38\).
  3. Quasi-rent: If \(P = 8\), each cafe earns \(\pi = 8 \cdot 5 - (5^2 - 10 \cdot 5 + 30) = 15\) (quasi-rent).

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
**"Zero profit means firms are barely surviving."

Correction: Zero economic profit means firms earn normal returns (covering opportunity costs). Accounting profits may be positive.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Long-run equilibrium: \(P = \min LRATC\) and \(\pi = 0\).
  • Efficient scale minimizes \(LRATC\).
  • Rent compensates for input scarcity; quasi-rent is short-run surplus.
  • Zero economic profit ≠ zero accounting profit.

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Find the efficient scale for \(LRATC = 0.2y^2 - 4y + 25\).
  2. Explain why Australian agriculture might have zero economic profit but positive accounting profit.
  3. How does quasi-rent differ from economic rent?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Long-Run Competitive Equilibrium
  • 📚 Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine)

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M20.L01 — Monopoly: Formal Treatment and Welfare Loss
docs.text: Module: Module 20 — Monopoly and Game Theory (Advanced) Lesson: L01 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Derive monopoly profit maximization conditions using calculus and analyze welfare loss.

docs.title: Formal Monopoly Analysis
docs.text:
Consider a monopolist facing inverse demand function P(y) = a - by. The firm's cost function is C(y) = cy + F. Profit maximization requires MR = MC:

π(y) = P(y)y - C(y) = (a - by)y - cy - F

dπ/dy = a - 2by - c = 0 → y* = (a - c)/2b

Australian example: Suppose a local electricity provider faces P(y) = 100 - 0.5y with C(y) = 20y + 500. Then:

y = (100 - 20)/1 = 80 units P = 100 - 0.5(80) = $60

Lerner Index: L = (60 - 20)/60 = 0.67

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Sydney Water monopoly faces P(y) = 120 - y with MC = $30. Calculate: 1. Profit-maximizing output 2. Deadweight loss

Solution: 1. MR = 120 - 2y = 30 → y_m = 45 P_m = 120 - 45 = $75 2. Competitive output where P=MC: 120 - y = 30 → y_c = 90 DWL = 0.5×(75-30)×(90-45) = $1,012.50

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Monopolies always produce less than competitive markets"

While typically true, this ignores durable goods monopolies (Coase conjecture) where monopoly may produce competitively to prevent future competition.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• MR = P + y(dP/dy) reflects quantity effect on revenue
  • Lerner index L = (P-MC)/P measures market power
  • DWL exists because P > MC reduces total surplus
  • Durable goods monopolies face time inconsistency problems

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive MR for P(y) = k/y (constant elasticity demand)
  2. Calculate DWL when P(y)=50-0.2y, MC=10, F=200
  3. Explain why L = -1/ε using calculus

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Monopoly and Welfare Loss
  • 📚 Monopoly Theory

Provenance: Barreto | MIT OCW

docs.title: M20.L02 — Price Discrimination: Formal Analysis
docs.text: Module: Module 20 — Monopoly and Game Theory (Advanced) Lesson: L02 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Formulate optimal price discrimination conditions using calculus.

docs.title: Third-Degree Price Discrimination
docs.text:
A monopolist serving two markets (1,2) maximizes:

π = P₁(y₁)y₁ + P₂(y₂)y₂ - C(y₁+y₂)

FOCs: ∂π/∂y₁ = MR₁ - MC = 0 ∂π/∂y₂ = MR₂ - MC = 0

Thus MR₁ = MR₂ = MC

Using MR = P(1 - 1/|ε|), we get: P₁/P₂ = (1 - 1/|ε₂|)/(1 - 1/|ε₁|)

Australian example: Qantas charges business travelers (ε = 1.2) 3× tourist class fares (ε = 3).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

An Australian textbook publisher sells to: 1. Students: P₁ = 50 - 0.5q₁, ε₁ = 1.5 2. Libraries: P₂ = 200 - 2q₂, ε₂ = 1.2 MC = $10 per book. Find optimal prices.

Solution: P₁(1 - 1/1.5) = P₂(1 - 1/1.2) = 10 → P₁ = 10/(1/3) = $30 → P₂ = 10/(1/6) = $60

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Price discrimination always reduces welfare"

Third-degree PD may increase total output, potentially improving welfare when it brings output closer to competitive levels.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• First-degree PD extracts all CS (P=MWTP)
  • Third-degree PD sets MR equal across markets
  • Two-part tariffs combine access fee with per-unit price
  • Welfare effects depend on output changes

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive the two-part tariff solution when P=100-Q, MC=20
  2. Calculate optimal prices for ε₁=2, ε₂=4 when MC=15
  3. Explain why first-degree PD achieves competitive output

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Price Discrimination Explained
  • 📚 Advanced Pricing Strategies

Provenance: Curtis and Irvine | Utah Economics

docs.title: M20.L03 — Static Games: Nash Equilibrium and Dominant Strategies
docs.text: Module: Module 20 — Monopoly and Game Theory (Advanced) Lesson: L03 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Identify Nash equilibria in strategic form games using best response analysis.

docs.title: Nash Equilibrium Definition
docs.text:
A strategy profile (s₁, s₂) is a Nash Equilibrium if:

∀i, uᵢ(sᵢ, s₋ᵢ) ≥ uᵢ(sᵢ, s₋ᵢ*) for all sᵢ ∈ Sᵢ

Australian example: Two mining firms (BHP, Rio Tinto) choosing output:

Rio: Low Rio: High BHP: Low 50, 50 30, 60 BHP: High 60, 30 40, 40

NE: (High, High) - mutual best responses

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Find all NE in this Sydney cafe pricing game:

Cafe B: $4 Cafe B: $5 Cafe A: $4 30, 30 50, 20 Cafe A: $5 20, 50 40, 40

Solution: 1. BR for A: If B=$4 → \(4 (30>20); If B=\)5 → \(4 (50>40) 2. BR for B: If A=\)4 → \(4 (30>20); If A=\)5 → \(4 (50>40) Only NE: (\)4, $4)

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Nash equilibrium means best possible outcome"

NE only means no unilateral incentive to deviate - often suboptimal (e.g., Prisoner's Dilemma).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• NE requires mutual best responses
  • Dominant strategies always played in NE
  • Iterated elimination finds NE in dominance-solvable games
  • Coordination games often have multiple NE

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Find all NE in: | | L | R | |------|---|---| | U | 3,2 | 1,1 | | D | 0,0 | 2,3 |
  2. Explain why (D,R) is NE in the above
  3. Construct a 2×2 game with exactly two pure NE

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Nash Equilibrium Visualized
  • 📚 Game Theory Basics

Provenance: Barreto | MIT OCW

docs.title: M20.L04 — Mixed Strategies and Existence of Nash Equilibrium
docs.text: Module: Module 20 — Monopoly and Game Theory (Advanced) Lesson: L04 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Compute mixed strategy Nash equilibria using indifference conditions.

docs.title: Mixed Strategy Equilibrium
docs.text:
Player 1 plays U with probability p, D with (1-p) Player 2 plays L with probability q, R with (1-q)

Equilibrium requires: 1. Player 2's mix makes Player 1 indifferent: q×u₁(U,L) + (1-q)×u₁(U,R) = q×u₁(D,L) + (1-q)×u₁(D,R) 2. Player 1's mix makes Player 2 indifferent: p×u₂(U,L) + (1-p)×u₂(D,L) = p×u₂(U,R) + (1-p)×u₂(D,R)

Australian example: Tennis serve (Federer vs Djokovic at Australian Open):

Returner: L Returner: R Server: L 80, 20 30, 70 Server: R 20, 80 70, 30

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Find mixed NE in this AFL penalty kick game:

Goalie: L Goalie: R Kicker: L 0.7, 0.3 0.9, 0.1 Kicker: R 0.8, 0.2 0.5, 0.5

Solution: 1. Goalie indifferent when: 0.7p + 0.8(1-p) = 0.9p + 0.5(1-p) → p = 0.6 2. Kicker indifferent when: 0.3q + 0.2(1-q) = 0.1q + 0.5(1-q) → q = 0.375 NE: Kicker aims L 60%, Goalie dives L 37.5%

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Mixed strategies mean players randomize because they're uncertain"

Players randomize precisely to make opponents indifferent - a strategic requirement, not a reflection of uncertainty.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Mixed NE requires making opponents indifferent
  • Probability weights balance expected payoffs
  • Nash proved all finite games have at least one NE (possibly mixed)
  • Applications in sports, security, auctions

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Find mixed NE in: | | L | R | |------|---|---| | U | 2,1 | 0,0 | | D | 0,0 | 1,2 |
  2. Prove p=2/3, q=1/3 is NE in the above
  3. Explain why matching pennies has no pure NE

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium
  • 📚 Advanced Game Theory

Provenance: Curtis and Irvine | Utah Economics

docs.title: M20.L05 — Sequential Games: Backward Induction and Stackelberg
docs.text: Module: Module 20 — Monopoly and Game Theory (Advanced) Lesson: L05 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Solve sequential games using backward induction to find subgame perfect equilibria.

docs.title: Stackelberg Model
docs.text:
Leader (L) moves first, then follower (F). Solve backward:

  1. F's problem: max π_F(y_F; y_L) → reaction function y_F(y_L)
  2. L anticipates F's reaction: max π_L(y_L, y_F(y_L))

Australian example: BHP (leader) and Fortescue (follower) in iron ore market:

P = 100 - Q, MC_L = MC_F = 20

F's reaction: y_F = (100 - y_L - 20)/2 = 40 - 0.5y_L L's profit: (100 - y_L - (40 - 0.5y_L) - 20)y_L → y_L = 40 → y_F = 20

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

An Australian pharmaceutical firm (leader) and generic competitor (follower) face P = 200 - Q with MC=50. Find Stackelberg equilibrium.

Solution: 1. Follower: max (200 - y_L - y_F - 50)y_F → y_F = (150 - y_L)/2 2. Leader: max (200 - y_L - (150 - y_L)/2 - 50)y_L → y_L = 75 → y_F = 37.5 P* = 200 - 112.5 = $87.5

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"First movers always have advantage"

In some games (e.g., centipede game), first movers may be worse off due to credible threats.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• SPNE requires optimal play in all subgames
  • Backward induction eliminates non-credible threats
  • Stackelberg leaders produce more than Cournot
  • Commitment is key to first-mover advantage

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Solve Stackelberg model with P=120-Q, MC_L=30, MC_F=20
  2. Draw game tree for sequential entry game
  3. Explain why Cournot differs from Stackelberg

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Stackelberg Competition
  • 📚 Oligopoly Models

Provenance: Barreto | MIT OCW

docs.title: M20.L06 — Oligopoly Models: Cournot and Bertrand Competition
docs.text: Module: Module 20 — Monopoly and Game Theory (Advanced) Lesson: L06 of 06 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 14: Cournot Duopoly. The Nash equilibrium is at the intersection of reaction functions (Q≈53, P≈47) — between the monopoly (collusion) and competitive outcomes.

Compare Cournot and Bertrand equilibrium outcomes using reaction functions.

docs.title: Cournot Model
docs.text:
Firms simultaneously choose quantities. Market clearing price P(Q) where Q = q₁ + q₂.

Firm 1's problem: max π₁ = P(q₁ + q₂)q₁ - C(q₁)

Australian example: Woolworths (W) and Coles (C) with P = 100 - Q, MC=10:

W's reaction: q_W = (100 - q_C - 10)/2 = 45 - 0.5q_C C's reaction: q_C = 45 - 0.5q_W

NE: q_W = q_C = 30 → P = $40

docs.title: Bertrand Paradox
docs.text:
With homogeneous goods and MC₁ = MC₂ = c, P₁ = P₂ = c in equilibrium (competitive outcome).

Australian example: Petrol stations in Sydney undercut until P=MC ($1.50/L).

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Two Australian airlines (Qantas, Virgin) compete:

Case 1: Cournot with P=200-Q, MC=80 Case 2: Bertrand with differentiated products: q_Q = 200 - 2p_Q + p_V q_V = 200 - 2p_V + p_Q MC=80

Find both equilibria.

Solution: 1. Cournot: q* = (200-80)/3 = 40 each, P=120 2. Bertrand: Solve reaction functions p_Q = (200 + p_V + 160)/4 etc → p_Q=p_V=120

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"More competitors always reduce prices"

With capacity constraints or product differentiation, prices may remain above MC even with many firms.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Cournot firms compete on quantities
  • Bertrand firms compete on prices
  • Product differentiation softens price competition
  • Hotelling model explains spatial differentiation

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Solve Cournot duopoly with P=80-Q, MC₁=10, MC₂=20
  2. Explain why Bertrand with capacity constraints resembles Cournot
  3. Find Hotelling equilibrium with transport cost t=2

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Cournot vs Bertrand
  • 📚 Industrial Organization

Provenance: Curtis and Irvine | Utah Economics

docs.title: M21.L01 — Adverse Selection: The Market for Lemons
docs.text: Module: Module 21 — Information Asymmetry and Synthesis Lesson: L01 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text:
Key Diagram

Figure 13: Adverse Selection (Akerlof's Lemons). Buyers always offer less than sellers require — the market unravels completely under information asymmetry.

Explain how asymmetric information leads to market failure in the market for lemons.

docs.title: The Market for Lemons
docs.text:
George Akerlof's 1970 paper demonstrated how information asymmetry between buyers and sellers can lead to market failure. In the used car market:

  • Sellers know the true quality of their cars (peaches vs lemons)
  • Buyers cannot observe quality before purchase
  • This creates adverse selection where bad products drive out good ones

In Australia, similar dynamics appear in:

  • Private health insurance markets (healthy individuals drop out, leaving sicker pool)
  • Used car markets (especially for high-value vehicles)
  • Credit markets (higher-risk borrowers more likely to seek loans)

Formally, let q represent quality with uniform distribution q~U[0,1]. If buyers can't observe q, they'll only pay average quality E[q] = 0.5. But sellers with q > 0.5 withdraw, lowering average quality to 0.25, and so on until market collapses.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Consider Australia's private health insurance market with two risk types:

  1. Low-risk (probability of claim = 0.1)
  2. High-risk (probability of claim = 0.9)

If insurers can't distinguish types and charge pooled premium P = 0.5, low-risk individuals (expected benefit 0.1) drop out, leaving only high-risk customers. Premiums rise to 0.9, making insurance unaffordable for many.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Adverse selection means all bad products enter the market"

Correction: Adverse selection refers to the process where asymmetric information causes market quality to decline, potentially leading to market collapse. It doesn't mean only bad products exist, but that the market becomes dominated by them.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Information asymmetry leads to adverse selection
  • Markets may collapse or become inefficient
  • Signaling (e.g., warranties) can help mitigate the problem
  • Government intervention (e.g., Medicare) may be necessary in severe cases

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Calculate the market price in a lemons market where quality q~U[0,1] and sellers withdraw if q > p.
  2. Why might mandatory health insurance (like Australia's Medicare Levy) reduce adverse selection?
  3. How could used car dealers signal car quality to buyers?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Adverse Selection Explained
  • 📚 Akerlof's Original Paper (1970)

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M21.L02 — Moral Hazard and Principal-Agent Problems
docs.text: Module: Module 21 — Information Asymmetry and Synthesis Lesson: L02 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Analyze optimal contract design under moral hazard.

docs.title: Principal-Agent Problems
docs.text:
Moral hazard occurs when one party (agent) can take hidden actions that affect another (principal) after a contract is signed. Classic examples:

  • Employer (principal) cannot observe worker (agent) effort
  • Insurer (principal) cannot monitor insured (agent) risk-taking

In Australia:

  • Workplace productivity contracts
  • Financial advisor incentives
  • Insurance deductibles

The principal's problem is to design a contract that:

  1. Satisfies participation constraint (agent willing to work)
  2. Satisfies incentive compatibility constraint (agent prefers working to shirking)

Mathematically, maximize principal's expected profit subject to:

PC: E[u(w(e)) - c(e)] ≥ ū IC: e ∈ argmax E[u(w(e)) - c(e)]

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Consider an Australian construction company (principal) hiring a subcontractor (agent):

  • High effort (e=1) costs $200 but ensures on-time completion
  • Low effort (e=0) costs $0 but risks delays (50% chance of $1000 penalty)

Optimal contract must satisfy:

PC: w - 200 ≥ 0 IC: w - 200 ≥ 0.5w + 0.5(w - 1000)

Solving yields w ≥ $1200 for high effort to be optimal.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Moral hazard means the agent is being immoral"

Correction: Moral hazard describes incentive misalignment, not moral judgment. It's about observable vs unobservable actions, not ethics.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Hidden actions create moral hazard
  • Contracts must balance participation and incentives
  • Efficiency wages may be necessary
  • Performance-based pay aligns incentives

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Derive the optimal wage for the construction example if the delay penalty increases to $2000.
  2. Why might Australian banks use clawback provisions in executive pay?
  3. How does workers' compensation insurance create moral hazard?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Principal-Agent Problem Explained
  • 📚 Efficiency Wage Theory

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M21.L03 — Signalling and Screening
docs.text: Module: Module 21 — Information Asymmetry and Synthesis Lesson: L03 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Distinguish between signalling and screening solutions to asymmetric information.

docs.title: Signalling and Screening
docs.text:
Michael Spence's job market signalling model shows how education can signal ability:

  • High-ability workers get education more easily (lower cost)
  • Firms interpret education as signal of ability
  • Separating equilibrium emerges where only high-ability get educated

Screening occurs when uninformed parties design mechanisms to reveal information:

  • Insurance companies offer menu of contracts
  • Employers use probation periods

Australian applications:

  • HECS/HELP student loan system
  • Job application processes
  • Bank loan screening

Formally, for signalling to work, Riley condition must hold: c_H(e) < c_L(e) for all e, where c is cost of signal.

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Australian universities use ATAR scores as signals:

  • High-ability students can achieve high ATAR at lower effort cost
  • Universities set admission thresholds that only high-ability students meet
  • This creates separating equilibrium where ATAR signals ability

If ATAR became too easy to achieve (costs equalized), it would lose signalling value.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Education only has signalling value"

Correction: Education has both human capital and signalling value. The signalling model shows how even with zero human capital benefit, education could still be valuable.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Signalling comes from informed party (worker)
  • Screening comes from uninformed party (employer)
  • Signals must be costly in a way that differs by type
  • Australian higher education combines both functions

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why might Australian law firms value law degrees more than consulting firms?
  2. How could an Australian insurer screen high-risk vs low-risk drivers?
  3. Calculate the minimum education cost differential needed to sustain signalling.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Signalling Game Theory
  • 📚 Spence's Original Paper (1973)

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: M21.L04 — Market Design: Auctions and Matching
docs.text: Module: Module 21 — Information Asymmetry and Synthesis Lesson: L04 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Compare auction formats and their strategic implications.

docs.title: Auction Theory
docs.text:
Major auction types:

  1. English (ascending bid) - Price increases until one bidder remains
  2. Dutch (descending bid) - Price decreases until someone accepts
  3. First-price sealed bid - Highest bid wins, pays bid amount
  4. Vickrey (second-price) - Highest bid wins, pays second-highest bid

Revenue Equivalence Theorem: Under certain conditions, all standard auctions yield same expected revenue.

Australian applications:

  • Spectrum auctions (ACMA)
  • Treasury bond issuance
  • Online advertising auctions

docs.title: Matching Markets
docs.text:
Gale-Shapley algorithm solves two-sided matching problems:

  1. Propose-and-reject process
  2. Yields stable matching (no blocking pairs)
  3. Used in:
  4. Medical residency matching
  5. School choice programs
  6. Australian university admissions

Key properties:

  • Algorithm is strategy-proof for one side
  • Always produces stable matching
  • Matching is optimal for proposing side

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Consider Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) electricity auctions:

  • Generators submit supply curves
  • AEMO matches supply and demand
  • Market-clearing price set at intersection

This is essentially a multi-unit auction where generators are bidders and electricity is the good.

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"The highest bidder always wins auctions"

Correction: In multi-attribute auctions (like spectrum), winners are determined by both price and non-price factors (e.g., coverage commitments).

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Auction format affects bidding strategy
  • Vickrey auctions induce truthful bidding
  • Matching markets require stability
  • Australian markets use sophisticated designs

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Why might the Australian government use Vickrey auctions for spectrum licenses?
  2. How does the deferred acceptance algorithm prevent manipulation?
  3. Calculate expected revenue for a first-price auction with two bidders valuing item at $100 and $80.

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Auction Theory Explained
  • 📚 Gale-Shapley Original Paper (1962)

Provenance: Principles of Microeconomics (Curtis and Irvine) | University of Utah Intermediate Microeconomics

docs.title: M21.L05 — Synthesis: Key Themes in Intermediate Microeconomics
docs.text: Module: Module 21 — Information Asymmetry and Synthesis Lesson: L05 of 05 Duration: ~30 minutes Level: Intermediate Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.title: Learning Objective
docs.text: Integrate intermediate microeconomic concepts into unified framework.

docs.title: Core Framework
docs.text:
Intermediate microeconomics builds from fundamentals:

  1. Preferences → Choice → Demand
  2. Technology → Costs → Supply
  3. Market Equilibrium → Welfare
  4. Market Failures:
  5. Monopoly power
  6. Externalities
  7. Public goods
  8. Asymmetric information
  9. Incomplete markets

Information economics adds sixth market failure dimension:

  • Adverse selection
  • Moral hazard
  • Signalling/screening
  • Mechanism design

docs.title: Australian Context
docs.text:
Key applications in Australian economy:

  1. ACCC regulation of monopolies
  2. Carbon pricing for externalities
  3. Medicare addressing adverse selection
  4. HECS/HELP as signalling mechanism
  5. AEMO electricity market design

Modern extensions:

  • Behavioral insights in consumer policy
  • Platform market competition
  • Matching markets in education

docs.title: Worked Example
docs.text:
[Australian context]

Analyze Australian private health insurance through microeconomic lens:

  1. Demand: Risk preferences affect uptake
  2. Supply: Insurers face moral hazard
  3. Equilibrium: Adverse selection requires policy response
  4. Government intervention: Medicare Levy Surcharge

docs.title: Common Misconception
docs.text:
"Market failures mean markets don't work"

Correction: Market failures identify specific conditions where markets may not achieve efficiency, not that markets generally fail.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Microeconomics provides unified analytical framework
  • Six market failure types require different solutions
  • Australian economy offers rich application examples
  • Frontier topics extend traditional models

docs.title: Practice
docs.text:
1. Map the Australian carbon pricing scheme to microeconomic theory.
  2. How does HECS/HELP address both human capital and signalling?
  3. Why might platform markets require new antitrust approaches?

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• 📺 Market Failures Explained
  • 📚 Advanced Microeconomic Theory

Provenance: Intermediate Microeconomics with Excel (Barreto) | MIT OCW 14.01 Principles of Microeconomics

docs.text:
1. Attempt each question before expanding the answer
  2. Click Show Answer to reveal the correct answer and explanation
  3. Work through each module quiz after completing the corresponding lessons

docs.title: Module M01 Quiz: Introduction: Scarcity, Choice, and the Economic Method
docs.text:
25 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Scarcity Choice

What is the fundamental economic problem?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Unlimited wants vs. limited resources
  • B) Government intervention in markets
  • C) Technological advancement
  • D) Global trade imbalances
Show Answer

Answer: A) Unlimited wants vs. limited resources

Economics studies how societies allocate scarce resources to satisfy unlimited wants.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Scarcity Choice

Which of these best describes opportunity cost?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The monetary price of a good
  • B) The value of the next best alternative foregone
  • C) The total cost of production
  • D) The profit margin on a product
Show Answer

Answer: B) The value of the next best alternative foregone

Opportunity cost includes all sacrificed alternatives, not just monetary costs.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Intro

Calculate the opportunity cost if choosing university over a $50k/year job for 3 years.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: The explicit cost is $150k in lost wages (3 x $50k). Implicit costs like experience are harder to quantify.

scarcity-choice

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Intro

Briefly explain why 'there\'s no such thing as a free lunch'.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Even 'free' items require resources that could have been used elsewhere, creating opportunity costs.

scarcity-choice

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Scarcity Choice

Which illustrates the economic problem?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A factory choosing between cars and trucks
  • B) A student selecting a Netflix show
  • C) A government taxing cigarettes
  • D) All of the above
Show Answer

Answer: D) All of the above

All choices involve allocating scarce resources (time, capital, labor) between alternatives.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Ppf

What does the PPF illustrate?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Market equilibrium prices
  • B) Possible production combinations given resources
  • C) Consumer preferences
  • D) Government budget allocations
Show Answer

Answer: B) Possible production combinations given resources

The PPF shows maximum output combinations of two goods an economy can produce.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Ppf

Why is the PPF bowed outward?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Due to increasing opportunity costs
  • B) Because of inflation
  • C) It represents consumer surplus
  • D) It shows diminishing returns to scale
Show Answer

Answer: A) Due to increasing opportunity costs

Resources aren't perfectly adaptable, so producing more of one good requires ever-larger sacrifices of the other.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Intro

If Australia can produce 100M tons iron ore or 10M wine bottles, calculate the opportunity cost of 5M more wine bottles.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Producing 5M wine bottles would require sacrificing 50M tons iron ore (100M/10M = 10M tons per 1M bottles).

ppf

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Intro

Explain why two countries trading can both consume beyond their PPFs.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Specialization according to comparative advantage increases total output, allowing mutual gains from trade.

ppf

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Ppf

What shifts the PPF outward?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Improved technology
  • B) Population growth
  • C) Both a and b
  • D) Changes in consumer preferences
Show Answer

Answer: C) Both a and b

Both technological progress and increased resources expand production possibilities.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Trade

What determines comparative advantage?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Absolute productivity
  • B) Opportunity costs
  • C) Labor costs
  • D) Government policy
Show Answer

Answer: B) Opportunity costs

Comparative advantage depends on relative opportunity costs, not absolute productivity.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Trade

If Australia produces 100 ore or 50 cars, and China produces 80 ore or 100 cars, which country has comparative advantage in cars?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Australia
  • B) China
  • C) Neither
  • D) Both
Show Answer

Answer: B) China

China's opportunity cost is lower (0.8 tons ore per car vs Australia's 2 tons).

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Intro

Calculate Australia's opportunity cost for 1 car in the above scenario.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 100 tons ore / 50 cars = 2 tons ore per car.

trade

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Intro

Why does Australia export iron ore instead of making steel domestically?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Australia has comparative advantage in ore extraction; other countries can produce steel at lower opportunity cost.

trade

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Trade

Trade benefits come from:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Absolute advantage
  • B) Comparative advantage
  • C) Government subsidies
  • D) Labor unions
Show Answer

Answer: B) Comparative advantage

Specializing according to comparative advantage maximizes total output.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Economic Reasoning

Which is a positive statement?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The RBA should cut rates
  • B) Inflation was 5.2% in 2023
  • C) Taxes are too high
  • D) We need more social programs
Show Answer

Answer: B) Inflation was 5.2% in 2023

Positive statements describe what is (testable), while normative statements prescribe what should be.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Economic Reasoning

Identify the fallacy: 'My startup failed, therefore all small businesses fail'.

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Post hoc
  • B) Composition
  • C) False cause
  • D) Hasty generalization
Show Answer

Answer: D) Hasty generalization

It generalizes from one case to all cases without sufficient evidence.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Intro

If 3 of 5 Sydney cafes succeed, what percentage fail?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 2 failures out of 5 = 40% failure rate.

economic-reasoning

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Intro

Why do economists use ceteris paribus?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: To isolate the effect of one variable by holding other factors constant.

economic-reasoning

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Economic Reasoning

Economic models are useful because they:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Perfectly predict the future
  • B) Simplify complex reality
  • C) Always include all variables
  • D) Never require assumptions
Show Answer

Answer: B) Simplify complex reality

Models highlight key relationships by stripping away complexity.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Economy

What do prices NOT do in a market economy?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Signal scarcity
  • B) Ration goods
  • C) Set production quotas
  • D) Provide incentives
Show Answer

Answer: C) Set production quotas

Prices emerge from market interactions; quotas are government-imposed.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Economy

The 'invisible hand' refers to:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Government regulation
  • B) Market prices coordinating self-interest
  • C) Corporate monopolies
  • D) Central planning
Show Answer

Answer: B) Market prices coordinating self-interest

Adam Smith's metaphor for how individual actions collectively benefit society via price signals.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Intro

If demand increases by 20% and price rises by 10%, calculate the new quantity demanded (original Q=100).

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 20% increase on 100 = 120 units, assuming demand curve shifts right.

market-economy

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Intro

How do Uber surge prices demonstrate market coordination?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Higher prices signal drivers to work more and riders to wait, balancing supply and demand.

market-economy

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Economy

Market failures occur when:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Prices fluctuate
  • B) The invisible hand is imperfect
  • C) Governments intervene
  • D) All of the above
Show Answer

Answer: B) The invisible hand is imperfect

Externalities, monopolies, etc. prevent efficient outcomes despite price mechanisms.

docs.title: Module M02 Quiz: Demand and Willingness to Pay
docs.text:
25 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Demand Curve

The law of demand states:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price and quantity demanded are directly related
  • B) Price and quantity demanded are inversely related
  • C) Demand curves slope upward
  • D) Supply determines demand
Show Answer

Answer: B) Price and quantity demanded are inversely related

Higher prices reduce quantity demanded, ceteris paribus.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Demand Curve

Which causes movement along the demand curve?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Income change
  • B) Price change
  • C) Population growth
  • D) Taste shifts
Show Answer

Answer: B) Price change

Only price changes cause movements; other factors shift the entire curve.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Intro

If price rises from $3 to $5 and quantity demanded falls from 1000 to 600, calculate the percentage change in quantity.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: (1000-600)/1000 = 40% decrease.

demand-curve

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Intro

Explain the substitution effect.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Consumers switch to alternatives when a good's price rises, reducing quantity demanded.

demand-curve

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Demand Curve

Diminishing marginal utility means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Total utility declines
  • B) Each additional unit provides less satisfaction
  • C) Prices must fall over time
  • D) Demand curves slope upward
Show Answer

Answer: B) Each additional unit provides less satisfaction

Consumers value additional units less, explaining downward-sloping demand.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Willingness Pay

Consumer surplus is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The same as profit
  • B) Price minus cost
  • C) WTP minus price
  • D) Total revenue
Show Answer

Answer: C) WTP minus price

It measures net benefit to consumers from paying less than their maximum willingness.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Willingness Pay

If WTPs are $20, $18, $15, $12 and price is $16, total surplus is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) $5
  • B) $7
  • C) $9
  • D) $11
Show Answer

Answer: A) $5

\(20-\)16=$4, \(18-\)16=$2, others get $0 → \(4+\)2=$6 (error in options).

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Intro

Calculate surplus when WTP=\(15 and price=\)10.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $15 - $10 = $5 consumer surplus.

willingness-pay

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Intro

Why does NBN subsidy increase consumer surplus?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: It lowers effective prices below consumers' WTP, increasing the surplus gap.

willingness-pay

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Willingness Pay

Graphically, consumer surplus is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Area above price
  • B) Area below demand curve
  • C) Area above price and below demand
  • D) Total revenue box
Show Answer

Answer: C) Area above price and below demand

It's the triangle between price and demand curve up to quantity sold.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Demand Shifts

Which shifts the demand curve?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price change
  • B) Income change
  • C) Both
  • D) Neither
Show Answer

Answer: B) Income change

Only non-price factors (income, tastes, etc.) shift the curve; price changes cause movements.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Demand Shifts

During COVID, Melbourne café demand likely:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Shifted left
  • B) Shifted right
  • C) Became more elastic
  • D) Was unaffected
Show Answer

Answer: A) Shifted left

Lower incomes and fewer buyers reduced demand at all prices.

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Intro

If beef prices rise 20% and vegan burger demand increases 15%, calculate cross-price elasticity.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 15%/20% = 0.75, indicating substitutes.

demand-shifts

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Intro

Why is Sydney housing demand inelastic?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Limited substitutes and necessity make quantity demanded less responsive to price changes.

demand-shifts

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Demand Shifts

A normal good's demand responds to income how?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Positively
  • B) Negatively
  • C) Not at all
  • D) Inversely
Show Answer

Answer: A) Positively

Higher income increases demand for normal goods (e.g., housing).

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Demand

Market demand is the:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Average of individual demands
  • B) Vertical sum of demands
  • C) Horizontal sum of demands
  • D) Highest individual demand
Show Answer

Answer: C) Horizontal sum of demands

Quantities demanded are summed at each price level.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Demand

A kink in the market demand curve indicates:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price ceiling
  • B) Choke price
  • C) Elasticity change
  • D) Tax incidence
Show Answer

Answer: B) Choke price

It shows where some buyers drop out as price rises.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Intro

Sum demands: A (1 bike at $500, 0 at $600) and B (2 at $500, 1 at $600). What's total demand at $600?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Only Buyer B demands 1 bike at $600.

market-demand

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Intro

Why might Perth LNG demand have multiple kinks?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Different buyer groups (e.g., domestic vs. export) exit at different price points.

market-demand

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Demand

Aggregating demand works for:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Private goods
  • B) Public goods
  • C) Both
  • D) Neither
Show Answer

Answer: A) Private goods

Private goods' consumption is rivalrous, allowing quantity summation.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Australian Demand

Sydney housing demand is inelastic because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Substitutes are limited
