  • B) It's a necessity
  • C) Both a and b
  • D) Prices never change
Show Answer

Answer: C) Both a and b

Few alternatives and essential nature make demand insensitive to price.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Australian Demand

Solar adoption makes electricity demand:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) More elastic
  • B) More inelastic
  • C) Unaffected
  • D) Vertical
Show Answer

Answer: A) More elastic

Solar provides substitutes, increasing price sensitivity.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Intro

If electricity demand falls 5% when prices rise 10%, calculate elasticity.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 5%/10% = 0.5 (inelastic).

australian-demand

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Intro

How did COVID affect food delivery vs. iron ore demand differently?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Delivery spiked (home consumption) while ore fluctuated with Chinese construction.

australian-demand

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Australian Demand

Essential goods typically have:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Elastic demand
  • B) Inelastic demand
  • C) Unitary elasticity
  • D) Perfect elasticity
Show Answer

Answer: B) Inelastic demand

Necessities see smaller quantity changes when prices change.

docs.title: Module M03 Quiz: Production and Costs
docs.text:
25 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

Marginal product of labour (MPL) is defined as:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Total output divided by number of workers
  • B) The change in total output from hiring one more worker
  • C) The wage paid to the last worker hired
  • D) Average output per worker
Show Answer

Answer: B) The change in total output from hiring one more worker

MPL = ΔTP/ΔL — the additional output from one more unit of labour, holding other inputs fixed.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

When marginal product is above average product, average product is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Falling
  • B) Rising — the marginal unit pulls the average up
  • C) Constant
  • D) Zero
Show Answer

Answer: B) Rising — the marginal unit pulls the average up

When MPL > APL, each new worker contributes more than the current average, pulling the average up — same logic as batting averages.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

A wheat farm employs 3 workers producing 90 tonnes. A 4th worker raises output to 112 tonnes. Calculate MPL for the 4th worker.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 22 tonnes

MPL = ΔTP/ΔL = (112-90)/(4-3) = 22 tonnes.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

Explain the difference between the short run and long run in production theory.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: In the short run at least one input (usually capital) is fixed and cannot be changed. In the long run all inputs are variable — the firm can adjust capital, labour, and other factors.

The short/long run distinction is about the flexibility of inputs, not a fixed calendar period.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

Average product of labour (APL) is maximised when:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Total product is maximised
  • B) MPL = APL (marginal product equals average product)
  • C) MPL is maximised
  • D) Total product begins to fall
Show Answer

Answer: B) MPL = APL (marginal product equals average product)

APL is maximised at the point where the MPL curve crosses the APL curve from above.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Costs

The law of diminishing marginal returns states that as more of a variable input is added to a fixed input:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Total output eventually falls to zero
  • B) Marginal product of the variable input eventually falls
  • C) Average product always rises
  • D) Fixed costs increase
Show Answer

Answer: B) Marginal product of the variable input eventually falls

Eventually each additional unit of the variable input adds less to total output — diminishing marginal returns.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Costs

Diminishing marginal returns occurs in the:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Long run when all inputs can vary
  • B) Short run when at least one input is fixed
  • C) Only in agriculture
  • D) Only when workers are unskilled
Show Answer

Answer: B) Short run when at least one input is fixed

Diminishing marginal returns is a short-run concept — it arises because additional variable inputs are combined with a fixed factor.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Costs

A Pilbara mine has fixed machinery. Workers 1-3 each produce 100 tonnes/day. Worker 4 produces 80, worker 5 produces 50. From which worker do diminishing returns set in?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Worker 4

MPL falls from 100 to 80 at worker 4 — diminishing marginal returns begin with the 4th worker.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Costs

Explain why diminishing marginal returns eventually occur in any production process.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: As more of a variable input is added to a fixed input, the fixed input becomes a bottleneck. Each additional worker has less of the fixed input (e.g. machinery, land) to work with, so their marginal contribution eventually declines.

The fixed factor becomes increasingly scarce relative to the variable factor.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Costs

Which scenario violates the law of diminishing marginal returns?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A farm where each additional worker after the 5th adds less output
  • B) A factory where the 10th worker adds less than the 9th
  • C) A mine where every additional worker permanently adds more output than the last
  • D) A restaurant where cramming more chefs reduces output per chef
Show Answer

Answer: C) A mine where every additional worker permanently adds more output than the last

Permanently increasing marginal returns would violate the law — in a short-run setting with fixed capital this cannot persist indefinitely.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

Average fixed cost (AFC) always:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Rises as output increases
  • B) Falls as output increases — fixed costs are spread over more units
  • C) Equals marginal cost
  • D) Is U-shaped
Show Answer

Answer: B) Falls as output increases — fixed costs are spread over more units

AFC = TFC/Q. As Q rises, fixed costs are spread over more units so AFC continuously declines.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

The marginal cost curve intersects the average total cost curve at:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The maximum point of ATC
  • B) The minimum point of ATC
  • C) Any arbitrary point
  • D) The point where AFC = AVC
Show Answer

Answer: B) The minimum point of ATC

MC intersects ATC at ATC's minimum. When MC < ATC, ATC falls; when MC > ATC, ATC rises.

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

A Sydney café has TFC=\(800/day. Variable costs: TC(10)=\)1,100, TC(11)=$1,210. Calculate MC of the 11th coffee and ATC at Q=11.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: MC=\(110; ATC=\)110

MC=ΔTC/ΔQ=(1210-1100)/1=\(110. ATC=TC/Q=1210/11=\)110.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

Why is the ATC curve U-shaped?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: At low output, high AFC pulls ATC up (fixed costs dominate). As output rises AFC falls rapidly, pulling ATC down. Eventually diminishing returns raise AVC faster than AFC falls, causing ATC to rise again — creating the U-shape.

The U-shape reflects the tension between falling AFC and rising AVC.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

A firm's TVC at Q=50 is $500 and TFC is $200. Calculate AVC and ATC.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: AVC=\(10; ATC=\)14

AVC=TVC/Q=500/50=\(10. ATC=(TVC+TFC)/Q=700/50=\)14.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Costs

Economies of scale means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Long-run average cost rises as output expands
  • B) Long-run average cost falls as output expands
  • C) Short-run marginal cost falls
  • D) Fixed costs increase with scale
Show Answer

Answer: B) Long-run average cost falls as output expands

Economies of scale: LRATC falls as output increases — often due to specialisation, efficient capital, and technology.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Costs

Diseconomies of scale occur when a firm grows too large because of:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Increasing returns to the variable factor
  • B) Coordination and management problems that raise per-unit costs
  • C) Government regulation
  • D) Too much competition
Show Answer

Answer: B) Coordination and management problems that raise per-unit costs

Very large firms face coordination failures, communication breakdowns, and bureaucratic inefficiency that raise LRATC.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Costs

Qantas moves from 50 aircraft to 100 aircraft. LRATC falls from $180/seat to $160/seat. What type of returns does this represent?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Economies of scale

LRATC fell as output doubled — economies of scale. The percentage fall in LRATC (11%) accompanies a 100% increase in scale.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Costs

What is the minimum efficient scale (MES)?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: The MES is the smallest output level at which a firm's long-run average total cost is minimised. Firms operating below MES have higher per-unit costs than larger rivals.

MES determines the natural size of firms in an industry and influences market structure.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Costs

An industry where MES is very large relative to market demand will tend toward:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Perfect competition with many small firms
  • B) Natural monopoly or oligopoly — only a few firms can operate at efficient scale
  • C) Monopolistic competition
  • D) Government ownership always
Show Answer

Answer: B) Natural monopoly or oligopoly — only a few firms can operate at efficient scale

When MES is large relative to demand, only a few firms can reach efficient scale — the industry tends toward concentrated market structures.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

Allocative efficiency requires:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) P = minimum ATC
  • B) P = MC (price equals marginal cost)
  • C) MR = 0
  • D) Zero economic profit
Show Answer

Answer: B) P = MC (price equals marginal cost)

Allocative efficiency: resources are allocated optimally when P=MC — the value placed on the last unit equals its production cost.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

BHP's Pilbara iron ore operations benefit from economies of scale. If LRATC at 200m tonnes/year is $35/tonne and at 100m tonnes is $50/tonne, calculate the cost saving from doubling output.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $15/tonne or $3bn total

Saving per tonne = \(50-\)35=$15. At 200m tonnes: total saving vs 100m tonne cost base = \(15×200m=\)3bn.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

Productive efficiency requires:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) P = MC
  • B) Production at minimum LRATC
  • C) MR = MC
  • D) Zero fixed costs
Show Answer

Answer: B) Production at minimum LRATC

Productive efficiency: the good is produced at the lowest possible per-unit cost (minimum LRATC).

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

Explain the relationship MC = PL/MPL.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: The marginal cost of producing one more unit equals the wage rate (PL) divided by the marginal product of labour. If workers are more productive (higher MPL) each unit costs less to produce; if wages rise, MC rises.

This connects the labour market to the firm's cost structure.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

Coles operates 800+ supermarkets. Grams per $1 of generic product vs premium: identical quality. The cost advantage comes from:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Monopoly pricing power
  • B) Economies of scale in procurement, distribution, and private label production
  • C) Government subsidies
  • D) Lower quality inputs
Show Answer

Answer: B) Economies of scale in procurement, distribution, and private label production

Large retailers achieve lower unit costs through bulk purchasing power, efficient distribution networks, and scale in own-brand manufacturing.

docs.title: Module M04 Quiz: Supply and Market Equilibrium
docs.text:
30 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

The law of supply states that as price rises:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Quantity supplied falls
  • B) Quantity supplied rises — higher price makes production more profitable attracting more supply
  • C) Supply curve shifts right
  • D) Demand increases
Show Answer

Answer: B) Quantity supplied rises — higher price makes production more profitable attracting more supply

Higher prices increase the profitability of producing a good, inducing firms to supply more — the fundamental law of supply.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

A firm's supply curve is based on its:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Average fixed cost curve
  • B) Marginal cost curve above average variable cost
  • C) Average revenue curve
  • D) Total cost curve
Show Answer

Answer: B) Marginal cost curve above average variable cost

A firm supplies where P ≥ AVC and produces at MC = P — so its short-run supply curve is its MC curve above AVC.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

A wheat farmer can supply 1,000 tonnes at $250/tonne or 1,400 tonnes at $300/tonne. Calculate the price elasticity of supply between these two points.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 0.8

PES = (%ΔQs/%ΔP) = ((1400-1000)/1000)/((300-250)/250) = (40%)/(20%) = 2.0. (Check: 400/1000=40%; 50/250=20%; PES=2.0 — elastic supply)

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

What is the difference between a change in quantity supplied and a change in supply?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A change in quantity supplied is a movement along the supply curve caused by a price change. A change in supply is a shift of the entire supply curve caused by a non-price determinant (input costs, technology, expectations, etc.).

Only price changes cause movements along the curve; everything else shifts the curve.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

Which is an example of a movement along the supply curve (not a shift)?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A drought reduces wheat supply
  • B) Iron ore price rises and miners supply more
  • C) A new technology reduces steel production costs
  • D) Government imposes a new mining royalty
Show Answer

Answer: B) Iron ore price rises and miners supply more

A price change causes a movement along the curve. The other options are non-price changes that shift the supply curve.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

Which factor would shift the supply curve of coffee to the LEFT (decrease supply)?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Improved harvesting technology
  • B) A fall in coffee bean prices
  • C) A drought destroying coffee crops in Brazil and Vietnam
  • D) Entry of new coffee producers
Show Answer

Answer: C) A drought destroying coffee crops in Brazil and Vietnam

A drought destroys supply capacity — this shifts supply left (decreases supply at every price).

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

If the price of natural gas (an input for manufacturing) rises, what happens to supply of manufactured goods?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Supply increases (shifts right)
  • B) Supply decreases (shifts left) — higher input costs raise MC and reduce profitability
  • C) Supply is unchanged
  • D) Demand also falls
Show Answer

Answer: B) Supply decreases (shifts left) — higher input costs raise MC and reduce profitability

Higher input prices raise the cost of production at every output level, shifting supply left.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

Australia's iron ore supply rises from 800m to 950m tonnes/year after new mines open. At constant price $120/tonne, estimate the revenue change for Australia.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $18bn increase

ΔRevenue = ΔQ × P = 150m × $120 = $18bn

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

Explain how expectations about future prices can shift current supply.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: If producers expect prices to rise in the future they may withhold supply now to sell later at higher prices, reducing current supply. Conversely, expected price falls may prompt increased current supply.

Forward-looking behaviour by producers shifts current supply based on expected future conditions.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

Which change would shift supply of Australian beef to the RIGHT?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A drought reducing pasture quality
  • B) Increased cost of stock feed
  • C) New feed lot technology reducing per-head costs
  • D) Higher export tariffs imposed by China
Show Answer

Answer: C) New feed lot technology reducing per-head costs

Lower production costs from new technology reduce MC at every output level, shifting supply right (increasing supply).

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

At market equilibrium:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) All consumers get the good they want
  • B) Quantity demanded equals quantity supplied and the market clears
  • C) Prices are always low
  • D) Supply exceeds demand
Show Answer

Answer: B) Quantity demanded equals quantity supplied and the market clears

Equilibrium: Qd = Qs. The market clears — no shortage or surplus at the equilibrium price.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

Demand: P=100-2Q. Supply: P=10+3Q. Find equilibrium price and quantity.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Q=18, P=$64

100-2Q=10+3Q → 90=5Q → Q=18; P=100-36=$64

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

Why do prices tend to fall when there is a surplus in a market?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: When quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded, producers have unsold inventory. To clear stock, they cut prices. As price falls, quantity demanded rises and quantity supplied falls until the market clears at equilibrium.

Excess supply creates downward pressure on price — sellers compete to attract buyers.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

Sydney's rental market has demand Qd=500-2P and supply Qs=100+P (P in $/week, Q in apartments). Calculate equilibrium rent and quantity.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: P=$133/week, Q=233

500-2P=100+P → 400=3P → P=$133.33; Q=100+133=233

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

A market is in equilibrium at P=$50. If the government announces a price freeze at $50 and supply then increases, what happens?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Nothing changes
  • B) A surplus develops — at $50 the increased supply exceeds demand but the price freeze prevents adjustment
  • C) A shortage develops
  • D) The equilibrium price falls to $50
Show Answer

Answer: B) A surplus develops — at $50 the increased supply exceeds demand but the price freeze prevents adjustment

If supply increases but price is frozen, quantity supplied rises while quantity demanded stays the same → surplus.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

If both supply and demand increase simultaneously, we can be certain that:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price rises
  • B) Quantity rises
  • C) Both price and quantity rise
  • D) Price falls
Show Answer

Answer: B) Quantity rises

Increased demand raises Q; increased supply also raises Q. Net effect on price is ambiguous (depends on relative magnitudes). Quantity unambiguously rises.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

COVID-19 simultaneously reduced restaurant supply (closures) and restaurant demand (fear). What can we predict about restaurant prices and quantity?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price rises, quantity rises
  • B) Quantity falls (both shifts reduce Q), price change is ambiguous
  • C) Price falls, quantity falls
  • D) Price rises, quantity is ambiguous
Show Answer

Answer: B) Quantity falls (both shifts reduce Q), price change is ambiguous

Both supply decrease (closures) and demand decrease (fear) reduce equilibrium quantity. Price effects offset — supply decrease raises price, demand decrease lowers price; net effect ambiguous.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

Iron ore demand: P=200-Q. Iron ore supply: P=50+0.5Q. A new mine shifts supply to P=30+0.5Q. Calculate the change in equilibrium price and quantity.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: P falls $13.33; Q rises 26.67

Original: 200-Q=50+0.5Q → Q=100, P=\(100. New: 200-Q=30+0.5Q → Q*=113.3, P*=\)86.67. ΔP=-$13.33, ΔQ=+13.3

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

What is comparative statics analysis?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Comparative statics compares two equilibrium positions — before and after a change in an exogenous variable (like income, input costs, or technology). It predicts the direction of change in equilibrium price and quantity without analysing the adjustment path.

We compare static equilibria rather than tracing the dynamic adjustment process.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

Income rises and the good is a normal good. Predict the effect on equilibrium price and quantity.

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price falls, quantity rises
  • B) Price rises, quantity rises
  • C) Price rises, quantity falls
  • D) Both fall
Show Answer

Answer: B) Price rises, quantity rises

Higher income increases demand for a normal good → demand shifts right → both P and Q rise at the new equilibrium.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Supply Demand

A shortage means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price is above equilibrium
  • B) Price is below equilibrium — quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied
  • C) There is unsold inventory
  • D) Producers are losing money
Show Answer

Answer: B) Price is below equilibrium — quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied

A shortage (excess demand) occurs when price is set below equilibrium — buyers want more than producers are willing to supply at that price.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Supply Demand

Sydney has a housing shortage. Quantity demanded is 400,000 dwellings and quantity supplied is 340,000 at current rents. The shortage is:

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 60,000 dwellings

Shortage = Qd - Qs = 400,000 - 340,000 = 60,000 dwellings.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Supply Demand

When a surplus exists in a market, what adjustment mechanism restores equilibrium?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: With a surplus sellers cannot sell all their output at the current price. They compete for buyers by cutting prices. As price falls demand rises and supply falls until Qd=Qs is restored.

Price competition among sellers drives prices down to clear the surplus.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Supply Demand

A price ceiling below equilibrium creates a shortage. Which statement about the long-run shortage is correct?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The shortage shrinks over time as demand falls
  • B) The shortage grows over time as supply becomes more elastic and decreases more
  • C) The shortage stays constant
  • D) Shortages only occur in the short run
Show Answer

Answer: B) The shortage grows over time as supply becomes more elastic and decreases more

Over time supply becomes more elastic (producers exit, reduce investment) while demand becomes more elastic too — the shortage typically worsens in the long run.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Supply Demand

Prices coordinate economic activity by:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Allowing government to allocate resources centrally
  • B) Conveying information about scarcity and profitability to decentralised decision-makers
  • C) Ensuring equal distribution of goods
  • D) Eliminating all shortages instantly
Show Answer

Answer: B) Conveying information about scarcity and profitability to decentralised decision-makers

Prices aggregate and transmit information: high prices signal scarcity and attract resources; low prices signal abundance and redirect them — Hayek's knowledge problem.

docs.title: Question 26
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Supply Demand

Hayek argued that the price system solves which fundamental economic problem?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The problem of government budget deficits
  • B) The knowledge problem — prices aggregate dispersed local information that no central planner could collect
  • C) The problem of income inequality
  • D) The problem of monopoly
Show Answer

Answer: B) The knowledge problem — prices aggregate dispersed local information that no central planner could collect

Prices aggregate millions of individual bits of knowledge about scarcity and value — far more than any central authority could collect or process.

docs.title: Question 27
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Supply Demand

A sudden drought destroys 30% of Australian wheat. Without government intervention, what happens to wheat prices and how does this allocate resources?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price stays flat; government rations wheat
  • B) Price rises; higher prices signal scarcity attracting new supply and reducing demand — the price system allocates the reduced supply without central direction
  • C) Price falls; producers exit
  • D) Price is controlled by the government
Show Answer

Answer: B) Price rises; higher prices signal scarcity attracting new supply and reducing demand — the price system allocates the reduced supply without central direction

The price rise signals scarcity to all market participants simultaneously — producers expand supply (if possible) and consumers economise, without any central coordination.

docs.title: Question 28
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Supply Demand

Iron ore price doubles from $60 to $120/tonne. Estimate how many new Australian mine projects become viable if each requires a minimum price of $80/tonne to break even.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Projects requiring \(80-\)120/tonne become viable

At \(60, projects needing >\)60 are unviable. At $120, projects needing up to $120 become profitable. The price rise activates marginal supply — the exact number depends on the distribution of project break-even costs.

docs.title: Question 29
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Supply Demand

Explain why market economies generally outperform command economies in resource allocation.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Market prices aggregate decentralised information about consumer preferences and production costs that no central planner can access. Price signals automatically direct resources to highest-value uses through profit and loss incentives — without requiring a central authority to coordinate millions of decisions.

The invisible hand uses prices to coordinate decentralised decisions more efficiently than central planning.

docs.title: Question 30
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Supply Demand

'Dollar votes' refers to:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Voting in elections with your income level
  • B) Consumers signalling preferences to producers by spending — firms follow consumer spending patterns to decide what to produce
  • C) A political donation system
  • D) Government allocating dollars based on votes
Show Answer

Answer: B) Consumers signalling preferences to producers by spending — firms follow consumer spending patterns to decide what to produce

In a market economy, consumer spending patterns guide production decisions — firms produce what consumers are willing to pay for.

docs.title: Module M05 Quiz: Economic Surplus and Elasticity
docs.text:
30 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Surplus

Consumer surplus is the:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Total spending by consumers
  • B) Difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay
  • C) Area above the supply curve
  • D) Producer profit
Show Answer

Answer: B) Difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay

CS = WTP - Price paid, summed over all units. Graphically it is the area under the demand curve above the market price.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Surplus

If a consumer's WTP for a concert ticket is $150 and the price is $90, their consumer surplus is:

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $60

CS = WTP - Price = $150 - $90 = $60.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Surplus

If price falls from $80 to $60 and quantity rises from 100 to 140, calculate the approximate change in consumer surplus.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $2,800

ΔCS ≈ (ΔP × Q_original) + 0.5 × (ΔP × ΔQ) = (20×100) + 0.5×(20×40) = $2,000 + $400 = \(2,400. (Trapezoid: 0.5×(100+140)×20=\)2,400)

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Surplus

Why does a lower price increase consumer surplus?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A lower price directly benefits existing buyers (they pay less than before) and also brings in new buyers whose WTP exceeds the new price. Both groups gain surplus — existing buyers gain more per unit and new buyers enter the market.

Price reductions benefit both inframarginal and marginal consumers.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Surplus

The Infrastructure Australia framework uses willingness-to-pay estimates to evaluate transport projects. This is an application of:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Producer surplus analysis
  • B) Consumer surplus analysis — aggregating the value road/rail users place on time and cost savings
  • C) Government revenue maximisation
  • D) Cost-benefit analysis using only financial returns
Show Answer

Answer: B) Consumer surplus analysis — aggregating the value road/rail users place on time and cost savings

CBA for public infrastructure estimates consumer surplus gains (time saved, cost reduced) as the main benefit measure.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Surplus

Producer surplus is the:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Area above the demand curve
  • B) Revenue minus variable costs / area above supply curve below price
  • C) Total revenue of all firms
  • D) Area below the demand curve
Show Answer

Answer: B) Revenue minus variable costs / area above supply curve below price

PS = price received - minimum acceptable price (MC), summed over all units. Graphically: area above supply curve, below price.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Surplus

Total economic surplus equals:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) GDP
  • B) Consumer surplus plus producer surplus
  • C) Government tax revenue
  • D) Total firm profit
Show Answer

Answer: B) Consumer surplus plus producer surplus

Total surplus (TS) = CS + PS. It measures the total gains from trade in a market.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Surplus

At equilibrium P*=\(70 with Qd=Qs=200. The supply curve starts at P=\)30. Calculate producer surplus if supply is linear.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $4,000

PS = 0.5 × base × height = 0.5 × 200 × (70-30) = 0.5 × 200 × 40 = $4,000

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Surplus

What is deadweight loss and why does it represent a social cost?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Deadweight loss is the reduction in total economic surplus caused by a market distortion (tax, price control, monopoly). It represents transactions that would create positive surplus but do not occur — value that is lost to society entirely, not transferred.

DWL is not a transfer from one party to another — it is surplus that disappears.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Surplus

Competitive equilibrium maximises total economic surplus because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Firms earn the most profit
  • B) All mutually beneficial trades occur — every unit where WTP ≥ MC is produced and no units where WTP < MC are produced
  • C) Government revenue is maximised
  • D) Consumer surplus is maximised at the expense of producer surplus
Show Answer

Answer: B) All mutually beneficial trades occur — every unit where WTP ≥ MC is produced and no units where WTP < MC are produced

At P*=MC, every unit that creates positive surplus (WTP > MC) is traded. Any restriction creates DWL.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Elasticity

Price elasticity of demand (PED) is calculated as:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) ΔP/ΔQ
  • B) (ΔQ/Q)/(ΔP/P) — percentage change in quantity demanded divided by percentage change in price
  • C) ΔQ/ΔP
  • D) (ΔP/P)/(ΔQ/Q)
Show Answer

Answer: B) (ΔQ/Q)/(ΔP/P) — percentage change in quantity demanded divided by percentage change in price

PED = %ΔQd / %ΔP. The absolute value indicates responsiveness; negative sign reflects the law of demand.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Elasticity

Petrol price rises 10% and quantity demanded falls 3%. What is the PED?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: PED = -0.3 (inelastic)

PED = %ΔQd/%ΔP = -3%/10% = -0.3. |PED| < 1 → inelastic demand.

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Elasticity

Why is demand for insulin relatively price inelastic?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Insulin is a medical necessity with no close substitutes. Diabetics must purchase it regardless of price — there are no alternatives and health consequences of not consuming are severe.

Medical necessities with no substitutes have highly inelastic demand.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Elasticity

Which good is likely to have the most price elastic demand?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Electricity
  • B) Water
  • C) A specific brand of bottled water (many close substitutes)
  • D) Prescription medication
Show Answer

Answer: C) A specific brand of bottled water (many close substitutes)

A specific brand of bottled water has many close substitutes — if its price rises, consumers easily switch to another brand. Many substitutes → more elastic demand.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Elasticity

Using the midpoint method, calculate PED when price rises from $8 to $10 and quantity falls from 120 to 100.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: PED = -0.82

Midpoint P=(8+10)/2=9; midpoint Q=(120+100)/2=110. %ΔQ=-20/110=-18.2%; %ΔP=2/9=22.2%. PED=-18.2/22.2≈-0.82 (inelastic)

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Elasticity

Along a downward-sloping linear demand curve, demand is elastic at:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Low prices and high quantities
  • B) High prices and low quantities
  • C) All points equally
  • D) Only at the midpoint
Show Answer

Answer: B) High prices and low quantities

At high prices and low quantities, %ΔQ is large relative to %ΔP — elastic. At low prices and high quantities, %ΔP is large relative to %ΔQ — inelastic.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Elasticity

When demand is elastic (|PED|>1) and a firm raises its price, total revenue:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Rises
  • B) Falls — quantity falls by a larger percentage than price rises
  • C) Stays the same
  • D) Rises then falls
Show Answer

Answer: B) Falls — quantity falls by a larger percentage than price rises

With elastic demand, a price rise causes a proportionally larger quantity fall, reducing total revenue (TR = P × Q).

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Elasticity

A cinema charges $15 per ticket and sells 200 tickets. It cuts price to $12 and sells 260 tickets. Calculate the change in total revenue and the approximate PED.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: TR rises $180; PED ≈ -1.5 (elastic)

TR: was $3,000, now \(3,120 → +\)120 (note: +$120 not $180; recheck). PED=(60/230)/(-3/13.5)≈26%/22%≈-1.18 (elastic — TR rose consistent with elastic demand below midpoint... check signs).

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Elasticity

At what point on a linear demand curve is total revenue maximised?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Total revenue is maximised at the midpoint of a linear demand curve where |PED|=1 (unit elastic). At this point MR=0 and TR is at its peak.

Above the midpoint demand is elastic (TR rises as P falls); below the midpoint demand is inelastic (TR falls as P falls).

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Elasticity

Australian tobacco tax raises the price of cigarettes. The government expects revenue to rise. This implies the government believes demand is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Perfectly elastic
  • B) Inelastic — a price rise reduces quantity less than proportionally so TR (and tax revenue) rises
  • C) Unit elastic
  • D) Perfectly inelastic
Show Answer

Answer: B) Inelastic — a price rise reduces quantity less than proportionally so TR (and tax revenue) rises

Tax revenue = tax × quantity sold. If demand is inelastic, quantity falls less than price rises → revenue rises. This is the Treasury assumption behind tobacco tax rises.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Elasticity

Price elasticity of supply (PES) measures:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) How supply shifts when demand changes
  • B) The percentage change in quantity supplied divided by percentage change in price
  • C) The slope of the supply curve
  • D) How fast firms enter the market
Show Answer

Answer: B) The percentage change in quantity supplied divided by percentage change in price

PES = %ΔQs/%ΔP. A high PES means suppliers respond strongly to price changes.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Elasticity

Income elasticity of demand (YED) for a normal good is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Negative
  • B) Positive — when income rises demand rises for a normal good
  • C) Zero
  • D) Greater than 1 always
Show Answer

Answer: B) Positive — when income rises demand rises for a normal good

YED > 0 for normal goods (income and demand move in the same direction).

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Elasticity

The cross-price elasticity between Coles homebrand and Woolworths homebrand bread is +1.2. This means they are:

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Substitutes

XED > 0 means the goods are substitutes. A rise in the price of one raises demand for the other.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Elasticity

Explain why housing supply in Sydney is relatively price inelastic.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Housing supply is inelastic because construction takes time (planning approvals, building), land in desirable areas is scarce and fixed, and zoning regulations restrict density. Suppliers cannot quickly increase quantity even when prices rise significantly.

Physical and regulatory constraints limit the supply response in the short to medium run.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Elasticity

A 10% rise in avocado prices leads to a 15% rise in demand for hummus (a complement ingredient). Calculate the cross-price elasticity and interpret.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: XED = +1.5 (substitutes? wait — hummus and avocado are complements not substitutes)

XED = +15%/+10% = +1.5. But avocado and hummus are complements — if avocado price rises and hummus demand also rises this seems odd. More likely they are substitutes on toast. XED > 0 → substitutes.

docs.title: Question 26
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Elasticity

A $10 per unit tax is imposed. If demand elasticity is -0.5 and supply elasticity is +2.0, who bears the larger share of the tax?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Producers
  • B) Consumers — less elastic side bears more
  • C) Split equally
  • D) Government bears the tax
Show Answer

Answer: B) Consumers — less elastic side bears more

Consumers (|PED|=0.5) are less elastic than producers (PES=2.0) so consumers bear the larger share: consumer share ≈ PES/(PES+|PED|) = 2/2.5 = 80%.

docs.title: Question 27
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Elasticity

Australia's tobacco excise raises cigarette prices by ~$0.80/cigarette. If a pack of 20 costs $50 and annual consumption is 3 billion packs, calculate annual excise revenue.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $48bn

Excise per pack = 20 × $0.80 = $16. Revenue = $16 × 3bn = \(48bn. (Note: approximate; actual revenue ~\)12bn suggesting lower pack consumption.)

docs.title: Question 28
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Elasticity

Sydney's housing supply is price inelastic and demand rises. Who gains most from the demand increase?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Buyers
  • B) Sellers/landowners — with inelastic supply most of the demand-driven price rise accrues to suppliers
  • C) Both equally
  • D) Government via stamp duty
Show Answer

Answer: B) Sellers/landowners — with inelastic supply most of the demand-driven price rise accrues to suppliers

Inelastic supply means suppliers cannot expand quantity much — most of the price rise from increased demand accrues to existing suppliers (landowners).

docs.title: Question 29
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Elasticity

A carbon tax of $30/tonne is applied to electricity generation. If electricity demand is inelastic (|PED|=0.2) describe the likely welfare effects.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Most of the tax is passed to consumers (inelastic demand → consumers pay ~80% of tax). Consumer surplus falls substantially. Producer surplus falls modestly. DWL is small because quantity barely changes. Revenue raised is large.

Inelastic demand → large revenue, small DWL, large consumer burden.

docs.title: Question 30
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Elasticity

Why do economists generally prefer a broad-based tax (like GST) over a narrow excise for revenue purposes?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) GST raises less revenue
  • B) Broad-based taxes spread the burden across many goods creating less distortion per dollar of revenue than narrow taxes which concentrate the burden on one good
  • C) Excise taxes are unconstitutional
  • D) Narrow taxes encourage more saving
Show Answer

Answer: B) Broad-based taxes spread the burden across many goods creating less distortion per dollar of revenue than narrow taxes which concentrate the burden on one good

A broad-based tax has lower DWL per dollar of revenue because each good faces a smaller rate — the efficiency cost rises with the square of the tax rate.

docs.title: Module M06 Quiz: Market Equilibrium and Perfect Competition
docs.text:
25 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Which market structure has the most firms?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Oligopoly
  • B) Monopoly
  • C) Perfect competition
  • D) Monopolistic competition
Show Answer

Answer: C) Perfect competition

Perfect competition has a very large number of small price-taking firms — the defining characteristic.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Monopolistic competition differs from perfect competition mainly because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Entry is blocked
  • B) Products are differentiated — each firm has some pricing power over its unique product
  • C) There are few firms
  • D) Firms make economic profit in the long run
Show Answer

Answer: B) Products are differentiated — each firm has some pricing power over its unique product

Monopolistic competition: many firms but differentiated products (brand, style, location) give each some monopoly-like pricing power.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Australia has four major banks (CBA, NAB, ANZ, Westpac) with combined market share >75%. This is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Perfect competition
  • B) Monopoly
  • C) Oligopoly
  • D) Monopolistic competition
Show Answer

Answer: C) Oligopoly

A few large firms with strategic interdependence and significant market share = oligopoly.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

What is a contestable market and why does it limit monopoly pricing?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A contestable market is one with free entry and exit even if currently served by one firm. The threat of entry by rivals disciplines pricing — if the monopolist charges above costs, a new firm will enter and undercut them.

The discipline of potential competition can be as effective as actual competition.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Which market structure is most likely to achieve both allocative and productive efficiency in long-run equilibrium?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Monopoly
  • B) Oligopoly
  • C) Perfect competition — P=MC=min ATC in long run
  • D) Monopolistic competition
Show Answer

Answer: C) Perfect competition — P=MC=min ATC in long run

In perfect competition long-run equilibrium P=MC (allocative) and P=min ATC (productive) — both efficiency conditions are satisfied.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

In perfect competition, the demand curve facing an individual firm is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Downward sloping like the market demand
  • B) Perfectly horizontal at the market price (P = AR = MR)
  • C) Upward sloping
  • D) Vertical
Show Answer

Answer: B) Perfectly horizontal at the market price (P = AR = MR)

A price-taking firm can sell any quantity at the market price — it faces a horizontal demand curve at P*.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

For a perfectly competitive firm P = AR = MR because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) It has market power
  • B) Each extra unit sold earns exactly the market price — the firm can sell any amount without affecting price
  • C) Government sets price equal to average revenue
  • D) Firms collude on price
Show Answer

Answer: B) Each extra unit sold earns exactly the market price — the firm can sell any amount without affecting price

As a price taker, the firm sells each unit at P. Total revenue = P×Q, so MR = P and AR = P always.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

Market price is $25. A wheat farmer's MC at Q=500 tonnes is $20 and at Q=520 is $28. What is the profit-maximising output?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Between 500 and 520 tonnes (approximately 510)

Profit max: MC=P=\(25. Since MC=\)20 at Q=500 (< P, expand) and MC=\(28 at Q=520 (> P, contract), Q* lies between 500-520, approximately where MC=\)25.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

Name two real-world markets that approximate perfect competition and explain why.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: 1. Wheat/grain markets — many small farmers selling a homogeneous product with prices set on commodity exchanges. 2. ASX share trading — many buyers and sellers of identical shares with publicly observable prices.

Both have many participants, standardised products, and price-taking behaviour.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

Why does the market demand curve slope downward even though each individual firm faces a horizontal demand curve?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) It is a contradiction — they cannot both be true
  • B) Market demand aggregates all consumers' behaviour — the market price must fall to induce more total consumption, even though each firm takes the market price as given
  • C) Individual firms control the market price
  • D) Market demand is always steeper than individual demand
Show Answer

Answer: B) Market demand aggregates all consumers' behaviour — the market price must fall to induce more total consumption, even though each firm takes the market price as given

There is no contradiction: the market demand is the aggregate of all consumers while the firm's demand is the price signal it receives from the market.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

The profit-maximisation condition MR=MC means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The firm earns zero profit
  • B) The firm expands output as long as the extra revenue from one more unit exceeds the extra cost, stopping when they are equal
  • C) Marginal cost equals average cost
  • D) The firm always maximises revenue
Show Answer

Answer: B) The firm expands output as long as the extra revenue from one more unit exceeds the extra cost, stopping when they are equal

If MR>MC, an extra unit adds more to revenue than cost — expand. If MR<MC, an extra unit adds more to cost — contract. Profit is maximised at MR=MC.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

P=\(40, ATC=\)35 at Q*=100 units. Calculate total profit.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $500

Profit=(P-ATC)×Q=(40-35)×100=$500

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

P=\(40, ATC=\)35, AVC=\(28, TFC=\)700, Q*=100. Calculate total fixed costs as a share of total cost.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 20%

TC=ATC×Q=\(3,500. TFC=\)700. Share=700/3500=20%

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

A firm's MC=\(50 and MR=\)60 at current output. What should it do?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Expand output. Since MR>MC, producing one more unit adds $60 to revenue but only $50 to costs — a net gain of $10. The firm should keep expanding until MR=MC.

When MR>MC, expanding output increases profit.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

Alternative firm objectives include revenue maximisation. A revenue-maximising firm produces where:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) MR=MC
  • B) MR=0 (sells as long as MR is positive)
  • C) P=ATC
  • D) MC=0
Show Answer

Answer: B) MR=0 (sells as long as MR is positive)

Revenue is maximised where MR=0 (unit elastic demand). This typically means higher output and lower price than profit maximisation.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

A firm should shut down in the short run if:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) It is making zero economic profit
  • B) Price falls below average variable cost (P < AVC)
  • C) Its fixed costs exceed variable costs
  • D) Average total cost exceeds price
Show Answer

Answer: B) Price falls below average variable cost (P < AVC)

If P < AVC, the firm cannot cover its variable costs — every unit produced deepens the loss beyond the fixed cost loss of shutting down.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

P=\(18, AVC=\)20, ATC=$28. Should the firm produce or shut down?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Shut down

P=\(18 < AVC=\)20 → shut down. The firm loses less by shutting down (loses only TFC) than by producing (loses TFC + per-unit shortfall on AVC).

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

P=\(25, AVC=\)22, ATC=$30, Q*=200. Calculate the firm's loss and compare to shutdown loss.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Loss operating = $1,000; Shutdown loss = TFC = $1,600

Operating loss=(P-ATC)×Q=(25-30)×200=-\(1,000. TFC=(ATC-AVC)×Q=(30-22)×200=\)1,600. Since operating loss $1,000 < TFC $1,600 → produce (minimise loss).

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

Why are fixed costs irrelevant to the shutdown decision?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Fixed costs are sunk in the short run — the firm pays them whether it produces or not. The relevant comparison is whether revenue covers variable costs. If P≥AVC the firm at least recovers its variable costs and contributes something toward fixed costs.

Only avoidable costs (variable costs) matter for the shutdown decision.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

The short-run supply curve of a perfectly competitive firm is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The entire MC curve
  • B) The MC curve above the AVC curve
  • C) The ATC curve
  • D) The AVC curve
Show Answer

Answer: B) The MC curve above the AVC curve

The firm supplies only if P≥AVC. Below that price it shuts down. So supply = MC curve above AVC.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

In long-run equilibrium in perfect competition, economic profit equals:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Maximum possible profit
  • B) Zero — entry of new firms drives price down to min ATC
  • C) A normal rate of return above zero
  • D) Whatever the firm desires
Show Answer

Answer: B) Zero — entry of new firms drives price down to min ATC

Economic profit attracts entry → supply increases → price falls → economic profit → zero. Long-run: P=min ATC=MC, zero economic profit.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

If existing firms are earning economic losses in a competitive industry, in the long run:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) They all immediately close
  • B) Some firms exit → supply decreases → price rises → remaining firms return to normal profit
  • C) Government subsidises the losses
  • D) Consumers must pay higher prices permanently
Show Answer

Answer: B) Some firms exit → supply decreases → price rises → remaining firms return to normal profit

Losses trigger exit → supply falls → price rises until remaining firms break even. The market self-corrects.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

In long-run competitive equilibrium P=\(45 and min ATC=\)45. If demand rises shifting equilibrium to P=$55, describe the adjustment.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: New entry; price returns to $45 in long run

At P=$55, firms earn \(55-\)45=$10 economic profit per unit. New firms enter, supply increases, price falls back toward $45 (min ATC) in the long run.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

Zero economic profit in long-run competitive equilibrium does not mean firms earn no accounting profit. Explain.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Economic profit subtracts the opportunity cost of all inputs including the owner's capital and time. Zero economic profit means the firm earns exactly its opportunity cost — a normal return. Accounting profit ignores implicit opportunity costs so it may be positive.

Normal profit = accounting profit - implicit costs. Zero economic profit = normal (accounting) profit.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

Perfect competition in the long run achieves which efficiency conditions simultaneously?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Allocative efficiency only (P=MC)
  • B) Productive efficiency only (P=min ATC)
  • C) Both allocative (P=MC) and productive (P=min ATC) efficiency
  • D) Neither — perfect competition is inefficient
Show Answer

Answer: C) Both allocative (P=MC) and productive (P=min ATC) efficiency

Long-run competitive equilibrium: P=MC=min ATC. Both allocative and productive efficiency are achieved simultaneously.

docs.title: Module M07 Quiz: Monopoly
docs.text:
30 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Which is a natural barrier to entry for a monopolist?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Government price controls
  • B) Economies of scale so large that one firm can supply the market at lower cost than two
  • C) Patents on all consumer goods
  • D) Advertising expenditure
Show Answer

Answer: B) Economies of scale so large that one firm can supply the market at lower cost than two

Natural monopolies arise when LRATC is still falling at the quantity demanded — one firm is most efficient.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Australia Post holds a legal monopoly on which service?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Parcel delivery
  • B) Standard letter delivery under 250g
  • C) Express post
  • D) International mail
Show Answer

Answer: B) Standard letter delivery under 250g

Australia Post holds a legislated monopoly on ordinary letter delivery — a statutory barrier to entry.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

A firm has 100% market share in widgets. Its monopoly profit is $2m/yr. If barriers fall and a competitor enters with 40% market share, estimate the original firm's new profit assuming linear demand and equal costs.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $720,000

With 60% market share, revenue roughly halves relative to full monopoly. Rough estimate: 60%^2 × $2m ≈ $720k (Cournot-style intuition).

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Explain why network effects create a barrier to entry in technology markets.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Network effects mean a product's value rises with users. An entrant with few users offers less value than the incumbent, making it very hard to attract customers away even with a superior product.

The incumbent's large user base is itself a barrier — switching costs and reduced network value deter defection.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Which of the following is NOT a barrier to entry?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Patent protection
  • B) High sunk costs
  • C) Lower costs than rivals due to scale
  • D) A new firm entering the market
Show Answer

Answer: D) A new firm entering the market

A new firm entering is the result of barriers being overcome, not a barrier itself.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

For a monopolist with linear demand P=100-Q, what is the marginal revenue function?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) MR=100-Q
  • B) MR=100-2Q
  • C) MR=50-Q
  • D) MR=100+Q
Show Answer

Answer: B) MR=100-2Q

For linear demand P=a-bQ, MR=a-2bQ — twice the slope. Here MR=100-2Q.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

Why is marginal revenue less than price for a monopolist?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The monopolist has lower costs
  • B) To sell one more unit the monopolist must lower price on ALL units sold not just the marginal one
  • C) Monopolists face elastic demand
  • D) Government regulation forces lower MR
Show Answer

Answer: B) To sell one more unit the monopolist must lower price on ALL units sold not just the marginal one

The price reduction to sell one more unit applies to all existing units — the revenue gain on the new unit is offset by revenue lost on inframarginal units.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

Demand: P=80-2Q. Calculate MR when Q=10.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $40

MR=80-4Q; at Q=10: MR=80-40=$40.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

Why does the MR curve lie below the demand curve for a monopolist but coincide with it for a perfectly competitive firm?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A price-taking firm can sell any quantity at the market price without affecting it — each unit adds exactly P to revenue. A monopolist must lower price to sell more, so each additional unit adds less than P to total revenue.

Market power means the firm faces a downward-sloping demand, creating a wedge between P and MR.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

At what output does a monopolist maximise revenue (not profit)?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Where MR=MC
  • B) Where MR=0 (unit elastic point on demand)
  • C) Where P=ATC
  • D) Where demand is inelastic
Show Answer

Answer: B) Where MR=0 (unit elastic point on demand)

Total revenue is maximised where MR=0 (|PED|=1). Profit maximisation requires MR=MC which is typically at lower output.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

A monopolist faces P=120-Q and MC=20. What is profit-maximising output?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Q*=50

MR=120-2Q=MC=20 → 2Q=100 → Q*=50

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

Using P=120-Q and MC=20 from Q1, what price does the monopolist charge?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: P*=$70

P=120-Q*=120-50=$70

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

A monopolist charges $70, ATC at profit-max output is $45. Output is 50 units. What is total profit?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $1,250

Profit=(P-ATC)×Q=(70-45)×50=$1,250

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

Why do monopolists always produce in the elastic portion of their demand curve?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: In the inelastic region MR is negative — producing more would reduce total revenue and increase costs, reducing profit. Profit maximisation (MR=MC≥0) requires operating where MR≥0, which is the elastic region.

Where |PED|<1, MR<0 — no profit-maximising firm would produce there.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Market Structure

A monopolist faces MC=ATC=$30 at all outputs. If demand is P=100-Q, what is economic profit?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $1,225

MR=100-2Q=30→Q=35; P=65; Profit=(65-30)×35=$1,225

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

Compared to perfect competition, a monopolist produces:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) More output at a lower price
  • B) Less output at a higher price
  • C) The same output at a higher price
  • D) Less output at a lower price
Show Answer

Answer: B) Less output at a higher price

Monopolist restricts Q below competitive level to raise P above MC, creating deadweight loss.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

Deadweight loss from monopoly arises because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The monopolist earns excess profit
  • B) Units that would create positive surplus are not produced because P>MC
  • C) The government taxes monopoly profit
  • D) Consumers have less income
Show Answer

Answer: B) Units that would create positive surplus are not produced because P>MC

DWL = units not produced where WTP > MC. The monopolist restricts output to Q* < Q_competitive.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

With P=100-Q and MC=20, calculate the deadweight loss from monopoly vs perfect competition.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $1,250

PC: Q=80,P=20. Monopoly: Q=50,P=70. DWL=0.5×(70-20)×(80-50)=0.5×50×30=\(750. (Note: exact value depends on method; triangle area = 0.5×base×height=\)750)

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

Why is natural monopoly difficult to regulate efficiently?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Setting P=MC (efficient) may force the firm to price below ATC causing losses and exit. Setting P=ATC (break-even) eliminates losses but still creates some DWL. The regulator faces a trade-off between efficiency and financial viability.

There is no pricing rule that achieves both allocative efficiency and financial sustainability without a subsidy.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

Which best describes the welfare transfer in a monopoly compared to competitive equilibrium?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Society gains welfare from monopoly pricing
  • B) Consumer surplus is transferred to producer surplus plus there is an irreversible DWL
  • C) Producer surplus is transferred to consumers
  • D) There is no welfare change
Show Answer

Answer: B) Consumer surplus is transferred to producer surplus plus there is an irreversible DWL

The monopoly profit rectangle represents a transfer from consumers to the monopolist; the DWL triangle is lost to everyone.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

Which condition is NOT required for price discrimination to work?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Market power
  • B) Ability to identify and separate consumer groups
  • C) No arbitrage (resale) between groups
  • D) Government permission to discriminate
Show Answer

Answer: D) Government permission to discriminate

Price discrimination requires market power, identifiable groups, and prevention of resale — no government permission is needed.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

Student discounts at cinemas are an example of:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) First-degree price discrimination
  • B) Second-degree price discrimination
  • C) Third-degree price discrimination
  • D) Predatory pricing
Show Answer

Answer: C) Third-degree price discrimination

Third-degree PD charges different prices to identifiable groups (students vs adults) based on different demand elasticities.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

A monopolist can perfectly price discriminate. Demand: P=100-Q, MC=20. What is total output and profit?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Q=80; Profit=$3,200

With perfect PD, firm sets P=MC at margin → Q=80 (same as PC). Profit = entire consumer surplus = area of triangle = 0.5×80×80=$3,200

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

Why does first-degree price discrimination eliminate deadweight loss?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: The firm charges each consumer exactly their WTP, so every unit where WTP≥MC is sold. No mutually beneficial transactions are foregone — the efficient quantity is produced.

With perfect PD, the firm captures all surplus but produces the efficient quantity.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

Airlines charge different prices to business and leisure travellers for the same route. This is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) First-degree PD
  • B) Second-degree PD
  • C) Third-degree PD
  • D) Not price discrimination because the seats are identical
Show Answer

Answer: C) Third-degree PD

Airlines identify business/leisure travellers (via booking timing, flexibility) and exploit their different elasticities — classic third-degree PD.

docs.title: Question 26
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Market Structure

The ACCC can authorise a merger if it finds the merger will not:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Increase profits for the merging firms
  • B) Substantially lessen competition in a market
  • C) Create a firm larger than $1bn
  • D) Reduce employment
Show Answer

Answer: B) Substantially lessen competition in a market

The ACCC assesses mergers under the substantial lessening of competition (SLC) test under the Competition and Consumer Act 2010.

docs.title: Question 27
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Market Structure

A natural monopoly has LRATC=\(30 at all outputs and demand P=80-Q. The regulator sets P=LRATC=\)30. What is output?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Q=50

P=80-Q=30 → Q=50. At this price the firm breaks even (zero economic profit) but does not achieve allocative efficiency (P>MC only if MC<LRATC).

docs.title: Question 28
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Market Structure

Which regulatory approach gives the NBN operator a guaranteed return on capital but weakest incentive for cost efficiency?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price cap regulation
  • B) Rate-of-return regulation
  • C) Public ownership
  • D) Deregulation
Show Answer

Answer: B) Rate-of-return regulation

Rate-of-return regulation allows the firm to earn a set return — it has no incentive to cut costs since higher costs are passed through.

docs.title: Question 29
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Market Structure

Describe one way the ACCC has used access regulation to promote competition.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: The ACCC can declare infrastructure (e.g. NBN network, gas pipelines, airports) as regulated access services, requiring the owner to provide access to competitors on non-discriminatory terms at regulated prices.

Access regulation prevents infrastructure owners from using their bottleneck position to foreclose downstream competition.

docs.title: Question 30
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Market Structure

Why might the Supermarket Code of Conduct be preferred over a forced divestiture of Coles or Woolworths?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Divestiture is illegal in Australia
  • B) Breaking up firms is irreversible and may sacrifice scale economies; a code addresses conduct without restructuring the industry
  • C) A code generates more ACCC revenue
  • D) Divestiture reduces competition
Show Answer

Answer: B) Breaking up firms is irreversible and may sacrifice scale economies; a code addresses conduct without restructuring the industry

Structural remedies (divestiture) are blunt and permanent. Conduct remedies address specific harmful behaviours while preserving efficiency gains from scale.

docs.title: Module M08 Quiz: Game Theory and Oligopoly
docs.text:
25 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Game Theory

In game theory a 'dominant strategy' is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The strategy that always beats every other strategy regardless of what rivals do
  • B) The strategy chosen by the largest firm
  • C) The strategy that maximises industry profit
  • D) Any strategy chosen in equilibrium
Show Answer

Answer: A) The strategy that always beats every other strategy regardless of what rivals do

A dominant strategy is optimal regardless of what opponents choose — it doesn't depend on their actions.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Game Theory

Coles and Woolworths each choose between High price and Low price. If Low price is a dominant strategy for both, what is the Nash equilibrium?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Both choose High
  • B) Both choose Low
  • C) Coles chooses High, Woolworths chooses Low
  • D) No equilibrium exists
Show Answer

Answer: B) Both choose Low

When both firms have Low as dominant strategy, (Low, Low) is the Nash equilibrium — neither wants to deviate unilaterally.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Game Theory

In a 2-player simultaneous game Firm A earns $4m if both advertise, $6m if only A advertises, $1m if only B advertises, $3m if neither advertises. What is Firm A's dominant strategy?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Advertise

If B advertises: A earns $4m (advertise) vs $1m (don't) → advertise. If B doesn't: A earns $6m (advertise) vs $3m (don't) → advertise. Advertise dominates.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Game Theory

Explain the concept of a Nash Equilibrium in plain language.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A Nash Equilibrium is a set of strategies where no player can improve their outcome by changing their own strategy, given what all other players are doing. Everyone is playing their best response to everyone else.

At Nash Equilibrium, each player's strategy is optimal given the strategies of all other players.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Game Theory

Which real-world situation is best modelled as a simultaneous game?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A firm deciding its price after observing a rival's price
  • B) Two firms simultaneously setting prices without knowing each other's choice
  • C) A firm deciding whether to enter a market after an incumbent has already invested
  • D) A worker negotiating a wage with their employer
Show Answer

Answer: B) Two firms simultaneously setting prices without knowing each other's choice

Simultaneous games model decisions made without knowledge of rivals' choices — common in pricing, advertising, and R&D races.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Game Theory

In the classic Prisoners' Dilemma both prisoners confess even though both would be better off staying silent. This happens because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) They are irrational
  • B) Confess is a dominant strategy for each prisoner individually even though mutual silence gives a better collective outcome
  • C) The police force them to confess
  • D) They don't know the payoffs
Show Answer

Answer: B) Confess is a dominant strategy for each prisoner individually even though mutual silence gives a better collective outcome

Confess dominates silence for each prisoner individually — the individually rational outcome is collectively suboptimal.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Game Theory

Two firms in a Prisoners' Dilemma each earn $5m if they collude, $2m each if both defect, \(8m for the defector/\)1m for the cooperator. What is the Nash equilibrium payoff for each firm?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $2m each

Both firms defect (dominant strategy) → each earns \(2m — the Nash equilibrium despite the fact that mutual cooperation (\)5m each) is better.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

Which feature of oligopoly markets makes game theory essential?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Many small firms
  • B) Strategic interdependence — each firm's profit depends on rivals' decisions
  • C) Perfect information
  • D) Free entry and exit
Show Answer

Answer: B) Strategic interdependence — each firm's profit depends on rivals' decisions

Oligopolists must consider how rivals will react to their decisions — classic strategic interdependence modelled by game theory.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Game Theory

Why does repetition of the Prisoners' Dilemma make cooperation more likely?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: In a repeated game players can use tit-for-tat strategies — cooperate if the other cooperated last period, defect if they defected. The threat of future punishment makes defection less attractive when both parties value the ongoing relationship.

Cooperation can be sustained if the future payoff from cooperation exceeds the one-time gain from defecting.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Game Theory

ACCC leniency policy encourages cartel members to report the cartel by offering immunity from prosecution. How does this affect cartel stability?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) It strengthens cartels by clarifying rules
  • B) It destabilises cartels by making defection (reporting) more attractive — turning the cartel into a Prisoners' Dilemma
  • C) It has no effect on cartel stability
  • D) It makes cartels more profitable
Show Answer

Answer: B) It destabilises cartels by making defection (reporting) more attractive — turning the cartel into a Prisoners' Dilemma

Leniency creates a race to report: each member fears a rival will report first, so the dominant strategy shifts toward defecting (reporting), destabilising the cartel.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

Australia's banking sector is dominated by the Big Four (CBA, NAB, ANZ, Westpac). This is an example of:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Perfect competition
  • B) Monopoly
  • C) Oligopoly
  • D) Monopolistic competition
Show Answer

Answer: C) Oligopoly

A market with a small number of large firms with strategic interdependence is an oligopoly.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

The kinked demand curve model predicts price rigidity in oligopoly because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Firms always collude on price
  • B) If a firm raises price rivals don't follow (so it loses customers) but if it cuts price rivals match (so it gains little) — demand is elastic above and inelastic below the current price
  • C) Government regulations prevent price changes
  • D) Oligopolists always charge MC
Show Answer

Answer: B) If a firm raises price rivals don't follow (so it loses customers) but if it cuts price rivals match (so it gains little) — demand is elastic above and inelastic below the current price

The asymmetric response creates a kink at the current price — the firm has little incentive to change price in either direction.

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

If an oligopolist in a kinked demand market has MC=\(20 and the kink is at P=\)50, what happens to price if MC rises to $35?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Price stays at $50

The MR curve has a gap at the kink. If MC stays within this gap price is unchanged. MC rising from $20 to $35 (still within the gap) leaves price rigid at $50.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

Why might tacit collusion be more stable in a market with few, transparent firms?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: With few firms it is easier to detect cheating on an implicit agreement. Price transparency means deviations are quickly observed and can be punished (e.g. price war), making the expected cost of defection high.

Monitoring and punishment are easier with fewer players and observable prices.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

Which Australian industry has been found by the ACCC to have engaged in price-fixing cartel conduct?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Wool farming
  • B) Visy/Amcor in the cardboard packaging industry
  • C) The NBN
  • D) Small supermarkets
Show Answer

Answer: B) Visy/Amcor in the cardboard packaging industry

The ACCC prosecuted Visy and Amcor for a decade-long cardboard packaging cartel — one of Australia's most significant cartel cases.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory

Price-fixing between competitors is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Legal if prices stay below the CPI
  • B) Illegal in Australia under the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
  • C) Only illegal for public companies
  • D) Legal if approved by the ACCC
Show Answer

Answer: B) Illegal in Australia under the Competition and Consumer Act 2010

Cartel conduct including price-fixing is a criminal offence in Australia under the CCA 2010 with penalties up to 10 years imprisonment and $50m fines.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory

Two airlines secretly agree to charge $400 for Sydney-Melbourne. Without the agreement each would charge $300. If market demand at $400 is 500 passengers per flight, estimate combined cartel profit premium vs competitive baseline.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $50,000 per flight

Extra revenue per flight = (\(400-\)300)×500=$50,000. This is the transfer from consumers to the cartel (before cost considerations).

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory

Why is a cartel inherently unstable even without regulatory intervention?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Each cartel member has an individual incentive to cheat by cutting price slightly below the agreed level to steal customers. Since all members face this incentive, the cartel tends to collapse — a classic Prisoners' Dilemma.

The cooperative outcome is not a Nash equilibrium: each member prefers to defect given others' cooperation.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory
