Which ACCC policy makes it easier to prosecute cartels by incentivising members to provide evidence?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Merger authorisation
  • B) Leniency policy (immunity for the first member to report the cartel)
  • C) Access regulation
  • D) Market study powers
Show Answer

Answer: B) Leniency policy (immunity for the first member to report the cartel)

The ACCC's immunity/leniency policy offers the first cartel member to self-report full immunity from prosecution, destabilising cartels.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory

Tacit collusion is harder to prosecute than explicit collusion because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) It is legal under Australian law
  • B) There is no direct communication or agreement to prove — firms independently arrive at similar strategies
  • C) It only occurs in competitive markets
  • D) The ACCC lacks jurisdiction over tacit collusion
Show Answer

Answer: B) There is no direct communication or agreement to prove — firms independently arrive at similar strategies

Tacit collusion involves parallel pricing without explicit agreement — no smoking gun communication exists, making legal proof very difficult.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

In a sequential entry game the incumbent invests in large capacity before the potential entrant decides. This is an example of:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Nash equilibrium in simultaneous games
  • B) First-mover advantage via credible commitment
  • C) Tacit collusion
  • D) Predatory pricing
Show Answer

Answer: B) First-mover advantage via credible commitment

By committing to high capacity first the incumbent credibly signals it will produce a lot if entry occurs, making entry unprofitable — a first-mover advantage.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

In backward induction what do we solve first?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The first decision in the game
  • B) The last decision in the game
  • C) All decisions simultaneously
  • D) The dominant strategy
Show Answer

Answer: B) The last decision in the game

Backward induction starts at the end of the game tree and works backwards, finding optimal decisions at each node given what will happen next.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

Incumbent earns $10m (monopoly) or $4m (duopoly). Entrant earns $3m (enter) or $0 (stay out). If incumbent can commit to 'fight entry' earning only $2m as duopolist, does entry occur?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: No entry

Entrant compares $3m (enter, face fight → duopoly $3m) vs $0. But if incumbent credibly commits to fight, duopoly profit for entrant may be negative. Assuming fight makes entry unprofitable → no entry.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

Why must a commitment device be credible to deter entry?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: An incredible threat (one the firm would not actually carry out after entry) will be ignored by the entrant. The commitment must change the incumbent's payoff so that fighting entry actually becomes rational ex post, not just a bluff.

Credibility requires the threat to be a best response after entry has occurred, not just before.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

Woolworths builds a new distribution centre before Aldi announces Australian expansion plans. This could be interpreted as:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Predatory pricing
  • B) A commitment to cost efficiency that makes the market less attractive to Aldi
  • C) Collusion with Aldi
  • D) Vertical integration
Show Answer

Answer: B) A commitment to cost efficiency that makes the market less attractive to Aldi

Investing in cost-reducing capacity before a rival's entry decision can credibly signal lower costs and higher future output, deterring entry.

docs.title: Module M09 Quiz: Price Regulation, Taxes, and Subsidies
docs.text:
25 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

A price ceiling set BELOW the market equilibrium price will cause:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A surplus
  • B) A shortage
  • C) No change in quantity
  • D) An increase in supply
Show Answer

Answer: B) A shortage

A binding price ceiling prevents the price from rising to equilibrium — quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied, creating a persistent shortage.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

Sydney rent control sets maximum rent at $400/wk when equilibrium is $600/wk. If demand is QD=1000-Q×(1/2) and supply QS=Q×(1/2), calculate the shortage at $400.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 400 units

QD at $400: 1000-(400/2)=800. QS at $400: 400/2=200. Shortage=800-200=600. (Exact answer depends on parameterisation; key insight is QD>QS.)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

Who benefits from rent control and who is harmed?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Existing tenants who keep their rent-controlled apartments benefit from lower rents. New entrants (e.g. young workers, migrants) are harmed because fewer units are available and landlords may allocate by non-price means (connections, discrimination).

Rent control redistributes from landlords and would-be tenants to existing tenants.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

A price ceiling causes a persistent shortage. What non-price allocation mechanisms might emerge?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The price rises to equilibrium
  • B) Queuing, rationing, black markets, and allocation by seller preference
  • C) Supply increases to meet demand
  • D) The good disappears from the market entirely
Show Answer

Answer: B) Queuing, rationing, black markets, and allocation by seller preference

When prices can't ration, other mechanisms emerge: queues, waiting lists, black markets, or allocating to preferred customers.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Supply Demand

In the long run a price ceiling is likely to cause even greater housing shortages than in the short run because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) More people move to the city
  • B) Supply is more elastic in the long run — landlords convert properties to other uses or reduce maintenance reducing housing stock
  • C) Demand falls over time
  • D) The ceiling is adjusted for inflation
Show Answer

Answer: B) Supply is more elastic in the long run — landlords convert properties to other uses or reduce maintenance reducing housing stock

Long-run supply is more elastic: landlords exit the market, convert to commercial use, or let quality deteriorate — shrinking supply further.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

Australia's minimum wage is set by:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The RBA
  • B) The Fair Work Commission
  • C) Individual enterprise agreements only
  • D) The ACCC
Show Answer

Answer: B) The Fair Work Commission

Australia's National Minimum Wage is set annually by the Fair Work Commission through a wage review process.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

A minimum wage of $25/hr is set above the competitive equilibrium of $20/hr. Using a labour demand/supply diagram, what is the effect on employment?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Employment rises
  • B) Employment falls — labour demand decreases and labour supply increases creating a surplus of labour (unemployment)
  • C) Employment is unchanged
  • D) Wages rise further
Show Answer

Answer: B) Employment falls — labour demand decreases and labour supply increases creating a surplus of labour (unemployment)

A binding wage floor creates excess supply of labour (unemployment): firms demand less labour at the higher wage while more workers seek employment.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

If labour demand is LD=1000-20W and supply is LS=200+10W, calculate the competitive equilibrium wage and employment.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: W=$26.67, L=467

LD=LS: 1000-20W=200+10W → 800=30W → W=$26.67; L=1000-20(26.67)≈467

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

Why might a minimum wage cause less unemployment than standard supply-demand analysis predicts when employers have monopsony power?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A monopsonist pays below the competitive wage and hires fewer workers. A minimum wage up to the competitive level can actually increase both wages and employment by counteracting monopsony power.

With monopsony, the competitive wage floor raises wages without necessarily reducing employment.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Supply Demand

Agricultural price support in Australia historically set a floor price for wheat above equilibrium. What problem did this create?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Wheat shortages
  • B) Wheat surpluses requiring government stockpiling or export subsidies
  • C) Lower wheat prices
  • D) Increased competition from imports
Show Answer

Answer: B) Wheat surpluses requiring government stockpiling or export subsidies

A price floor above equilibrium creates a surplus — producers supply more than consumers demand at the supported price.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

If demand is perfectly inelastic and supply is elastic, who bears the entire burden of a tax?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Producers
  • B) Consumers
  • C) Split equally
  • D) Government
Show Answer

Answer: B) Consumers

When demand is perfectly inelastic, consumers pay the same quantity regardless of price — the entire tax is passed on to consumers.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

Australia's fuel excise is approximately 49.6 cents per litre. If petrol demand elasticity is -0.25 and supply is elastic, roughly what share of the excise do consumers pay?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: ~80%

With inelastic demand (|PED|=0.25) consumers bear the larger share. Share borne by consumers ≈ Es/(Es+|Ed|) ≈ large/large+0.25 ≈ ~80% (with elastic supply).

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

A per-unit tax of $T causes deadweight loss because:

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: The tax creates a wedge between the price consumers pay and producers receive, reducing quantity below the efficient level. Units that would have created positive surplus (WTP>MC) are not traded.

The tax distorts the quantity decision away from the efficient level where supply meets demand.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

A tax on cigarettes in Australia raises $12bn/year. If quantity sold falls 15% after the tax, is this consistent with the goal of reducing smoking or maximising revenue?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Only maximising revenue
  • B) Only reducing smoking
  • C) Consistent with both — significant revenue is raised while consumption falls
  • D) Neither goal is met
Show Answer

Answer: C) Consistent with both — significant revenue is raised while consumption falls

A well-designed tobacco tax achieves both: raises revenue and reduces smoking. Australia's tobacco tax is explicitly designed for health and revenue objectives.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Supply Demand

Who bears more of a tax burden: the side with more elastic demand/supply or the side with less elastic demand/supply?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) More elastic side
  • B) Less elastic side — tax burden falls on whichever side can less easily adjust quantity
  • C) Equally on both sides
  • D) Always the consumer
Show Answer

Answer: B) Less elastic side — tax burden falls on whichever side can less easily adjust quantity

The less elastic side has fewer alternatives and cannot easily reduce quantity — they absorb more of the price change from the tax.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

A per-unit subsidy shifts the supply curve:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Upward by the subsidy amount
  • B) Downward by the subsidy amount (rightward shift)
  • C) Has no effect on supply
  • D) Increases consumer price
Show Answer

Answer: B) Downward by the subsidy amount (rightward shift)

A subsidy reduces the effective cost of production by the subsidy amount, shifting supply right (down) — producers supply more at each price.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

Australia's childcare subsidy reduces the price parents pay. A subsidy of $50/day is introduced. If market price was $120/day and demand elasticity is -0.8, estimate the consumer price reduction.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: ~$28

With elastic demand, consumers capture more of the subsidy. Consumer price falls by roughly subsidy × (Es/(Es+|Ed|)). Assuming Es=1.5: \(50×(1.5/2.3)≈\)33. Approximate answer in range \(25-\)35.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

Why does a subsidy create a deadweight loss even though it benefits both buyers and sellers?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A subsidy encourages overproduction — units are produced where the social cost (supply price) exceeds the social benefit (demand price). The government pays for units that create less value than they cost.

Subsidies push output beyond the efficient level, wasting resources on low-value production.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

Australia's Renewable Energy Target subsidised solar power. From an economic efficiency perspective this is justified if:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Solar firms are profitable
  • B) Solar power has positive externalities (reduced emissions) so the social benefit exceeds the private benefit — a subsidy corrects the externality
  • C) The government wants to support manufacturing
  • D) Energy prices are too high
Show Answer

Answer: B) Solar power has positive externalities (reduced emissions) so the social benefit exceeds the private benefit — a subsidy corrects the externality

Subsidising positive externalities (clean energy) corrects market underproduction — the private market undersupplies because it ignores the social benefit of lower emissions.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Supply Demand

The cost to government of a per-unit subsidy equals:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The change in consumer surplus
  • B) Subsidy amount × quantity sold after subsidy
  • C) The deadweight loss only
  • D) Producer surplus gain only
Show Answer

Answer: B) Subsidy amount × quantity sold after subsidy

Government cost = subsidy per unit × equilibrium quantity after subsidy. This is greater than the net welfare gain because some goes to DWL.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Externalities

Australia's carbon price (2012-14) was set at $23/tonne of CO2. This is an example of:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A price floor
  • B) A Pigouvian tax
  • C) A production quota
  • D) A subsidy
Show Answer

Answer: B) A Pigouvian tax

A carbon price is a Pigouvian tax designed to make emitters pay the social cost of carbon, internalising the negative externality.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Externalities

The Safeguard Mechanism (2023) requires large emitters to reduce emissions intensity or purchase offsets. This is closer to:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A Pigouvian tax
  • B) A tradeable permit scheme
  • C) A command-and-control regulation with trading
  • D) A price subsidy
Show Answer

Answer: C) A command-and-control regulation with trading

The Safeguard Mechanism sets limits (command) but allows trading of offsets (permit-like flexibility) — a hybrid instrument.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Supply Demand

Australia's fuel excise is 49.6c/L. If a car uses 8L/100km and drives 15,000km/year, calculate annual fuel excise paid.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $595.20

Fuel used = 0.08×15,000=1,200L. Excise = 1,200×\(0.496=\)595.20

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Externalities

Compare the efficiency of a carbon tax vs a command-and-control emissions standard.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A carbon tax lets firms reduce emissions where it is cheapest, achieving a given emissions reduction at minimum cost (cost-effective). A uniform standard forces all firms to meet the same target regardless of their abatement costs, which is usually more expensive in aggregate.

Taxes achieve environmental goals cost-effectively; standards may not equate marginal abatement costs across firms.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Supply Demand

Rent Assistance is a Commonwealth payment to low-income renters. How does this differ from rent control?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Both are price ceilings
  • B) Rent Assistance is a demand-side subsidy that raises renters' effective purchasing power without distorting the rental market price; rent control is a supply-side price ceiling that creates shortages
  • C) Rent Assistance reduces supply of rental housing
  • D) They are identical in economic effect
Show Answer

Answer: B) Rent Assistance is a demand-side subsidy that raises renters' effective purchasing power without distorting the rental market price; rent control is a supply-side price ceiling that creates shortages

Rent Assistance increases demand-side affordability while leaving market prices intact. Rent control caps prices and creates shortages — very different economic effects.

docs.title: Module M10 Quiz: Externalities and Market Failure
docs.text:
25 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Externalities

A negative externality occurs when:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A firm earns negative profit
  • B) The production or consumption of a good imposes costs on third parties not reflected in the market price
  • C) A good is produced at too low a cost
  • D) Government fails to provide a public good
Show Answer

Answer: B) The production or consumption of a good imposes costs on third parties not reflected in the market price

Negative externalities impose uncompensated costs on third parties — classic market failure.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Externalities

Coal mining imposes dust and noise costs on nearby residents estimated at $8/tonne. If the market price of coal is $80/tonne and the private marginal cost is $60/tonne, what is the social marginal cost?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $68/tonne

MSC = MPC + external cost = $60 + $8 = $68/tonne

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Externalities

From Q2, the efficient price of coal (where P=MSC) would be \(68. The market produces at P=MC=\)60. Is the market overproducing or underproducing coal?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Underproducing
  • B) Overproducing — market price is too low and output too high relative to the social optimum
  • C) Producing the efficient quantity
  • D) It depends on demand elasticity
Show Answer

Answer: B) Overproducing — market price is too low and output too high relative to the social optimum

Because the private market ignores the $8 external cost, it prices too low and produces more than the socially optimal quantity.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Externalities

Give one example each of a positive and negative externality relevant to Australia.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Negative: carbon emissions from coal-fired power (imposes climate costs on all). Positive: vaccination against influenza (reduces infection risk for non-vaccinated people).

Any valid Australian examples accepted — the key is third-party effects not captured in market prices.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Externalities

Which market outcome does a negative production externality cause?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Too little output at too high a price
  • B) Too much output at too low a price
  • C) Efficient output but wrong distribution
  • D) Zero output
Show Answer

Answer: B) Too much output at too low a price

MPC < MSC means producers ignore part of the cost → market price is too low → overproduction relative to the efficient level.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Externalities

The Coase theorem states that private bargaining achieves efficiency if:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Government sets the right tax
  • B) Property rights are clearly defined and transaction costs are low
  • C) Both parties have equal bargaining power
  • D) The good is a public good
Show Answer

Answer: B) Property rights are clearly defined and transaction costs are low

Coase's insight: if property rights are clear and transactions are costless, parties will bargain to the efficient outcome regardless of who holds the rights.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Externalities

A factory pollutes a river damaging a fishery downstream. Pollution abatement costs $100k; damage to fishery is $150k. Under Coase what is the efficient outcome?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Abate pollution; net gain $50k

It's efficient to abate: social gain = $150k damage avoided - $100k abatement cost = $50k. Regardless of property rights, parties should bargain to this outcome if transaction costs are low.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Externalities

Why does the Coase theorem often fail in practice for environmental problems like river pollution?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Transaction costs are high — many affected parties (fishers, residents, recreational users) make negotiation costly. Free-riding on others' negotiations and difficulty measuring damages further impede private bargaining.

Large numbers of affected parties and high transaction costs prevent the costless bargaining the theorem requires.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Externalities

Australia's water trading scheme in the Murray-Darling Basin is an example of:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A Pigouvian tax
  • B) A Coasian approach — property rights in water are defined and tradeable, allowing efficient allocation through market exchange
  • C) A price ceiling on water
  • D) A public good provision
Show Answer

Answer: B) A Coasian approach — property rights in water are defined and tradeable, allowing efficient allocation through market exchange

Defining water entitlements as tradeable property rights allows market exchange to reallocate water to highest-value uses — the Coasian solution.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Externalities

Which condition makes the Coase theorem most likely to work?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Large numbers of affected parties
  • B) Low transaction costs and clearly defined property rights between a small number of parties
  • C) Government-mandated compensation
  • D) Identical valuations by both parties
Show Answer

Answer: B) Low transaction costs and clearly defined property rights between a small number of parties

The Coase theorem works best with few parties, clear rights, and low transaction costs — conditions rarely met in large-scale environmental problems.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Externalities

A Pigouvian tax is set equal to:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The market price of the good
  • B) The marginal external cost at the efficient quantity
  • C) The total profit of the polluting firm
  • D) The government's abatement cost
Show Answer

Answer: B) The marginal external cost at the efficient quantity

The optimal Pigouvian tax equals the marginal external cost at the socially efficient output — it forces producers to internalise the full social cost.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Externalities

A factory's MPC=\(40 and external pollution cost=\)15/unit. An optimal Pigouvian tax is imposed. What price do consumers pay and what price do producers receive?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Consumers pay $55; producers receive $40 (before tax)

Tax=$15. Producers set MPC+tax=price: \(40+\)15=$55. Producers receive $55 but remit $15 tax, netting $40 = their MPC. Efficient outcome.

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Externalities

Why might tradeable permits be preferred over a Pigouvian tax when abatement costs are uncertain?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: With a tax we know the price of emissions but not the quantity. With permits we set the quantity directly — useful when we have a firm emission reduction target. If abatement costs vary across firms, permits also achieve the target cost-effectively through trading.

Permits give certainty over the quantity of pollution; taxes give certainty over the price.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Externalities

A positive externality from education means the market:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Overproduces education
  • B) Underproduces education — private demand is below social demand so the market equilibrium is below the efficient level
  • C) Produces the efficient level
  • D) Produces education only if subsidised
Show Answer

Answer: B) Underproduces education — private demand is below social demand so the market equilibrium is below the efficient level

The private demand for education reflects only private benefits. Social benefits (lower crime, productivity spillovers) mean MSB > MPB → market underproduces.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Externalities

Which policy instrument gives polluters the most flexibility in HOW they reduce emissions?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Uniform emission standard
  • B) Technology mandate (requires specific equipment)
  • C) Pigouvian tax or tradeable permit (firms choose cheapest abatement method)
  • D) Command-and-control regulation
Show Answer

Answer: C) Pigouvian tax or tradeable permit (firms choose cheapest abatement method)

Taxes and permits set a price on emissions but let firms choose the least-cost abatement method — maximising flexibility and cost-effectiveness.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Externalities

Australia's carbon tax (2012-2014) was set at $23/tonne. Which government repealed it?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Rudd Labor
  • B) Abbott Coalition
  • C) Gillard Labor
  • D) Morrison Coalition
Show Answer

Answer: B) Abbott Coalition

The Abbott Coalition government repealed the carbon price in July 2014, replacing it with the Direct Action policy.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Externalities

Under the Safeguard Mechanism a firm emits 1.2 million tonnes CO2 against a 1 million tonne baseline. It must surrender credits for 200,000 tonnes at $30/credit. Calculate the compliance cost.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $6 million

200,000 tonnes × $30/tonne = $6 million

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Externalities

Compare the economic efficiency of Australia's current Safeguard Mechanism with the 2012-14 carbon tax.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Both put a price on carbon. The carbon tax was simpler and covered more emissions. The Safeguard Mechanism covers only large emitters (>100k tonnes) and uses baselines rather than an economy-wide price — potentially less cost-effective but more politically durable.

The carbon tax provided a uniform price signal; the Safeguard Mechanism is more targeted and complex.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Externalities

Why is fuel excise NOT a pure Pigouvian tax on carbon emissions?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) It is set too high
  • B) It was designed primarily for road funding and revenue not to price the carbon externality from fuel combustion — and it applies uniformly rather than proportionally to emissions
  • C) It only applies to diesel
  • D) It applies to all goods
Show Answer

Answer: B) It was designed primarily for road funding and revenue not to price the carbon externality from fuel combustion — and it applies uniformly rather than proportionally to emissions

Australia's fuel excise predates carbon pricing and was designed as a user charge. It happens to reduce fuel use but is not calibrated to the marginal external cost of CO2.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Externalities

What does 'carbon leakage' mean in the context of Australian climate policy?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Carbon being physically captured and stored underground
  • B) Emissions-intensive industries relocating to countries without carbon pricing, potentially increasing global emissions
  • C) The carbon price declining over time
  • D) Government revenue from carbon permits being spent inefficiently
Show Answer

Answer: B) Emissions-intensive industries relocating to countries without carbon pricing, potentially increasing global emissions

Carbon leakage occurs when domestic carbon pricing makes Australian industry uncompetitive, causing production to shift offshore to lower-regulation countries.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Externalities

A network externality means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Networks are more expensive to build
  • B) The value of a product or service increases as more people use it
  • C) Externalities only affect computer networks
  • D) Network goods are non-rival
Show Answer

Answer: B) The value of a product or service increases as more people use it

Network externalities (or network effects): each additional user increases the value of the network for all existing users (e.g. phone, social media, payment systems).

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Externalities

If Afterpay has 5 million Australian users and each additional user adds $2 of value to all existing users, calculate total network value added when the 5,000,001st user joins.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $10 million

Value added = $2 × 5,000,000 existing users = $10 million. This illustrates how network value grows super-linearly.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Externalities

Why do markets with strong network effects tend toward 'winner-take-all' outcomes?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Users prefer the platform with the most users (highest network value). This creates a positive feedback loop: more users → more value → more users → dominant platform. Smaller rivals offer less value, making it hard to overcome the incumbent's lead.

Positive feedback loops from network effects concentrate users on one platform.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Externalities

EFTPOS in Australia has high merchant adoption (near-universal). A rival payment system faces:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) No barriers to entry
  • B) A chicken-and-egg problem — merchants won't adopt without consumers and consumers won't use without merchants; the incumbent's large installed base is a major barrier
  • C) Only cost barriers
  • D) A legal monopoly
Show Answer

Answer: B) A chicken-and-egg problem — merchants won't adopt without consumers and consumers won't use without merchants; the incumbent's large installed base is a major barrier

Two-sided network effects create the chicken-and-egg problem: both sides need to be on the platform simultaneously, making it very hard for a new entrant to reach critical mass.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Externalities

From a competition policy perspective network externalities are concerning because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) They always lead to lower prices
  • B) They can entrench dominant platforms even if superior alternatives exist reducing consumer choice and innovation
  • C) They increase economic efficiency
  • D) The ACCC has no jurisdiction over digital markets
Show Answer

Answer: B) They can entrench dominant platforms even if superior alternatives exist reducing consumer choice and innovation

Network effects can create durable market power that resists competitive pressure even from better products — a major concern for digital markets regulation.

docs.title: Module M11 Quiz: Public Goods and Common Resources
docs.text:
25 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Public Goods

A good that is both non-rival and non-excludable is called:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A private good
  • B) A club good
  • C) A public good
  • D) A common resource
Show Answer

Answer: C) A public good

Public goods are non-rival (one person's consumption doesn't reduce availability) and non-excludable (cannot prevent non-payers from consuming).

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Public Goods

National defence is a classic example of a public good because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The government provides it
  • B) Once provided it protects all citizens simultaneously (non-rival) and no citizen can be excluded from its protection (non-excludable)
  • C) It is very expensive
  • D) Only taxpayers benefit
Show Answer

Answer: B) Once provided it protects all citizens simultaneously (non-rival) and no citizen can be excluded from its protection (non-excludable)

Defence is non-rival (protecting one citizen doesn't reduce protection for others) and non-excludable (can't protect some citizens while leaving others unprotected).

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Public Goods

Classify each good: (a) a toll road (b) a crowded public beach (c) a private gym (d) ABC Radio.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: (a) Club good (excludable non-rival until congested) (b) Common resource (rival non-excludable) (c) Private good (rival excludable) (d) Public good (non-rival non-excludable)

Classification depends on rivalry and excludability — the 2×2 matrix of good types.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Public Goods

Explain why a fish in the ocean is a common resource but a fish in an aquaculture farm is a private good.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Ocean fish are non-excludable (anyone can fish in open waters) and rival (catching one fish reduces availability to others) — a common resource. Farmed fish are owned by the farm operator (excludable) — a private good with a clear property right.

Property rights transform common resources into private goods.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Public Goods

Which good is most likely to be under-provided by the private market?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A private gym
  • B) A toll road
  • C) Flood warning systems (non-rival, non-excludable)
  • D) A restaurant meal
Show Answer

Answer: C) Flood warning systems (non-rival, non-excludable)

Public goods like flood warnings cannot be profitably provided privately because non-excludability prevents the provider from charging all beneficiaries.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Public Goods

The free-rider problem means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Some consumers ride public transport for free
  • B) People consume a public good without paying because they cannot be excluded — private firms cannot profitably provide the good
  • C) Public goods are always free
  • D) Government always provides public goods
Show Answer

Answer: B) People consume a public good without paying because they cannot be excluded — private firms cannot profitably provide the good

Non-excludability creates the free-rider problem: rational individuals withhold payment knowing they'll receive the good anyway — so private provision fails.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Public Goods

The efficient quantity of a public good is where:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Supply equals individual demand
  • B) The sum of all individuals' marginal willingness to pay equals the marginal cost (vertical summation of demand)
  • C) One consumer's WTP equals MC
  • D) Government decides how much to provide
Show Answer

Answer: B) The sum of all individuals' marginal willingness to pay equals the marginal cost (vertical summation of demand)

Because public goods are non-rival, all citizens consume the same quantity simultaneously — the social benefit is the SUM of all willingness-to-pay values.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Public Goods

Three citizens have WTP for fireworks: $30, $50, $20. MC of the fireworks display is $80. Should it be provided?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Yes — total WTP=\(100>MC=\)80

Total social WTP = \(30+\)50+\(20=\)100 > MC=\(80 → net social benefit=\)20 → should be provided. Vertical summation: the display benefits all three simultaneously.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Public Goods

Why is ABC Radio justified as a public good even though private radio stations exist?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: ABC Radio is non-rival (one listener doesn't reduce availability to others) and non-excludable (anyone with a radio can listen without paying). Private stations survive via advertising but may underprovide non-commercial content with high social value.

Market failure in non-excludable goods justifies government provision even when private alternatives exist.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Public Goods

Which public good would be hardest to provide privately due to the free-rider problem?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A private toll bridge
  • B) National flood levee systems protecting entire cities
  • C) A cinema screening
  • D) A mobile phone network
Show Answer

Answer: B) National flood levee systems protecting entire cities

Flood levees protect all property in an area regardless of contribution — impossible to exclude non-payers, creating severe free-rider problems.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Public Goods

The tragedy of the commons predicts that common resources will be:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Under-used due to high prices
  • B) Over-used because each user ignores the cost their consumption imposes on others
  • C) Optimally used by community agreement
  • D) Protected by government automatically
Show Answer

Answer: B) Over-used because each user ignores the cost their consumption imposes on others

Each user's private incentive is to maximise their own extraction, ignoring the negative externality on other users — leading to overuse and potential depletion.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Public Goods

Murray-Darling Basin irrigators over-extract water when each farm ignores the external cost on downstream users. If 200 farms each extract 10ML and the socially optimal extraction per farm is 7ML, calculate the total overextraction.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: 600 ML

Total actual extraction = 200×10=2,000ML. Socially optimal = 200×7=1,400ML. Overextraction = 600ML.

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Public Goods

Elinor Ostrom won the Nobel Prize for showing that common resources can be managed sustainably through:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Government nationalisation only
  • B) Privatisation always
  • C) Community-based governance with local rules and monitoring — a third way between privatisation and government control
  • D) Free markets without rules
Show Answer

Answer: B) Privatisation always

Ostrom documented many cases where communities successfully managed common resources through local institutions — disproving the inevitability of the tragedy.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Public Goods

Explain three possible solutions to the tragedy of the commons.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: 1. Privatisation: give individuals property rights in the resource so they internalise the cost of depletion. 2. Government regulation: set quotas, licences, or taxes on extraction. 3. Community management: Ostrom-style collective governance with local rules and enforcement.

Each solution addresses the missing property right or incentive differently.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Public Goods

Australia's fishing quota system assigns transferable catch rights to fishing operators. This is primarily an example of:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Government regulation (command-and-control)
  • B) A Pigouvian tax
  • C) A market-based solution assigning property rights to internalise the common resource externality
  • D) A public good provision
Show Answer

Answer: C) A market-based solution assigning property rights to internalise the common resource externality

Transferable quotas create property rights in fishing — operators bear the cost of depletion and can trade rights to the highest-value users. A market-based Coasian solution.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Public Goods

Cost-benefit analysis (CBA) for public projects evaluates:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Whether the project is profitable for the government
  • B) Whether total social benefits exceed total social costs — including non-market values like time savings, environmental impacts, and lives saved
  • C) Only financial returns on investment
  • D) Whether taxpayers approve of the project
Show Answer

Answer: B) Whether total social benefits exceed total social costs — including non-market values like time savings, environmental impacts, and lives saved

Public sector CBA extends beyond financial returns to include all social costs and benefits, including non-market values difficult to price in markets.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Public Goods

A road upgrade costs $500m. It saves 10 million travel-hours per year valued at $30/hour, lasts 20 years, discount rate 5%. Calculate the rough NPV.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Positive (~$2.24bn benefit vs $500m cost)

Annual benefit = 10m × $30 = $300m. PV of $300m for 20 years at 5% ≈ $300m × 12.46 = $3.74bn. NPV = $3.74bn - $0.5bn = $3.24bn (positive — proceed).

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Public Goods

Why is choosing the discount rate important in CBA for long-lived public projects?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A higher discount rate reduces the present value of future benefits — making long-lived projects look less attractive. Infrastructure with benefits 30-50 years out is particularly sensitive: a 5% vs 3% discount rate can reverse a project's net benefit.

The discount rate reflects society's preference for present vs future benefits — a critical and contested choice in public project evaluation.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Public Goods

Infrastructure Australia evaluates major projects using a benefit-cost ratio (BCR). A BCR of 2.5 means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The project costs $2.50 for every $1 of benefit
  • B) For every $1 of cost the project delivers $2.50 of social benefit
  • C) The project is 2.5 times over budget
  • D) 25% of benefits are social
Show Answer

Answer: B) For every $1 of cost the project delivers $2.50 of social benefit

BCR = PV(benefits)/PV(costs). BCR=2.5 means $2.50 social benefit per $1 cost — a strong positive return.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Public Goods

Which of the following should be included as a benefit in the CBA of a new metro line?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Revenue from ticket sales only
  • B) Ticket revenue plus: reduced road congestion for non-users, lower emissions, agglomeration productivity benefits, reduced accidents
  • C) Construction employment only
  • D) The political benefit to the government
Show Answer

Answer: B) Ticket revenue plus: reduced road congestion for non-users, lower emissions, agglomeration productivity benefits, reduced accidents

CBA must capture all social benefits including externalities and spillovers, not just the operator's financial returns.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Public Goods

Sydney Harbour Tunnel is a club good because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) It is jointly owned by Coles and Woolworths
  • B) It is non-rival at low traffic but excludable via toll — a classic club good
  • C) It is a pure public good
  • D) It is a common resource
Show Answer

Answer: B) It is non-rival at low traffic but excludable via toll — a classic club good

Club goods: excludable (toll) but non-rival up to a congestion threshold. The tunnel becomes rival at peak hour — congestion is the key complication.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Public Goods

WestConnex tolls average $8 per trip. If daily traffic is 200,000 vehicles, calculate daily toll revenue.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $1.6 million

Revenue = $8 × 200,000 = $1,600,000 per day.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Public Goods

Australia's NBN was built with a universal service obligation (USO) requiring coverage of remote areas unlikely to be commercially viable. What economic justification supports this?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Remote broadband access generates positive externalities (enabling remote work, health services, education) and reduces regional inequality. Natural monopoly characteristics mean private provision would cherry-pick profitable urban areas, leaving rural Australians without access.

USO corrects market failure from natural monopoly and positive externalities in underserved regions.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Public Goods

Congestion pricing on roads (charging more at peak times) is efficient because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) It raises maximum government revenue
  • B) It prices the negative externality of congestion — drivers at peak times impose costs on other drivers, and the toll forces them to account for this externality
  • C) It discourages all driving
  • D) It is fairer to charge at peak times
Show Answer

Answer: B) It prices the negative externality of congestion — drivers at peak times impose costs on other drivers, and the toll forces them to account for this externality

Congestion is a negative externality — each additional driver slows all other drivers. A congestion charge internalises this external cost.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Public Goods

Karijini National Park charges an $18 entry fee. This makes it a:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Pure public good
  • B) Common resource
  • C) Club good — excludable (entry fee) but non-rival (additional visitor doesn't reduce others' enjoyment up to a congestion point)
  • D) Private good
Show Answer

Answer: C) Club good — excludable (entry fee) but non-rival (additional visitor doesn't reduce others' enjoyment up to a congestion point)

Entry fees make national parks excludable. Non-rival up to congestion → club good. The user-pays debate: entry fees may reduce access for lower-income Australians.

docs.title: Module M12 Quiz: International Trade and Synthesis
docs.text:
25 questions · Introductory · Mix of multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer. For calculation questions, write out your working first.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Trade

Comparative advantage means a country should specialise in goods where it has:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The lowest absolute cost of production
  • B) The lowest opportunity cost of production relative to other goods
  • C) The highest wages
  • D) The most natural resources
Show Answer

Answer: B) The lowest opportunity cost of production relative to other goods

Comparative advantage is about opportunity cost, not absolute cost. A country has comparative advantage if it gives up less of other goods to produce this good.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Trade

Australia produces iron ore at an opportunity cost of 2 tonnes wheat per tonne of ore. Japan produces at an opportunity cost of 5 tonnes wheat per tonne of ore. Who has comparative advantage in iron ore?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Australia (lower opportunity cost: 2 vs 5 wheat/tonne ore)

Australia gives up less wheat per tonne of ore → lower opportunity cost in ore → comparative advantage in ore.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Trade

Even if Australia is more efficient at producing everything, why should it still trade?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Comparative advantage means even a more efficient country has varying relative efficiencies. By specialising in goods with lowest opportunity cost and trading, Australia can consume beyond its own production possibilities — trade expands consumption options for all parties.

Absolute advantage is irrelevant — only relative opportunity costs determine trade gains.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Trade

Australia's main export advantage lies in:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Manufacturing electronics
  • B) Mining (iron ore, LNG, coal) and agriculture — reflecting our factor endowments (land, mineral resources)
  • C) Tourism only
  • D) Financial services
Show Answer

Answer: B) Mining (iron ore, LNG, coal) and agriculture — reflecting our factor endowments (land, mineral resources)

Factor endowments (abundant land and mineral resources, small population) give Australia comparative advantage in resource-intensive exports.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Trade

The terms of trade refers to:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The tariff rate on imports
  • B) The relative price at which two countries exchange goods — it must lie between each country's domestic opportunity costs for both to gain
  • C) The balance of trade (exports minus imports)
  • D) Trade agreement conditions
Show Answer

Answer: B) The relative price at which two countries exchange goods — it must lie between each country's domestic opportunity costs for both to gain

Terms of trade: the price ratio between exported and imported goods. Both countries gain from trade when the terms of trade lie between their domestic opportunity costs.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Trade

When Australia opens to cheaper imported manufactured goods, which group gains?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Only domestic manufacturers
  • B) Domestic consumers (lower prices) and export industries (trading partners earn AUD to buy Australian exports)
  • C) No one gains from imports
  • D) Only foreign countries
Show Answer

Answer: B) Domestic consumers (lower prices) and export industries (trading partners earn AUD to buy Australian exports)

Imports benefit domestic consumers through lower prices and support Australia's export industries by giving trading partners the income to purchase Australian goods.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Trade

World steel price is $500/tonne; domestic production cost is $700/tonne. Australia opens to imports. Change in consumer surplus if demand is Q=1000-0.5P.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Consumer surplus increases by approx $30,000

At $700: Q_d=650. At $500: Q_d=750. ΔCS ≈ (700-500)×650 + 0.5×(700-500)×100 = $130,000 + $10,000 = $140,000 (using trapezoid method).

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Trade

A tariff on imported steel benefits which group at whose expense?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Consumers at the expense of producers
  • B) Domestic steel producers at the expense of steel-using manufacturers and consumers
  • C) Both producers and consumers
  • D) Foreign steel producers
Show Answer

Answer: B) Domestic steel producers at the expense of steel-using manufacturers and consumers

A tariff raises domestic steel prices — domestic producers gain but all steel-using industries (cars, construction) and consumers face higher costs.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Trade

Australia's automotive industry closed manufacturing by 2017 partly due to import competition. From a welfare perspective this is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Purely negative — jobs were lost
  • B) Ambiguous — workers and shareholders lost but consumers gained lower prices and resources were freed for higher-productivity uses
  • C) Purely positive — consumers benefited
  • D) Neither positive nor negative
Show Answer

Answer: B) Ambiguous — workers and shareholders lost but consumers gained lower prices and resources were freed for higher-productivity uses

Import competition creates distributional effects. The aggregate welfare calculation may be positive but transition costs for displaced workers are real.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Trade

The compensation principle in trade theory states:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) All workers must be compensated for job losses
  • B) The gains from trade are large enough that winners could in principle compensate losers and still be better off — though this may not happen in practice
  • C) Tariffs should compensate domestic producers
  • D) Consumers should pay producers for lower prices
Show Answer

Answer: B) The gains from trade are large enough that winners could in principle compensate losers and still be better off — though this may not happen in practice

The Kaldor-Hicks compensation principle: net positive welfare change means gainers could compensate losers. Whether they actually do is a policy choice.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Trade

A tariff on imports creates deadweight loss because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Government revenue is wasted
  • B) Production is distorted — domestic producers expand at higher than world cost, and consumption is suppressed below the efficient level
  • C) Consumers pay more tax
  • D) Imports are always better than domestic production
Show Answer

Answer: B) Production is distorted — domestic producers expand at higher than world cost, and consumption is suppressed below the efficient level

Tariff DWL: (1) inefficient domestic production that costs more than imports; (2) consumption foregone by consumers who would value imports above world price but less than tariff price.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Trade

World price=\(50; tariff=\)20; domestic supply at $70: Qs=400; domestic demand at $70: Qd=600; imports=200. Government tariff revenue=

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $4,000

Revenue = tariff × imports = $20 × 200 = $4,000

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Trade

Why is a quota generally considered less desirable than a tariff that achieves the same import reduction?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A tariff generates government revenue (area C) while achieving the same import reduction and market price as an equivalent quota. Under a quota, area C accrues to quota licence holders (often importers), not the government — a transfer with no efficiency advantage.

Tariffs and equivalent quotas have the same welfare effects except for who receives area C.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Trade

Australia's tariff on imported cars was 5% when it was phased out. If a $30,000 car had a 5% tariff, what was the tariff amount?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: $1,500

Tariff = 5% × $30,000 = $1,500. This adds $1,500 to the domestic price.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Trade

Which argument for tariff protection has the strongest economic basis?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Protecting all domestic industries equally
  • B) The infant industry argument — temporary protection for industries with learning-by-doing potential that will become internationally competitive
  • C) Protecting high-wage industries
  • D) Retaliating against all trading partners
Show Answer

Answer: B) The infant industry argument — temporary protection for industries with learning-by-doing potential that will become internationally competitive

The infant industry argument has theoretical validity: temporary protection can allow an industry to move down its learning curve to international competitiveness. It requires eventual removal and careful selection.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Trade

Australia's single largest export by value is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Coal
  • B) Education services
  • C) Iron ore
  • D) LNG
Show Answer

Answer: C) Iron ore

Iron ore is consistently Australia's largest export by value, accounting for ~$130bn/year, primarily exported to China.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Trade

ChAFTA (China-Australia Free Trade Agreement) entered into force in 2015. China's tariff on Australian beef was 25%. If Australian beef exports to China were $600m pre-FTA, and doubled to $1.2bn after tariff elimination, estimate the approximate annual tariff removed.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: ~$150m

Pre-FTA: $600m of beef faced 25% tariff = $150m tariff. After FTA, this $150m no longer paid, benefiting Australian producers and Chinese consumers.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Trade

In 2020 China imposed tariffs on Australian barley and restrictions on wine. What economic risk does this illustrate?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Trade concentration risk: when a large share of exports go to a single partner, that partner can use market access as political leverage. Australia's high dependence on China (~30% of exports) creates vulnerability to politically motivated trade restrictions.

Diversification of export markets reduces the leverage any single partner has.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Trade

Which best describes the CPTPP (Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership)?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A bilateral FTA between Australia and Japan
  • B) A regional multilateral trade agreement covering 11 Pacific Rim countries including Australia, Japan, Canada, and Mexico
  • C) A WTO agreement
  • D) A trade deal between Australia and China only
Show Answer

Answer: B) A regional multilateral trade agreement covering 11 Pacific Rim countries including Australia, Japan, Canada, and Mexico

The CPTPP is a 11-country regional FTA covering ~500m people and 13% of global GDP, with deep provisions on services, investment, and IP.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Trade

Australia's education exports (~$35bn pre-COVID) were disrupted by border closures in 2020-21. This illustrates:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The benefits of trade protection
  • B) The costs of service trade disruption — education exports depend on international student mobility and are highly vulnerable to border policies
  • C) That service exports are unimportant
  • D) That Australia should focus only on goods exports
Show Answer

Answer: B) The costs of service trade disruption — education exports depend on international student mobility and are highly vulnerable to border policies

Service exports (especially education requiring physical presence) are more disruption-prone than goods — a key vulnerability revealed by COVID.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

The five main sources of market failure are:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Taxes, regulation, government spending, inflation, unemployment
  • B) Market power, externalities, public goods, common resources, and information asymmetry
  • C) Supply shocks, demand shocks, bubbles, crashes, and regulation
  • D) Monopoly, oligopoly, monopsony, duopsony, and cartel
Show Answer

Answer: B) Market power, externalities, public goods, common resources, and information asymmetry

The five canonical market failures where competitive markets fail to achieve allocative efficiency: power, externalities, public goods, commons, and information.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

The efficiency-equity trade-off means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Efficient policies always increase equity
  • B) Policies that improve efficiency may worsen the distribution of income — and redistribution may reduce productive incentives
  • C) Equity and efficiency always improve together
  • D) Only equity matters in policy
Show Answer

Answer: B) Policies that improve efficiency may worsen the distribution of income — and redistribution may reduce productive incentives

A key tension in economics: many efficiency-improving policies create winners and losers; redistribution may dampen incentives. Policy must balance both.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

Australia's Gini coefficient is approximately 0.33. A Gini of 0 represents perfect equality and 1 represents maximum inequality. Australia is:

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Moderately unequal — more equal than the US (~0.39) but less equal than Nordic countries (~0.25-0.28)

Gini=0.33 places Australia in the middle of OECD countries — less unequal than developing nations but more unequal than Scandinavian countries.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

Name the five types of market failure and give one Australian policy example for each.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: 1. Market power: ACCC competition regulation. 2. Externalities: carbon price/Safeguard Mechanism. 3. Public goods: ABC, national defence funding. 4. Common resources: Murray-Darling water licences. 5. Information asymmetry: APRA prudential regulation of banks.

Each market failure type requires a different policy instrument.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Market Structure

Which debate in Australian microeconomics most closely mirrors the core efficiency vs equity trade-off?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Whether the RBA should raise rates
  • B) Housing affordability policy — restricting negative gearing reduces investment returns (efficiency cost) but may improve first-home buyer access (equity benefit)
  • C) Whether to join CPTPP
  • D) Whether to privatise Australia Post
Show Answer

Answer: B) Housing affordability policy — restricting negative gearing reduces investment returns (efficiency cost) but may improve first-home buyer access (equity benefit)

Negative gearing reform is the quintessential micro efficiency-equity debate in Australia — investors benefit from the tax concession (efficiency of capital allocation?) vs first home buyers priced out (equity concern).

What does the slope of the budget constraint represent?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Marginal utility
  • B) Opportunity cost of good 1 in terms of good 2
  • C) Total utility
  • D) Income elasticity
Show Answer

Answer: B) Opportunity cost of good 1 in terms of good 2

The slope (-P1/P2) shows how much of good 2 must be sacrificed to obtain more of good 1

Budget constraints are homogeneous of degree zero in prices and income

Answer: B) Violates monotonicity

Thick curves would contain bundles with more of both goods but same utility, violating 'more is better'

docs.text:

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Welfare

The Slutsky equation is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) ∂x1/∂p1 = ∂x1^h/∂p1 - x1(∂x1/∂m)
  • B) ∂x1/∂p1 = ∂x1/∂m - x1^h(∂x1^h/∂p1)
  • C) ∂x1/∂p1 = ∂x1^h/∂p1 + x1(∂x1/∂m)
  • D) ∂x1/∂p1 = ∂x1/∂m + x1^h(∂x1^h/∂p1)
Show Answer

Answer: A) ∂x1/∂p1 = ∂x1^h/∂p1 - x1(∂x1/∂m)

Slutsky: Total effect = Substitution effect (Hicksian) + Income effect

  • D) c1 + c2 = (m1 + m2)/(1+r)

Opportunity cost of leisure is the wage rate
