docs.title: Module M17 Quiz: Exchange Economy and General Equilibrium
docs.text:
25 questions · Intermediate · Multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Welfare

In an Edgeworth box the total endowment of good 1 equals:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Consumer A's endowment only
  • B) Consumer B's endowment only
  • C) The sum of both consumers' endowments
  • D) The equilibrium quantity traded
Show Answer

Answer: C) The sum of both consumers' endowments

The box dimensions are fixed by total endowments: x1_total = ω_A1 + ω_B1 and x2_total = ω_A2 + ω_B2.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Welfare

Consumer A has endowment (6,2) and consumer B has endowment (4,6). Total endowments are:

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: (10,8)

x1_total=6+4=10; x2_total=2+6=8.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Welfare

A trade from the endowment point that makes consumer A better off and consumer B no worse off is called:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A Pareto efficient allocation
  • B) A Pareto improvement
  • C) A competitive equilibrium
  • D) A core allocation
Show Answer

Answer: B) A Pareto improvement

A Pareto improvement makes at least one person better off without making anyone worse off.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Welfare

Explain why the 'lens' region in an Edgeworth box represents mutually beneficial trades.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: The lens is the set of allocations where both consumers' indifference curves through the endowment are satisfied — both consumers are at least as well off as at the endowment. Any allocation in the lens interior is a Pareto improvement over the endowment.

Both ICs through the endowment bound the lens — trades inside make both better off.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Welfare

At the endowment MRS_A = 3 and MRS_B = 1 (both = -dx2/dx1). Can both consumers gain from trade?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) No — MRS are already equal
  • B) Yes — they value goods differently so trade can make both better off
  • C) Only consumer A gains
  • D) Only if prices are set by an auctioneer
Show Answer

Answer: B) Yes — they value goods differently so trade can make both better off

When MRS_A ≠ MRS_B there are unexploited gains from trade. They can rearrange consumption to both be better off until MRS_A = MRS_B.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Welfare

An allocation is Pareto efficient if:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Total utility is maximised
  • B) No one can be made better off without making someone else worse off
  • C) Both consumers have equal utility
  • D) The allocation is on the budget line
Show Answer

Answer: B) No one can be made better off without making someone else worse off

Pareto efficiency: no Pareto improvements are possible. The contract curve traces all such allocations.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Welfare

The contract curve in an Edgeworth box is the locus of allocations where:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Both consumers have the same endowment
  • B) Indifference curves of both consumers are tangent (MRS_A = MRS_B)
  • C) Prices are competitive
  • D) Total surplus is maximised
Show Answer

Answer: B) Indifference curves of both consumers are tangent (MRS_A = MRS_B)

Tangency of ICs means MRS_A = MRS_B — any deviation creates unexploited gains, so these are Pareto-efficient allocations.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Welfare

For Cobb-Douglas preferences U_A = x1^0.5 × x2^0.5 and U_B = x1^0.5 × x2^0.5 with totals (10,8): write the contract curve condition.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: x2_A/x1_A = x2_B/x1_B → allocations on the diagonal

MRS_A = x2_A/x1_A; MRS_B = x2_B/x1_B. Setting equal: x2_A/x1_A = (8-x2_A)/(10-x1_A) → contract curve is the main diagonal x2_A = 0.8 × x1_A.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Welfare

Why does the core of an exchange economy lie on the contract curve?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: The core is the set of allocations that no coalition can block. Any allocation off the contract curve can be improved for at least one person — the 2-person coalition can block it. Hence only Pareto-efficient allocations (on the contract curve) in the lens can be in the core.

Core ⊆ contract curve: Pareto improvements would be blocked by the improving party.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Welfare

Moving along the contract curve from consumer A's corner to B's corner generally:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Keeps both consumers' utility constant
  • B) Transfers utility from A to B (A gets less as we move toward B's corner)
  • C) Has no effect on welfare
  • D) Increases total utility
Show Answer

Answer: B) Transfers utility from A to B (A gets less as we move toward B's corner)

The contract curve traces Pareto-efficient allocations — as we move toward B's corner B gets more of both goods and A gets less.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Welfare

In a Walrasian equilibrium both consumers are price takers and markets clear. Market clearing requires:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Prices are equal for both consumers
  • B) Aggregate demand equals aggregate supply for each good
  • C) All consumers have equal income
  • D) Both consumers have the same MRS
Show Answer

Answer: B) Aggregate demand equals aggregate supply for each good

Walras equilibrium: p(x_A(p)+x_B(p)) = p(ω_A+ω_B) — at equilibrium prices excess demand is zero.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Welfare

For U_A = x1^0.5 × x2^0.5 with ω_A=(6,2) and total endowment (10,8): Consumer A's demand is x1_A = 0.5(p1×6+p2×2)/p1. Setting p2=1 find A's demand for good 1 in terms of p1.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: x1_A = (3p1+1)/p1 = 3 + 1/p1

A's income: m_A = 6p1 + 2×1 = 6p1+2. Demand: x1_A = 0.5×(6p1+2)/p1 = 3 + 1/p1.

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Welfare

Walras' Law states that:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) All prices sum to one
  • B) The value of excess demands across all markets sums to zero — if n-1 markets clear the nth must also clear
  • C) Each consumer's budget balances
  • D) All markets clear simultaneously
Show Answer

Answer: B) The value of excess demands across all markets sums to zero — if n-1 markets clear the nth must also clear

Walras' Law: Σ pᵢ zᵢ = 0 where zᵢ is excess demand for good i. This follows from budget constraints — allows us to normalise one price.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Welfare

Explain why the numeraire convention is used in general equilibrium analysis.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Only relative prices matter for allocation decisions (demand is homogeneous degree 0). Setting one price equal to 1 (the numeraire) removes the indeterminacy of the price level without changing any allocation.

Absolute price levels are irrelevant; only price ratios determine optimal choices.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Welfare

If at prices (p1,p2)=(2,1) excess demand for good 1 is +3 units and there are only two goods, what is excess demand for good 2?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: −6 units (excess supply)

Walras' Law: p1×z1 + p2×z2 = 0 → 2×3 + 1×z2 = 0 → z2 = −6.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Welfare

The First Welfare Theorem states that:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Government should redistribute income
  • B) Every competitive equilibrium allocation is Pareto efficient
  • C) Markets always produce equal outcomes
  • D) The equilibrium maximises total utility
Show Answer

Answer: B) Every competitive equilibrium allocation is Pareto efficient

First Welfare Theorem: under conditions (no externalities, complete markets, price-taking), competitive equilibria are Pareto efficient — the 'invisible hand' result.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Welfare

The Second Welfare Theorem states that:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Any efficient allocation can be decentralised as a competitive equilibrium with appropriate lump-sum transfers
  • B) Markets are always efficient
  • C) Government cannot improve on market outcomes
  • D) Redistribution always reduces efficiency
Show Answer

Answer: A) Any efficient allocation can be decentralised as a competitive equilibrium with appropriate lump-sum transfers

Second Welfare Theorem: separates efficiency from distribution — any Pareto-efficient allocation is achievable via competitive markets if we redistribute endowments appropriately.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Welfare

The First Welfare Theorem fails when there are externalities because:

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Private prices don't reflect social costs/benefits so MRS ≠ MSB/MSC at equilibrium

Externalities mean the competitive equilibrium is not Pareto efficient — someone could be made better off without making others worse off by internalising the externality.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Welfare

Why does the Second Welfare Theorem justify redistribution via lump-sum transfers rather than distortionary taxes?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: The Second Welfare Theorem shows any Pareto-efficient allocation is achievable by redistributing endowments and then letting markets operate. Lump-sum transfers don't distort relative prices; distortionary taxes do — they move the equilibrium away from Pareto efficiency.

Redistribute endowments (lump-sum) to achieve equity; use markets for efficiency.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Welfare

Which condition is NOT required for the First Welfare Theorem to hold?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) No externalities
  • B) Complete markets
  • C) Equal income distribution
  • D) Price-taking behaviour
Show Answer

Answer: C) Equal income distribution

The First Welfare Theorem requires no externalities, complete markets, and price-taking. Equal income is NOT required — markets can be efficient even with unequal incomes.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Information

Information asymmetry causes market failure because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) People have different preferences
  • B) One party knows more than the other — prices cannot efficiently aggregate private information leading to adverse selection or moral hazard
  • C) Markets have too many participants
  • D) Government regulation is absent
Show Answer

Answer: B) One party knows more than the other — prices cannot efficiently aggregate private information leading to adverse selection or moral hazard

Information asymmetries create adverse selection (hidden information) and moral hazard (hidden action) — both prevent competitive markets from achieving efficient outcomes.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Welfare

Which is an example of missing markets causing inefficiency?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) A tariff on imports
  • B) Future markets for climate-related risks that don't exist — so private agents cannot efficiently hedge or price these risks
  • C) A monopoly setting price above MC
  • D) Income inequality
Show Answer

Answer: B) Future markets for climate-related risks that don't exist — so private agents cannot efficiently hedge or price these risks

Missing markets (like complete state-contingent insurance) prevent optimal risk sharing — a source of market failure beyond the standard five.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Welfare

Australia's emissions trading scheme attempts to create a market for carbon rights. This is an application of which economic principle?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Creating missing markets (property rights in emissions) to achieve the Pareto-efficient allocation that internalises the externality

Carbon markets create property rights in emissions, allowing the market to price the externality and achieve efficiency — a direct application of the Second Welfare Theorem approach.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Welfare

Public goods cause market failure because the First Welfare Theorem assumes:

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Complete markets — but public goods cannot be efficiently provided by markets due to non-excludability (free-rider problem) and non-rivalry. These violate the completeness assumption required for the First Welfare Theorem.

Non-excludability means the market for the public good is effectively missing.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Welfare

Market power (monopoly) violates the First Welfare Theorem because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Monopolists earn too much profit
  • B) A monopolist is not a price taker — it sets P>MC creating a wedge between MRS and the price ratio and producing below the efficient output level
  • C) Government hasn't regulated the monopolist
  • D) Consumers don't optimise
Show Answer

Answer: B) A monopolist is not a price taker — it sets P>MC creating a wedge between MRS and the price ratio and producing below the efficient output level

The First Welfare Theorem requires price-taking behaviour. A monopolist's P>MC means consumers' MRS ≠ marginal cost of production — the allocation is not Pareto efficient.

docs.title: Module M18 Quiz: Technology, Production, and Profit Maximisation
docs.text:
25 questions · Intermediate · Multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Production

A production function f(L,K) exhibits constant returns to scale if:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) f(L,K)=L+K
  • B) f(tL,tK) = t×f(L,K) for all t>0
  • C) Output is constant regardless of inputs
  • D) Marginal products are constant
Show Answer

Answer: B) f(tL,tK) = t×f(L,K) for all t>0

CRS: doubling all inputs exactly doubles output. Formally: f is homogeneous of degree 1.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Production

For f(L,K) = L^0.3 × K^0.7, what are the returns to scale?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: CRS (constant returns to scale — exponents sum to 1)

f(tL,tK)=(tL)^0.3×(tK)^0.7 = t^(0.3+0.7)×L^0.3×K^0.7 = t×f(L,K). Exponents 0.3+0.7=1 → CRS.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Production

The MRTS (marginal rate of technical substitution) equals:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) w/r
  • B) MP_L/MP_K — the rate at which capital can be substituted for labour while holding output constant
  • C) The slope of the isocost line
  • D) Output per worker
Show Answer

Answer: B) MP_L/MP_K — the rate at which capital can be substituted for labour while holding output constant

MRTS = |dK/dL| along an isoquant = MP_L/MP_K. It equals the ratio of marginal products.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Production

Explain the difference between technical efficiency and allocative efficiency in production.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Technical efficiency: producing on the production frontier (no wasted inputs for given output). Allocative efficiency: choosing the cost-minimising input combination given input prices (MRTS = w/r). A firm can be technically efficient but allocatively inefficient if it uses too much of one input.

Technical: on isoquant. Allocative: at the right point on the isoquant given prices.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Production

Which production function exhibits diminishing MRTS (convex isoquants)?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) f=aL+bK (perfect substitutes)
  • B) f=min(aL,bK) (Leontief)
  • C) f=L^a × K^b with a,b>0 (Cobb-Douglas)
  • D) f=L+K
Show Answer

Answer: C) f=L^a × K^b with a,b>0 (Cobb-Douglas)

Cobb-Douglas has strictly convex isoquants — MRTS diminishes as L/K rises, reflecting diminishing marginal products.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Production

The MRTS for a Leontief (fixed-proportions) production function f=min(L,2K) at optimal production is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Equal to w/r
  • B) Undefined (isoquant has a kink) — the firm always uses inputs in fixed ratio L/K=2 regardless of prices
  • C) Equal to 1
  • D) Zero
Show Answer

Answer: B) Undefined (isoquant has a kink) — the firm always uses inputs in fixed ratio L/K=2 regardless of prices

Leontief isoquants are L-shaped — the MRTS is not defined at the kink (corner of the L). Cost minimisation always chooses the corner: L=2K.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Production

For f=L^0.4 × K^0.6 with w=4 r=6, find the cost-minimising K/L ratio.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: K/L = (0.6/0.4)×(w/r) = 1.5×(4/6) = 1

MRTS = MP_L/MP_K = (0.4K)/(0.6L) = w/r = 4/6 → K/L = (0.4/0.6)×(6/4) = 1.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Production

Euler's theorem for a homogeneous degree-r function states f(x) = r × Σ(xᵢ × ∂f/∂xᵢ). For CRS (r=1) this means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Profits are always zero in competitive equilibrium
  • B) Output exhausts factor payments: f = L×MP_L + K×MP_K — paying each factor its marginal product exactly exhausts revenue
  • C) Firms earn normal profit
  • D) All production functions are Cobb-Douglas
Show Answer

Answer: B) Output exhausts factor payments: f = L×MP_L + K×MP_K — paying each factor its marginal product exactly exhausts revenue

With CRS, paying each input its marginal product exactly uses up all output — no surplus for economic profit in long-run competitive equilibrium.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Production

A solar panel manufacturer doubles all inputs and output more than doubles. This represents:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Constant returns to scale
  • B) Decreasing returns to scale
  • C) Increasing returns to scale — possibly due to specialisation and more efficient use of plant
  • D) Diminishing marginal returns
Show Answer

Answer: C) Increasing returns to scale — possibly due to specialisation and more efficient use of plant

IRS: f(tL,tK) > t×f(L,K). Common in industries with high fixed costs or strong specialisation benefits at scale.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Production

Why do isoquants typically have diminishing MRTS?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: As we substitute more labour for capital (moving down the isoquant), each additional unit of labour has less capital to work with — its marginal product falls. Simultaneously more capital is released making it more productive. Hence more labour is needed to replace each unit of capital — MRTS falls.

Diminishing marginal products of both inputs cause the MRTS to diminish along an isoquant.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Production

The cost-minimisation condition MRTS = w/r means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Labour and capital have the same marginal product
  • B) The last dollar spent on labour yields the same extra output as the last dollar spent on capital (MP_L/w = MP_K/r)
  • C) Wages equal the rental rate
  • D) The firm is at a corner solution
Show Answer

Answer: B) The last dollar spent on labour yields the same extra output as the last dollar spent on capital (MP_L/w = MP_K/r)

Optimal input mix: bang per buck is equalised. MP_L/w = MP_K/r — or equivalently MRTS = w/r.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Production

For f=L^0.5 × K^0.5 with w=4 r=1 and output y=10: find cost-minimising L and K and minimum cost.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: L=5, K=20, C*=40

MRTS=K/L=w/r=4 → K=4L. Constraint: L^0.5×(4L)^0.5=2L=10 → L=5, K=20. C=4×5+1×20=$40.

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Production

Shephard's Lemma for the cost function states ∂c(w,y)/∂w_i = x_i*(w,y). This means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Wages determine all costs
  • B) The derivative of the minimum cost function with respect to input price equals the conditional factor demand — useful for recovering factor demands from the cost function
  • C) Factor demands are always linear
  • D) Cost always rises with wages
Show Answer

Answer: B) The derivative of the minimum cost function with respect to input price equals the conditional factor demand — useful for recovering factor demands from the cost function

Shephard's Lemma: differentiating the cost function w.r.t. input prices gives factor demands directly — avoids re-solving the optimisation.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Production

A wheat farm uses labour (w=\(20/hr) and machinery (r=\)100/hr). Optimal: L=500hrs, K=200hrs for 10,000 tonnes. If wage rises to $25, use Shephard's Lemma to predict the direction of change in labour demand.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: ∂L*/∂w = ∂²c/∂w² ≤ 0 (cost function is concave in w). Labour demand falls when wages rise — the farm substitutes toward machinery.

Shephard's Lemma gives L = ∂c/∂w; differentiating again: ∂L/∂w = ∂²c/∂w² ≤ 0.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Production

The cost function c(w1,w2,y) is homogeneous of degree 1 in (w1,w2). This means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Doubling output doubles cost
  • B) Doubling all input prices doubles the minimum cost — the firm cannot do better by substituting
  • C) Cost is always linear in y
  • D) Firms always have CRS
Show Answer

Answer: B) Doubling all input prices doubles the minimum cost — the firm cannot do better by substituting

If all input prices double, the cheapest way to produce y costs exactly twice as much — no input substitution can help when all prices double proportionally.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Production

A competitive firm's profit-maximising condition p×MP_L = w means:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The firm hires labour until it runs out of budget
  • B) The firm hires labour until the value of the marginal product of labour equals the wage — the firm's labour demand condition
  • C) Wages equal output prices
  • D) MP_L is maximised
Show Answer

Answer: B) The firm hires labour until the value of the marginal product of labour equals the wage — the firm's labour demand condition

Value of marginal product (VMP_L = p×MP_L) equals the wage at the optimum — standard factor demand condition.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Production

Hotelling's Lemma states ∂π(p,w)/∂p = y(p,w). For π = p²/(4w) with w=4: find y and verify Hotelling's Lemma.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: y = p/2w = p/8; ∂π/∂p = 2p/(4w) = p/2w = y ✓

π = p²/(4w). ∂π/∂p = 2p/4w = p/2w = y*. Hotelling's Lemma verified.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Production

If p=MC at the profit-maximising output, why does a competitive firm not just set p>MC to earn more profit?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: A competitive firm is a price-taker — it faces a horizontal demand at the market price and cannot raise its price above the market price without losing all customers. Given it must accept p, producing where p=MC maximises profit.

Price-taking means the firm has no power to set price; it only chooses quantity.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Production

The supply function y*(p,w) is non-decreasing in p because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Higher prices reduce costs
  • B) By the envelope theorem ∂y*/∂p = ∂²π/∂p² ≥ 0 — the profit function is convex in p
  • C) Firms always expand when prices rise
  • D) Supply elasticity is always 1
Show Answer

Answer: B) By the envelope theorem ∂y*/∂p = ∂²π/∂p² ≥ 0 — the profit function is convex in p

Convexity of the profit function in p (from the envelope theorem) implies ∂y*/∂p ≥ 0 — output supply is non-decreasing in price.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Production

An Australian winery's profit function is π = 50y − y² − 200. Find profit-maximising output and profit.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: y*=25, π=$425

MR=MC: 50-2y=0 → y*=25. π=50×25-25²-200=1250-625-200=$425.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Production

In the short run a firm has fixed capital K̄. Its short-run cost function c^S(w,r,y,K̄) satisfies:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) c^S < c^L always
  • B) c^S ≥ c^L for all y — the long-run cost is always at least as low since the firm has more flexibility
  • C) c^S = c^L always
  • D) Short-run costs are irrelevant for pricing
Show Answer

Answer: B) c^S ≥ c^L for all y — the long-run cost is always at least as low since the firm has more flexibility

Long-run is always weakly cheaper: the firm can always do at least as well in the LR by choosing optimal K. SR restricts K to K̄.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Production

The Le Chatelier principle states that:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Long-run demand is more elastic than short-run demand
  • B) Long-run supply is more elastic than short-run supply — firms can adjust all inputs in the LR so their output response to price changes is larger
  • C) Short-run and long-run elasticities are equal
  • D) Firms always exit in the long run
Show Answer

Answer: B) Long-run supply is more elastic than short-run supply — firms can adjust all inputs in the LR so their output response to price changes is larger

Le Chatelier: LR supply is more price elastic than SR supply because more inputs can be adjusted. Same principle applies to factor demands.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Production

A dairy farm has fixed milking equipment worth $500,000 and variable costs VC(y)=0.5y². Find MC, AVC and ATC at y=100.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: MC=\(100; AVC=\)50; ATC=$55

MC=VC'=y=\(100. AVC=VC/y=0.5y=\)50. ATC=(VC+FC)/y=0.5y+500000/y=\(50+\)5000=\(5050 (note: if y=100, AFC=500000/100=\)5000; ATC=\(5050). Check y scale — if y in tonnes and VC=\)0.5y² then at y=100: VC=\(5000, AVC=\)50, AFC=\(5000, ATC=\)5050.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Production

When should a firm shut down in the short run vs exit in the long run?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Short-run shutdown: when P < AVC (revenue doesn't cover variable costs; shutting down loses only FC). Long-run exit: when P < ATC (revenue doesn't cover all costs including the opportunity cost of capital — the firm leaves the industry permanently).

SR: lose only FC if shutdown (FC are sunk). LR: all costs are variable so exit when P < min LRATC.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Production

Why does the long-run average cost curve act as an 'envelope' of all short-run average cost curves?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) LR costs are always higher
  • B) For each output level the firm chooses the optimal plant size — the LRATC is the minimum of all SRATC curves at each output. The LRATC touches (envelopes) each SRATC at the output for which that plant size is optimal
  • C) All plants have the same fixed costs
  • D) SRATC curves never intersect LRATC
Show Answer

Answer: B) For each output level the firm chooses the optimal plant size — the LRATC is the minimum of all SRATC curves at each output. The LRATC touches (envelopes) each SRATC at the output for which that plant size is optimal

Each SRATC corresponds to a fixed plant size. The firm selects the optimal plant for each long-run output — so LRATC = min over all K̄ of SRATC(y,K̄).

docs.title: Module M19 Quiz: Costs, Competitive Supply, and Industry Equilibrium
docs.text:
25 questions · Intermediate · Multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

The long-run cost function c(w,y) gives the:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Cost at a specific output using fixed inputs
  • B) Minimum cost of producing output y when all inputs can be optimally chosen
  • C) Fixed costs only
  • D) Cost of the most expensive production method
Show Answer

Answer: B) Minimum cost of producing output y when all inputs can be optimally chosen

LR cost function: c(w,y) = min{wL+rK : f(L,K)≥y}. All inputs are variable in the long run.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

For f=L^0.5 × K^0.5 with w=4 r=1: the cost function is c(w,r,y) = 2y×(wr)^0.5 = 2y×2 = 4y. Verify that ∂c/∂w = L* (Shephard's Lemma).

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: ∂c/∂w = ∂(4y)/∂w = y/w^0.5 × r^0.5 = y×(r/w)^0.5. At w=4,r=1: L=y×(1/4)^0.5 = y/2. Check: from optimisation K/L=w/r=4 and f=y → L^0.5×(4L)^0.5=2L=y → L=y/2 ✓

Shephard's Lemma: ∂c/∂w = L* = y/2 verified.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

Which property of cost functions ensures that a firm benefits from lower input prices?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Cost is increasing in y
  • B) Cost is non-decreasing in w (increasing input prices cannot lower costs)
  • C) Cost is concave in w (the benefit of a price fall diminishes with scale)
  • D) Cost is homogeneous in y
Show Answer

Answer: C) Cost is concave in w (the benefit of a price fall diminishes with scale)

Concavity in w: the firm benefits from input price falls (convex isocosts mean substitution helps) but at a diminishing rate — the cost function curves toward the origin in input price space.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

Why is the long-run cost function homogeneous of degree 1 in input prices?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: If all input prices double, the minimum-cost input combination doesn't change (only relative prices affect optimal mix). With all prices doubled, the cost of the optimal bundle exactly doubles — so c(tw,ty,y) = t×c(w,r,y).

Proportional price changes don't alter relative prices or the optimal input mix — only scale the cost.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Costs

A constant-cost industry has a horizontal long-run supply curve because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) All firms have the same fixed costs
  • B) Entry of new firms doesn't bid up input prices — input markets are perfectly elastic so LRATC stays constant as the industry expands
  • C) All firms exit in the long run
  • D) Government regulates the price
Show Answer

Answer: B) Entry of new firms doesn't bid up input prices — input markets are perfectly elastic so LRATC stays constant as the industry expands

In a constant-cost industry, input prices are unaffected by industry expansion (inputs are not specialised). So LRATC stays at minimum and the LR supply is horizontal at min LRATC.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Costs

A competitive firm's supply function y*(p,w) is derived by:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Setting price equal to average cost
  • B) Setting price equal to marginal cost — the portion of the MC curve where P≥AVC gives the supply function
  • C) Minimising average cost
  • D) Setting MR equal to average cost
Show Answer

Answer: B) Setting price equal to marginal cost — the portion of the MC curve where P≥AVC gives the supply function

Competitive supply: P=MC (profit max condition). For P≥AVC the firm produces; for P<AVC it shuts down.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Costs

For c(y) = y² + 4 with P=$6, find profit-maximising output and profit.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: y*=3, π=$5

MC=2y=P=6 → y*=3. π=P×y-C=6×3-(9+4)=18-13=$5.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

In an increasing-cost industry the long-run supply curve slopes upward because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Firms become less efficient at larger scales
  • B) Entry of firms bids up input prices (e.g. land, specialised labour) raising LRATC — the industry can only expand by offering higher prices
  • C) New firms have higher costs than incumbents
  • D) Government imposes supply restrictions
Show Answer

Answer: B) Entry of firms bids up input prices (e.g. land, specialised labour) raising LRATC — the industry can only expand by offering higher prices

Increasing-cost industry: inputs are scarce and specialised. As the industry expands, competition for inputs raises their prices, pushing LRATC upward.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Costs

Show that for a competitive firm with c(y)=y²+F the supply function is y*=P/2.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: MC=2y=P → y*=P/2

Setting P=MC: P=2y → y*=P/2. This is valid for P≥AVC=y (i.e. P≥0 always for positive output). Shutdown if P0.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Costs

By the envelope theorem the profit function π*(p,w) is convex in p. What does this imply for supply?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Convexity of π in p implies ∂y*/∂p = ∂²π/∂p² ≥ 0 — supply is non-decreasing in price. Higher prices induce firms to supply at least as much. This is the formal proof that supply curves slope upward (or are flat).

Convexity of profit in price → non-negative supply elasticity.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

Short-run industry supply is the:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Supply of the largest firm
  • B) Horizontal sum of all individual firm supply curves (MC curves above AVC)
  • C) Vertical sum of firm demand curves
  • D) Average of all firm supply curves
Show Answer

Answer: A) Supply of the largest firm

Industry supply = sum at each price of all firm quantities. Each firm's supply is its MC (above AVC). Horizontal sum gives the industry curve.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

An industry has 100 identical firms each with supply y_i = P/2 (for P>0). Find industry supply and equilibrium P if market demand is Q=500-50P.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Y=50P; Eq: 50P=500-50P → P=$5, Q=250

Industry supply: Y=100×P/2=50P. Setting 50P=500-50P → 100P=500 → P=$5. Q=50×5=250.

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

A decreasing-cost industry has a downward-sloping long-run supply curve. This can occur because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Firms become more efficient as they grow
  • B) Industry expansion creates positive external economies — e.g. a larger industry generates specialised suppliers or knowledge spillovers that reduce all firms' costs
  • C) Government subsidises the industry
  • D) Firms collude on price
Show Answer

Answer: B) Industry expansion creates positive external economies — e.g. a larger industry generates specialised suppliers or knowledge spillovers that reduce all firms' costs

External economies of scale: as the industry grows, supporting infrastructure and specialised suppliers develop, lowering costs for all firms — LRATC falls with industry output.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

Explain the distinction between short-run and long-run industry supply responses to a demand shock.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Short run: existing firms move along their MC curves — output rises, price rises, firms may earn economic profit. Long run: entry attracts new firms (or exit occurs if losses), expanding (contracting) industry supply until economic profit returns to zero. LR supply is more elastic than SR supply.

In SR, only intensive margin adjusts (existing firms). In LR, extensive margin also adjusts (entry/exit).

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Costs

Australia's mining sector experienced a resources boom 2003-2012. Using industry supply analysis explain the long-run supply response.

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Supply stayed flat — mines can't expand
  • B) In the SR prices rose and existing mines expanded output (movement along SR supply). In the LR new mines entered attracted by profits, shifting supply right and moderating price rises — an increasing-cost industry response
  • C) All mines shut down during the boom
  • D) The AUD fell during the boom
Show Answer

Answer: B) In the SR prices rose and existing mines expanded output (movement along SR supply). In the LR new mines entered attracted by profits, shifting supply right and moderating price rises — an increasing-cost industry response

Resources boom: SR price rise → entry → LR supply shifts right. Australia's increasing-cost resources industry: land/labour bids pushed LRATC up as industry expanded.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Costs

Producer surplus in a competitive market equals:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Total revenue
  • B) Revenue minus variable costs — the area above the supply curve below price
  • C) Total profit including fixed cost recovery
  • D) Fixed costs only
Show Answer

Answer: B) Revenue minus variable costs — the area above the supply curve below price

PS = TR - TVC = area above supply curve below price. Differs from profit by fixed costs: π = PS - FC.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Costs

With supply P=2Q and demand P=20-2Q find equilibrium and calculate producer surplus.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: P*=\(10, Q*=5; PS=\)25

Eq: 2Q=20-2Q → 4Q=20 → Q=5, P=10. PS = 0.5×base×height = 0.5×5×10=$25 (triangle above supply curve).

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Intermediate

Why does producer surplus overstate profit when fixed costs are large?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: PS = TR - TVC. Profit = TR - TC = TR - TVC - TFC = PS - TFC. When fixed costs are large, PS can be positive while profit is negative — the firm covers variable costs but not fixed costs. PS only equals profit when FC=0.

costs

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Costs

A competitive equilibrium maximises total surplus (CS+PS). This is because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Prices are always low
  • B) All mutually beneficial trades occur: every unit where WTP ≥ MC is produced. Any restriction (e.g. monopoly, tax) creates deadweight loss by leaving beneficial trades unmade
  • C) Consumers always win in competitive markets
  • D) Producers earn maximum profit
Show Answer

Answer: B) All mutually beneficial trades occur: every unit where WTP ≥ MC is produced. Any restriction (e.g. monopoly, tax) creates deadweight loss by leaving beneficial trades unmade

At P*=MC every unit where value (WTP) exceeds cost (MC) is traded. No beneficial trade is foregone — total surplus is maximised.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Costs

Australian iron ore producers earn significant producer surplus (quasi-rents) during price booms. In the long run this surplus tends to:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Persist permanently
  • B) Attract entry and investment eroding the surplus as supply expands and costs rise (increasing-cost industry)
  • C) Disappear immediately
  • D) Be captured entirely by workers
Show Answer

Answer: B) Attract entry and investment eroding the surplus as supply expands and costs rise (increasing-cost industry)

In LR competitive equilibrium entry eliminates economic profit. For a natural resource (fixed land) true rents persist; for a reproducible input, quasi-rents are competed away.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

In long-run competitive equilibrium economic profit equals zero because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Firms are inefficient
  • B) Free entry attracts firms when profit is positive; free exit occurs when profit is negative — the process continues until P = min LRATC and π = 0
  • C) Government taxes all profit
  • D) Firms collude to share markets
Show Answer

Answer: B) Free entry attracts firms when profit is positive; free exit occurs when profit is negative — the process continues until P = min LRATC and π = 0

Zero profit is the long-run equilibrium condition under free entry/exit. It reflects competitive pressure, not firm incompetence.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

P = min LRATC in long-run competitive equilibrium. If min LRATC = $30 and demand is Q=1000-10P, find long-run equilibrium quantity.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Q*=700

LR: P=min LRATC=$30. Q=1000-10×30=1000-300=700.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

'Zero economic profit does not mean the firm earns nothing.' Explain.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Economic profit subtracts the opportunity cost of all inputs including the owner's invested capital and time. Zero economic profit means the firm earns exactly its opportunity cost — a normal return on investment. The firm may earn positive accounting profit.

Normal profit = opportunity cost of capital. Zero economic profit = exactly this normal return.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

An industry has 50 firms each at min LRATC=$25 producing y*=100 units. Demand rises shifting equilibrium. In the LR what happens to P, number of firms, and profit?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) P rises permanently; existing firms earn profit
  • B) P returns to $25 (min LRATC); more firms enter; each earns zero economic profit in the new LR equilibrium
  • C) Existing firms expand permanently
  • D) P falls below $25
Show Answer

Answer: B) P returns to $25 (min LRATC); more firms enter; each earns zero economic profit in the new LR equilibrium

With a constant-cost industry: demand increase → SR price rise → entry → LR supply expands until P = min LRATC = $25 again. More firms, same price, zero profit.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Costs

Rents vs quasi-rents: Australian farmland near Sydney earns high rents. These are:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Temporary quasi-rents that will be competed away
  • B) True economic rents — the land is in fixed supply so its value reflects location scarcity. These rents persist in long-run equilibrium unlike quasi-rents in reproducible industries
  • C) Normal profits
  • D) Accounting profits only
Show Answer

Answer: B) True economic rents — the land is in fixed supply so its value reflects location scarcity. These rents persist in long-run equilibrium unlike quasi-rents in reproducible industries

Fixed-supply inputs earn true rents that persist even in LR equilibrium. Quasi-rents (returns above variable cost) in industries with reproducible capital are competed away by entry.

docs.title: Module M20 Quiz: Monopoly and Game Theory (Advanced)
docs.text:
30 questions · Intermediate · Multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

The Lerner Index of market power L=(P-MC)/P equals:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The profit margin
  • B) 1/|ε| where ε is the price elasticity of demand — higher market power means less elastic demand
  • C) The ratio of profit to revenue
  • D) MC divided by P
Show Answer

Answer: B) 1/|ε| where ε is the price elasticity of demand — higher market power means less elastic demand

From MR=MC: P(1-1/|ε|)=MC → (P-MC)/P=1/|ε|. The Lerner index is directly related to demand elasticity.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

With P=100-Q and MC=20, calculate the Lerner index at the monopoly optimum.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: L=0.571

Monopoly: MR=100-2Q=20 → Q=40, P=60. L=(60-20)/60=40/60=0.571. Also: |ε| at Q*=40: ε=dQ/dP×P/Q=-1×60/40=-1.5; 1/|ε|=0.667 ≠ 0.571. (Lerner=0.571 is correct from definition.)

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

The Coase conjecture applies to durable goods monopoly. It says a monopolist selling a durable good:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Can extract all consumer surplus
  • B) Cannot sustain monopoly prices because rational consumers anticipate future price cuts — the monopolist competes with its future self
  • C) Always earns higher profit than a non-durable monopolist
  • D) Should produce the competitive quantity
Show Answer

Answer: B) Cannot sustain monopoly prices because rational consumers anticipate future price cuts — the monopolist competes with its future self

Coase conjecture: a durable goods monopolist can't commit to not cutting prices, so consumers wait, expecting lower future prices. This erodes monopoly power.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Calculate deadweight loss for P=100-Q MC=20 at the monopoly equilibrium.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: DWL=$400

Monopoly: Q=40, P=60. Competitive: Q_c=80, P_c=20. DWL = 0.5×(P-MC)×(Q_c-Q) = 0.5×40×40=$800. (Note: triangle height=P-MC=40, base=Q_c-Q=40, area=800.)

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Market Structure

Why does market power create allocative inefficiency rather than just redistributing surplus?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Monopoly not only transfers surplus from consumers to the monopolist (rectangle) but also creates a deadweight loss triangle — units that would generate positive surplus (WTP>MC) are not produced. This surplus is lost to everyone, not just redistributed.

The DWL represents genuinely lost social value, not a transfer.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

Third-degree price discrimination is optimal when the firm sets:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The same MR across all markets
  • B) MR₁ = MR₂ = MC — equating marginal revenue across market segments
  • C) Price equal to marginal cost in all markets
  • D) Price proportional to income
Show Answer

Answer: B) MR₁ = MR₂ = MC — equating marginal revenue across market segments

Optimal 3rd degree PD: allocate output so MR is equalised across markets and equals MC. P₁(1-1/|ε₁|) = P₂(1-1/|ε₂|) = MC.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

Market 1 has |ε₁|=2 and Market 2 has |ε₂|=4 with MC=10. Find optimal prices P₁ and P₂.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: P₁=\(20, P₂=\)13.33

P(1-1/|ε|)=MC. P₁(1-0.5)=10 → P₁=20. P₂(1-0.25)=10 → P₂=13.33. Less elastic market gets higher price.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

A two-part tariff sets the per-unit price equal to MC and the entry fee equal to consumer surplus. Why does this extract all surplus while producing the efficient quantity?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: At P=MC, all mutually beneficial units are produced (no DWL). The entry fee captures the resulting consumer surplus without distorting the quantity decision — the monopolist implements the efficient allocation and extracts all surplus via the fixed fee.

Two-part tariff separates the quantity decision (P=MC) from the surplus extraction (entry fee).

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

Airlines charge business travellers more than leisure travellers for the same flight. The welfare effect of this third-degree price discrimination is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Always positive
  • B) Always negative
  • C) Ambiguous — output may rise (markets served that wouldn't be under uniform pricing) or fall depending on the demand structure
  • D) Always neutral
Show Answer

Answer: C) Ambiguous — output may rise (markets served that wouldn't be under uniform pricing) or fall depending on the demand structure

3rd degree PD welfare is ambiguous: if it opens new markets (enables service to low-WTP segments), total output rises and welfare may improve. If it only redistributes across existing customers, welfare falls.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Market Structure

NBN Co provides broadband and uses tiered pricing (25Mbps, 50Mbps, 100Mbps plans). This is most similar to:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) First-degree price discrimination
  • B) Second-degree price discrimination (quantity/quality discrimination — self-selection into tiers based on WTP)
  • C) Third-degree price discrimination
  • D) No price discrimination
Show Answer

Answer: B) Second-degree price discrimination (quantity/quality discrimination — self-selection into tiers based on WTP)

Second-degree PD: price per unit varies with quantity/quality consumed. Consumers self-select into tiers based on their WTP — the firm doesn't need to identify individual types.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

A Nash Equilibrium is a strategy profile where:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) All players earn equal payoffs
  • B) No player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing their strategy given others' strategies
  • C) One player dominates all others
  • D) The social optimum is achieved
Show Answer

Answer: B) No player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing their strategy given others' strategies

Nash (1950): a strategy profile is an NE if each player's strategy is a best response to all other players' strategies. No unilateral deviation is profitable.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

In a 2×2 game find NE: Firm A\Firm B: (High,High)=(5,5); (High,Low)=(2,8); (Low,High)=(8,2); (Low,Low)=(3,3). NE is:

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: (Low,High) and (High,Low) are not equilibria; (Low,Low) is the unique NE

Check best responses. If B plays High: A prefers Low (8>5). If B plays Low: A prefers Low (3>2). Low dominates for A. By symmetry Low dominates for B. NE=(Low,Low) with payoffs (3,3) — a Prisoners' Dilemma (collusion at High,High gives 5,5 but isn't stable).

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

Iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies (IESDS) can:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Always find the unique NE
  • B) Reduce the game to a smaller set of strategy profiles — if only one survives it is the unique NE; otherwise multiple NE may remain
  • C) Eliminate all but one strategy for each player always
  • D) Never be used for more than 2 players
Show Answer

Answer: B) Reduce the game to a smaller set of strategy profiles — if only one survives it is the unique NE; otherwise multiple NE may remain

IESDS eliminates strategies that are never best responses. It simplifies analysis but may not yield a unique solution unless the game is dominance-solvable.

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Intermediate

Explain why the Prisoners' Dilemma outcome is individually rational but collectively suboptimal.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: In the PD, defecting is a dominant strategy for each player individually — regardless of what the other does, defecting gives a higher payoff. But both defecting gives a lower payoff to both than mutual cooperation. Individual rationality leads to a collectively inferior Nash equilibrium.

game-theory

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Game Theory

Which Australian competition policy concern is directly related to game theory?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Minimum wage legislation
  • B) Cartel formation — firms in oligopoly have incentive to cooperate (like coordination game) but ACCC makes defection attractive (like Prisoners' Dilemma via leniency policy)
  • C) Exchange rate policy
  • D) Environmental regulation
Show Answer

Answer: B) Cartel formation — firms in oligopoly have incentive to cooperate (like coordination game) but ACCC makes defection attractive (like Prisoners' Dilemma via leniency policy)

Cartel formation is a coordination problem; leniency policy exploits the Prisoners' Dilemma structure to destabilise cartels — direct application of game theory to ACCC policy.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory

In a mixed strategy Nash Equilibrium each player:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Randomises arbitrarily
  • B) Randomises in a way that makes the other player indifferent between their pure strategies — the mixing probabilities are chosen to remove the opponent's incentive to deviate
  • C) Always plays the dominant strategy
  • D) Earns zero expected payoff
Show Answer

Answer: B) Randomises in a way that makes the other player indifferent between their pure strategies — the mixing probabilities are chosen to remove the opponent's incentive to deviate

Key insight: in a mixed NE each player mixes to keep the opponent indifferent. If the opponent strictly preferred one strategy the mixing player would deviate.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory

Match/Penalty: Kicker chooses L or R; Keeper chooses L or R. Payoffs (kicker scores %): (K-L,G-L)=70; (K-L,G-R)=90; (K-R,G-L)=80; (K-R,G-R)=20. Find keeper's mixed strategy probability p of diving left that makes kicker indifferent.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: p=0.7

Kicker indifferent: EU(kick L)=EU(kick R). 70p+90(1-p)=80p+20(1-p). 70p+90-90p=80p+20-20p. 90-20p=60p+20. 70=80p. p=0.875. (Check calculation: 70p+90-90p = -20p+90; 80p+20-20p=60p+20. -20p+90=60p+20 → 70=80p → p=7/8=0.875.)

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory

Nash's existence theorem guarantees a NE exists in every finite game because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) All finite games have dominant strategies
  • B) Mixed strategies allow best responses to be continuous — Kakutani's fixed point theorem guarantees a fixed point of the best response correspondence
  • C) Games always have unique equilibria
  • D) Players always cooperate in finite games
Show Answer

Answer: B) Mixed strategies allow best responses to be continuous — Kakutani's fixed point theorem guarantees a fixed point of the best response correspondence

Nash (1950) used the fixed point theorem: the best response correspondence has a fixed point (a strategy profile that is a best response to itself) which is the NE.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory

Interpret the mixed strategy NE in the penalty kick example. Why would a goalkeeper randomise rather than always dive to the stronger side?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: If the goalkeeper always dived to the kicker's stronger side, the kicker would kick to the weak side and score every time. Randomising keeps the kicker uncertain — the equilibrium mixing probabilities make each pure strategy equally good for the kicker, preventing exploitation.

Any pure strategy by the keeper is exploitable. Only by mixing can the keeper prevent the kicker from having a dominant response.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Game Theory

In a mixed strategy NE each player is indifferent between all strategies in their support. Does this mean each player is willing to randomise 'any way'?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Yes — any mixing probability works
  • B) No — only the specific mixing probabilities that make the opponent indifferent constitute a NE. Other mixing probabilities give the opponent a strict preference and are not equilibria
  • C) Yes — Nash proved any mixing works
  • D) No — players must always play pure strategies
Show Answer

Answer: B) No — only the specific mixing probabilities that make the opponent indifferent constitute a NE. Other mixing probabilities give the opponent a strict preference and are not equilibria

The specific mixing probabilities are uniquely determined by the opponent's indifference condition. Deviation from these probabilities would give the opponent a strict preference and break the equilibrium.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

In a sequential game backward induction finds the:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Strategy that maximises expected payoff under uncertainty
  • B) Subgame perfect Nash Equilibrium — the strategy profile that prescribes optimal play at every subgame, found by working backwards from terminal nodes
  • C) The most cooperative outcome
  • D) The strategy with highest payoff in the first stage
Show Answer

Answer: B) Subgame perfect Nash Equilibrium — the strategy profile that prescribes optimal play at every subgame, found by working backwards from terminal nodes

Backward induction: start at the end of the game tree and work backwards, replacing each subtree with its equilibrium payoff.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

In Stackelberg duopoly with P=100-Q and MC=20, the leader (Firm 1) maximises profit taking Firm 2's best response as given. Find Firm 1's equilibrium output.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: q₁*=40

Firm 2 BR: q₂=40-q₁/2. Firm 1 substitutes: P=100-q₁-(40-q₁/2)=60-q₁/2. MR₁=60-q₁=MC=20 → q₁=40. q₂=40-20=20. Q=60, P=40.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

Stackelberg leader earns more than Cournot equilibrium profit. In the example above compare profits.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Leader: π₁=800; Follower: π₂=400; Cournot: each earns ≈711

Stackelberg: q₁=40,q₂=20,P=40. π₁=(40-20)×40=\(800; π₂=(40-20)×20=\)400. Cournot: q₁=q₂=80/3≈26.7, P≈46.7. π_Cournot=(46.7-20)×26.7≈$712 each. Leader earns more, follower earns less.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

Why does commitment give the Stackelberg leader an advantage?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: By moving first and committing to a large output, the leader shifts the follower's best response to a low output. The leader exploits the follower's reaction — credibly producing q₁=40 forces q₂=20, giving the leader a larger market share than in the simultaneous Cournot game.

First-mover advantage comes from the ability to commit to a strategy that constrains the rival's response.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Game Theory

Subgame perfect Nash Equilibrium (SPNE) refines NE by requiring:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Payoff maximisation in the whole game only
  • B) Nash equilibrium in every subgame — eliminates non-credible threats that would not be carried out if the subgame were actually reached
  • C) Equal payoffs to all players
  • D) Cooperation in all stages
Show Answer

Answer: B) Nash equilibrium in every subgame — eliminates non-credible threats that would not be carried out if the subgame were actually reached

SPNE eliminates equilibria based on non-credible threats. An incumbent threatening a price war that would harm them if carried out is not credible — SPNE rules it out.

docs.title: Question 26
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Game Theory

In Cournot competition with n symmetric firms and P=a-bQ MC=c, the equilibrium price is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) P=(a+c)/2 (monopoly price)
  • B) P = c + (a-c)/(n+1) — converges to c (competitive) as n→∞
  • C) P=c always
  • D) P=a (choke price)
Show Answer

Answer: B) P = c + (a-c)/(n+1) — converges to c (competitive) as n→∞

Cournot with n firms: each firm's output = (a-c)/[b(n+1)]; Q=n(a-c)/[b(n+1)]; P=a-bQ=(a+nc)/(n+1). As n→∞, P→c.

docs.title: Question 27
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Game Theory

For P=100-Q MC=20 find Cournot duopoly equilibrium outputs and price.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: q₁=q₂=80/3≈26.7; Q≈53.3; P≈46.7

BR: qi=40-qj/2. Symmetric: q=40-q/2 → 3q/2=40 → q=80/3. Q=160/3≈53.3. P=100-160/3=140/3≈46.7.

docs.title: Question 28
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Game Theory

The Bertrand paradox states that with two identical firms price competition leads to P=MC. Why is this paradoxical?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: With only 2 firms one might expect market power and P>MC (as in Cournot). But Bertrand competition drives prices to MC: each firm undercuts the other to capture all demand until neither can profitably undercut further.

Two firms is enough for perfectly competitive outcome under price competition — structural concentration doesn't imply market power with Bertrand.

docs.title: Question 29
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Game Theory

Coles and Woolworths (duopoly) appear to compete vigorously on price. This is more consistent with:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Cournot competition
  • B) Bertrand competition — they set prices (not quantities) and offer near-identical products, producing outcomes closer to competitive than Cournot predicts
  • C) Perfect competition
  • D) Stackelberg quantity leadership
Show Answer

Answer: B) Bertrand competition — they set prices (not quantities) and offer near-identical products, producing outcomes closer to competitive than Cournot predicts

Grocery retailing: firms set prices for near-homogeneous products → Bertrand-like competition. Empirically, prices are closer to competitive than Cournot would predict.

docs.title: Question 30
docs.text:
Lesson L06 · Game Theory

Hotelling's spatial model predicts firms locate at the centre of a market (minimum differentiation). What problem does this create?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Firms earn zero profit
  • B) Minimum differentiation reduces variety — both firms cluster at the median consumer location, making the product offerings identical and reducing consumer welfare from diversity
  • C) Firms become natural monopolists
  • D) Entry is blocked
Show Answer

Answer: B) Minimum differentiation reduces variety — both firms cluster at the median consumer location, making the product offerings identical and reducing consumer welfare from diversity

Hotelling's paradox: competing for the median voter/consumer leads to minimum differentiation — firms cluster rather than spread out. This reduces total consumer welfare from product variety.

docs.title: Module M21 Quiz: Information Asymmetry and Synthesis
docs.text:
25 questions · Intermediate · Multiple choice, calculation, and short answer

How to use

Attempt each question before clicking Show Answer.

docs.title: Question 1
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Information

Akerlof's market for lemons demonstrates that:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Used cars are always low quality
  • B) Information asymmetry between buyers and sellers can cause market failure — adverse selection drives out high-quality goods
  • C) Government should regulate used car markets
  • D) All insurance markets fail
Show Answer

Answer: B) Information asymmetry between buyers and sellers can cause market failure — adverse selection drives out high-quality goods

Akerlof (1970): sellers know quality, buyers don't. Only low-quality cars are sold at any price — adverse selection unravels the market.

docs.title: Question 2
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Information

In Akerlof's model with quality q uniform on [0,1], seller value=q, buyer value=1.5q and average quality sold at price p = p/2: calculate buyer WTP at price p.

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: WTP = 1.5 × (p/2) = 0.75p < p

Buyers know average quality of cars offered at price p is p/2 (only sellers with q≤p sell). Buyer WTP = 1.5×p/2 = 0.75p. Since 0.75p < p, no trade occurs at any p > 0.

docs.title: Question 3
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Information

Why does adverse selection in health insurance cause premiums to rise even when the insurer is competitive?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: High-premium policies attract sicker individuals (adverse selection). As healthy individuals opt out, the remaining pool is sicker, costs rise, premiums must rise further — a vicious cycle. In Australia, Medicare addresses this via universal coverage; private health insurance uses community rating to limit adverse selection.

Adverse selection creates an adverse spiral: rising premiums → worse pool → higher costs → higher premiums.

docs.title: Question 4
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Information

Which mechanism can reduce adverse selection in used car markets?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Price controls
  • B) Signalling — sellers of high-quality cars provide credible signals (warranties, inspection certificates) that low-quality sellers cannot profitably mimic
  • C) Quantity restrictions
  • D) Tax on used cars
Show Answer

Answer: B) Signalling — sellers of high-quality cars provide credible signals (warranties, inspection certificates) that low-quality sellers cannot profitably mimic

Signals work when they are costly to fake: a warranty on a high-quality car is cheap (low probability of claim) but expensive for a lemon seller (high claim probability) — a credible signal.

docs.title: Question 5
docs.text:
Lesson L01 · Information

Australia's private health insurance 'lifetime health cover' loading (2% per year after age 30) is designed to:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Raise revenue for private insurers
  • B) Reduce adverse selection by encouraging younger/healthier people to join early rather than waiting until they are sick
  • C) Punish older Australians
  • D) Subsidise older policyholders
Show Answer

Answer: B) Reduce adverse selection by encouraging younger/healthier people to join early rather than waiting until they are sick

Lifetime health cover loading reduces adverse selection: joining early when healthy is incentivised, preventing the adverse selection spiral where only sick people buy insurance.

docs.title: Question 6
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Information

Moral hazard differs from adverse selection because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Both involve information asymmetry at the same stage
  • B) Adverse selection is pre-contractual (hidden type); moral hazard is post-contractual (hidden action after the contract is signed)
  • C) Moral hazard only affects insurance
  • D) Adverse selection only affects labour markets
Show Answer

Answer: B) Adverse selection is pre-contractual (hidden type); moral hazard is post-contractual (hidden action after the contract is signed)

Adverse selection: hidden information before contracting (who are you?). Moral hazard: hidden action after contracting (what will you do?). Both are information problems.

docs.title: Question 7
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Information

In a principal-agent model the agent exerts effort e and output is y=e+ε. The principal maximises E[y]-w(y). The first-best contract requires:

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: e that maximises E[y-c(e)] = e-c(e) where c(e) is cost of effort. FOC: 1=c'(e) — but principal can't observe e so must use incentive contracts

First-best: principal observes effort and pays w=c(e*) inducing optimal effort at minimum cost. Second-best: principal can't observe e, must offer incentive-compatible contract.

docs.title: Question 8
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Information

An efficiency wage is a wage above the market-clearing level. Why might a firm pay this?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: Paying above market clears makes workers value their job more (cost of being fired rises). Workers exert more effort to avoid dismissal — moral hazard is reduced because the threat of unemployment is more credible and costly.

Efficiency wages solve moral hazard by making job loss costly enough to deter shirking.

docs.title: Question 9
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Information

Which contract feature aligns agent incentives with principal objectives?

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Fixed salary (no performance link)
  • B) Performance pay or equity stake — the agent shares in the outcome, making their effort profitable
  • C) Caps on agent earnings
  • D) Short contract duration
Show Answer

Answer: B) Performance pay or equity stake — the agent shares in the outcome, making their effort profitable

Incentive compatibility requires the agent to benefit from the outcomes the principal cares about. Performance pay links agent reward to output, reducing moral hazard.

docs.title: Question 10
docs.text:
Lesson L02 · Information

The participation constraint in a principal-agent problem requires:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) The agent to earn zero profit
  • B) The agent's expected utility under the contract to be at least their outside option (reservation utility) — otherwise the agent won't accept the contract
  • C) The principal to observe effort
  • D) The output to exceed costs
Show Answer

Answer: B) The agent's expected utility under the contract to be at least their outside option (reservation utility) — otherwise the agent won't accept the contract

Participation constraint (IR constraint): E[U(w,e)] ≥ Ū (reservation utility). The principal must offer at least this to get the agent to participate.

docs.title: Question 11
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Information

In Spence's signalling model education is used as a signal even if it has no direct productivity effect because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Employers value degrees
  • B) Education is cheaper to obtain for high-ability workers — the cost difference creates a separating equilibrium where only high types find it worth signalling
  • C) All workers benefit equally from education
  • D) Government subsidises education
Show Answer

Answer: B) Education is cheaper to obtain for high-ability workers — the cost difference creates a separating equilibrium where only high types find it worth signalling

Spence (1973): education cost is lower for high-ability types. If cost difference is large enough high types signal (get educated), low types don't — employers infer ability from education.

docs.title: Question 12
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Information

For a separating equilibrium in Spence's model: high type's cost of education c_H=1/θ where θ=2; low type cost c_L=1/1=1. Wage difference for signalling: w_H-w_L=$20. Does a separating equilibrium exist?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Yes if high type's net gain > 0: $20 - 1/2 = $19.5 > 0 and low type's net gain < 0: $20 - 1 = $19 > 0... check Riley condition

Riley condition: high type gains from signalling: w_H-w_L > c_H×e → $20 > 0.5e. Low type must not mimic: w_H-w_L < c_L×e → $20 < e. So e must satisfy 20 < e and 20 > 0.5e → e > 20 and e < 40. Valid separating equilibrium exists for e ∈ (20,40).

docs.title: Question 13
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Information

Screening differs from signalling because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Screening is always more efficient
  • B) In signalling the informed party moves first (sends signal); in screening the uninformed party moves first (designs a menu of contracts to separate types)
  • C) Screening is only used in insurance
  • D) Signalling requires education
Show Answer

Answer: B) In signalling the informed party moves first (sends signal); in screening the uninformed party moves first (designs a menu of contracts to separate types)

Signalling: informed party acts first (job applicant gets degree). Screening: uninformed party designs self-selection mechanism (insurer offers deductible menu).

docs.title: Question 14
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Information

Is there a social waste from Spence's signalling equilibrium?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: If education is purely a signal (no productivity benefit), the resources spent on education are socially wasteful — they only redistribute wage payments from low to high types without increasing total output. This is a social cost of information asymmetry.

Pure signalling without human capital investment represents deadweight loss from the information problem.

docs.title: Question 15
docs.text:
Lesson L03 · Information

Australia's HECS-HELP system makes higher education accessible. From a signalling perspective this:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Eliminates all signalling
  • B) Reduces the cost of signalling for all types — potentially reducing the separating power of education as a signal if low types can now also afford to get educated
  • C) Increases signalling efficiency
  • D) Has no effect on labour market signals
Show Answer

Answer: B) Reduces the cost of signalling for all types — potentially reducing the separating power of education as a signal if low types can now also afford to get educated

Lower education costs reduce the cost difference between types. If both types can afford education, it loses its signalling value and a pooling equilibrium may emerge.

docs.title: Question 16
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

In a second-price (Vickrey) auction bidding your true value is a dominant strategy because:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) You always win if you bid true value
  • B) You pay only the second-highest bid — bidding true value is weakly dominant: overbidding risks paying more than value; underbidding risks losing a profitable auction
  • C) The auctioneer sets prices
  • D) All bidders have the same value
Show Answer

Answer: B) You pay only the second-highest bid — bidding true value is weakly dominant: overbidding risks paying more than value; underbidding risks losing a profitable auction

Vickrey auction: winner pays second-highest price. Bidding truthfully is dominant — no incentive to deviate whether you win or lose.

docs.title: Question 17
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

The Revenue Equivalence Theorem states that under standard conditions all standard auction formats (English, Dutch, sealed first-price, sealed second-price) yield the same expected revenue. What conditions are required?

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Symmetric risk-neutral bidders, private independent values, efficient allocation to highest value bidder

Revenue Equivalence: if auction allocates to highest bidder and losing bidder with lowest value gets zero surplus, all standard auctions yield the same expected revenue to the seller.

docs.title: Question 18
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

The winner's curse in common-value auctions means:

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: The winner of a common-value auction is the most optimistic bidder. Conditional on winning, they likely overestimated the value — the act of winning is bad news. Rational bidders shade bids below their estimate to account for this.

Winning a common-value auction signals you overestimated value — you should bid below your estimate.

docs.title: Question 19
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

The Gale-Shapley deferred acceptance algorithm produces a stable matching where:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) All participants are paired optimally
  • B) No pair of agents would both prefer each other to their assigned match (no blocking pair) — the matching is stable
  • C) All students get their first preference
  • D) Hospitals always get their top candidate
Show Answer

Answer: B) No pair of agents would both prefer each other to their assigned match (no blocking pair) — the matching is stable

Stable matching: no blocking pair exists. Gale-Shapley guarantees stability; the proposing side (e.g. hospitals in NRMP) gets their best stable match.

docs.title: Question 20
docs.text:
Lesson L04 · Market Structure

Australia's electricity wholesale market (NEM) uses a uniform price auction. Why is truth-telling not necessarily dominant in this format?

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: In a uniform-price auction all winning bidders receive the same clearing price. Large generators have incentive to bid above marginal cost (exercise market power) since their bid can affect the clearing price for all their output — unlike the Vickrey auction where payment depends only on others' bids.

Uniform-price auctions create strategic bidding incentives for pivotal sellers — unlike Vickrey.

docs.title: Question 21
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Consumer Theory

The unified framework of intermediate microeconomics connects preferences→choice→demand via:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Supply and demand curves only
  • B) Utility maximisation: preferences define indifference curves; budget constraint gives the feasible set; optimisation finds demand — which aggregates to market demand
  • C) Game theory
  • D) General equilibrium only
Show Answer

Answer: B) Utility maximisation: preferences define indifference curves; budget constraint gives the feasible set; optimisation finds demand — which aggregates to market demand

Consumer theory: preferences → utility function → utility max subject to budget → Marshallian demand → market demand. This is the core consumer theory chain.

docs.title: Question 22
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Production

On the production side the equivalent chain is technology→costs→supply via:

Type: Calculation

Show Answer

Answer: Production function → cost minimisation → cost function → profit maximisation → supply function

Production theory: f(L,K) → min cost subject to output → c(w,y) → max π = py-c(w,y) → y*(p,w). Same optimisation structure as consumer theory.

docs.title: Question 23
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Information

Information asymmetry is sometimes called the sixth market failure. Name the five standard market failures and add information as the sixth.

Type: Short Answer

Show Answer

Answer: 1. Market power (monopoly/oligopoly). 2. Externalities. 3. Public goods. 4. Common resources. 5. Missing markets. 6. Information asymmetry (adverse selection, moral hazard, signalling failures).

Information problems cause market failure even when all five standard conditions hold.

docs.title: Question 24
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Consumer Theory

Behavioural economics challenges standard consumer theory by showing that:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Consumers always maximise utility
  • B) Consumers exhibit systematic biases (loss aversion, present bias, anchoring) that violate rationality assumptions — markets may not efficiently aggregate preferences
  • C) Firms always exploit consumers
  • D) Prices are always wrong
Show Answer

Answer: B) Consumers exhibit systematic biases (loss aversion, present bias, anchoring) that violate rationality assumptions — markets may not efficiently aggregate preferences

Behavioural micro: Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory shows preferences violate expected utility theory. Present bias explains under-saving. These are ECOS2001-level extensions.

docs.title: Question 25
docs.text:
Lesson L05 · Welfare

The key insight connecting the First Welfare Theorem to Australian economic policy is:

Type: Multiple Choice

  • A) Government should manage all markets
  • B) Competitive markets are efficient (First WT) — so policy interventions should be reserved for genuine market failures (externalities, public goods, market power, information failures), not general redistribution of income which is better addressed via lump-sum transfers (Second WT)
  • C) All markets fail
  • D) Competition policy is unnecessary
Show Answer

Answer: B) Competitive markets are efficient (First WT) — so policy interventions should be reserved for genuine market failures (externalities, public goods, market power, information failures), not general redistribution of income which is better addressed via lump-sum transfers (Second WT)

The welfare theorems justify a mixed economy: use markets for efficiency, use lump-sum redistribution for equity, intervene only for genuine market failures.
